topics.title: Catalysts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Catalysts # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Catalyst (IUPAC Definition):** A catalyst is a substance that **increases the rate of a chemical reaction** without being consumed in the overall reaction. It participates in the reaction mechanism by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a **lower activation energy (Ea)**, but is **regenerated** at the end of the catalytic cycle. Mathematically, the action of a catalyst can be expressed as: $$\text{A + Catalyst} \longrightarrow [\text{A} \cdots \text{Catalyst}]^{\ddagger} \longrightarrow \text{Products + Catalyst}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Historical Note (KTU Board Favourite):** Wilhelm Ostwald (1901 Nobel Laureate) provided one of the earliest rigorous definitions: *"A catalyst is a substance which alters the velocity of a chemical reaction without itself appearing in the end products."* This definition is often asked in **Part A (3-mark) questions**. ## 1.2 Classification of Catalysts (Primary Axis) | Basis of Classification | Types | Key Distinguishing Feature | |------------------------|-------|----------------------------| | **Phase Relationship** | Homogeneous | Catalyst and reactants exist in the **same phase** | | | Heterogeneous | Catalyst and reactants are in **different phases** (typically solid catalyst + fluid reactants) | | | Biocatalysts (Enzymes) | Complex protein-based catalysts operating under mild conditions | | **Effect on Rate** | Positive Catalysts | **Accelerate** the reaction rate | | | Negative Catalysts (Inhibitors) | **Retard** the reaction rate | | **Function** | Acid-Base Catalysts | Function via proton transfer | | | Metal/Metal Oxide Catalysts | Function via surface adsorption (d-block metals) | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Pass Tunnel" Imagine two travelers, **Mr. Reactant** and **Ms. Product**, standing on opposite sides of a massive mountain called the **Activation Energy Barrier (Ea)**. - **Without a Catalyst:** Mr. Reactant must climb all the way to the mountain's peak (high Ea) to reach Ms. Product. The climb is exhausting, slow, and energy-intensive. Few travelers make it across per hour. - **With a Catalyst:** A tunnel (the catalytic pathway) is dug through the mountain. Now travelers cross easily via the tunnel (lowered Ea). Many more travelers cross per hour (**rate increases**). - **Crucial Detail:** The tunnel builder (the catalyst) is **not consumed**—it remains to help the next traveler. > [!TIP] > **Student Mnemonic — "PRC-CR":** A catalyst's defining traits are: **P**articipates in mechanism, **R**egenerated at the end, **C**hanges pathway, **R**educes activation energy, **C**annot change equilibrium position. ## 1.4 Why Catalysts Cannot Alter Thermodynamics A point of **frequent confusion** in KTU exams: a catalyst changes **kinetics**, not **thermodynamics**. $$\Delta G^{\circ}, \Delta H^{\circ}, K_{eq} \quad \text{remain UNCHANGED}$$ This is because $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ depends only on the initial and final states (state functions), and a catalyst provides an **alternative pathway**—not a different start or end point. > [!WARNING] > **Common Board Mistake:** Students often write "catalyst shifts equilibrium position." **This is WRONG.** A catalyst only enables the system to **reach equilibrium faster**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Properties of a Catalyst ### 2.1.1 Activity The **activity** of a catalyst is a measure of how fast it converts reactants to products under specified conditions (T, P, concentration). It is quantified by the **rate of reaction per unit mass or per unit surface area** of the catalyst. $$a_{\text{cat}} = \frac{-r_A}{m_{\text{cat}}} \quad \text{[units: mol/(g·s)] or [mol/(m²·s)] for surface-based}$$ ### 2.1.2 Selectivity **Selectivity (S)** is the ability of a catalyst to direct a reaction towards a **specific desired product** when multiple products are thermodynamically possible. $$S = \frac{\text{Moles of desired product formed}}{\text{Moles of reactant consumed}} \times 100\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Selectivity is the **single most economically important** property in industrial heterogeneous catalysis. For example, in the **Fischer-Tropsch synthesis**, Fe or Co catalysts selectively produce long-chain hydrocarbons rather than methane. ### 2.1.3 Stability The ability of a catalyst to **retain its activity and selectivity** over extended periods under reaction conditions. Loss of stability leads to **catalyst deactivation** via: - **Sintering** — loss of surface area due to particle coalescence - **Coking** — deposition of carbonaceous species - **Poisoning** — irreversible chemisorption of impurities ### 2.1.4 Regenerability The ability to **restore activity** after deactivation, typically via regeneration cycles (e.g., oxidative coke removal in catalytic cracking). ## 2.2 Promoters and Poisons (Critical for KTU Board Exams) | Term | Definition | Example | |------|------------|---------| | **Promoter** | A substance that **enhances** the activity, selectivity, or stability of a catalyst **without having significant catalytic activity itself** | $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ in Haber-Bosch (Fe catalyst), $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ in $\text{Fe}/\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3/\text{K}_2\text{O}$ | | **Poison** | A substance that **reduces or destroys** catalyst activity by strongly adsorbing on active sites | S, P, As compounds poison Pt; CO poisons Fe in ammonia synthesis | | **Inhibitor** | A substance that **moderates** the rate (often reversible); used in negative catalysis | Antioxidants in polymer chemistry | ## 2.3 Turnover Number (TON) and Turnover Frequency (TOF) These are the **gold-standard metrics** for catalyst performance evaluation in KTU numerical problems. **Turnover Number (TON):** Total number of reactant molecules (or product molecules) that **one active site** can convert **before the catalyst becomes deactivated**. $$\text{TON} = \frac{\text{Moles of product formed}}{\text{Moles of active sites in catalyst}}$$ **Turnover Frequency (TOF):** Number of catalytic cycles (turnovers) per unit **time** per active site. $$\text{TOF} = \frac{\text{TON}}{t} = \frac{\text{Moles of product formed}}{(\text{Moles of active sites}) \times (\text{time})}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Unit Note:** TOF has units of **s⁻¹** (or h⁻¹ for industrial processes). A TOF of 1 s⁻¹ means each active site produces one product molecule per second. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes | |---|----------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius equation (homogeneous) | $A$ = pre-exponential factor | | 2 | $\dfrac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{-\Delta E_a / RT}$ | Rate enhancement ratio | $\Delta E_a = E_{a,\text{uncat}} - E_{a,\text{cat}}$ | | 3 | $\text{Activity} = -r_A / m_{\text{cat}}$ | Catalyst activity | mol g⁻¹ s⁻¹ | | 4 | $S = n_{\text{desired}} / n_{\text{reacted}} \times 100\%$ | Selectivity | Dimensionless % | | 5 | $\text{TON} = n_{\text{product}} / n_{\text{active sites}}$ | Turnover number | Dimensionless | | 6 | $\text{TOF} = \text{TON} / t$ | Turnover frequency | s⁻¹ | | 7 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Thermodynamics unchanged | A catalyst cannot alter this | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Industry | Industrial Process | Catalyst | Function Highlighted | |--------------------|----------|----------------------| | **Haber-Bosch (NH₃)** | Fe / K₂O / Al₂O₃ | Activity + Promoter synergy | | **Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** | Zeolite (ZSM-5) | Shape selectivity | | **Three-Way Catalytic Converter** | Pt-Rh-Pd on Al₂O₃ | Multiple reaction selectivity | | **Contact Process (H₂SO₄)** | V₂O₅ | Activity + Poison resistance | | **Enzymatic (Fermentation)** | Yeast (zymase) | Bio-catalysis under mild T | > [!TIP] > **Sabatier's Principle (often asked in KTU viva):** *"The best catalyst binds the substrate neither too strongly nor too weakly."* Too strong → poisoning; too weak → no activation. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantitative Rate Enhancement by a Catalyst Consider an uncatalyzed reaction with activation energy $E_{a,\text{uncat}}$ and a catalyzed reaction with $E_{a,\text{cat}}$. **Starting Point — Arrhenius Equation:** $$k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$$ **Step 1: Write Arrhenius equation for uncatalyzed and catalyzed paths respectively.** $$k_{\text{uncat}} = A_{\text{uncat}} \, e^{-E_{a,\text{uncat}} / RT}$$ $$k_{\text{cat}} = A_{\text{cat}} \, e^{-E_{a,\text{cat}} / RT}$$ **Step 2: Take the ratio (rate enhancement).** $$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = \frac{A_{\text{cat}}}{A_{\text{uncat}}} \, e^{-(E_{a,\text{cat}} - E_{a,\text{uncat}}) / RT}$$ **Step 3: Apply the simplifying assumption that the pre-exponential factor is approximately unchanged for a similar pathway family (a standard KTU assumption for short-answer problems).** $$\frac{A_{\text{cat}}}{A_{\text{uncat}}} \approx 1$$ **Step 4: Define the activation energy reduction.** $$\Delta E_a = E_{a,\text{uncat}} - E_{a,\text{cat}} > 0$$ **Step 5: Substitute into the rate ratio.** $$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{\Delta E_a / RT}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{\Delta E_a / RT}}$$ > **Interpretation:** A reduction in activation energy of just **$\Delta E_a \approx 10 \text{ kJ/mol}$** at $T = 300 \text{ K}$ gives: > > $$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{10000 / (8.314 \times 300)} = e^{4.01} \approx 55$$ > > i.e., the reaction is **~55 times faster** — illustrating why catalysts are economically vital. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantitative Form of TOF from First Principles **Step 1: Define moles of product generated in time $t$.** $$n_{\text{product}} = r \times V \times t$$ where $r$ = rate per unit volume (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹), $V$ = reactor volume. **Step 2: Define the number of active sites in the catalyst sample.** $$n_{\text{sites}} = \frac{m_{\text{cat}} \times N_{\text{sites per gram}}}{N_A}$$ **Step 3: Write the formal TOF expression.** $$\text{TOF} = \frac{n_{\text{product}}}{n_{\text{sites}} \cdot t}$$ **Step 4: Substitute Step 1 and Step 2 into Step 3.** $$\text{TOF} = \frac{r \, V \, N_A}{m_{\text{cat}} \, N_{\text{sites per gram}}}$$ **Step 5: Recognise that $N_{\text{sites per gram}}$ can be measured via chemisorption (e.g., CO pulse chemisorption for Pt).** $$\text{TOF} = \frac{r \, V \, N_A}{m_{\text{cat}} \, N_{\text{chemi}}}$$ where $N_{\text{chemi}}$ = chemisorbed molecules per gram. > This is the standard **KTU laboratory-style derivation** often appearing in numerical Part B sub-questions. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Problem > **Problem:** A catalyst lowers the activation energy of a first-order decomposition from $E_{a,\text{uncat}} = 90 \text{ kJ/mol}$ to $E_{a,\text{cat}} = 55 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at $400 \text{ K}$. Calculate the rate enhancement factor. ($R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.) **Solution:** $$\Delta E_a = 90 - 55 = 35 \text{ kJ/mol} = 35000 \text{ J/mol}$$ $$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{\Delta E_a / RT} = e^{35000 / (8.314 \times 400)}$$ $$= e^{35000 / 3325.6} = e^{10.526}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} \approx 3.74 \times 10^{4}}$$ **Answer:** The catalyzed reaction is approximately **37,400 times faster** than the uncatalyzed reaction. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous vs Biocatalysts | Property | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Biocatalysts (Enzymes) | |----------|-------------|---------------|-------------------------| | **Phase** | Same as reactants | Different from reactants | Typically aqueous phase | | **Activity (TOF, s⁻¹)** | 1 – 10⁴ | 10⁻² – 10² | 10 – 10⁶ | | **Selectivity** | High | Moderate–High (improving) | Extremely High (stereo-specific) | | **Operating T** | Mild (50–200 °C) | Wide (200–800 °C) | Very mild (20–80 °C) | | **Stability** | Moderate | High (regenerable) | Low (denatures easily) | | **Separation from products** | Difficult (key drawback) | Easy (filtration) | Moderate | | **Poison sensitivity** | Low | High | Very High | | **Cost per kg** | High | Moderate | Very High | | **Example** | $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ in esterification | Fe in Haber-Bosch | Zymase in fermentation | ## 3.5 Derivation 3 — Effect of Promoters on Activation Energy (Qualitative) **Step 1:** Pure metal Fe has a certain activation energy $E_{a,0}$ for ammonia synthesis. **Step 2:** Adding $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ as a promoter creates **new active sites** with a different electronic environment, lowering the effective $E_a$. **Step 3:** Adding $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ as a structural promoter prevents sintering, maintaining a **high surface area** over time, thereby increasing $A$ (the pre-exponential factor). **Step 4:** Combined effect on Arrhenius parameters: $$k_{\text{promoted}} = A_{\text{new}} \, e^{-(E_{a,0} - \Delta E_a^{\text{prom}}) / RT}$$ > Net effect: **both higher $A$ and lower $E_a$**, leading to a dramatic improvement in catalyst activity. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Catalysts ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["CATALYSTS"] --> node2["Based on Phase Relationship"] node1 --> node3["Based on Effect on Rate"] node1 --> node4["Based on Origin"] node2 --> node5["HOMOGENEOUS
Same phase
e.g. H2SO4 in esterification"] node2 --> node6["HETEROGENEOUS
Different phase
e.g. Pt in catalytic converter"] node2 --> node7["BIO CATALYSTS
Enzymes
e.g. Zymase in fermentation"] node3 --> node8["POSITIVE CATALYSTS
Increase reaction rate"] node3 --> node9["NEGATIVE CATALYSTS
Decrease reaction rate
aka Inhibitors"] node4 --> node10["NATURAL
Enzymes, minerals"] node4 --> node11["SYNTHETIC
Zeolites, metals, oxides"] node5 --> node12["ACID BASE
Proton transfer"] node5 --> node13["ORGANOMETALLIC
Wacker process"] node6 --> node14["METAL CATALYSTS
Fe, Pt, Ni, Pd"] node6 --> node15["METAL OXIDES
V2O5, MoO3"] node6 --> node16["SUPPORTED
Metal on Al2O3 or SiO2"] node6 --> node17["ZEOLITES
Shape selective"] node8 --> node18["ACTIVITY ENHANCERS"] node8 --> node19["SELECTIVITY ENHANCERS"] node11 --> node20["Examples:
Pt-Rh-Pd
ZSM-5 zeolite
Fe-K2O-Al2O3"] style node1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style node2 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node3 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node4 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node6 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node14 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node15 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node16 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff style node17 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Reaction Coordinate Diagram — Uncatalyzed vs Catalyzed Pathway ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Reactants
Energy level"]:::react nodeB["Transition State
Uncatalyzed Ea"]:::peak1 nodeC["Transition State
Catalyzed Ea"]:::peak2 nodeD["Intermediate
Catalyst Complex"]:::inter nodeE["Products
Energy level"]:::prod nodeA -->|"Ea uncat"| nodeB nodeB -->|"Energy released"| nodeE nodeA -->|"Ea cat"| nodeC nodeC -->|"Energy released"| nodeD nodeD -->|"Catalyst regenerated"| nodeE classDef react fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef peak1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef peak2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef inter fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000 classDef prod fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The catalyst introduces an **intermediate complex** (a lower-energy transition state), effectively **splitting one high barrier into two smaller barriers**, but the **initial and final energy levels (and hence $\Delta G$) are identical**. ## 4.3 Block Architecture — Roles of Promoters, Supports, and Poisons ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["CATALYST SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE"]:::title nodeB["ACTIVE COMPONENT
Provides active sites
e.g. Fe, Pt, Ni"]:::active nodeC["PROMOTER
Enhances activity/selectivity
e.g. K2O, Al2O3"]:::promoter nodeD["SUPPORT CARRIER
Disperses active phase
Increases surface area
e.g. SiO2, Al2O3, TiO2"]:::support nodeE["POISON
Irreversibly binds active sites
Lowers activity
e.g. S, P, As, CO"]:::poison nodeF["REACTOR ENVIRONMENT
T, P, Concentration gradients"]:::env nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG["ACTIVE SITE
where reaction occurs"]:::site nodeC -.->|"enhances"| nodeG nodeD -.->|"disperses"| nodeG nodeE -.->|"blocks"| nodeG classDef title fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef active fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef promoter fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef support fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef poison fill:#000000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef env fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef site fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Catalytic Cycle (Heterogeneous) — Step-by-Step Block Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeS1["STEP 1
Diffusion of reactant
to external surface"]:::step nodeS2["STEP 2
Internal diffusion
through pores"]:::step nodeS3["STEP 3
CHEMISORPTION
on active site"]:::chemi nodeS4["STEP 4
SURFACE REACTION
between adsorbed species"]:::react nodeS5["STEP 5
DESORPTION
of products"]:::des nodeS6["STEP 6
Diffusion of products
away from surface"]:::step nodeS1 --> nodeS2 --> nodeS3 --> nodeS4 --> nodeS5 --> nodeS6 nodeS3 -.->|"Active site
regenerated"| nodeS3 classDef step fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef chemi fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef react fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef des fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Define a catalyst. State Ostwald's definition with an example.** **Model Answer:** A catalyst is a substance that alters the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed in the overall stoichiometry. **Ostwald's Definition:** *"A catalyst is a substance that changes the velocity of a chemical reaction without itself appearing in the end products of the reaction."* **Example:** Manganese dioxide ($\text{MnO}_2$) catalyzes the decomposition of potassium chlorate: $$2\text{KClO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{MnO}_2, \Delta} 2\text{KCl} + 3\text{O}_2$$ Here $\text{MnO}_2$ remains chemically and quantitatively unchanged at the end of the reaction. --- ### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q: Distinguish between promoters and poisons with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Promoter | Poison | |---------|----------|--------| | **Function** | Enhances catalyst activity or selectivity | Reduces or destroys catalyst activity | | **Nature** | Reversible or structural enhancement | Often irreversible chemisorption | | **Example 1** | $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ in Haber-Bosch (Fe catalyst) | Sulfur compounds poisoning Pt in catalytic reforming | | **Example 2** | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ in $\text{Fe}/\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3/\text{K}_2\text{O}$ | CO poisoning of Fe in ammonia synthesis | A promoter itself has little or no catalytic activity but improves the parent catalyst. A poison binds so strongly to active sites that reactant molecules can no longer access them. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question B1 **or** Question B2. --- ### **QUESTION B1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Classify catalysts in detail with examples. Discuss the characteristic properties that make a catalyst industrially important. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With the help of a reaction coordinate diagram, explain how a catalyst increases the rate of a reaction. Prove that the rate enhancement factor is $k_{\text{cat}}/k_{\text{uncat}} = e^{\Delta E_a/RT}$. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution for B1(a):** **Classification of Catalysts:** **(i) Based on Phase:** 1. **Homogeneous Catalysts** — Same phase as reactants. - *Example:* Concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ catalyzing esterification (all species in liquid phase). 2. **Heterogeneous Catalysts** — Different phase from reactants (typically solid catalyst with gas/liquid reactants). - *Example:* Finely divided iron in the Haber-Bosch process for ammonia synthesis. 3. **Biocatalysts (Enzymes)** — Protein-based catalysts operating under mild biological conditions. - *Example:* Zymase in fermentation of glucose to ethanol. **(ii) Based on Effect on Rate:** 1. **Positive Catalysts** — Increase reaction rate (most common). 2. **Negative Catalysts** — Decrease reaction rate; e.g., antioxidants in rubber. **(iii) Based on Function:** 1. **Acid-Base Catalysts** — Proton donors/acceptors; e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ in alkylation. 2. **Metal Catalysts** — d-block transition metals; e.g., Pt, Pd, Ni. 3. **Metal Oxide Catalysts** — e.g., $\text{V}_2\text{O}_5$ in $\text{SO}_2$ oxidation. 4. **Supported Catalysts** — Active phase dispersed on high-surface-area support; e.g., $\text{Pt/Al}_2\text{O}_3$. **Characteristic Properties of an Industrially Important Catalyst:** | # | Property | Engineering Significance | |---|----------|---------------------------| | 1 | **High Activity** | Reduces reactor volume and residence time; high TOF desired | | 2 | **High Selectivity** | Minimizes side products → reduces downstream separation cost | | 3 | **Stability** | Long operational life (years) under operating conditions | | 4 | **Regenerability** | Can be reactivated when deactivated, lowering overall cost | | 5 | **Poison Resistance** | Tolerates feed impurities (cheap feedstock acceptable) | | 6 | **Mechanical Strength** | Resists attrition in fluidized beds | | 7 | **Reproducible Synthesis** | Batch-to-batch consistency for industrial scale-up | --- #### **Model Solution for B1(b):** **Reaction Coordinate Diagram (Refer SECTION 4.2).** The reaction coordinate diagram shows: - **Reactant energy level** at the starting point - **Uncatalyzed transition state** at peak height $E_{a,\text{uncat}}$ - **Catalyzed transition state** at peak height $E_{a,\text{cat}} < E_{a,\text{uncat}}$ - **Intermediate catalyst-substrate complex** at a local minimum - **Product energy level** identical for both pathways **Derivation of Rate Enhancement Factor:** Starting from the Arrhenius equations: $$k_{\text{uncat}} = A \, e^{-E_{a,\text{uncat}}/RT}$$ $$k_{\text{cat}} = A \, e^{-E_{a,\text{cat}}/RT}$$ (Assuming the pre-exponential factor $A$ is approximately unchanged.) Dividing: $$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = \frac{e^{-E_{a,\text{cat}}/RT}}{e^{-E_{a,\text{uncat}}/RT}}$$ $$= e^{(E_{a,\text{uncat}} - E_{a,\text{cat}})/RT}$$ Defining $\Delta E_a = E_{a,\text{uncat}} - E_{a,\text{cat}}$: $$\boxed{\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{\Delta E_a / RT}}$$ **Numerical Illustration:** If $\Delta E_a = 20 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at $T = 500 \text{ K}$: $$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{20000/(8.314 \times 500)} = e^{4.81} \approx 122$$ The catalyzed reaction is **~122 times faster** with no change in $\Delta G^{\circ}$ or $K_{eq}$. --- ### **QUESTION B2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Define Turnover Number (TON) and Turnover Frequency (TOF). A catalyst sample of 0.5 g contains $2.5 \times 10^{19}$ active sites. In 10 minutes, $1.5 \times 10^{21}$ product molecules are formed. Calculate TON and TOF. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** What are promoters and inhibitors? Explain with reference to the Haber-Bosch process and any one petroleum refining process. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution for B2(a):** **Definitions:** - **Turnover Number (TON):** Total number of reactant molecules that one active site converts to product before the catalyst is deactivated. $$\text{TON} = \frac{\text{Moles of product formed}}{\text{Moles of active sites}}$$ - **Turnover Frequency (TOF):** Number of catalytic cycles per active site per unit time. $$\text{TOF} = \frac{\text{TON}}{t}$$ **Calculation:** Number of active sites: $N_{\text{sites}} = 2.5 \times 10^{19}$ Number of product molecules: $N_{\text{product}} = 1.5 \times 10^{21}$ **TON:** $$\text{TON} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{21}}{2.5 \times 10^{19}} = 60$$ **TOF:** $$t = 10 \text{ min} = 600 \text{ s}$$ $$\text{TOF} = \frac{60}{600 \text{ s}} = 0.1 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\text{TON} = 60 \quad ; \quad \text{TOF} = 0.1 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A frequent mistake is computing TOF as $N_{\text{product}} / (N_{\text{sites}} \times t)$ and **forgetting that TON must first be calculated**. Also, ensure $t$ is converted to **seconds** before TOF calculation. Marks are typically deducted for unit inconsistency. --- #### **Model Solution for B2(b):** **Promoter:** A promoter is a substance that, when added in small quantities to a catalyst, **enhances the activity, selectivity, or stability** of the catalyst without possessing significant catalytic activity of its own. **Inhibitor (Negative Catalyst):** An inhibitor is a substance that **decreases the rate** of a chemical reaction. It may act by blocking active sites or by deactivating intermediates. **Case Study 1: Haber-Bosch Process** $$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$$ The industrial catalyst is **doubly promoted iron**: $$\text{Fe / K}_2\text{O / Al}_2\text{O}_3$$ | Component | Role | |-----------|------| | **Fe** | Active component — dissociatively chemisorbs $\text{N}_2$ | | **$\text{K}_2\text{O}$** | Chemical promoter — donates electrons to Fe, weakening the N–N bond and lowering $E_a$ | | **$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$** | Structural promoter — prevents sintering of Fe particles, maintaining high surface area | **Case Study 2: Catalytic Cracking in Petroleum Refining (FCC)** In Fluid Catalytic Cracking, **Zeolite Y (Faujasite)** is the active catalyst. - **Promoter:** Rare earth cations (e.g., $\text{La}^{3+}$, $\text{Ce}^{3+}$) exchanged into zeolite cages enhance **thermal stability and selectivity** towards gasoline-range hydrocarbons. - **Inhibitor/Poison:** **Organometallic compounds of Ni and V** present in crude oil feedstocks deposit on zeolite and act as poisons. Ni promotes unwanted dehydrogenation (coke and dry gas), while V attacks the zeolite framework, destroying its structure. > **Conclusion:** The role of promoters and inhibitors is critical — promoters multiply catalyst effectiveness while inhibitors/poisons can completely deactivate even the best catalyst, dictating the need for feed pretreatment (e.g., hydrodesulfurization). --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Areas in Catalysts Questions:** > 1. **Writing "catalyst shifts equilibrium"** — wrong; it only **speeds up** attainment of equilibrium. > 2. **Confusing TON with TOF** — TON is dimensionless; TOF has units of **s⁻¹** (or h⁻¹). > 3. **Forgetting to convert time units** in TOF numerical problems. > 4. **Confusing promoter with support** — a support **disperses** the active phase; a promoter **enhances** activity. > 5. **Treating catalyst as a reactant** in stoichiometric calculations — catalysts must be **balanced and cancelled**. > 6. **Not mentioning catalyst regeneration** when asked about catalyst properties — losing 1–2 easy marks. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - ✅ **Ostwald's Definition** is the most cited catalyst definition in KTU papers. - ✅ A catalyst provides an **alternative pathway with lower activation energy** but does **not change $\Delta G$, $\Delta H$, or $K_{eq}$**. - ✅ **Three classifications** are: Homogeneous, Heterogeneous, Biocatalysts. - ✅ **Activity** = rate per unit mass of catalyst; **Selectivity** = ratio of desired product to reactant consumed. - ✅ **Promoter** enhances; **Poison** destroys; **Inhibitor** retards. - ✅ **TON** = total turnovers per active site (dimensionless); **TOF** = TON per unit time (s⁻¹). - ✅ **Rate enhancement formula:** $\dfrac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{uncat}}} = e^{\Delta E_a / RT}$. - ✅ **Haber-Bosch catalyst** = Fe + $\text{K}_2\text{O}$ (chemical promoter) + $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (structural promoter). - ✅ **Sabatier's Principle** — best catalysts bind substrate with **intermediate strength**. - ✅ **Catalyst deactivation** occurs via **sintering, coking, or poisoning**. - ✅ **TOF values** for enzymes can reach $10^6 \text{ s}^{-1}$ — the highest among catalyst classes. - ✅ Remember: **Catalyst ≠ Reactant** — it is **regenerated** after the catalytic cycle.

topics.title: Types of Catalysts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Catalysts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **catalyst** is a substance that alters the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed in the overall process, by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. In the context of **heterogeneous reactions and catalysis**, catalysts are classified primarily on the basis of the **phase relationship between the catalyst and the reacting species**, their **physical form**, and their **chemical nature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight (Board-Exact Terminology):**
> A **heterogeneous catalyst** is a solid catalyst that operates on gaseous or liquid reactants, where the reaction occurs at the **solid–fluid interface**. This is distinguished from **homogeneous catalysts** (same phase as reactants) and **biocatalysts/enzymes** (biological macromolecules).

### 1.2 Broad Classification of Catalysts

Based on phase, the broad classes of catalysts are:

1. **Homogeneous Catalysts** – Catalyst and reactants are in the same phase (typically liquid). Example: H₂SO₄ in esterification, Ziegler–Natta soluble complexes in polymerization.
2. **Heterogeneous Catalysts** – Catalyst (solid) and reactants (gas/liquid) are in different phases. Example: Pt in ammonia oxidation, Fe in Haber–Bosch.
3. **Biocatalysts (Enzymes)** – Macromolecular proteins that catalyze biochemical reactions. Example: Amylase, Lipase, Zymase.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Mountain Tunnel**
> Think of a chemical reaction as climbing over a **tall mountain** to reach the other side (the product). The mountain is the **activation energy barrier**.
> - **Without catalyst (no tunnel):** Everyone must climb over the mountain — slow, exhausting journey.
> - **With a catalyst (a tunnel through the mountain):** Reactants take a *shorter, easier path*. The tunnel is the **catalyst surface**, and travelers (molecules) are the **reactants**.
> - The tunnel doesn't move or get used up — it just provides an alternative route.
> - **Heterogeneous catalyst** = a tunnel carved in *solid rock* (solid phase) used by *cars* (gas/liquid phase) — a two-phase system. This is exactly the principle behind catalytic converters in cars and industrial reactors.

### 1.4 Why "Types of Catalysts" Matters in Industry

- **Selection of catalyst type determines reactor design** (packed-bed, slurry, fluidized-bed, monolith).
- **Physical form** affects pressure drop, mass transfer, and heat removal.
- **Chemical nature** governs activity, selectivity, and resistance to poisoning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** Catalyst performance is often quantified by **Turnover Frequency (TOF)** in **s⁻¹** (moles of reactant converted per mole of active site per second), and **Turnover Number (TON)** as a dimensionless measure of total cycles before deactivation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Heterogeneous Catalysts (Detailed)

#### A. Based on **Physical Form**

| Form | Description | Typical Application |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Powders** | Fine particles, high external surface area | Liquid-phase slurry reactors |
| **Granules** | Irregular coarse particles | Fixed-bed reactors |
| **Pellets** | Cylindrical/spherical compressed shapes | Industrial fixed-bed reactors |
| **Extrudates** | Long cylindrical rods formed by extrusion | High-pressure fixed-bed reactors |
| **Monoliths** | Honeycomb structured ceramics/metals | Automotive catalytic converters |
| **Foams** | Open-cell porous metallic/ceramic | High thermal conductivity applications |

#### B. Based on **Chemical Nature**

1. **Metals** – Pt, Pd, Ni, Cu, Fe, Ag, Au
   - Active for **hydrogenation, dehydrogenation, oxidation**.
   - Example: **Pt in catalytic converters**, **Ni in vegetable oil hydrogenation**.

2. **Metal Oxides** – Al₂O₃, SiO₂, TiO₂, V₂O₅, MoO₃, Cr₂O₃
   - **Semiconducting oxides** → oxidation catalysts (V₂O₅ for SO₂ → SO₃).
   - **Insulating oxides** → used as **supports**.

3. **Zeolites** – Crystalline aluminosilicates (e.g., ZSM-5, Faujasite/Y-zeolite, Mordenite)
   - **Shape-selective catalysis** due to uniform micropores.
   - Example: **Fluid catalytic cracking (FCC)** in petroleum refining.

4. **Clays and Pillared Clays (PILCs)** – Layered silicates with intercalated metal oxide pillars.

5. **Sulfides** – MoS₂, WS₂, NiS, CoMoS
   - **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS), hydrodenitrogenation (HDN)** in petroleum refining.

6. **Carbons** – Activated carbon, carbon black, carbon nanotubes, graphene
   - Used as **supports** or as catalysts themselves (e.g., oxidative dehydrogenation).

7. **Solid Acids and Bases** – Sulfated zirconia, zeolites, anion-exchange resins
   - **Isomerization, alkylation, cracking**.

8. **Supported Catalysts** – Active phase (metal/oxide) dispersed on a high-surface-area support (Al₂O₃, SiO₂, TiO₂, carbon).

9. **Unsupported (Bulk) Catalysts** – Raney Ni, skeletal catalysts, fused iron (Haber–Bosch).

#### C. Based on **Method of Preparation**

- **Precipitated catalysts** (e.g., Cu–ZnO–Al₂O₃ for methanol synthesis)
- **Impregnated catalysts** (active metal salts impregnated onto support)
- **Ion-exchanged catalysts** (e.g., metal-exchanged zeolites)
- **Skeletal (Raney) catalysts** (e.g., Raney Ni, Raney Cu)
- **Fused catalysts** (e.g., fused Fe for ammonia synthesis)
- **Mixed oxide / co-precipitated catalysts**

### 2.2 Comparison: Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous vs Biocatalysts

| Property | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Biocatalyst |
|----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Phase** | Same as reactants | Different (solid catalyst) | Different (solid enzyme) |
| **Active site** | Single, well-defined | Multiple, surface | Single (active pocket) |
| **Selectivity** | High (often chemo- and stereo-) | Moderate to high | Extremely high (regio/stereo) |
| **Activity (TOF)** | High (10²–10⁴ s⁻¹) | Variable (10⁻²–10² s⁻¹) | Very high (10²–10⁶ s⁻¹) |
| **Operating conditions** | Mild | Often harsh | Very mild (ambient T, pH) |
| **Separation** | Difficult | Easy (filtration) | Easy (centrifugation) |
| **Recycling** | Hard | Easy | Possible |
| **Stability** | High thermal | High thermal | Low (degrades > 50–80 °C) |
| **Examples** | H₂SO₄, Wacker catalyst | Pt, Fe, Zeolites | Amylase, Lipase |
| **Disadvantages** | Corrosion, separation | Mass transfer limitations | Narrow pH/T range, cost |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Characterization of Different Catalyst Types)

| Formula | Meaning | Variables | Units |
|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| $D = \dfrac{n_s}{n_t}$ | **Dispersion** (fraction of exposed metal atoms) | $n_s$ = surface atoms, $n_t$ = total atoms | Dimensionless |
| $\bar{d} = \dfrac{6 \cdot v_m}{a_m \cdot D}$ | **Average metal crystallite size** | $v_m$ = molar volume, $a_m$ = metal surface area per mole | nm |
| $S_{metal} = \dfrac{n_s \cdot N_A}{M}$ | **Metal surface area per gram** | $M$ = molar mass | m²/g |
| $TOF = \dfrac{r}{n_{active}}$ | **Turnover frequency** | $r$ = reaction rate (mol/s), $n_{active}$ = moles active sites | s⁻¹ |
| $TON = TOF \cdot t$ | **Turnover number** | $t$ = time | Dimensionless |
| $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot N_A \cdot \sigma}{V_{STP}}$ | **BET surface area** | $V_m$ = monolayer volume, $\sigma$ = cross-sectional area | m²/g |
| $\varepsilon_p = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_s}$ | **Porosity of pellet** | $\rho_p$ = pellet density, $\rho_s$ = skeletal density | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering:**
> Choosing a catalyst *type* is governed by dispersion, surface area, and porosity. For instance, a **supported metal catalyst** with high $D$ (small crystallites) gives more active sites per gram — critical for expensive noble metals like Pt and Pd.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility Summary

- **Petroleum refining** → Zeolites (FCC), CoMoS (HDS).
- **Automotive exhaust** → Pt-Pd-Rh on monolithic ceramic (three-way catalyst).
- **Ammonia synthesis** → Fused iron (magnetite, Fe₃O₄ with promoters).
- **Polymerization** → Ziegler–Natta (homogeneous) and supported Cr/SiO₂ (heterogeneous, Phillips).
- **Pharma & food** → Lipases, esterases (biocatalysts).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Metal Dispersion and Average Particle Size

This derivation is **high-yield** for KTU problems on supported metal catalysts.

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Metal crystallites are **spherical** with average diameter $\bar{d}$.
- The molar volume of the metal is $v_m$ (m³/mol).
- The atomic cross-sectional area of the metal is $a_m$ (m²/atom).
- Avogadro's number is $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹.

**Step 1: Volume of one metal atom in a crystallite**

The total volume of one mole of metal atoms packed in crystallites is:

$$
V_{mole} = v_m \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/mol]}
$$

**Step 2: Total number of atoms per mole**

$$
n_t = N_A \quad \text{[atoms/mol]}
$$

**Step 3: Volume occupied by one atom**

$$
v_{atom} = \dfrac{v_m}{N_A} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/atom]}
$$

**Step 4: Number of surface atoms per unit area of crystallite**

The surface atom density is:

$$
n_s^* = \dfrac{1}{a_m} \quad \text{[atoms/m}^2\text{]}
$$

**Step 5: For a spherical crystallite of diameter $\bar{d}$**

Surface area of one crystallite:

$$
A_c = \pi \bar{d}^{\,2}
$$

Number of surface atoms on one crystallite:

$$
n_s^{c} = A_c \cdot n_s^* = \dfrac{\pi \bar{d}^{\,2}}{a_m}
$$

**Step 6: Total atoms in one crystallite (volume of sphere / volume per atom)**

$$
n_t^{c} = \dfrac{\frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\dfrac{\bar{d}}{2}\right)^3}{v_{atom}} = \dfrac{\pi \bar{d}^{\,3}}{6 \cdot v_{atom}} = \dfrac{\pi \bar{d}^{\,3} \cdot N_A}{6 \cdot v_m}
$$

**Step 7: Define dispersion as ratio of surface atoms to total atoms**

$$
D = \dfrac{n_s^{c}}{n_t^{c}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
D = \dfrac{\dfrac{\pi \bar{d}^{\,2}}{a_m}}{\dfrac{\pi \bar{d}^{\,3} N_A}{6 v_m}} = \dfrac{6 v_m}{a_m \cdot \bar{d} \cdot N_A}
$$

**Step 8: Re-arranging for $\bar{d}$**

$$
\boxed{\bar{d} = \dfrac{6 v_m}{a_m \cdot N_A \cdot D}}
$$

This is the **standard KTU Board formula** linking dispersion to average particle size.

**Step 9: Example Numerical Check**

For Pt: $v_m = 9.09 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol, $a_m = 7.7 \times 10^{-20}$ m²/atom.

If $D = 0.5$ (50% dispersion):

$$
\bar{d} = \dfrac{6 \times 9.09 \times 10^{-6}}{7.7 \times 10^{-20} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.5}
$$

$$
\bar{d} = \dfrac{5.454 \times 10^{-5}}{2.319 \times 10^{4}} \approx 2.35 \text{ nm}
$$

This size falls in the **nanoscale regime** — typical of highly dispersed supported catalysts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** As dispersion $D$ increases, average crystallite size $\bar{d}$ decreases. This is why industrial supported catalysts aim for **small nanoparticles** (1–10 nm) to maximize dispersion and hence activity per gram of metal.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selecting Catalyst Type by Reaction

| Reaction Type | Preferred Catalyst Type | Reason | Industrial Example |
|---------------|------------------------|--------|---------------------|
| **Hydrogenation** (C=C, C=O) | Supported metals (Pd/C, Ni/SiO₂) | High H₂ activation on metal surface | Margarine manufacture |
| **Oxidation** (SO₂, CO, VOCs) | Transition metal oxides (V₂O₅, CuO, MnO₂) | Variable oxidation state, lattice O | Contact process, catalytic converter |
| **Acid catalysis** (cracking, alkylation) | Solid acids (Zeolites, sulfated ZrO₂) | Brønsted/Lewis acid sites | FCC unit |
| **Hydrodesulfurization** | Metal sulfides (CoMoS, NiMoS) | Sulfur-resistant active phase | Petroleum HDS |
| **Ammonia synthesis** | Fused iron with K₂O, Al₂O₃ promoters | N₂ dissociation on Fe surface | Haber–Bosch |
| **Polymerization** | Ziegler–Natta (heterogeneous supported) | Stereo-regularity control | Polyethylene, polypropylene |
| **Methanol synthesis** | Cu-ZnO-Al₂O₃ | Cu activates CO, ZnO provides H spillover | Low-pressure methanol |
| **Enantioselective synthesis** | Homogeneous chiral complexes (e.g., BINAP-Rh) | Single-site, chiral environment | L-DOPA synthesis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common Board question asks to *justify the choice of catalyst type for a given industrial reaction*. Use the principle: **match active site chemistry to reaction mechanism**.

### 3.3 Selection Logic Step-by-Step (Pedagogical)

**Step 1:** Identify the **rate-determining step** (RDS) of the desired reaction.
- Bond breaking of H–H? → Metals (Pt, Pd, Ni).
- Proton transfer? → Solid acids (zeolites).
- Electron transfer? → Transition metal oxides.

**Step 2:** Identify the **operating conditions** (T, P, pH).
- High T (> 500 °C)? → Avoid enzymes; prefer oxides/metals.
- High pressure? → Avoid polymeric supports; prefer pellets/extrudates.

**Step 3:** Identify the **selectivity requirement**.
- High shape-selectivity? → Zeolites (uniform micropores).
- High chemo-selectivity? → Homogeneous or enzymatic.
- High thermal stability + ease of separation? → Heterogeneous.

**Step 4:** Identify **deactivation pathway** (coking, sintering, poisoning).
- Coking? → Use zeolites (acidic, regenerable).
- Poisoning by S? → Use sulfide catalysts.
- Sintering? → Use supports with high Tammann temperature.

This 4-step decision matrix is **exactly the KTU Board marking rubric** for "justify the choice" type questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[CATALYSTS] --> B1[Homogeneous]
    A0 --> B2[Heterogeneous]
    A0 --> B3[Biocatalyst]

    B1 --> C1[Acid Base Liquid]
    B1 --> C2[Organometallic Complexes]
    B1 --> C3[Ziegler Natta Soluble]

    B2 --> D1[By Physical Form]
    B2 --> D2[By Chemical Nature]
    B2 --> D3[By Preparation]

    D1 --> E1[Powder]
    D1 --> E2[Granule]
    D1 --> E3[Pellet]
    D1 --> E4[Extrudate]
    D1 --> E5[Monolith]
    D1 --> E6[Foam]

    D2 --> F1[Metals Pt Pd Ni Cu Fe]
    D2 --> F2[Metal Oxides Al2O3 V2O5 TiO2]
    D2 --> F3[Zeolites ZSM5 Y Mordenite]
    D2 --> F4[Sulfides MoS2 CoMoS]
    D2 --> F5[Carbons Activated CNT]
    D2 --> F6[Solid Acids Bases]

    D3 --> G1[Precipitated]
    D3 --> G2[Impregnated]
    D3 --> G3[Ion Exchanged]
    D3 --> G4[Skeletal Raney]
    D3 --> G5[Fused]
    D3 --> G6[Co Precipitated]

    B3 --> H1[Oxidoreductases]
    B3 --> H2[Transferases]
    B3 --> H3[Hydrolases]
    B3 --> H4[Lyases]
    B3 --> H5[Isomerases]
    B3 --> H6[Ligases]

    style A0 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style B3 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Catalyst Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Industrial Reaction] --> P1[Step 1 Identify RDS]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2 Operating Conditions]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Selectivity Need]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Deactivation Path]
    P4 --> DEC{Decision Node}
    DEC -->|High T P| OUT1[Use Heterogeneous Oxide or Metal]
    DEC -->|Mild T P| OUT2[Use Homogeneous Complex]
    DEC -->|Bio Substrate| OUT3[Use Biocatalyst Enzyme]
    DEC -->|Shape Selectivity| OUT4[Use Zeolite]
    DEC -->|High Activity per Gram| OUT5[Use Supported Metal Nanoparticle]
    DEC -->|Sulfur Feed| OUT6[Use Metal Sulfide]

    style IN1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style DEC fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
    style OUT1 fill:#a9d08e
    style OUT2 fill:#a9d08e
    style OUT3 fill:#a9d08e
    style OUT4 fill:#a9d08e
    style OUT5 fill:#a9d08e
    style OUT6 fill:#a9d08e
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Structure of a Supported Metal Catalyst (Heterogeneous)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUP[SUPPORT High Surface Area Al2O3 SiO2 TiO2] --> PORE[Micropore Mesopore Network]
    PORE --> ACT[Active Metal Nanoparticle 1 to 10 nm]
    ACT --> SITE[Active Site Surface Atom]
    SUP --> PROM[Promoter K2O CeO2]
    PROM --> MOD[Electronic Geometric Modification]
    MOD --> ACT
    GAS[Reactant Gas H2 CO O2] --> DIFF[Diffusion to Surface]
    DIFF --> ADS[Adsorption on Active Site]
    ADS --> RXN[Surface Reaction]
    RXN --> DES[Desorption of Product]
    DES --> OUTG[Product Leaves to Bulk]

    style SUP fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style ACT fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style SITE fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
    style PROM fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style GAS fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style OUTG fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A typical **heterogeneous supported catalyst** consists of a **high-surface-area support** (provides dispersion and thermal stability), an **active phase** (small metal nanoparticles), and **promoters** (electronic/geometric modifiers that enhance activity or selectivity). The reaction sequence — *diffusion → adsorption → surface reaction → desorption* — is the **unified mechanism for all heterogeneous catalysts**.

### 4.4 Comparative Performance Bar (Qualitative Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Homogeneous] -- HIGH --> S1[Selectivity]
    A -- MOD --> S2[Stability]
    B[Heterogeneous] -- EASY --> S3[Separability]
    B -- VAR --> S4[Selectivity]
    C[Biocatalyst] -- VHI --> S5[Activity TOF]
    C -- POOR --> S6[Thermal Stability]

    style A fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

#### **Q1. Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts. Give two industrial examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Phase | Same as reactants (usually liquid) | Different phase (solid catalyst, gas/liquid reactants) |
| Active site | Single, well-defined | Multiple, on surface |
| Selectivity | High (chemo-, regio-, stereo-) | Moderate to high |
| Separation | Difficult (often impossible without distillation) | Easy (filtration) |
| Recovery | Hard | Easy |
| Industrial Examples | (i) H₂SO₄ in esterification (ii) Wacker process (PdCl₂/CuCl₂ in C₂H₄ → CH₃CHO) | (i) Fe in Haber–Bosch (NH₃ synthesis) (ii) Pt in catalytic converter (CO/HC oxidation) |

> **Marking:** 1 mark for each correct difference (any 2), 1 mark for examples.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

#### **Q2. What are zeolites? Why are they called "shape-selective" catalysts? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Zeolites are **crystalline aluminosilicates** with the general formula:

$$
M_{x/n}^n \left[ \left( AlO_2 \right)_x \left( SiO_2 \right)_y \right] \cdot z H_2 O
$$

They possess a **3-D framework of SiO₄ and AlO₄ tetrahedra** linked by oxygen bridges, with uniform micropores (typically 0.3–1.5 nm).

**Shape-Selectivity (1.5 marks):** The uniform pore size allows only molecules of specific **size and geometry** to enter, react, and leave. This gives:
- **Reactant shape-selectivity** (large molecules excluded)
- **Product shape-selectivity** (only small products can diffuse out)
- **Transition-state shape-selectivity** (bulky intermediates forbidden)

**Example (0.5 mark):** ZSM-5 in **Methanol-to-Gasoline (MTG)** process, where only C₁₀ aromatics are formed (no heavier).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** 

*Discuss in detail the various **types of heterogeneous catalysts** based on (a) physical form and (b) chemical nature, with industrial examples. Compare heterogeneous catalysts with homogeneous and biocatalysts.**

**Sub-part (a) – 7 Marks:**
*Classify heterogeneous catalysts based on physical form and chemical nature with examples.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1) Physical Form (3.5 marks):**

1. **Powder catalysts:** Fine particles (< 100 µm), high external area, used in slurry reactors — e.g., Raney Ni powder for hydrogenation.
2. **Granules:** Irregular coarse particles (1–5 mm), used in fixed-bed reactors — e.g., Claus catalyst (Al₂O₃).
3. **Pellets:** Cylindrical or spherical compressed forms (3–10 mm), uniform flow distribution — e.g., ZnO desulfurization pellets.
4. **Extrudates:** Long cylindrical rods (1–3 mm diameter), high mechanical strength — e.g., HDS extrudates (CoMo/Al₂O₃).
5. **Monoliths:** Honeycomb ceramic structures, low pressure drop, high geometric area — e.g., automotive three-way catalyst.
6. **Foams:** Open-cell ceramic/metallic, high thermal conductivity — e.g., NiCrAl foam for steam reforming.

**(2) Chemical Nature (3.5 marks):**

1. **Metals** – Pt, Pd, Ni, Cu, Fe → hydrogenation, dehydrogenation, oxidation.
2. **Metal oxides** – V₂O₅, MoO₃, Cr₂O₃, CuO → oxidation (e.g., V₂O₅/TiO₂ for SO₂ → SO₃).
3. **Zeolites** – ZSM-5, Y-zeolite, Mordenite → cracking, isomerization.
4. **Sulfides** – MoS₂, CoMoS, NiMoS → HDS, HDN.
5. **Carbons** – Activated carbon, CNT → supports and oxidation catalysts.
6. **Solid acids/bases** – Sulfated ZrO₂, anion resins → alkylation.
7. **Supported catalysts** – Active phase + high-area support.
8. **Skeletal (Raney) metals** – Raney Ni, Raney Cu.

---

**Sub-part (b) – 7 Marks:**
*Compare heterogeneous catalysts with homogeneous and biocatalysts. Discuss advantages of heterogeneous catalysts.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Tabular Comparison (5 marks):**

| Property | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous | Biocatalyst |
|----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| Phase | Same as reactants | Different (solid) | Different (solid) |
| Active sites | Single, uniform | Multiple, on surface | Single (active pocket) |
| Selectivity | High | Moderate–High | Very High (stereo) |
| TOF (s⁻¹) | 10²–10⁴ | 10⁻²–10² | 10²–10⁶ |
| T range | Wide | Wide | Narrow (20–80 °C) |
| pH range | Wide | Wide | Narrow (specific) |
| Separation | Difficult | Easy | Easy |
| Recycling | Hard | Easy | Possible |
| Stability | High | High | Low (degrades) |
| Cost | High (often noble) | Variable | Very High (production) |

**Advantages of Heterogeneous Catalysts (2 marks):**

1. **Easy separation** from products (simple filtration) — enables continuous operation.
2. **High thermal stability** — suitable for high-temperature industrial processes.
3. **Reusability** — supports regeneration and reduces cost.
4. **Suitable for fixed-bed/fluidized-bed** continuous reactors.
5. **No corrosion of equipment** (unlike liquid acids).

**Disadvantages (for balance):**
- Lower selectivity vs homogeneous.
- Mass transfer limitations (external/internal).
- Active site heterogeneity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

*Explain the classification of catalysts by **method of preparation**. Describe in detail the preparation, properties, and applications of (a) **supported metal catalysts** and (b) **skeletal (Raney) catalysts**. Discuss how **dispersion** and **average particle size** are related.**

**Sub-part (a) – 7 Marks: Supported Metal Catalysts and Skeletal Catalysts – Preparation and Properties**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Classification by Preparation (2 marks):**

- **Precipitated** — e.g., Cu–ZnO–Al₂O₃
- **Impregnated** — e.g., Pt/Al₂O₃
- **Ion-exchanged** — e.g., Cu-zeolite
- **Skeletal** — e.g., Raney Ni
- **Fused** — e.g., Fused Fe (Haber)
- **Co-precipitated** — e.g., Cu–ZnO
- **Sol-gel / hydrothermal** — e.g., TiO₂

**2. Supported Metal Catalysts (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** A catalytically active metal dispersed as **nanoparticles (1–10 nm)** on a high-surface-area **support** (Al₂O₃, SiO₂, TiO₂, activated C).
- **Preparation methods:**
  - **Impregnation:** Support soaked in metal salt solution → dried → calcined → reduced in H₂.
  - **Ion-exchange:** Metal cations exchanged into zeolite framework.
  - **Deposition–precipitation:** Metal hydroxide precipitated onto support.
- **Properties:** High dispersion, high metal surface area, tunable acidity/basicity via support, resistance to sintering.
- **Examples:** Pd/C (hydrogenation), Pt/Al₂O₃ (reforming), Ag/α-Al₂O₃ (ethylene epoxidation).

**3. Skeletal (Raney) Catalysts (2 marks):**

- **Preparation:** Alloy of active metal (Ni, Cu, Co) with a soluble metal (Al or Zn) is treated with **NaOH** to **leach out the soluble component**, leaving a **spongy, porous skeletal structure** with high surface area (50–100 m²/g).
- **Example:** Raney Ni, Raney Cu, Raney Co.
- **Properties:** Pyrophoric (stored under water), very high activity, used in **slurry reactors** for hydrogenation (e.g., sugars → sorbitol, oils → fats).

---

**Sub-part (b) – 7 Marks: Relation Between Dispersion and Average Particle Size**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):** (See Section 3.1 of these notes for full derivation.)

Key result:

$$
\bar{d} = \dfrac{6 v_m}{a_m \cdot N_A \cdot D}
$$

**Definitions (1 mark):**
- $D$ = **dispersion** = fraction of metal atoms exposed on the surface.
- $\bar{d}$ = average crystallite diameter (nm).
- $v_m$ = molar volume of metal (m³/mol).
- $a_m$ = atomic cross-sectional area of metal (m²/atom).

**Numerical (1 mark):** For Pt with $D = 0.4$, $v_m = 9.09 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/mol, $a_m = 7.7 \times 10^{-20}$ m²/atom, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$:

$$
\bar{d} = \dfrac{6 \times 9.09 \times 10^{-6}}{7.7 \times 10^{-20} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.4} \approx 2.94 \text{ nm}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Higher dispersion → smaller particles → more active sites per gram → **economical use of expensive noble metals** (Pt, Pd, Rh).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Types of Catalysts" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "types" with "examples":** Students list Pt, Ni, Fe without classifying them under *physical form* / *chemical nature* / *preparation*. Always present a **classification scheme first**, then examples.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the support in supported catalysts:** Saying "Pt catalyst" loses 1 mark; correct is **"Pt supported on γ-Al₂O₃"** (support + active phase + sometimes promoter).
>
> 3. **In derivation questions, skipping intermediate steps:** Showing only the final formula $\bar{d} = 6v_m / (a_m N_A D)$ **without derivation** fetches 2/5 marks. Always show: volume per atom → surface atoms per crystallite → total atoms per crystallite → ratio = D.
>
> 4. **In comparison tables, missing the "operating condition" column:** Examiner often awards a full mark only if T, pH, and stability are explicitly mentioned.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Raney Ni (skeletal) with fused Fe (fused):** Both are unsupported, but their **preparation methods differ** — Raney uses alkaline leaching, Fused uses electric arc melting.
>
> 6. **Not stating units explicitly** in numerical problems involving $\bar{d}$ (should be **nm**).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 1 – Types of Catalysts):**

- ✅ **Three main classes:** Homogeneous, Heterogeneous, Biocatalysts — distinguished by **phase** with reactants.
- ✅ **Heterogeneous catalysts classified by:**
  - **Physical form** → Powder, Granule, Pellet, Extrudate, Monolith, Foam.
  - **Chemical nature** → Metals, Metal oxides, Zeolites, Sulfides, Carbons, Solid acids/bases.
  - **Preparation** → Precipitated, Impregnated, Ion-exchanged, Skeletal, Fused, Co-precipitated.
- ✅ **Key examples to memorize:**
  - Haber–Bosch: **Fused Fe + K₂O/Al₂O₃ promoters**.
  - FCC: **Zeolite Y (REY/USY)**.
  - HDS: **CoMoS/Al₂O₃**.
  - Automotive: **Pt-Pd-Rh on cordierite monolith**.
  - Methanol: **Cu-ZnO-Al₂O₃**.
  - Hydrogenation: **Raney Ni** (slurry) or **Pd/C** (slurry/fixed-bed).
- ✅ **Zeolites = Shape-selective** (reactant, product, transition-state selectivity).
- ✅ **Supported metal catalyst** = Active metal + Support + (Promoter).
- ✅ **Raney Ni** = Prepared by **alkaline leaching of Ni–Al alloy**; **pyrophoric**; high activity.
- ✅ **High-Yield Formula:** $\bar{d} = \dfrac{6 v_m}{a_m \cdot N_A \cdot D}$ — smaller $\bar{d}$ → higher $D$ → more active sites.
- ✅ **Turnover Frequency (TOF)** in **s⁻¹** quantifies intrinsic activity per active site.
- ✅ **BET surface area** quantifies total (internal + external) surface of porous catalysts in **m²/g**.
- ✅ **Engineering selection logic:** Match catalyst *type* to **RDS**, **operating conditions**, **selectivity**, and **deactivation resistance**.
- ✅ **KTU exam gold:** Always present answers using **classification tables** + **examples** + **engineering justification**.

> **End of Module 1 – Types of Catalysts Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalytic Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Catalytic Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Catalytic Properties"?

In the context of **Heterogeneous Reactions and Catalysis (PECHT633)**, the term *Catalytic Properties* refers to the **ensemble of measurable, qualitative, and quantitative attributes** that collectively determine how efficiently and effectively a solid (or supported) catalyst performs its function when the reacting species exist in a different phase than the catalyst itself.

Formally, a **catalyst** is a substance that **increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed in the overall stoichiometry**, by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a **lower Gibbs free energy of activation** ($\Delta G^{\ddagger}$). The *properties* are the lens through which we judge "how good" a catalyst is for a given industrial or laboratory reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):** Catalytic properties are the physico-chemical and surface characteristics of a solid catalyst — such as **activity, selectivity, stability, regenerability, and resistance to poisoning** — that govern its performance in accelerating a specific heterogeneous reaction.

---

## 1.2 The Five Primary Catalytic Properties

A KTU-level answer must always enumerate these properties in a logical hierarchy:

| # | Property | One-Line Meaning |
|---|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Activity** | How fast the catalyst makes reactants convert to products |
| 2 | **Selectivity** | How preferentially it forms the *desired* product over side products |
| 3 | **Stability / Life** | How long it retains activity under reaction conditions |
| 4 | **Regenerability** | Whether deactivated catalyst can be restored |
| 5 | **Poison Resistance** | How well it withstands feed impurities |

> [!NOTE]
> Secondary but frequently tested properties include **mechanical strength**, **thermal conductivity**, **acid–base character**, **porosity**, and **active-site dispersion**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Tollbooth" Model

Imagine a busy city with **100 different toll booths (active sites)** on a single highway. A heterogeneous catalyst is like the **entire toll plaza** sitting on a roadside, while reactant molecules are the cars driving past.

- **Activity** = How many cars per minute each booth processes.
- **Selectivity** = Whether the booth directs cars only into the "express lane" (desired product) or mistakenly lets some into the "service lane" (by-product).
- **Stability** = How long the booth stays operational during a heat-wave or rainstorm (thermal and chemical endurance).
- **Poisoning** = What happens when a truck spills sticky tar on the sensors — the booth slows or stops.
- **Regenerability** = Sending a maintenance crew at night to clean the tar off.

This analogy captures the **surface-bound, site-specific** nature of heterogeneous catalysis: not every part of the catalyst works — only discrete **active sites**.

---

## 1.4 Why Catalytic Properties Matter in Engineering

In **chemical process industries** (ammonia synthesis, FCC cracking, hydrodesulfurization, automotive catalytic converters, three-way catalysts), the **economic viability** of a process hinges on these five properties. A catalyst that is highly active but poorly selective may waste expensive feedstock. A selective catalyst that deactivates in a week is commercially unviable.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever a question asks "Discuss the requirements of an industrial catalyst," always answer in the order: Activity → Selectivity → Stability → Regenerability → Mechanical/Physical Strength. This is the exact sequence expected in the valuation key.

---

## 1.5 Classification Context

Catalytic properties are discussed across the spectrum of heterogeneous catalysts:

- **Metal catalysts** (Pt, Pd, Ni, Cu) — high hydrogenation activity.
- **Metal oxide catalysts** (V₂O₅, MoO₃, Cr₂O₃) — selective oxidation.
- **Zeolites and acid solids** (HZSM-5, HY, MOR) — shape-selective cracking.
- **Supported catalysts** (metal on SiO₂, Al₂O₃, TiO₂) — dispersed active phase.

Each family exhibits these properties in **different proportions**, which is the conceptual foundation for catalyst selection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Property 1 — Catalytic Activity

### 2.1.1 Definition

**Activity** is a measure of *how fast* a catalyst accelerates a reaction under specified conditions of **temperature, pressure, and reactant composition**.

It is **not** an absolute property; it must always be quoted *per unit* of something:
- per unit **mass** of catalyst,
- per unit **surface area**,
- per unit **volume** of reactor,
- or per **active site** (turnover-based metrics).

### 2.1.2 Quantitative Metrics

**a) Specific Activity (Rate per unit mass):**

$$ r_{\text{specific}} = \frac{\text{moles converted}}{\text{time} \cdot \text{mass of catalyst}} \quad [\text{mol} \cdot \text{g}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}] $$

**b) Intrinsic Activity (Rate per unit surface area):**

$$ r_{\text{int}} = \frac{\text{moles converted}}{\text{time} \cdot \text{BET surface area}} \quad [\text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}] $$

**c) Turnover Frequency (TOF)** — the most rigorous and KTU-favoured metric:

$$ \text{TOF} = \frac{\text{moles of product formed per second}}{\text{moles of active sites}} \quad [\text{s}^{-1}] $$

**d) Turnover Number (TON)** — measures *catalyst life*:

$$ \text{TON} = \frac{\text{moles of reactant converted before deactivation}}{\text{moles of active sites}} \quad [\text{dimensionless}] $$

### 2.1.3 Activity and the Arrhenius Framework

Because catalysis lowers the **activation energy barrier**, activity is strongly temperature-dependent. The apparent rate constant $k_{\text{app}}$ follows:

$$ k_{\text{app}} = A \, e^{-E_a / RT} $$

A good catalyst *lowers* $E_a$ while leaving the pre-exponential factor $A$ largely unchanged (mostly an entropy effect).

> [!NOTE]
> **Catalyst Comparison via Apparent Activation Energy:** If Catalyst A gives $E_a = 80 \text{ kJ/mol}$ and Catalyst B gives $E_a = 110 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at the same temperature, Catalyst A is intrinsically more active — provided transport limitations have been rigorously eliminated.

---

## 2.2 Property 2 — Catalytic Selectivity

### 2.2.1 Definition

**Selectivity** is the fraction of converted reactant that proceeds through the *desired* pathway to form the *target* product.

For a complex reaction network:

$$ A \xrightarrow{k_1} B \quad (\text{desired}), \quad A \xrightarrow{k_2} C \quad (\text{side}) $$

The **instantaneous selectivity** to product $B$ is:

$$ S_B = \frac{r_B}{r_B + r_C} = \frac{k_1 [A]}{k_1 [A] + k_2 [A]} = \frac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2} $$

The **overall (integral) selectivity** over the reactor:

$$ \bar{S}_B = \frac{n_{B,\text{out}} - n_{B,\text{in}}}{n_{A,\text{in}} - n_{A,\text{out}}} $$

### 2.2.2 Types of Selectivity (KTU-favoured classification)

1. **Regio-selectivity** — favours one positional isomer (e.g., *para*-xylene over *ortho*-xylene).
2. **Stereo-selectivity** — favours one enantiomer/diastereomer.
3. **Shape-selectivity** (zeolites) — three sub-types:
   - **Reactant shape-selectivity** — only molecules below pore aperture enter.
   - **Product shape-selectivity** — only molecules small enough diffuse out.
   - **Transition-state (restricted transition-state) selectivity** — pore geometry forbids bulky transition states.
4. **Chemo-selectivity** — favours one functional group over another.

---

## 2.3 Property 3 — Catalytic Stability (Catalyst Life)

### 2.3.1 Definition

Stability is the **resistance of a catalyst to loss of activity, selectivity, or physical integrity** during prolonged exposure to reaction conditions.

It is conventionally reported as **time-on-stream** before activity drops below a threshold (often 10–20 % of fresh activity).

### 2.3.2 Three Sub-Modalities

| Sub-type | Cause | Industrial Example |
|----------|-------|--------------------|
| **Thermal stability** | Sintering, phase transition | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ methanol catalyst at 250 °C |
| **Chemical stability** | Reaction with feed/products, leaching | Sulfur attack on Ni in hydrotreating |
| **Mechanical stability** | Attrition, crushing, pellet fracture | FCC catalyst in fluidized bed |

### 2.3.3 Sintering Kinetics

Loss of active surface area by sintering often follows a **power-law expression** (a KTU favourite):

$$ -\frac{d(D)}{dt} = k_s \, (D - D_{\text{eq}})^n $$

where $D$ is the metal crystallite diameter, $D_{\text{eq}}$ is the equilibrium diameter, $k_s$ is the sintering rate constant (Arrhenius-type), and $n$ is typically 2 to 4.

---

## 2.4 Property 4 — Regenerability

A catalyst that deactivates must be **regenerable** to be industrially viable. Regeneration is the reverse process of deactivation, e.g.:

- Coking → burn-off in air (FCC regenerator at ~700 °C).
- Sulfur poisoning → reductive regeneration in H₂.
- Sintering → *not* fully reversible — only partial redispersion possible.

Regenerability is typically reported as the **% activity recovered per regeneration cycle** and the **number of cycles before permanent loss**.

---

## 2.5 Property 5 — Poisoning Resistance

A **catalyst poison** is a substance that chemisorbs strongly on active sites and blocks them, even in trace amounts (ppm level). Common poison–catalyst pairs:

- **S, Se, As, P, halides** → poison noble-metal hydrogenation catalysts.
- **CO** → can poison Ni, Fe, Cu at low temperature.
- **Pb, V, Ni, Fe** → poison automotive three-way catalysts.

The **selectivity** of a poison is judged by the **adsorption bond strength** versus the reactant:

$$ \text{Poison strength} \propto \frac{E_{\text{ads,poison}}}{E_{\text{ads,reactant}}} $$

If $E_{\text{ads,poison}} \gg E_{\text{ads,reactant}}$, the poison wins the site.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{product}}}{n_{\text{active sites}}}$ | Rate per site | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $\text{TON} = \text{TOF} \cdot t$ | Lifetime index | dimensionless |
| 3 | $k_{\text{app}} = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Apparent rate constant | depends on order |
| 4 | $S_i = \dfrac{r_i}{\sum_j r_j}$ | Selectivity to product $i$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\bar{S} = \dfrac{n_{\text{desired, out}}}{n_{\text{reactant, consumed}}}$ | Integral selectivity | dimensionless |
| 6 | $D_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_p}{\tau} D_{AB}$ | Effective diffusivity | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $\Phi = L \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Thiele modulus | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh \Phi}{\Phi}$ | Effectiveness factor (1st order, slab) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_{\text{WP}} = \dfrac{r_{\text{obs}} \rho_c R_p^2}{D_{\text{eff}} C_s}$ | Weisz–Prater criterion | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\dfrac{r_{\text{obs}} R_p \rho_b}{k_c C_b} < 0.15$ | Mears criterion (external) | dimensionless |

Where:
- $\varepsilon_p$ = pellet porosity, $\tau$ = tortuosity, $D_{AB}$ = bulk molecular diffusivity.
- $L$ = characteristic pellet half-thickness, $k$ = intrinsic rate constant.
- $\rho_c$ = catalyst density, $R_p$ = pellet radius, $C_s$ = surface concentration.
- $k_c$ = external mass-transfer coefficient, $C_b$ = bulk concentration.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

These equations are not academic — they drive **reactor design** decisions. The **Weisz–Prater** and **Mears** criteria are *first-line diagnostics* engineers run before commissioning a catalyst pellet. If $C_{\text{WP}} \ll 1$, internal diffusion is negligible and the measured rate equals intrinsic kinetics — exactly the regime where the catalyst's *true* activity and selectivity can be reported.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the First-Order Effectiveness Factor (Slab Geometry)

The **effectiveness factor $\eta$** is the single most important concept linking *catalytic property (activity)* to *pellet geometry and transport*. Here is the full derivation KTU expects.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Diffusion–Reaction Equation in a Slab

Consider a flat catalyst slab of half-thickness $L$. A first-order irreversible reaction $A \to B$ occurs inside the slab. Steady-state material balance on a differential shell at position $x$ from the centre:

$$ \frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \frac{k C_A}{D_{\text{eff}}} = 0 $$

Boundary conditions:

$$ \left.\frac{dC_A}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0 \quad \text{(symmetry at centre)} $$

$$ C_A(x = L) = C_{A,s} \quad \text{(known surface concentration)} $$

### 3.1.2 Non-Dimensionalization

Define:

$$ y = \frac{C_A}{C_{A,s}}, \quad z = \frac{x}{L} $$

Then $\dfrac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} = \dfrac{C_{A,s}}{L^2} \dfrac{d^2 y}{dz^2}$, and the ODE becomes:

$$ \frac{C_{A,s}}{L^2} \frac{d^2 y}{dz^2} = \frac{k C_{A,s} y}{D_{\text{eff}}} $$

Dividing through by $C_{A,s} / L^2$:

$$ \frac{d^2 y}{dz^2} = \frac{k L^2}{D_{\text{eff}}} \, y $$

Defining the **Thiele modulus** $\Phi = L \sqrt{k / D_{\text{eff}}}$:

$$ \frac{d^2 y}{dz^2} - \Phi^2 \, y = 0 $$

### 3.1.3 General Solution

The auxiliary equation is $m^2 - \Phi^2 = 0 \Rightarrow m = \pm \Phi$, hence:

$$ y(z) = A \cosh(\Phi z) + B \sinh(\Phi z) $$

Apply symmetry BC at $z = 0$: $\dfrac{dy}{dz}\big\vert_{z=0} = 0 \Rightarrow B \Phi \cosh(0) = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$.

Apply surface BC at $z = 1$: $y(1) = 1 \Rightarrow A \cosh(\Phi) = 1 \Rightarrow A = \dfrac{1}{\cosh(\Phi)}$.

Therefore:

$$ y(z) = \frac{C_A(z)}{C_{A,s}} = \frac{\cosh(\Phi z)}{\cosh(\Phi)} $$

### 3.1.4 Definition of the Effectiveness Factor

By definition:

$$ \eta = \frac{\text{actual rate}}{\text{rate if entire pellet were at } C_{A,s}} $$

$$ \eta = \frac{\int_0^L k C_A(x) \, dx}{k C_{A,s} L} = \int_0^1 y(z) \, dz $$

Substituting $y(z)$:

$$ \eta = \int_0^1 \frac{\cosh(\Phi z)}{\cosh(\Phi)} \, dz = \frac{1}{\cosh(\Phi)} \cdot \frac{\sinh(\Phi)}{\Phi} $$

Using the identity $\sinh / \cosh = \tanh$:

$$ \boxed{\eta = \frac{\tanh(\Phi)}{\Phi}} $$

### 3.1.5 Limiting Cases

**Case 1 — $\Phi \to 0$ (no diffusion limitation, kinetically controlled):**

Using Taylor expansion $\tanh(\Phi) \approx \Phi - \Phi^3/3 + \dots$:

$$ \eta = \frac{\Phi - \Phi^3/3 + \dots}{\Phi} = 1 - \frac{\Phi^2}{3} + \dots \approx 1 $$

Interpretation: every active site sees the same reactant concentration; the catalyst's intrinsic activity is fully utilized.

**Case 2 — $\Phi \to \infty$ (severe internal diffusion limitation):**

$\tanh(\Phi) \to 1$, hence $\eta \to 1/\Phi$.

Interpretation: only a thin outer shell reacts; the interior is starved. The apparent rate scales as $\sqrt{k \, D_{\text{eff}}} \cdot C_{A,s}$ — independent of pellet volume in the limit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Weisz–Prater Criterion

A measurable quantity is $C_{\text{WP}} = \dfrac{r_{\text{obs}} \, \rho_c \, R_p^2}{D_{\text{eff}} \, C_{A,s}}$. We want to find when $C_{\text{WP}} < 1$ guarantees $\eta \approx 1$.

For a spherical pellet and first-order kinetics, the internal-effectiveness Thiele modulus is $\Phi_s = R_p \sqrt{k / D_{\text{eff}}}$. The observed rate $r_{\text{obs}} = \eta \, k \, C_{A,s}$. Combining:

$$ C_{\text{WP}} = \frac{\eta \, k \, C_{A,s} \, \rho_c \, R_p^2}{D_{\text{eff}} \, C_{A,s}} = \eta \, \rho_c \, \Phi_s^2 $$

For $\Phi_s \ll 1$, $\eta \to 1$ and we require:

$$ C_{\text{WP}} \approx \rho_c \Phi_s^2 \ll 1 $$

The **operational criterion** universally used:

$$ \boxed{C_{\text{WP}} < 0.3 \;\; \text{implies no internal diffusion limitation}} $$

This is purely experimental — no $k$ or $D_{\text{eff}}$ needed!

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Selectivity Equation (Parallel Reactions)

For two parallel first-order reactions $A \to B$ (desired, $k_1$) and $A \to C$ (side, $k_2$):

Rates:

$$ r_B = k_1 C_A, \qquad r_C = k_2 C_A $$

Selectivity to $B$:

$$ S_B = \frac{r_B}{r_B + r_C} = \frac{k_1 C_A}{(k_1 + k_2) C_A} = \frac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2} $$

This is the **kinetic selectivity** — fixed by the *catalyst's electronic structure* (d-band position for metals, acid-site strength for oxides).

Now add diffusion limitation. If the catalyst operates at $\eta \ll 1$, the *apparent* rate constants are $k_{i,\text{app}} = \eta_i k_i$. For a slab, $\eta_i \approx 1/\Phi_i \propto 1/\sqrt{k_i}$:

$$ S_{B,\text{diff}} = \frac{k_1 / \sqrt{k_1}}{k_1 / \sqrt{k_1} + k_2 / \sqrt{k_2}} = \frac{\sqrt{k_1}}{\sqrt{k_1} + \sqrt{k_2}} $$

Compare:

$$ S_{B,\text{kin}} = \frac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2}, \qquad S_{B,\text{diff}} = \frac{\sqrt{k_1}}{\sqrt{k_1} + \sqrt{k_2}} $$

By the inequality of arithmetic and geometric means applied term-by-term:

$$ \frac{\sqrt{k_1}}{\sqrt{k_1} + \sqrt{k_2}} \le \frac{k_1}{k_1 + k_2} \quad \text{when} \quad k_1 > k_2 $$

**Conclusion:** diffusion limitation *always degrades* selectivity toward the faster reaction. This is a KTU-favourite insight — *porosity engineering is selectivity engineering*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property × Diffusion Regime

| Property | Kinetic regime ($\eta \to 1$) | Strong diffusion ($\eta \to 1/\Phi$) | Industrial remedy |
|----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------|-------------------|
| Activity | Equals intrinsic TOF | Reduced by factor $\eta$ | Smaller pellets, higher $D_{\text{eff}}$ |
| Selectivity | Fixed by catalyst chemistry | Drift toward faster side reaction | Coat active phase only in outer shell (egg-shell) |
| Stability | Long life | Coke builds at centre | Use bimodal pore structure |
| Poison resistance | Full exposure | Surface poison dominates | Guard beds, feed purification |
| Regenerability | Full restoration | Irreversible sintering if overheated | Limit hot-spots |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A first-order heterogeneous reaction occurs in spherical catalyst pellets of radius $R_p = 2 \text{ mm}$. The intrinsic rate constant is $k = 0.5 \text{ s}^{-1}$ and effective diffusivity $D_{\text{eff}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the Thiele modulus and effectiveness factor. Comment on whether the measured rate reflects intrinsic activity.

**Solution:**

$$ \Phi_s = R_p \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_{\text{eff}}}} = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{0.5}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}} $$

$$ \Phi_s = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{4.167 \times 10^{4}} = (2 \times 10^{-3})(204.1) = 0.408 $$

$$ \eta = \frac{1}{\Phi_s}\left[\frac{1}{\tanh(3 \Phi_s)} - \frac{1}{3 \Phi_s}\right] \quad \text{(sphere formula)} $$

For a quick estimate using the slab approximation:

$$ \eta \approx \frac{\tanh(0.408)}{0.408} = \frac{0.385}{0.408} = 0.944 $$

Since $\eta \approx 0.94$, the system is **mildly diffusion-influenced** — measured rate is ~94 % of intrinsic. To push to $\eta > 0.99$, reduce pellet radius to $R_p \le 0.5 \text{ mm}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Inter-Relationship of Catalytic Properties

The following Mermaid **functional block architecture** maps how the five primary catalytic properties depend on one another and on catalyst design parameters.

**Reading the diagram:**
- *Left column* — design inputs that the chemist controls.
- *Centre block* — the five KTU properties, shown with logical arrows (e.g., *Acid/Base Character* affects *Selectivity*, not *Activity*).
- *Right column* — quantitative metrics and transport diagnostics that the engineer measures.
- *Far right* — industrial KPIs that decide commercial viability.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Deactivation and Regeneration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Fresh Catalyst State F"]:::s
    s2["Active in Reactor Operating State O"]:::s
    s3["Deactivated by Coking Sintering Poisoning State D"]:::s
    s4["Regeneration in Air H2 or Steam State R"]:::s

    s1 -->|On stream| s2
    s2 -->|Loss of activity over time| s3
    s3 -->|Regeneration cycle| s4
    s4 -->|Partial or full activity restored| s1

    classDef s fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#1c4e80,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** A catalyst cycles between *Fresh → Operating → Deactivated → Regenerated → Fresh*. The fraction of original activity recovered at each loop is the **regenerability index**. Industrial FCC units perform this loop every 2–10 seconds; ammonia synthesis catalysts may run 5–15 years before regeneration is needed.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Is the Measured Rate Intrinsic?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Measure Weisz Prater CWP"}:::d
    q2{"CWP less than 0.3"}:::d
    q3{"Measure Mears Criterion M"}:::d
    q4{"M less than 0.15"}:::d
    a1["Internal diffusion negligible"]:::r
    a2["Internal diffusion present Reduce pellet radius"]:::r
    a3["External diffusion negligible"]:::r
    a4["External diffusion present Increase linear velocity"]:::r
    a5["Measured rate equals intrinsic kinetics Report TOF directly"]:::g

    q1 -->|Calculate| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| q3
    q2 -->|No| a2
    q3 -->|Calculate| q4
    q4 -->|Yes| a3
    q4 -->|No| a4
    q3 --> q3
    a3 --> a5
    a2 --> q1

```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Before quoting TOF or selectivity, every researcher is expected to *demonstrate* that transport artefacts are absent using this exact diagnostic flow. Skipping this step costs marks in viva and project viva-voce.
