<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**Define the term "Turnover Frequency" (TOF) and explain its significance as a measure of intrinsic catalytic activity.**

**Model Answer:**

**Turnover Frequency (TOF)** is defined as the number of molecules of reactant converted (or product formed) per **active site** of the catalyst per unit **time**, under specified reaction conditions.

$$ \text{TOF} = \frac{\text{moles of product formed per second}}{\text{moles of active sites on the catalyst}} \quad [\text{s}^{-1}] $$

**Significance:**
1. It is an **intensive** property — independent of catalyst mass or surface area.
2. It allows **fair comparison** between different catalysts (different metals, supports, dispersions).
3. It is the **true measure of intrinsic activity** once transport artefacts (Weisz–Prater and Mears) are eliminated.
4. Typical industrial TOFs range from $10^{-2}$ to $10^{3} \text{ s}^{-1}$.

**Distinguish from TON:** $\text{TON} = \text{TOF} \times t$ and represents the *lifetime* productivity of a single active site.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**List any four catalytic properties of solid catalysts and state which property is most affected by internal diffusion limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

The four catalytic properties are:

1. **Activity** — speed of conversion.
2. **Selectivity** — preference for the desired product.
3. **Stability** — resistance to deactivation.
4. **Regenerability** — ability to recover activity after deactivation.

(The fifth property, **Poison Resistance**, may also be cited.)

**Most affected by internal diffusion:** **Selectivity**. Because internal diffusion limits the reactant from reaching the catalyst interior, the *relative* rates of parallel reactions shift — the faster reaction is favoured, but overall selectivity to the desired product typically **decreases** when $\eta \ll 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style / PECHT633 Module 1]**

### Question A

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the five essential catalytic properties that an ideal industrial catalyst must possess. Give at least one industrial example for each property.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the effectiveness factor $\eta = \tanh(\Phi)/\Phi$ for a first-order reaction in a planar (slab) catalyst pellet. Comment on its limiting values for $\Phi \to 0$ and $\Phi \to \infty$.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a)

The five essential properties of an industrial catalyst are:

**1. Activity:**
Activity is the rate at which the catalyst converts reactants to products. It is measured as specific rate (mol g⁻¹ s⁻¹), intrinsic rate per unit surface area, or as the **Turnover Frequency (TOF)**. *Industrial example:* Pt in automotive catalytic converters gives TOF $\sim 1\text{–}10 \text{ s}^{-1}$ for CO oxidation at 200 °C.

**2. Selectivity:**
Selectivity is the fraction of converted reactant that forms the desired product. *Industrial example:* Zeolite **HZSM-5** in methanol-to-gasoline (MTG) process is ~80 % selective to C₅–C₁₁ gasoline-range hydrocarbons over C₁–C₄ gases.

**3. Stability / Catalyst Life:**
Stability is the ability to retain activity and selectivity over long time-on-stream. *Industrial example:* The fused-iron catalyst for ammonia synthesis (Fe + K₂O + Al₂O₃ + CaO) operates for **5–15 years** at 400–500 °C and 150–300 bar.

**4. Regenerability:**
This is the ability to restore activity after deactivation. *Industrial example:* **FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking) catalyst** (RE-USY zeolite in a silica-alumina matrix) is regenerated every 2–10 seconds by burning off coke with air at ~700 °C in the regenerator.

**5. Resistance to Poisoning:**
The catalyst must withstand feed impurities such as S, P, As, Pb, halides. *Industrial example:* Three-way automotive catalysts (Pt/Rh/CeO₂–Al₂O₃) suffer severely from **Pb and S poisoning**, which is why unleaded, low-sulphur fuels are mandated.

A sixth, sometimes-listed property is **mechanical strength** (attrition resistance), critical for fluidized-bed catalysts like FCC.

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Derivation — see Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step working.**

The boundary-value problem is:

$$ \frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} = \frac{k C_A}{D_{\text{eff}}}, \qquad \left.\frac{dC_A}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0, \qquad C_A(L) = C_{A,s} $$

Non-dimensionalizing with $y = C_A / C_{A,s}$ and $z = x / L$, and defining the Thiele modulus $\Phi = L \sqrt{k / D_{\text{eff}}}$, the ODE reduces to:

$$ \frac{d^2 y}{dz^2} = \Phi^2 \, y $$

General solution: $y(z) = A \cosh(\Phi z) + B \sinh(\Phi z)$. Symmetry gives $B = 0$. Surface BC gives $A = 1 / \cosh(\Phi)$. Therefore:

$$ \frac{C_A(z)}{C_{A,s}} = \frac{\cosh(\Phi z)}{\cosh(\Phi)} $$

The effectiveness factor:

$$ \eta = \int_0^1 y(z) \, dz = \frac{1}{\cosh(\Phi)} \cdot \frac{\sinh(\Phi)}{\Phi} = \frac{\tanh(\Phi)}{\Phi} $$

**Limiting cases:**

- **$\Phi \to 0$** (kinetic regime): $\tanh(\Phi) \to \Phi$, so $\eta \to 1$. Every active site sees the same reactant concentration as the surface. *The measured rate = intrinsic rate.*

- **$\Phi \to \infty$** (strong diffusion regime): $\tanh(\Phi) \to 1$, so $\eta \to 1/\Phi$. Only a thin outer shell is active. *The measured rate $\propto \sqrt{k D_{\text{eff}}} \cdot C_{A,s}$*, independent of pellet thickness $L$. Catalyst is being **wasted**.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 style / PECHT633 Module 1]**

### Question B

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concepts of activity and selectivity in heterogeneous catalysis. Discuss how pore-diffusion limitations affect both properties quantitatively. Use the Weisz–Prater criterion to explain how engineers confirm the absence of internal diffusion limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A first-order heterogeneous reaction is carried out in spherical catalyst pellets. Given: $R_p = 1.5 \text{ mm}$, $D_{\text{eff}} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, observed rate $r_{\text{obs}} = 0.08 \text{ mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}\text{-cat} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$, surface concentration $C_{A,s} = 2.0 \text{ mol/m}^3$, catalyst density $\rho_c = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate the Weisz–Prater criterion and comment on whether internal diffusion is significant.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Activity and Selectivity — Definitions:**

- **Activity** is the rate at which the catalyst accelerates the reaction. Quantitative measures: specific activity (per mass), intrinsic activity (per surface area), and **TOF** (per active site).

- **Selectivity** for parallel reactions $A \to B$ (desired) and $A \to C$ (side) is:

$$ S_B = \frac{r_B}{r_B + r_C} $$

In the **kinetic regime** ($\eta = 1$), selectivity is fixed purely by catalyst chemistry: $S_B = k_1/(k_1 + k_2)$.

**Pore-Diffusion Effects:**

When internal diffusion limits the reaction ($\eta \ll 1$), the *apparent* rate constants become $k_{i,\text{app}} \approx k_i / \Phi_i \propto \sqrt{k_i}$. The selectivity in the diffusion-limited regime is:

$$ S_{B,\text{diff}} = \frac{\sqrt{k_1}}{\sqrt{k_1} + \sqrt{k_2}} $$

Since $\sqrt{k} < k$ when $k < 1$, but the *ratio* changes, $S_{B,\text{diff}} < S_{B,\text{kin}}$ whenever $k_1 > k_2$. **Diffusion always degrades selectivity** toward the faster-reacting pathway, which may or may not be the desired one.

**Activity is also reduced** by the factor $\eta$: $r_{\text{obs}} = \eta \cdot k \cdot C_{A,s}$. The pellet interior is reactant-starved.

**Weisz–Prater Criterion:**

A measurable, transport-property-free check is:

$$ C_{\text{WP}} = \frac{r_{\text{obs}} \, \rho_c \, R_p^2}{D_{\text{eff}} \, C_{A,s}} $$

Rule of thumb: **$C_{\text{WP}} < 0.3$** implies no significant internal diffusion limitation; the observed rate equals the intrinsic kinetics, and TOF can be quoted without further correction.

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Given:**
- $R_p = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $D_{\text{eff}} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $r_{\text{obs}} = 0.08 \text{ mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}\text{-cat} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$
- $C_{A,s} = 2.0 \text{ mol/m}^3$
- $\rho_c = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Weisz–Prater Calculation:**

$$ C_{\text{WP}} = \frac{r_{\text{obs}} \, \rho_c \, R_p^2}{D_{\text{eff}} \, C_{A,s}} $$

Substitute:

$$ C_{\text{WP}} = \frac{(0.08)(1200)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(4 \times 10^{-6})(2.0)} $$

Numerator:

$$ (0.08)(1200) = 96; \quad (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ \text{Numerator} = 96 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} = 216 \times 10^{-6} = 2.16 \times 10^{-4} $$

Denominator:

$$ (4 \times 10^{-6})(2.0) = 8.0 \times 10^{-6} $$

Therefore:

$$ C_{\text{WP}} = \frac{2.16 \times 10^{-4}}{8.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 27 $$

**Interpretation:**

$C_{\text{WP}} = 27 \gg 0.3$ — **internal diffusion is severely limiting**. The observed rate is not the intrinsic rate. Remedies:
1. Reduce pellet radius — halving $R_p$ reduces $C_{\text{WP}}$ by factor 4.
2. Increase pellet porosity to raise $D_{\text{eff}}$.
3. Use a washcoat/egg-shell distribution of active phase on a low-resistance support.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in Weisz–Prater:** $r_{\text{obs}}$ must be per unit *pellet volume*; $C_{A,s}$ must be in *bulk gas/liquid* concentration. Mixing SI and CGS units is the #1 error.
> 2. **Forgetting to square $R_p$:** The criterion is $\propto R_p^2$, not $R_p$. A factor-of-2 error in pellet size becomes a factor-of-4 error in $C_{\text{WP}}$.
> 3. **Mis-stating the limit:** Students often write "$C_{\text{WP}} < 1$"; the correct and KTU-accepted threshold is **< 0.3** for first-order kinetics.
> 4. **Ignoring the Mears criterion:** Internal diffusion being negligible does *not* guarantee external diffusion is negligible — both must be checked.
> 5. **Conflating TOF and TON:** TOF has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$ (rate); TON is dimensionless (total count over catalyst life).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Catalytic Properties**

- ☐ The **five** primary properties of an industrial catalyst: **Activity, Selectivity, Stability, Regenerability, Poison Resistance** (in this exact order).
- ☐ **TOF** (s⁻¹) is the intrinsic activity metric; **TON** is dimensionless lifetime metric. **Never** confuse the two.
- ☐ **Selectivity** for parallel reactions: $S_i = r_i / \sum_j r_j$. Integral selectivity uses moles in/out across the reactor.
- ☐ **Three kinds of selectivity** for zeolites: **reactant, product, and transition-state shape-selectivity**.
- ☐ **Stability** has three flavours: thermal (sintering), chemical (leaching), mechanical (attrition).
- ☐ **Sintering** kinetics follow a power law: $-dD/dt = k_s (D - D_{\text{eq}})^n$, $n \in [2, 4]$.
- ☐ **Poisons** are species with adsorption bond strength greater than that of the reactant (S, As, P, Pb, halides for noble metals).
- ☐ **Thiele modulus** $\Phi = L\sqrt{k/D_{\text{eff}}}$ — dimensionless measure of reaction-vs-diffusion.
- ☐ **Effectiveness factor** for 1st order slab: $\eta = \tanh(\Phi)/\Phi$. Limits: $\eta \to 1$ as $\Phi \to 0$; $\eta \to 1/\Phi$ as $\Phi \to \infty$.
- ☐ **Weisz–Prater criterion** $C_{\text{WP}} = r_{\text{obs}} \rho_c R_p^2 / (D_{\text{eff}} C_{A,s})$. Rule: $C_{\text{WP}} < 0.3$ ⇒ kinetic regime.
- ☐ **Mears criterion** $r_{\text{obs}} R_p \rho_b / (k_c C_b) < 0.15$ ⇒ external mass transfer negligible.
- ☐ **Diffusion always degrades selectivity** toward the faster pathway — a frequently tested insight.
- ☐ Always check **Weisz–Prater AND Mears** before reporting TOF — never quote activity without transport diagnostics.
- ☐ **Order of arguments in ESE answers:** Definition → Mathematical metric → Quantitative example → Industrial relevance → Limitation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Adsorption Equilibrium Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In heterogeneous catalysis, the **Adsorption Equilibrium Constant** (often denoted as $K_A$, $K_{ads}$, or $b$) is the thermodynamic equilibrium constant that governs the reversible attachment of gas-phase (or liquid-phase) molecules (the *adsorbate*) onto the exposed surface of a solid (the *adsorbent* or catalyst). It quantitatively expresses the dynamic balance between the rate of adsorption onto vacant surface sites and the rate of desorption from occupied surface sites, once the system has reached a steady state at a given temperature and partial pressure.

Mathematically, for a generic monolayer adsorption reaction:

$$A_{(g)} + S \rightleftharpoons A \cdot S$$

The equilibrium constant is defined in terms of fractional surface coverage $\theta$ (the fraction of surface sites occupied) and the partial pressure $P_A$ of the adsorbate:

$$K_A = \frac{\theta_A}{P_A (1 - \theta_A)}$$

where $1 - \theta_A$ represents the fraction of *vacant* sites remaining on the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *adsorption equilibrium constant* is the cornerstone for deriving every classical adsorption isotherm (Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin, BET). Every isotherm is essentially a different *mathematical model* describing how $K_A$ and $P_A$ jointly determine $\theta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Convention (KTU Board):** The symbol $b$ is widely used in the Langmuir isotherm to denote the adsorption equilibrium constant (also called the *adsorption coefficient* or *affinity constant*), having units of $\text{atm}^{-1}$ or $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ when concentration is expressed as pressure.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a packed movie theatre with **100 identical seats** (the *catalyst surface sites*) and a steady stream of people (*adsorbate molecules*) entering the lobby. The door is the *surface*; once a person sits, they occupy one seat. Initially, finding a seat is easy, so most newcomers sit down — but as the theatre fills, it becomes harder to find a free seat.

- The **adsorption equilibrium constant** $K_A$ measures how *strongly* a molecule prefers to *sit* on a vacant site rather than *float around* in the gas phase.
- A **large $K_A$** means the surface has a strong *affinity* for the molecule (people love these seats — they grab them eagerly even when the theatre is mostly empty).
- A **small $K_A$** means weak interaction (people are picky — they only sit when almost every other seat is taken).
- **Temperature** acts like a *disturbance*: heat kicks seated people back into the lobby (favors desorption), reducing $K_A$ for exothermic adsorption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy in Engineering:** This is exactly how a *drying agent* (silica gel) works in your medicine bottle — it has a high $K_A$ for water vapour, pulling moisture from the air and trapping it on its surface until the air becomes dry.

---

### 1.3 Physical Significance of the Equilibrium Constant

The value of $K_A$ encapsulates three fundamental thermodynamic quantities that govern whether adsorption is *favourable* and how strongly it is favoured:

| Thermodynamic Property | Symbol | Effect on $K_A$ |
|------------------------|--------|-----------------|
| Standard Gibbs Free Energy Change | $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ | $K_A = e^{-\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}/RT}$ |
| Standard Enthalpy Change | $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$ | Governs temperature sensitivity |
| Standard Entropy Change | $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$ | Reflects order gained on the surface |

Because adsorption is almost always **exothermic** ($\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} < 0$) and is accompanied by a **loss of translational freedom** ($\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads} < 0$), the spontaneous regime requires $\vert \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} \vert > \vert T \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads} \vert$ at the operating temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Mechanism

To understand *how* the adsorption equilibrium constant arises, we examine the molecular events at a catalytic surface:

1. **Step 1 — Surface Encounter:** A gas-phase molecule $A$ strikes the catalyst surface with a frequency proportional to its partial pressure $P_A$ and the fraction of *vacant* sites $(1 - \theta)$.
2. **Step 2 — Adsorption Event:** The molecule either sticks (if it has sufficient energy to overcome the activation barrier of physisorption/chemisorption) or reflects. Successful sticking produces an occupied site.
3. **Step 3 — Surface Migration (optional):** The adsorbed species may diffuse along the surface, finding a more stable binding configuration.
4. **Step 4 — Desorption Event:** The adsorbed molecule gains enough thermal energy to leave the surface, returning to the gas phase and freeing a site.
5. **Step 5 — Dynamic Equilibrium:** At equilibrium, the *forward adsorption rate* equals the *backward desorption rate*, and a *time-averaged* steady coverage $\theta_{eq}$ is established.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key:** The equilibrium constant $K_A$ is *not* measured instantaneously — it is defined only when the rates of opposing processes are equal. It is a *thermodynamic state function*, not a kinetic parameter.

---

### 2.2 Relationship to Adsorption Kinetics

The kinetic definition provides the most direct path to understanding $K_A$. Let:
- $r_{ads} = k_a P_A (1 - \theta)$ — rate of adsorption (first-order in pressure and vacant sites)
- $r_{des} = k_d \theta$ — rate of desorption (first-order in occupied sites)

At equilibrium:

$$k_a P_A (1 - \theta_{eq}) = k_d \theta_{eq}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{k_a}{k_d} = \frac{\theta_{eq}}{P_A (1 - \theta_{eq})} = K_A$$

The ratio of the forward and reverse rate constants equals the **adsorption equilibrium constant** $K_A$. This is a direct consequence of the principle of *detailed balance* (microscopic reversibility).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table compiles every essential equation related to the adsorption equilibrium constant that a KTU 2024 student is expected to know. **All symbols are KTU-board standard.**

| Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Description / Use |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Equilibrium Constant Definition** | $K_A = \dfrac{\theta}{P_A(1-\theta)}$ | Fundamental definition |
| **Langmuir Isotherm** | $\theta = \dfrac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$ | Coverage as a function of $P_A$ |
| **Langmuir Linear Form** | $\dfrac{P_A}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_A} + P_A$ | Used to extract $K_A$ experimentally |
| **Langmuir (in terms of volume)** | $\dfrac{P_A}{V} = \dfrac{1}{K_A V_m} + \dfrac{P_A}{V_m}$ | $V_m$ = monolayer volume |
| **Dissociative Langmuir** | $\theta = \dfrac{\sqrt{K_A P_A}}{1 + \sqrt{K_A P_A}}$ | For $A_2 \rightleftharpoons 2A^*$ |
| **Freundlich Isotherm** | $\theta = K_F P_A^{1/n}$ | Empirical; $n > 1$ |
| **Temkin Isotherm** | $\theta = \dfrac{RT}{b_T} \ln(K_T P_A)$ | Accounts for surface heterogeneity |
| **BET Isotherm (linear form)** | $\dfrac{P_A}{V(P_0 - P_A)} = \dfrac{1}{K_B V_m C} + \dfrac{(C-1)P_A}{K_B V_m C \, P_0}$ | Multilayer adsorption |
| **Thermodynamic Link** | $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln K_A$ | Connects $K_A$ to free energy |
| **van't Hoff Equation** | $\dfrac{d \ln K_A}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT^2}$ | Temperature dependence of $K_A$ |
| **Integrated van't Hoff** | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{A,2}}{K_{A,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Two-temperature evaluation |
| **Gibbs–Helmholtz Form** | $\ln K_A = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}$ | Used to obtain $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}$ |
| **Henry's Law Limit** | $\theta \approx K_A P_A \quad (K_A P_A \ll 1)$ | Low-pressure limit |
| **Saturation Limit** | $\theta \rightarrow 1 \quad (K_A P_A \gg 1)$ | High-pressure limit |
| **Coverage in terms of $b$** | $\theta = \dfrac{b P_A}{1 + b P_A}$ | $b \equiv K_A$ (Langmuir convention) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention for KTU:** $K_A$ expressed using *partial pressure* has units of $\text{atm}^{-1}$ (or $\text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\text{bar}^{-1}$, $\text{torr}^{-1}$). If the isotherm is written using *concentration* $C_A$ (mol/L), the constant becomes dimensionless or has units of $\text{L/mol}$, depending on the standard state chosen.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The adsorption equilibrium constant is not a textbook abstraction — it is a *design parameter* used daily in chemical engineering practice:

- **Catalyst Screening:** $K_A$ values measured for candidate catalysts predict *coverage* of reactants at industrial partial pressures. A catalyst with a $K_A$ that is *too high* poisons itself (the product cannot desorb); a $K_A$ that is *too low* means reactants barely adsorb.
- **Sabatier's Principle (Volcano Plot):** The *optimal* catalyst has an intermediate $K_A$ for the rate-limiting intermediate. This is the conceptual basis for volcano-shaped activity plots used in ammonia synthesis and fuel-cell catalyst design.
- **Adsorptive Separations:** Pressure-Swing Adsorption (PSA) for oxygen/nitrogen separation relies on the temperature and pressure dependence of $K_A$ for each gas.
- **Hazardous Gas Capture:** Activated carbon respirators and gas masks are designed using the $K_A$ of toxic gases (e.g., $K_A$ for benzene on activated carbon).
- **Heterogeneous Reactor Design:** The Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate expressions used in reactor modelling depend explicitly on $K_A$ of each reactant.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Langmuir Isotherm and the Adsorption Equilibrium Constant

**Starting Postulate (Langmuir, 1918):**

1. The surface possesses a fixed number of equivalent adsorption sites.
2. Each site can hold only one adsorbate molecule (monolayer coverage).
3. The adsorption energy is the same on every site (homogeneous surface).
4. There is no interaction between adsorbate molecules on neighbouring sites.
5. At equilibrium, a dynamic balance exists between adsorption and desorption.

**Step 1: Express the rates of the two opposing processes.**

The rate of adsorption is proportional to the partial pressure of the gas $P_A$ and the fraction of *empty* surface sites $(1 - \theta)$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a \, P_A \, (1 - \theta)$$

The rate of desorption is proportional to the fraction of *occupied* sites:

$$r_{des} = k_d \, \theta$$

**Step 2: Apply the condition of dynamic equilibrium.**

At equilibrium, the net rate of adsorption is zero; forward and reverse rates are equal:

$$k_a \, P_A \, (1 - \theta_{eq}) = k_d \, \theta_{eq}$$

**Step 3: Algebraic rearrangement.**

Divide both sides by $k_d$:

$$\frac{k_a}{k_d} \, P_A \, (1 - \theta) = \theta$$

Define the equilibrium constant as the ratio of rate constants:

$$K_A \equiv \frac{k_a}{k_d}$$

Substitute:

$$K_A P_A (1 - \theta) = \theta$$

Expand the parenthesis:

$$K_A P_A - K_A P_A \theta = \theta$$

Collect the $\theta$ terms on one side:

$$K_A P_A = \theta + K_A P_A \theta = \theta (1 + K_A P_A)$$

**Step 4: Solve explicitly for $\theta$.**

$$\theta = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Langmuir Isotherm** — a direct algebraic consequence of the equilibrium condition. The adsorption equilibrium constant $K_A$ appears as the single parameter that quantifies the surface's affinity for the adsorbate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearisation of the Langmuir Isotherm (for Experimental Determination of $K_A$)

**Why linearise?** A direct plot of $\theta$ vs $P_A$ gives a curve, making it hard to extract $K_A$ precisely. Rewriting the equation in a linear form allows the use of a simple straight-line fit.

**Step 1: Start with the Langmuir equation.**

$$\theta = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

**Step 2: Invert both sides.**

$$\frac{1}{\theta} = \frac{1 + K_A P_A}{K_A P_A} = \frac{1}{K_A P_A} + 1$$

**Step 3: Multiply through by $P_A$.**

$$\frac{P_A}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_A} + P_A$$

This has the form $y = mx + c$ with:

- $y = \dfrac{P_A}{\theta}$
- $x = P_A$
- slope $m = 1$
- intercept $c = \dfrac{1}{K_A}$

**Step 4: Experimental use.**

Measure equilibrium coverage at several pressures, plot $P_A/\theta$ against $P_A$, fit a straight line. The intercept gives $1/K_A$ directly:

$$K_A = \frac{1}{\text{intercept}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermodynamic Link Between $K_A$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$

For any reaction at equilibrium, the standard Gibbs free energy change is related to the equilibrium constant by:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$

For the adsorption reaction $A_{(g)} + S \rightleftharpoons A \cdot S$:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln K_A$$

**Expanding the Gibbs free energy:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} - T \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$$

Therefore:

$$-RT \ln K_A = \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} - T \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$$

Divide by $-RT$:

$$\ln K_A = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}$$

This is a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$ when $\ln K_A$ is plotted against $1/T$:

- Slope = $-\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} / R$
- Intercept = $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads} / R$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** A van't Hoff plot of $\ln K_A$ vs $1/T$ is the standard experimental method for determining the *isosteric heat of adsorption* and the entropy change upon adsorption.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — The van't Hoff Equation (Temperature Dependence of $K_A$)

Starting from $\ln K_A = -\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}/(RT) + \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}/R$, differentiate both sides with respect to temperature, assuming $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$ are approximately constant over the temperature range of interest:

$$\frac{d \ln K_A}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R T^2}$$

This is the **van't Hoff equation**. Integrating between two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\int_{K_{A,1}}^{K_{A,2}} d \ln K_A = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

Evaluating:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{A,2}}{K_{A,1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Application:** If $K_A$ is measured at two temperatures, the heat of adsorption can be calculated:

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} = -R \cdot \frac{\ln(K_{A,2}/K_{A,1})}{(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Coverage Expression for Dissociative Adsorption

For gases like $H_2$, $O_2$, $N_2$ that adsorb *dissociatively*, each molecule occupies *two* adjacent sites:

$$A_{2(g)} + 2S \rightleftharpoons 2(A \cdot S)$$

**Step 1: Write the rate expressions.**

The rate of dissociative adsorption depends on $P_{A_2}$ and the square of the vacant fraction (two sites required):

$$r_{ads} = k_a \, P_{A_2} \, (1 - \theta)^2$$

The rate of desorption (recombination) depends on the square of the coverage:

$$r_{des} = k_d \, \theta^2$$

**Step 2: Equate at equilibrium.**

$$k_a P_{A_2} (1 - \theta)^2 = k_d \theta^2$$

**Step 3: Define the equilibrium constant and solve.**

$$K_A = \frac{k_a}{k_d} = \frac{\theta^2}{P_{A_2}(1 - \theta)^2}$$

Take the square root of both sides:

$$\sqrt{K_A} = \frac{\theta}{(1 - \theta) \sqrt{P_{A_2}}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\theta = \frac{\sqrt{K_A P_{A_2}}}{1 + \sqrt{K_A P_{A_2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mistake:** Students frequently write the dissociative form incorrectly as $\theta = K_A P / (1 + K_A P)$. The *square root* of $K_A P$ is essential when the molecule splits into two surface species.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Behaviour of $K_A$

The following matrix consolidates the limiting cases of the Langmuir isotherm, which the KTU examiner frequently tests:

| Regime | Mathematical Condition | Approximate Form | Physical Meaning |
|--------|------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Low Pressure / Henry Region** | $K_A P_A \ll 1$ | $\theta \approx K_A P_A$ | Coverage grows linearly with pressure |
| **Intermediate Coverage** | $K_A P_A \approx 1$ | $\theta \approx 1/2$ | Half the surface covered |
| **High Pressure / Saturation** | $K_A P_A \gg 1$ | $\theta \approx 1$ | Monolayer complete; no more sites |
| **Dissociative, Low $P$** | $\sqrt{K_A P_{A_2}} \ll 1$ | $\theta \approx \sqrt{K_A P_{A_2}}$ | Sub-linear growth |
| **Dissociative, High $P$** | $\sqrt{K_A P_{A_2}} \gg 1$ | $\theta \approx 1$ | Monolayer saturation |

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** The adsorption of CO on a platinum catalyst follows the Langmuir isotherm. At 300 K, the equilibrium constant $K_A = 1.2 \text{ atm}^{-1}$. Calculate the fractional surface coverage at a CO partial pressure of (a) 0.1 atm and (b) 5 atm.

**Solution (a):**

$$\theta = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A} = \frac{(1.2)(0.1)}{1 + (1.2)(0.1)} = \frac{0.12}{1.12} \approx 0.107$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\theta = \frac{(1.2)(5)}{1 + (1.2)(5)} = \frac{6.0}{7.0} \approx 0.857$$

> [!NOTE]
> This problem illustrates the dramatic non-linearity of the Langmuir isotherm: a 50-fold pressure increase (from 0.1 to 5 atm) raises coverage only from ~11% to ~86%, demonstrating the *asymptotic* approach to monolayer saturation.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Derivation Logic of the Langmuir Isotherm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Molecule A Approaches Surface] --> B{Is Site Vacant?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Adsorption Event: A + S becomes AS]
    B -- No --> D[Reflection Back to Gas Phase]
    C --> E[Occupied Site: Fraction theta]
    D --> F[Vacant Site: Fraction 1 minus theta]
    E --> G{Adsorbed Long Enough?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Desorption Event: AS becomes A + S]
    G -- No --> I[Stays Adsorbed]
    H --> F
    I --> J[Equilibrium Steady State]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Apply Rate Equality: ka Pa 1 minus theta equals kd theta]
    K --> L[Solve for theta]
    L --> M[Langmuir Isotherm: theta equals Ka Pa divided by 1 plus Ka Pa]
    M --> N[Determine Ka Experimentally from Linear Plot]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Factors Governing the Adsorption Equilibrium Constant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Process Inputs]
        T[Operating Temperature T]
        Pa[Partial Pressure Pa]
        S[Surface Site Density]
    end
    subgraph SURFACE[Surface Properties]
        E[Heat of Adsorption Delta H]
        sigma[Entropy Change Delta S]
        het[Surface Homogeneity]
    end
    subgraph CONST[Equilibrium Output]
        Ka[Adsorption Equilibrium Constant Ka]
    end
    subgraph RESULT[Resulting Behaviour]
        theta[Surface Coverage theta]
        rate[Langmuir Hinshelwood Rate]
    end
    T --> Ka
    E --> Ka
    sigma --> Ka
    het --> Ka
    Pa --> theta
    Ka --> theta
    theta --> rate
    S --> theta
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Experimental Determination of $K_A$ from Isotherm Data

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Reactor as Adsorption Reactor
    participant Sensor as Pressure Sensor
    participant Analyst as Data Analyst
    participant Plot as Langmuir Plot Tool
    Reactor->>Sensor: Introduce gas at known pressure Pa
    Sensor->>Analyst: Record equilibrium pressure P eq
    Analyst->>Analyst: Calculate coverage theta from gas balance
    Analyst->>Plot: Plot Pa divided by theta vs Pa
    Plot->>Analyst: Fit straight line
    Analyst->>Analyst: Read intercept equals 1 over Ka
    Analyst->>Analyst: Compute Ka equals 1 divided by intercept
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Limiting Regimes of the Langmuir Isotherm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Langmuir Equation] --> LowP[Low Pressure: Ka Pa much less than 1]
    Start --> MidP[Intermediate: Ka Pa approximately 1]
    Start --> HighP[High Pressure: Ka Pa much greater than 1]
    LowP --> Henry[Henry Region: theta approximately Ka Pa linear]
    MidP --> Half[Half Coverage: theta equals 0.5]
    HighP --> Sat[Monolayer Saturation: theta approaches 1]
    Henry --> Note1[Linear dependence on pressure]
    Half --> Note2[Transition region]
    Sat --> Note3[All sites occupied no further uptake]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid and not a physical surface sketch?** In the digital KTU answer-sheet environment, free-hand surface diagrams (showing molecular species on a catalyst) cannot be reproduced. The block-flow schematics above convey the *process logic* with maximum clarity and are accepted by KTU examiners.
