## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define the adsorption equilibrium constant. Explain how it is related to the rate constants of adsorption and desorption.**

**Model Answer:**

The adsorption equilibrium constant $K_A$ is the thermodynamic constant that characterises the dynamic balance between the rate at which molecules adsorb onto a solid surface and the rate at which they desorb from it at a given temperature.

For a generic adsorption process $A_{(g)} + S \rightleftharpoons A \cdot S$, the rate of adsorption is $r_{ads} = k_a P_A (1 - \theta)$ and the rate of desorption is $r_{des} = k_d \theta$. At equilibrium, these rates are equal:

$$k_a P_A (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

Hence:

$$K_A = \frac{k_a}{k_d} = \frac{\theta}{P_A(1 - \theta)}$$

The ratio of the forward (adsorption) and reverse (desorption) rate constants is equal to the equilibrium constant, a direct consequence of the principle of detailed balance. The larger $K_A$, the stronger the surface's affinity for the adsorbate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is the physical significance of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm? State its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer:**

The Langmuir isotherm, proposed by Irving Langmuir in 1918, gives the fraction of a homogeneous catalyst surface that is covered by an adsorbate at equilibrium, as a function of pressure and temperature. It is based on the assumptions of monolayer coverage, equivalent adsorption sites, and no interactions between adsorbed molecules.

Its mathematical form is:

$$\theta = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

where $\theta$ is the fractional surface coverage, $P_A$ is the partial pressure of the adsorbate, and $K_A$ is the adsorption equilibrium constant.

Physically, the isotherm captures two limits: (i) at very low pressures, coverage grows linearly with pressure ($\theta \approx K_A P_A$); (ii) at very high pressures, the surface becomes saturated with a complete monolayer ($\theta \rightarrow 1$). The constant $K_A$ is the *single* parameter that controls the shape of this transition.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** and offer an *internal choice* (either-or). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm starting from the kinetic definition of the equilibrium constant. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The adsorption of nitrogen on a catalyst follows the Langmuir isotherm. The following data were obtained at 77 K:

| $P$ (torr) | 50 | 100 | 200 | 300 | 400 |
|------------|-----|------|------|------|------|
| $V$ (cm³ STP) | 12.0 | 22.0 | 38.0 | 50.0 | 60.0 |

Using a Langmuir plot, determine the monolayer volume $V_m$ and the adsorption equilibrium constant $K_A$ in $\text{torr}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Assumptions of the Langmuir isotherm:**

1. The surface has a fixed, finite number of well-defined adsorption sites.
2. Each site can hold only one adsorbate molecule (monolayer coverage).
3. The adsorption energy is identical on every site (energetically homogeneous surface).
4. There is no lateral interaction between molecules adsorbed on neighbouring sites.
5. Adsorption is reversible; at equilibrium, the rates of adsorption and desorption are equal.

**Step 1: Write the rate of adsorption.**

The rate at which molecules strike and stick to the surface is proportional to the partial pressure $P_A$ and the fraction of vacant sites $(1 - \theta)$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a P_A (1 - \theta)$$

**Step 2: Write the rate of desorption.**

The rate at which adsorbed molecules leave the surface is proportional to the fraction of occupied sites:

$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

**Step 3: Apply the equilibrium condition.**

$$k_a P_A (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

**Step 4: Define the equilibrium constant.**

$$K_A = \frac{k_a}{k_d} = \frac{\theta}{P_A (1 - \theta)}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\theta$.**

$$K_A P_A (1 - \theta) = \theta \implies K_A P_A = \theta (1 + K_A P_A)$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}}$$

**Step 6: Discussion of limiting cases.**

- At low $P_A$: $\theta \approx K_A P_A$ (Henry's law).
- At high $P_A$: $\theta \rightarrow 1$ (monolayer saturation).
- The parameter $K_A$ uniquely characterises the affinity of the surface for the adsorbate.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

The Langmuir linear form in terms of adsorbed volume is:

$$\frac{P}{V} = \frac{1}{K_A V_m} + \frac{P}{V_m}$$

We calculate $P/V$ for each data point:

| $P$ (torr) | $V$ (cm³) | $P/V$ (torr/cm³) |
|------------|-----------|-------------------|
| 50 | 12.0 | 4.167 |
| 100 | 22.0 | 4.545 |
| 200 | 38.0 | 5.263 |
| 300 | 50.0 | 6.000 |
| 400 | 60.0 | 6.667 |

**Linear regression** of $P/V$ vs $P$:

- Slope $\approx 0.00833$ (cm³)$^{-1}$
- Intercept $\approx 3.75$ torr/cm³

**Determine $V_m$:**

$$V_m = \frac{1}{\text{slope}} = \frac{1}{0.00833} \approx 120 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ STP}$$

**Determine $K_A$:**

$$K_A = \frac{1}{V_m \cdot \text{intercept}} = \frac{1}{(120)(3.75)} \approx 0.00222 \text{ torr}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx 120 \text{ cm}^3, \quad K_A \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-3} \text{ torr}^{-1}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of relevant equations, explain how the adsorption equilibrium constant $K_A$ is related to the standard thermodynamic functions $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$, $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$, and $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$. Derive the van't Hoff equation for the temperature dependence of $K_A$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The adsorption of a gas on a catalyst surface was studied at two temperatures. At $T_1 = 300$ K, the equilibrium constant was $K_{A,1} = 5.0 \text{ atm}^{-1}$. At $T_2 = 350$ K, $K_{A,2} = 1.2 \text{ atm}^{-1}$. Calculate the standard enthalpy of adsorption $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$ and the standard entropy of adsorption $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$. Assume $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$ are temperature-independent. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Relate $K_A$ to $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$.**

For the adsorption reaction $A_{(g)} + S \rightleftharpoons A \cdot S$, the standard Gibbs free energy change is:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln K_A$$

**Step 2: Expand $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$.**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} - T \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$$

**Step 3: Combine.**

$$-RT \ln K_A = \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} - T \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$$

$$\ln K_A = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}$$

This is a linear equation in $1/T$; the slope of a $\ln K_A$ vs $1/T$ plot gives $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$, and the intercept gives $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$.

**Step 4: Derive the van't Hoff equation.**

Differentiate $\ln K_A = -\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}/(RT) + \Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}/R$ with respect to $T$, treating $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$ as constants:

$$\frac{d \ln K_A}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R T^2}$$

**Step 5: Integrate between two temperatures.**

$$\int_{K_{A,1}}^{K_{A,2}} d \ln K_A = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{A,2}}{K_{A,1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$

**Engineering Note:** The van't Hoff equation shows that for exothermic adsorption ($\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} < 0$), $K_A$ decreases as $T$ increases — a fact routinely exploited in Temperature-Programmed Desorption (TPD) experiments to characterise catalyst surfaces.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Apply the integrated van't Hoff equation.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{A,2}}{K_{A,1}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.2}{5.0}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{300}\right)$$

$$-1.4271 = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{8.314}\left(-4.762 \times 10^{-4}\right)$$

$$-1.4271 = \Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} \cdot (5.728 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} = \frac{-1.4271}{5.728 \times 10^{-5}} \approx -24{,}920 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} \approx -24.9 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

The negative sign confirms the adsorption is exothermic, as expected for physical adsorption.

**Step 3: Calculate $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$ from the Gibbs relation.**

Using $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln K_A$ at $T_1 = 300$ K:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads,1} = -(8.314)(300)\ln(5.0) = -3994 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 4: Use $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$.**

$$-3994 = -24{,}920 - (300)\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}$$

$$(300)\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads} = -24{,}920 + 3994 = -20{,}926$$

$$\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads} = \frac{-20{,}926}{300} \approx -69.75 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads} \approx -69.8 \text{ J/(mol·K)}}$$

The negative entropy change reflects the loss of translational freedom when a gas-phase molecule becomes localised on the surface — consistent with the second law of thermodynamics for spontaneous adsorption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention for the van't Hoff equation:** Many students incorrectly write $\ln(K_2/K_1) = +\Delta H / R \cdot (1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$. The correct form has a **negative sign** when written in the *integrated* form above. Mixing signs results in a *wrong sign* for $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}$.
> 2. **Unit consistency for $K_A$:** $K_A$ must be *dimensionless* (or expressed in the same units as the standard state) when substituted into $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$. Using $K_A$ in atm$^{-1}$ and then into a "dimensionless" equation gives an incorrect magnitude.
> 3. **Dissociative vs non-dissociative forms:** Writing $\theta = K_A P/(1 + K_A P)$ for hydrogen on nickel is **wrong** — the dissociative form $\theta = \sqrt{K_A P}/(1 + \sqrt{K_A P})$ must be used.
> 4. **Linearisation for experimental $K_A$:** Students often plot $\theta$ vs $P$ (curved) instead of $P/\theta$ vs $P$ (linear). The KTU examiner expects the *linearised* form for data analysis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** $K_A = \dfrac{\theta}{P_A(1 - \theta)}$ — the equilibrium constant for the reversible adsorption of a gas on a solid surface.
- **Langmuir Isotherm:** $\theta = \dfrac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$ — the central equation governing monolayer adsorption on a homogeneous surface.
- **Langmuir Linear Form:** $\dfrac{P_A}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_A} + P_A$ — the working equation for experimental determination of $K_A$.
- **Dissociative Langmuir:** $\theta = \dfrac{\sqrt{K_A P_{A_2}}}{1 + \sqrt{K_A P_{A_2}}}$ — for gases that split into two surface species.
- **Rate-Constant Link:** $K_A = k_a / k_d$ — adsorption equilibrium constant equals the ratio of forward to reverse rate constants (detailed balance).
- **Thermodynamic Link:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln K_A$ — relates $K_A$ to standard free energy of adsorption.
- **Enthalpy–Entropy Form:** $\ln K_A = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}$ — linear in $1/T$, slope gives $\Delta H$, intercept gives $\Delta S$.
- **van't Hoff Equation:** $\dfrac{d \ln K_A}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT^2}$ — temperature sensitivity of $K_A$.
- **Integrated van't Hoff:** $\ln(K_{A,2}/K_{A,1}) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ — two-temperature form.
- **Limiting Behaviour:** Low $P$: $\theta \approx K_A P_A$ (linear). High $P$: $\theta \rightarrow 1$ (saturation).
- **Units of $K_A$:** $\text{atm}^{-1}$ (or $\text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\text{torr}^{-1}$, $\text{bar}^{-1}$) when $P$ is in pressure units; $\text{L/mol}$ (or dimensionless) when $C$ is in concentration units.
- **Assumptions to Memorise:** Homogeneous surface, monolayer, equivalent sites, no lateral interactions, dynamic equilibrium.
- **Exothermic Adsorption:** $\Delta H^{\circ}_{ads} < 0$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}_{ads} < 0$ — universal for physical and chemisorption.
- **Industrial Relevance:** Catalyst screening, Sabatier volcano plots, PSA separations, gas-mask design, Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate laws.
- **Solved Key:** $\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}}$ and $\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln K_A}$ are the two equations a KTU 2024 student must *memorise verbatim* for the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Desorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Desorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Desorption** is the reverse process of adsorption in which adsorbed species (atoms, molecules, or reaction products) on a solid catalyst surface are released back into the gas or liquid phase. It is the final and often rate-limiting step in heterogeneous catalytic cycles, since it regenerates the active site and completes the catalytic turnover.

Mathematically, for a generic adsorbate–surface pair $\text{A}^*$, desorption is represented as:

$$\text{A}^* \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{A}_{(g)} + *$$

where the asterisk ($*$) denotes a vacant active site on the catalyst surface.

> [!NOTE] **Key Distinction from Adsorption**
> Desorption is **endothermic** (requires energy input equal to the **activation energy of desorption, $E_d$**), while adsorption is **exothermic**. The heat released during adsorption equals the energy required for desorption at equilibrium, by virtue of microscopic reversibility.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **sponge soaked with water** resting on a counter:
- The sponge = catalyst surface
- Water trapped inside the pores = adsorbed reactant/product
- **Squeezing the sponge over a sink** = **desorption**

The harder the water clings to the sponge (strong adsorption), the more energy (heat, vacuum, or concentration gradient) you must apply to release it. In catalysis, if products cling **too tightly** to the surface, the active site gets *poisoned* and the reaction stalls — a phenomenon central to the **Sabatier Principle**.

> [!TIP] **Real-World Analogy — Magnet Release**
> Think of a magnet stuck to a fridge (adsorption). Pulling it off requires effort proportional to the magnetic force. Desorption is the "pull-off" energy, and the fridge surface is "regenerated" once the magnet is removed.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Desorption Activation Energy | $E_d$ | **40 – 200 kJ/mol** |
| Pre-exponential Factor | $\nu_d$ | $10^{13}$ – $10^{15}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| Surface Coverage | $\theta$ | 0 – 1 (dimensionless) |
| Desorption Enthalpy | $\Delta H_{des}$ | 40 – 250 kJ/mol |
| Typical TPD Heating Rate ($\beta$) | $\beta = dT/dt$ | 1 – 20 K/min |

> [!NOTE]
> The **pre-exponential factor** for desorption typically matches vibrational frequencies of surface bonds, on the order of $10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ (the "normal" attempt frequency at surfaces).

---

## 1.4 Why Desorption Matters in Heterogeneous Catalysis

A complete catalytic cycle consists of **five sequential steps**:

1. **External diffusion** of reactants to the surface
2. **Internal diffusion** through pores
3. **Chemisorption** of reactants on active sites
4. **Surface reaction** (the chemical transformation)
5. **Desorption** of products — *regenerating the active site*

If step 5 is slow, products accumulate, sites remain occupied, and the **turnover frequency (TOF)** drops dramatically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Desorption

Desorption can be broken down into two primary physical mechanisms:

### 2.1.1 Activated (Chemical) Desorption
- The adsorbate is **chemically bonded** to the surface.
- A significant activation barrier $E_d$ must be overcome.
- **Slow** compared to physisorption-based release.
- Common for products of industrial reactions (e.g., $\text{NH}_3$ from Fe catalyst in Haber process).

### 2.1.2 Non-Activated (Spontaneous) Desorption
- Energy released upon adsorption is sufficient to overcome $E_d$.
- Occurs for **weakly bound (physisorbed)** species.
- Often the limiting factor is simply the **thermal population** of the desorbing state.

### 2.1.3 Associative (Recombinative) Desorption
- Two adsorbed atoms combine and leave as a molecule.
$$\text{A}^* + \text{B}^* \;\longrightarrow\; \text{AB}_{(g)} + 2*$$
- Example: $2\text{H}^* \rightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)}$ on metal catalysts (Pd, Pt, Ni).
- Highly relevant in **hydrogenation**, **ammonia synthesis**, and **Fischer–Tropsch** chemistry.

---

## 2.2 Thermodynamics of Desorption

For an adsorption–desorption equilibrium, the standard free energy change of desorption is:

$$\Delta G_{des} = \Delta H_{des} - T \Delta S_{des}$$

- $\Delta H_{des} > 0$ (endothermic)
- $\Delta S_{des} > 0$ (gas-phase molecule has more entropy than surface-bound state)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **At equilibrium, $\Delta G_{des} = 0$**, giving the desorption temperature:
> $$T_{des} = \frac{\Delta H_{des}}{\Delta S_{des}}$$
> This is the principle exploited in **Temperature-Programmed Desorption (TPD)** experiments to measure binding strength.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Save this table — these are the equations KTU examiners expect you to reproduce and apply.**

| # | Equation | Description | Notes |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $r_d = \nu_d\, \theta^n \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_d}{RT}\right)$ | Polanyi–Wigner rate law | $n$ = desorption order |
| 2 | $E_d = \Delta H_{ads}$ | Activation energy ↔ adsorption enthalpy | Microscopic reversibility |
| 3 | $\theta = \dfrac{K P}{1 + K P}$ | Langmuir isotherm (single site) | For non-dissociative adsorption |
| 4 | $r_d = k_d\, \theta^n$ | Desorption rate (general form) | $k_d$ is desorption rate constant |
| 5 | $k_d = \nu_d \exp(-E_d/RT)$ | Arrhenius form of $k_d$ | — |
| 6 | $\dfrac{E_d}{RT_p^2} = \dfrac{\nu_d\, n\, \theta_p^{n-1}}{\beta}$ | Redhead TPD equation | $T_p$ = peak temperature, $\beta$ = heating rate |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\theta_A}{1-\theta_A} = \dfrac{1}{K_d P_A}$ | Equilibrium for desorption | $K_d$ = desorption equilibrium constant |
| 8 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_p^2}{\beta}\right) = \dfrac{E_d}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T_p} + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_d}{\nu_d R}\right)$ | Linearized Redhead form | For TPD data plotting |
| 9 | $\Delta G_{des} = \Delta H_{des} - T\Delta S_{des}$ | Gibbs free energy of desorption | — |
| 10 | $T_{des} = \Delta H_{des}/\Delta S_{des}$ | Equilibrium desorption temperature | At $\Delta G_{des}=0$ |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Catalyst Design**: The strength of product–surface binding determines the optimal operating temperature.
- **TPD Spectroscopy**: Experimental measurement of $E_d$ by heating a surface and recording mass-spectrometer signals of desorbing species.
- **Microkinetic Modeling**: Desorption rate constants are essential inputs for reactor simulations.
- **Regeneration Cycles**: Coked catalysts are regenerated by burning off coke — a forced, high-temperature desorption-like process.

> [!TIP] **Examiner Cue**
> KTU frequently tests the relationship $E_d \approx -\Delta H_{ads}$ and asks students to compute the order of desorption from TPD peak shapes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Polanyi–Wigner Equation (Desorption Rate Law)

The Polanyi–Wigner equation is the **fundamental kinetic expression** governing desorption. We derive it from transition state theory.

### Step 1 — Define the Desorption Event
Consider a desorption event in which $n$ surface species on a single site (or pair of sites) leave the surface:

$$\text{A}_n^* \;\xrightarrow{k_d}\; \text{A}_{n(g)} + *$$

### Step 2 — Apply Transition State Theory
According to TST, the rate constant for crossing an energy barrier is:

$$k_d = \nu_d \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

where $\nu_d$ is the pre-exponential factor (frequency of attempts, $\sim 10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$) and $E_d$ is the activation energy barrier for desorption.

### Step 3 — Multiply by Surface Concentration
The rate is the product of $k_d$ and the surface concentration of the adsorbate raised to the power $n$:

$$r_d = k_d\, \theta^n = \nu_d\, \theta^n \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

This is the **Polanyi–Wigner equation**.

### Step 4 — Interpret the Desorption Order $n$
- $n = 1$: Molecular (non-dissociative) desorption
- $n = 2$: Associative desorption (e.g., $\text{H}_2$ from $2\text{H}^*$)
- $n = 0$: Zero-order (e.g., multilayer desorption)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Redhead Equation for TPD Peak Temperature

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU board exams for this module.

### Step 1 — Write the Polanyi–Wigner Rate for First-Order Desorption
For $n = 1$:

$$r_d = -\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \nu_d\, \theta\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Introduce a Linear Heating Program
The sample temperature is ramped at a constant heating rate $\beta$:

$$T = T_0 + \beta t \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{dT}{dt} = \beta$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dT} = \frac{1}{\beta}\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\frac{\nu_d}{\beta}\, \theta\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Peak Maximum Condition
At the TPD peak, $\dfrac{d^2 \theta}{dT^2} = 0$, which reduces (by differentiating the rate equation) to:

$$\frac{d}{dT}\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)\right] = 0 \text{ at } T = T_p$$

Carrying out the differentiation:

$$\frac{E_d}{RT_p^2} = \frac{\nu_d}{\beta}$$

This is the **Redhead equation** for first-order desorption:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{E_d}{R T_p} = \ln\!\left(\frac{\nu_d T_p}{\beta}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{E_d}{R T_p}\right)\;}$$

### Step 4 — Practical Approximation
For typical values $\nu_d \approx 10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ and $\beta \approx 10\ \text{K/s}$, the equation is often simplified to:

$$E_d \;\approx\; R T_p \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{\nu_d T_p}{\beta}\right) - 3.64 \right]$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: TPD Peak Shape vs. Desorption Order

| Desorption Order $n$ | TPD Peak Shape | Position vs. Coverage | Diagnostic Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (molecular) | Asymmetric, peak tail to high $T$ | $T_p$ **independent** of $\theta_0$ | Most chemisorbed molecules |
| 2 (associative) | Symmetric, broad | $T_p$ **decreases** as $\theta_0$ increases | $\text{H}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, $\text{N}_2$ desorption |
| 0 (multilayer) | Common leading edge; $T_p$ fixed | $T_p$ **independent** of $\theta_0$ | Physisorbed multilayers |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Favorite Question**
> "From a TPD spectrum, how do you determine the desorption order?"
> **Answer**: Vary the initial coverage $\theta_0$. If $T_p$ shifts, it is 2nd order; if it remains fixed, it is 1st order.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A first-order TPD peak for CO desorbing from a Pd surface appears at $T_p = 450\ \text{K}$ when the heating rate is $\beta = 5\ \text{K/s}$. Take $\nu_d = 10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ and $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$. Estimate the desorption activation energy $E_d$.

**Solution:**

Using the Redhead approximation:

$$E_d \approx R T_p \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{\nu_d T_p}{\beta}\right) - 3.64 \right]$$

Step 1 — Compute the argument of the logarithm:

$$\frac{\nu_d T_p}{\beta} = \frac{10^{13} \times 450}{5} = 9 \times 10^{14}$$

Step 2 — Take the natural log:

$$\ln(9 \times 10^{14}) = \ln(9) + 14 \ln(10) = 2.197 + 32.236 = 34.43$$

Step 3 — Subtract 3.64:

$$34.43 - 3.64 = 30.79$$

Step 4 — Multiply by $R T_p$:

$$E_d = 8.314 \times 450 \times 30.79 = 1.152 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J/mol} \approx 115.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!TIP] **Answer:** $E_d \approx 115\ \text{kJ/mol}$, in the expected range for CO/Pd chemisorption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Catalytic Cycle — Position of Desorption

The following flowchart locates desorption within the broader catalytic cycle and shows how it regenerates the active site.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactants in bulk gas phase] --> B[External diffusion to surface]
    B --> C[Internal diffusion into pores]
    C --> D[Adsorption on active site]
    D --> E[Surface chemical reaction]
    E --> F[Desorption of products]
    F --> G[Vacant active site regenerated]
    G --> D
    F --> H[Products diffuse back to bulk]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cycle loops from `G` (vacant site) back to `D` (adsorption) — this is the **catalytic turnover loop**.

---

## 4.2 Energy Profile Along the Reaction Coordinate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Gas-phase reactant] -->|Adsorption Ea_ads| B[Adsorbed intermediate]
    B -->|Surface reaction E_rxn| C[Adsorbed product]
    C -->|Desorption Ea_des| D[Gas-phase product]
```

**Label meanings in the diagram:**
- `Ea_ads` — activation energy of adsorption
- `E_rxn` — activation energy of surface reaction
- `Ea_des` — activation energy of desorption (largest in many cases)

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of TPD Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Sample_Chamber
        S1[Single crystal or powder catalyst] --> S2[Heated by programmable furnace]
    end
    S2 --> S3[Temperature controller with linear ramp beta]
    S3 --> S4[Quartz reactor]
    S4 --> S5[Quadrupole mass spectrometer QMS]
    S5 --> S6[Desorption signal vs temperature]
    S4 --> S7[Vacuum pump for UHV conditions]
    S7 --> S4
```

> [!TIP]
> The **TPD experiment** is essentially a programmed thermal desorption measured by a mass spectrometer. The peak temperature $T_p$ is converted into $E_d$ via the Redhead equation.

---

## 4.4 Effect of Heating Rate on TPD Peak

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low heating rate beta small] --> B[Tp shifts to lower temperature]
    C[High heating rate beta large] --> D[Tp shifts to higher temperature]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Doubling $\beta$ approximately **increases $T_p$ by 5–10%** — used experimentally to confirm desorption order and to extract $\nu_d$ from multiple-ramp TPD data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define desorption. What is its role in heterogeneous catalysis?**

**Model Answer:**
Desorption is the process by which a species (atom, molecule, or ion) that is adsorbed on a solid surface is released back into the gas or liquid phase. In heterogeneous catalysis, it is the **final step** of the catalytic cycle, in which the product molecule leaves the catalyst surface, thereby **regenerating the active site** for the next cycle. The desorption rate directly controls the **turnover frequency** and the overall rate of reaction. If desorption is slow, products accumulate, block active sites, and the reaction rate decreases (product inhibition).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between first-order and second-order desorption with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | First-Order Desorption | Second-Order Desorption |
|---|---|---|
| Rate law | $r_d = \nu_d \theta \exp(-E_d/RT)$ | $r_d = \nu_d \theta^2 \exp(-E_d/RT)$ |
| Process | Molecular release | Recombinative release |
| Example | $\text{CO}^* \rightarrow \text{CO}_{(g)} + *$ | $2\text{H}^* \rightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} + 2*$ |
| TPD peak | Asymmetric; $T_p$ independent of $\theta_0$ | Symmetric; $T_p$ decreases with $\theta_0$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Polanyi–Wigner equation** for desorption. Clearly explain the significance of the desorption order $n$ and pre-exponential factor $\nu_d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Redhead equation** for first-order thermal desorption and discuss how TPD peak temperature varies with heating rate $\beta$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Define the desorption event:

$$\text{A}_n^* \;\longrightarrow\; \text{A}_{(g)} + n*$$

**Step 2:** From transition state theory:

$$k_d = \nu_d \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by surface concentration to obtain:

$$r_d = \nu_d\, \theta^n \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Discuss significance:
- $n$ = kinetic order; $n=1$ molecular, $n=2$ associative, $n=0$ multilayer.
- $\nu_d \sim 10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ corresponds to typical surface vibrational frequencies.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** For $n=1$, Polanyi–Wigner:

$$-\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \nu_d\, \theta\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Convert time derivative to temperature derivative using $\beta = dT/dt$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dT} = -\frac{\nu_d}{\beta}\, \theta\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3:** At peak maximum, $d^2\theta/dT^2 = 0$, which yields:

$$\frac{E_d}{RT_p^2} = \frac{\nu_d}{\beta} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{E_d}{RT_p} = \ln\!\left(\frac{\nu_d T_p}{\beta}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{E_d}{RT_p}\right)$$

**Step 4:** As $\beta$ increases, $T_p$ **increases** logarithmically, since faster heating gives species less time to desorb at any given temperature.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **thermodynamics of desorption**. Derive the relation between the equilibrium desorption temperature, the enthalpy of desorption, and the entropy change. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TPD experiment for $\text{NH}_3$ desorbing from a zeolite shows a peak at $T_p = 580\ \text{K}$ with $\beta = 10\ \text{K/min}$. Assuming first-order desorption with $\nu_d = 10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ and $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, calculate the desorption activation energy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** At equilibrium, $\Delta G_{des} = 0$:

$$\Delta H_{des} - T \Delta S_{des} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Solve for temperature:

$$T_{des} = \frac{\Delta H_{des}}{\Delta S_{des}}$$

**Step 3:** Interpret: this is the temperature at which the adsorbate population is half-desorbed — the basis of TPD peak interpretation.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Use the Redhead approximation:

$$E_d \approx R T_p \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{\nu_d T_p}{\beta}\right) - 3.64 \right]$$

**Step 2:** Convert $\beta$ to SI: $\beta = 10\ \text{K/min} = 1/6\ \text{K/s} \approx 0.1667\ \text{K/s}$.

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$\frac{\nu_d T_p}{\beta} = \frac{10^{13} \times 580}{0.1667} = 3.48 \times 10^{16}$$

$$\ln(3.48 \times 10^{16}) = 38.08$$

**Step 4:**

$$E_d = 8.314 \times 580 \times (38.08 - 3.64) = 8.314 \times 580 \times 34.44$$

$$E_d = 1.661 \times 10^{5}\ \text{J/mol} \approx 166.1\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> **Common mistake:** Forgetting to convert $\beta$ from K/min to K/s. The Redhead equation requires **SI units** for $\beta$ (K/s). Failing this conversion leads to an error of ~2 orders of magnitude in the result. Always double-check units before substituting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Desorption** is the reverse of adsorption and the **final step** of the catalytic cycle.
- ✅ It is **endothermic**; $E_d \approx \Delta H_{ads}$ in magnitude.
- ✅ **Polanyi–Wigner equation**: $r_d = \nu_d \theta^n \exp(-E_d/RT)$ — universal desorption rate law.
- ✅ **Desorption order $n$** distinguishes molecular (1), associative (2), and multilayer (0) release.
- ✅ **Redhead equation** relates TPD peak temperature $T_p$ to $E_d$, $\nu_d$, and heating rate $\beta$.
- ✅ **Higher $\beta$ → higher $T_p$** (logarithmic dependence).
- ✅ **First-order TPD**: $T_p$ **independent** of $\theta_0$; **second-order**: $T_p$ **decreases** with $\theta_0$.
- ✅ **Sabatier principle**: optimum catalyst balances adsorption and desorption — neither too strong nor too weak.
- ✅ **Slow desorption** = rate-limiting step → low TOF, possible product inhibition.
- ✅ **TPD experiments** are the principal experimental tool to quantify $E_d$ and $\nu_d$.
- ✅ Pre-exponential factor $\nu_d \sim 10^{13}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ is the standard assumption in board calculations.
- ✅ Always convert $\beta$ to **K/s** before applying the Redhead equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Surface Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Reaction (KTU 2024 – PECHT633 Definition):**
> A **surface reaction** is the chemical transformation step in a heterogeneous catalytic cycle in which one or more reactant molecules, **chemically bound (adsorbed) onto the active sites of a solid catalyst surface**, undergo an elementary rearrangement, redistribution of bonds, or reaction with another adsorbed or impinging gas-phase species to form product intermediates. This step follows adsorption and precedes desorption in the classical **seven-step heterogeneous catalytic sequence**.

Mathematically, for a generic reaction $A + B \rightarrow C + D$, the surface reaction step is represented as:

$$A \cdot S + B \cdot S \rightleftharpoons (A-B)\cdot S + S \rightarrow C \cdot S + D \cdot S$$

where $S$ denotes an active surface site, and the dot denotes the chemisorbed state.

### 1.2 Position in the Heterogeneous Catalytic Cycle

The complete catalytic cycle comprises:

1. External (film) diffusion of reactants to the pellet
2. Internal (pore) diffusion within the catalyst
3. **Adsorption** of reactants onto active sites
4. **Surface reaction** ← *(focus of this module)*
5. **Desorption** of products
6. Internal pore diffusion of products outwards
7. External film diffusion of products into bulk

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Molecular Marriage Counselor":**
> Imagine a busy dance floor (the catalyst surface) where single dancers (reactant molecules) keep arriving. A strict bouncer (the active site) checks IDs at the door (adsorption) and only allows compatible partners onto the floor. Once on the dance floor, the partners are paired up and perform a coordinated routine (the **surface reaction**) to form a new dance team (product). Once the routine is finished, the new team walks off the floor (desorption). The *speed* of the coordinated dance determines how fast products form. The bouncer cannot let new dancers in until spots free up — this is the principle of **site balance** that governs surface reactions.

### 1.4 Classification of Surface Reactions (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Classification – Memorize This Hierarchy:**
> - **Type I – Unimolecular Surface Reaction:** A single adsorbed species decomposes.
> - **Type II – Bimolecular (Langmuir–Hinshelwood) Surface Reaction:** Two adsorbed species react.
> - **Type III – Eley–Rideal (Rideal–Eley) Mechanism:** One adsorbed species reacts with a gas-phase (non-adsorbed) species.
> - **Type IV – Mars–van Krevelen (Redox) Mechanism:** The catalyst lattice participates; relevant to oxidation reactions.

The standard **operating temperature** for most industrial surface reactions ranges from **200 °C – 600 °C**, with site densities of typical industrial catalysts of the order of **$10^{18}$ to $10^{19}$ sites/m²**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Surface Reaction Theory

The derivation of all surface-reaction rate laws rests on four pillars:

1. **Active sites are identical and energetically uniform** (Langmuir assumption).
2. **Each site can hold only one adsorbed species** (monolayer, no multi-layer adsorption at reaction site).
3. **Adsorption quasi-equilibrium** is established for all adsorbed reactants (when adsorption/desorption is fast relative to reaction).
4. **Site balance is conserved** at all times: total sites = vacant + occupied by each species.

### 2.2 Site Balance Equation (The Master Constraint)

For a reaction involving species $A$, $B$, and products $C$, $D$ on a surface with site density $C_t$:

$$C_t = C_v + C_A + C_B + C_C + C_D$$

where:
- $C_t$ = total active site concentration (mol/kg catalyst)
- $C_v$ = vacant site concentration
- $C_i$ = concentration of sites occupied by species $i$

### 2.3 Adsorption Equilibrium (Langmuir Isotherm Coupling)

When adsorption is at equilibrium, the fractional coverage $\theta_i$ of species $i$ is:

$$\theta_i = \frac{K_i P_i}{1 + \sum_j K_j P_j}$$

where:
- $K_i$ = adsorption equilibrium constant for species $i$ ($K_i = k_{a,i}/k_{d,i}$)
- $P_i$ = partial pressure of species $i$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Compilation Table – Surface Reactions (KTU 2024)**

| **#** | **Mechanism / Scenario** | **Rate Law (Surface-Reaction Controlling)** | **Units of $r$** | **Key Limiting Cases** |
|:-----:|:-------------------------|:---------------------------------------------|:----------------:|:----------------------|
| 1 | Unimolecular $A \cdot S \rightarrow$ Products | $r = k C_t \theta_A = \dfrac{k C_t K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{kg}_{cat}\cdot\text{s})$ | $\theta_A \ll 1$: 1st order; $\theta_A \rightarrow 1$: zero order |
| 2 | Bimolecular LH: $A\cdot S + B\cdot S \rightarrow C\cdot S + D\cdot S$ | $r = k C_t^2 \theta_A \theta_B = \dfrac{k C_t^2 K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + \cdots)^2}$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{kg}_{cat}\cdot\text{s})$ | Weak adsorption: 2nd order; Strong adsorption: 0 order |
| 3 | Eley–Rideal: $A\cdot S + B_{(g)} \rightarrow$ Products | $r = k C_t \theta_A P_B = \dfrac{k C_t K_A P_A P_B}{1 + K_A P_A}$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{kg}_{cat}\cdot\text{s})$ | Linear in $P_B$, Langmuir in $P_A$ |
| 4 | Unimolecular with inert $I$: $A\cdot S \rightarrow R + S$ | $r = \dfrac{k C_t K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_I P_I}$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{kg}_{cat}\cdot\text{s})$ | Inerts depress rate (site competition) |
| 5 | Bimolecular LH with product inhibition (R) | $r = \dfrac{k C_t^2 K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_R P_R)^2}$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{kg}_{cat}\cdot\text{s})$ | Self-poisoning by products |
| 6 | Mars–van Krevelen (oxidation) | $r = \dfrac{k P_A P_O}{(a P_A + b P_O)}$ | $\text{mol}/(\text{kg}_{cat}\cdot\text{s})$ | Requires lattice oxygen diffusion |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Surface reaction kinetics underpin the design of:
- **Catalytic converters** (automotive exhaust treatment) — LH-type CO + NO oxidation.
- **Ammonia synthesis reactors** (Haber–Bosch) — N₂ dissociation is rate-controlling surface step.
- **FCC units** in petroleum refineries — cracking of heavy hydrocarbons on zeolite acid sites.
- **Three-way catalysts** — simultaneous CO, HC, and NO$_x$ removal.
- **Fuel cells** — surface redox reactions on Pt/C electrodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Surface Reaction Rate Laws Matter in Engineering:**
> The correct rate expression dictates **reactor sizing ($W/F_A0$)**, **optimal operating pressure**, **heat removal strategy (exothermic surface reactions are highly temperature-sensitive)**, and **catalyst regeneration frequency**. A wrong assumption (e.g., using an Eley–Rideal expression when the actual mechanism is LH) can lead to a factor of 5–10 error in predicted catalyst weight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Unimolecular Surface Reaction (Type I)

**Mechanism:**
$$A + S \underset{k_{d,A}}{\overset{k_{a,A}}{\rightleftharpoons}} A \cdot S$$
$$A \cdot S \overset{k}{\rightarrow} \text{Products} + S$$

**Step 1: Identify the rate-controlling step.**
The surface reaction is controlling, hence it gives the rate.

**Step 2: Write the rate expression in terms of surface coverage.**

$$r = k \, C_A \cdot S = k \, C_t \, \theta_A$$

**Step 3: Use the Langmuir adsorption isotherm for $\theta_A$.**
Since only A adsorbs:

$$\theta_A = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the rate expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= k \, C_t \left(\frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}\right) \\[6pt]
r &= \frac{k \, C_t \, K_A \, P_A}{1 + K_A \, P_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Analyze limiting cases.**

- **Low pressure / weak adsorption ($K_A P_A \ll 1$):** $\;\;r \approx k \, C_t \, K_A \, P_A \;\Rightarrow\;$ **First order** in A.
- **High pressure / strong adsorption ($K_A P_A \gg 1$):** $\;\;r \approx k \, C_t \;\Rightarrow\;$ **Zero order** in A (site saturation).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Bimolecular Langmuir–Hinshelwood (Type II)

**Mechanism (single-site, both species adsorbed):**

$$A + S \rightleftharpoons A \cdot S, \qquad B + S \rightleftharpoons B \cdot S$$
$$A \cdot S + B \cdot S \overset{k}{\rightarrow} C \cdot S + D \cdot S$$
$$C \cdot S \rightleftharpoons C + S, \qquad D \cdot S \rightleftharpoons D + S$$

**Step 1: Rate of surface reaction (rate controlling).**

$$r = k \, C_{A \cdot S} \, C_{B \cdot S} = k \, C_t^2 \, \theta_A \, \theta_B$$

**Step 2: Apply Langmuir isotherms for A and B (assuming only A and B adsorb significantly).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_A &= \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B} \\[4pt]
\theta_B &= \frac{K_B P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= k \, C_t^2 \, \left(\frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B}\right)\left(\frac{K_B P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B}\right) \\[8pt]
r &= \frac{k \, C_t^2 \, K_A K_B \, P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Limiting behaviors.**

- **Weak adsorption of both:** $r \approx k C_t^2 K_A K_B P_A P_B$ → **Second order**.
- **Strong adsorption of A (B weakly adsorbed):** $r \approx \dfrac{k C_t^2 K_B P_B}{K_A P_A}$ → **Negative order** in A.
- **Strong adsorption of both:** $r \approx \dfrac{k C_t^2 K_A K_B P_A P_B}{K_A^2 P_A^2} = \dfrac{k C_t^2 K_B P_B}{K_A P_A}$ → **Zero order** overall.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Eley–Rideal Mechanism (Type III)

**Mechanism:** A is adsorbed; B reacts from gas phase.

$$A + S \rightleftharpoons A \cdot S$$
$$A \cdot S + B_{(g)} \overset{k}{\rightarrow} \text{Products} + S$$

**Step 1: Rate expression.** Reaction requires A on the surface and B in the gas phase impinging on it.

$$r = k \, C_{A \cdot S} \, P_B = k \, C_t \, \theta_A \, P_B$$

**Step 2: Langmuir isotherm for A (single adsorbed reactant).**

$$\theta_A = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= k \, C_t \, \left(\frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}\right) P_B \\[6pt]
r &= \frac{k \, C_t \, K_A \, P_A \, P_B}{1 + K_A P_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Limiting cases.**

- **Weak A adsorption:** $r \approx k C_t K_A P_A P_B$ → **First order in both A and B**.
- **Strong A adsorption:** $r \approx k C_t P_B$ → **Zero order in A, first order in B**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 – Unimolecular with Inert and Product Inhibition

**Mechanism:** A adsorbs, R (product) and I (inert) compete for sites.

$$r = k C_t \theta_A = \frac{k C_t K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_R P_R + K_I P_I}$$

This shows **product/inert inhibition** because the denominator grows, suppressing the rate. This is the basis of **catalyst deactivation kinetics** in KTU Module 1.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 – Mars–van Krevelen (Redox)

For oxidation $A + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow AO$ on a metal oxide $MO$:

$$A + MO \rightarrow AO + M \quad \text{(surface reaction)}$$
$$M + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow MO \quad \text{(re-oxidation)}$$

Equating rates of reduction and re-oxidation at steady state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 P_A \theta_M &= k_2 P_O \, \theta_{MO} \\[4pt]
\theta_M + \theta_{MO} &= 1 \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow r &= \frac{k_1 k_2 P_A P_O}{k_1 P_A + 2 k_2 P_O}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical expression for **Mars–van Krevelen kinetics** used for V$_2$O$_5$/TiO$_2$ SO$_2$ oxidation catalysts.

---

### 3.6 Step-by-Step Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Style):**
> The reaction $A \rightarrow B$ occurs on a catalyst via Type I unimolecular surface reaction. Given: $k = 0.05 \; \text{mol}/(\text{kg·s})$, $K_A = 2 \; \text{atm}^{-1}$, $P_A = 0.8 \; \text{atm}$, $C_t = 1 \; \text{mol/kg}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{k C_t K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A} = \frac{(0.05)(1)(2)(0.8)}{1 + (2)(0.8)} \\[6pt]
r &= \frac{0.08}{2.6} = 0.0308 \;\text{mol/(kg·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with low-pressure approximation: $r \approx 0.08$ mol/(kg·s) → significant error if you ignore coverage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 – Position of Surface Reaction in the Catalytic Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Reactant Bulk Fluid] --> B1[External Film Diffusion]
    B1 --> C1[Internal Pore Diffusion]
    C1 --> D1[Adsorption on Active Site]
    D1 --> E1[Surface Reaction on Catalyst]
    E1 --> F1[Desorption of Products]
    F1 --> G1[Internal Pore Diffusion Out]
    G1 --> H1[External Film Diffusion Out]
    H1 --> I1[Product Bulk Stream]
    style E1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

The **gold-highlighted node E1** is the surface reaction step — the focal point of this module.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 – Mechanism Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LH["LANGMUIR HINSHELWOOD MECHANISM"]
        LH1[Reactant A Adsorbs] --> LH2[Reactant B Adsorbs]
        LH2 --> LH3[Two Adsorbed Species React on Surface]
        LH3 --> LH4[Products Desorb]
    end
    subgraph ER["ELEY RIDEAL MECHANISM"]
        ER1[Reactant A Adsorbs] --> ER2[Gas Phase B Strikes Adsorbed A]
        ER2 --> ER3[Product Forms and Releases Site]
    end
    subgraph UN["UNIMOLECULAR SURFACE REACTION"]
        UN1[A Adsorbs] --> UN2[A Decomposes on Surface]
        UN2 --> UN3[Product Desorbs]
    end
    style LH fill:#E0F7FA
    style ER fill:#FFE0B2
    style UN fill:#F8BBD0
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 – Site Balance and Coverage Interaction Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CTV[Total Sites Ct] --> CV[Vacant Sites Cv]
    CTV --> CAS[A Adsorbed CAS]
    CTV --> CBS[B Adsorbed CBS]
    CTV --> CCR[Product R Adsorbed]
    CAS --> REACT[Surface Reaction Rate r]
    CBS --> REACT
    REACT --> CCR
    CCR --> DES[Desorption Step]
    DES --> CV
    CV -->|Vacant site available| ADS[Adsorption Step]
    ADS --> CAS
    ADS --> CBS
    style REACT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style CTV fill:#90CAF9
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 – Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Surface Reaction Rate Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{How many species adsorb?}
    Q1 -->|One: A only| Q2{Decomposition or reaction with gas phase B?}
    Q1 -->|Two: A and B| LH[Use Langmuir Hinshelwood LH Rate Law]
    Q1 -->|Catalyst lattice participates| MVK[Use Mars van Krevelen MVK Rate Law]
    Q2 -->|A decomposes on surface| UNI[Use Unimolecular Type I Rate Law]
    Q2 -->|B strikes adsorbed A| ER[Use Eley Rideal Type III Rate Law]
    LH --> OUT1[r = k Ct squared KA KB PA PB divided by 1 plus KA PA plus KB PB squared]
    UNI --> OUT2[r = k Ct KA PA divided by 1 plus KA PA]
    ER --> OUT3[r = k Ct KA PA PB divided by 1 plus KA PA]
    MVK --> OUT4[r = k1 k2 PA PO divided by k1 PA plus 2 k2 PO]
    style LH fill:#C8E6C9
    style UNI fill:#C8E6C9
    style ER fill:#C8E6C9
    style MVK fill:#C8E6C9
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model answers framed for KTU 2-mark conceptual + 1-mark application layout.**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between the **Langmuir–Hinshelwood (LH)** and **Eley–Rideal (ER)** mechanisms for bimolecular surface reactions. Give one industrial example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Langmuir–Hinshelwood | Eley–Rideal |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|
| Number of adsorbed reactants | Both A and B adsorbed | Only A adsorbed; B from gas phase |
| Rate dependence | Function of $\theta_A \theta_B$ | Function of $\theta_A P_B$ |
| Industrial example | CO oxidation on Pt (CO·Pt + O·Pt → 2CO₂·Pt) | CO₂ + H₂ on Cu-ZnO (H·Cu + CO₂(g) → HCOO·Cu) |
| Order at high pressure | Zero order (saturation) | Zero order in adsorbed A |

*(1 mark each for differentiation row + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the **site balance equation** for a surface reaction involving adsorbed species A, B, R (product), and an inert I. How does inert adsorption affect the rate?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The site balance equation is:

$$C_t = C_v + C_A + C_B + C_R + C_I$$

Inert species $I$ occupies sites without reacting. The denominator of the rate expression becomes $1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_R P_R + K_I P_I$. Higher $P_I$ ⇒ larger denominator ⇒ **reduced rate (reversible inhibition)**. *(2 marks site balance + 1 mark effect.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each question has two sub-parts worth **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the rate expression for the **bimolecular Langmuir–Hinshelwood surface reaction** $A + B \rightarrow C + D$ when the surface reaction is the rate-controlling step and both A and B adsorb on the same type of active site. Discuss the limiting cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the catalytic oxidation of SO₂ to SO₃ on V₂O₅, the following rate expression has been observed:

$$r = \frac{k P_{SO_2} P_{O_2}}{(1 + K_1 P_{SO_2} + K_2 P_{SO_3})^2}$$

Identify the mechanism and the rate-controlling step. If $P_{SO_2} = 0.05$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm, $k = 0.5$ mol/(kg·atm²·s), $K_1 = 8$ atm⁻¹, $K_2 = 4$ atm⁻¹, calculate the rate when $P_{SO_3} = 0.02$ atm. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Mechanism & Rate Equation (2 marks)**
Adsorption equilibria:
$$A + S \rightleftharpoons A\cdot S, \quad B + S \rightleftharpoons B\cdot S$$
Rate controlling surface reaction:
$$A\cdot S + B\cdot S \xrightarrow{k} C\cdot S + D\cdot S$$
$$r = k C_{A\cdot S} C_{B\cdot S} = k C_t^2 \theta_A \theta_B$$

**Step 2: Langmuir Isotherms (2 marks)**

$$\theta_A = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D}$$

$$\theta_B = \frac{K_B P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D}$$

**Step 3: Final Expression (1 mark)**

$$r = \frac{k C_t^2 K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D)^2}$$

**Step 4: Limiting Cases (2 marks)**

- Weak adsorption of both: $r \approx k C_t^2 K_A K_B P_A P_B$ → 2nd order.
- A strongly adsorbed, B weakly: $r \approx \dfrac{k C_t^2 K_B P_B}{K_A P_A}$ → 1st order in B, **negative 1st order in A**.
- Both strongly adsorbed: $r \approx k C_t^2$ → zero order overall (saturation).

---

**Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Mechanism Identification (2 marks)**
The denominator is squared — characteristic of the **Langmuir–Hinshelwood mechanism** with **surface reaction as the rate-controlling step**. SO₂ and O₂ both adsorb; SO₃ adsorbs and inhibits.

**Step 2: Rate Equation (1 mark)**
$$r = \frac{k P_{SO_2} P_{O_2}}{(1 + K_1 P_{SO_2} + K_2 P_{SO_3})^2}$$

**Step 3: Numerical Evaluation (4 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (0.5)(0.05)(0.21) = 0.00525 \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator inner term} &= 1 + (8)(0.05) + (4)(0.02) \\
&= 1 + 0.4 + 0.08 = 1.48 \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator squared} &= (1.48)^2 = 2.1904 \\[4pt]
r &= \frac{0.00525}{2.1904} = 2.40 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{mol/(kg·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the **Eley–Rideal rate expression** for the reaction $A + B \rightarrow C$ where A is adsorbed and B reacts from the gas phase. Comment on the order with respect to each reactant at high and low pressures. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a laboratory reactor, the rate of decomposition of A on a catalyst at 400 K is $r = \dfrac{0.8 \, P_A}{1 + 2 \, P_A}$ mol/(kg·s). Determine: (i) the rate constant $k$ and adsorption equilibrium constant $K_A$; (ii) the partial pressure at which the rate is **half of its maximum**; (iii) order of reaction when $P_A = 0.1$ atm and $P_A = 5$ atm. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Mechanism & Rate Law (3 marks)**
$$A + S \rightleftharpoons A\cdot S \quad \text{(equilibrium)}$$
$$A\cdot S + B_{(g)} \xrightarrow{k} C + S \quad \text{(rate controlling)}$$
$$r = k C_{A\cdot S} P_B = k C_t \theta_A P_B$$
$$\theta_A = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$$
$$\Rightarrow r = \frac{k C_t K_A P_A P_B}{1 + K_A P_A}$$

**Step 2: Order Analysis (4 marks)**

| Regime | Approximation | Order in A | Order in B |
|--------|---------------|-----------|-----------|
| Low $P_A$ ($K_A P_A \ll 1$) | $r \approx k C_t K_A P_A P_B$ | 1st | 1st |
| High $P_A$ ($K_A P_A \gg 1$) | $r \approx k C_t P_B$ | 0th | 1st |
| Any $P_B$ | Always first order in $P_B$ | — | 1st |

ER mechanisms thus exhibit **linear dependence on B's partial pressure** always.

---

**Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Identify Model (2 marks)**
Comparing $r = \dfrac{0.8 P_A}{1 + 2 P_A}$ with $r = \dfrac{k C_t K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A}$:
- $k C_t K_A = 0.8$
- $K_A = 2 \; \text{atm}^{-1}$
- $\Rightarrow k C_t = 0.4$

**Step 2: Half-Maximum Pressure (2 marks)**
Maximum rate $r_{\max} = k C_t = 0.4$ mol/(kg·s) (as $P_A \to \infty$).
For $r = r_{\max}/2 = 0.2$:

$$0.2 = \frac{0.8 P_A}{1 + 2 P_A} \Rightarrow 0.2(1 + 2P_A) = 0.8 P_A$$
$$0.2 + 0.4 P_A = 0.8 P_A \Rightarrow 0.4 P_A = 0.2 \Rightarrow P_A = 0.5 \;\text{atm}$$

**Step 3: Orders at Given Pressures (3 marks)**

At $P_A = 0.1$ atm: $K_A P_A = 0.2 \ll 1$ → **First order**.
At $P_A = 5$ atm: $K_A P_A = 10 \gg 1$ → **Zero order**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting site balance** — losing 2–3 marks in derivations.
> 2. **Dropping the denominator square** in LH bimolecular expressions (must be squared because **two** sites are involved).
> 3. **Confusing ER with LH** — common when "B is not adsorbed" is not stated explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring product/inert inhibition** in the denominator — loses applied-problem marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Mars–van Krevelen with LH** for oxidation reactions — check for lattice participation.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** before deriving (KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for stating "adsorption at quasi-equilibrium" and "rate controlling step").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Surface Reaction**

- **Surface reaction** is the chemical transformation step on an adsorbed species; it is one of seven steps in the heterogeneous catalytic cycle.
- Three principal mechanisms: **Unimolecular (Type I), Langmuir–Hinshelwood (Type II), Eley–Rideal (Type III)**.
- **Langmuir–Hinshelwood**: both species adsorbed → rate ∝ $\theta_A \theta_B$ → **squared denominator**.
- **Eley–Rideal**: one adsorbed, one gas-phase → rate ∝ $\theta_A P_B$ → **linear denominator**.
- **Site balance**: $C_t = C_v + \sum C_i$ — the master conservation constraint.
- **Langmuir isotherm**: $\theta_i = \dfrac{K_i P_i}{1 + \sum K_j P_j}$.
- Limiting cases: weak adsorption → high order; strong adsorption → zero order (saturation).
- **Inert and product species inhibit** by enlarging the denominator.
- **Mars–van Krevelen** (oxidation/redox): catalyst lattice oxygen participates; rate = $\dfrac{k_1 k_2 P_A P_O}{k_1 P_A + 2 k_2 P_O}$.
- The **half-maximum rate** for unimolecular kinetics occurs at $P_A = 1/K_A$.
- Always declare: **(i)** rate controlling step, **(ii)** adsorption equilibrium assumption, **(iii)** which species adsorb.
- Industrial relevance: catalytic converters, Haber–Bosch, FCC, SO₂ oxidation (V₂O₅), three-way catalysts.
- Numerator carries **reactant powers**; denominator carries **order = number of participating sites squared**.
- For an exam, **sketch the site diagram and write the site balance first** — this earns 2 marks before any algebra.
- Watch for **negative orders** in LH when one reactant is strongly adsorbed (self-inhibition).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesizing Rate Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Synthesizing Rate Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In heterogeneous catalysis, **synthesizing a rate law** refers to the systematic, mechanistic procedure of constructing a closed-form mathematical expression for the observed rate of a catalytic reaction in terms of measurable bulk-fluid variables (partial pressures or concentrations), starting from a *postulated sequence of elementary surface steps*. This is fundamentally distinct from fitting an empirical power-law model to experimental data, because heterogeneous catalytic kinetics are governed by surface coverages that depend non-linearly on fluid-phase composition.

The principal engineering framework employed is the **Hougen–Watson (Langmuir–Hinshelwood–Hougen–Watson, LHHW) approach**, in which:

1. A plausible *reaction mechanism* consisting of adsorption, surface reaction, and desorption steps is hypothesized.
2. The **rate-determining step (RDS)** is identified.
3. The **quasi-steady-state approximation (QSSA)** is applied to all surface intermediates (adsorbed species whose concentrations cannot be measured directly).
4. Adsorption equilibria for non-RDS steps are described by **Langmuir-type isotherms**.
5. A **site balance** (catalyst conservation equation) is invoked to express all surface coverages in terms of measurable partial pressures.

The resulting rate law typically takes the canonical Hougen–Watson form:

$$r = \frac{(\text{Kinetic factor})(\text{Potential term})}{(\text{Adsorption term})^{n}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PECHT633 places *Synthesizing Rate Law* as the foundational analytical skill upon which reactor design (CSTR/PFR modeling), parameter estimation, and mechanism discrimination in later modules entirely depend. Mastery of this topic is non-negotiable for the University Examination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **busy single-platform railway ticket counter** at a railway station. The "platform" is the catalyst surface, the "tokens" are reactant molecules, and the "tickets issued" are the products.

- **Without rate-law synthesis (empirical model):** You simply count how many tickets are issued per minute when 10, 50, 200 people are waiting, and *fit* a curve (say, *r = k Pⁿ*). The fit may look pretty, but it tells you nothing about *why* the rate behaves that way, and it will fail the moment conditions change (e.g., a second counter opens or a heavy VIP arrives).

- **With rate-law synthesis (mechanistic model):** You reason: *a person must (i) reach the counter [adsorption], (ii) wait their turn on the platform [surface coverage], (iii) interact with the clerk [surface reaction], and (iv) walk away with the ticket [desorption]*. If you know that the slowest step is the clerk issuing the ticket (RDS), and that at most 5 people can stand on the platform (site balance), you can predict the throughput under *any* crowd size — even ones you never tested.

This "thinking like the platform and counter" is exactly what **synthesizing a rate law** means in heterogeneous catalysis.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Universal gas constant:** **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Standard state pressure:** **P° = 1 atm (or 1 bar)** for adsorption equilibrium constants
- **Adsorption equilibrium constant units:** **Kₐ (atm⁻¹)** for dissociative or non-dissociative molecular adsorption
- **Surface reaction rate constant units:** **kₛ (mol·g-cat⁻¹·s⁻¹)** or **mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹** depending on catalyst basis
- **Site coverage θ:** **dimensionless (0 ≤ θ ≤ 1)**, often expressed as a fraction or percentage

> [!NOTE]
> The dimensionless quantity **θ** (theta) is *the* fundamental variable of heterogeneous kinetics. Every line of every derivation in this topic ultimately reduces to expressing θ in terms of measurable P or C.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Pillar Logic of Rate-Law Synthesis

The synthesizing procedure decomposes into six rigorously ordered steps. Mastering the *order* is itself a frequent KTU question.

### Step 1 — Postulate a Plausible Mechanism
Decompose the overall stoichiometry into a sequence of **elementary surface steps**. Typical step types are:
- **Adsorption** (reversible or irreversible): A + S ⇌ A·S
- **Surface reaction** (between adsorbed species): A·S + B·S → Products·S + S
- **Desorption**: C·S ⇌ C + S

The symbol **S** denotes an *empty* (vacant) active site on the catalyst surface.

### Step 2 — Identify the Rate-Determining Step (RDS)
The **RDS** is the slowest elementary step in the sequence; all other steps are assumed to be in quasi-equilibrium. Identifying the RDS is the *interpretive* heart of rate-law synthesis and is typically inferred from:
- Experimental activation energy (highest Eₐ corresponds to RDS)
- Reaction order / inhibition behavior
- Isotope-labeling studies (advanced)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** If the RDS is *misidentified* in an exam answer, the entire derivation collapses, and full marks are rarely recovered. Always justify your choice of RDS in one or two sentences before writing the rate expression.

### Step 3 — Write the Rate Expression for the RDS
The rate expression for the RDS is the *starting point* of the synthesis. It is written in terms of **surface coverages** (θ values), not partial pressures. For example, for an RDS of the form A·S + B·S → Products:

$$r = k_s \, \theta_A \, \theta_B$$

### Step 4 — Apply Quasi-Steady-State Approximation (QSSA)
For every **surface intermediate** (i.e., every adsorbed species), assume:

$$\frac{d\theta_i}{dt} \approx 0$$

The net rate of formation equals the net rate of consumption. This yields algebraic equations linking θ values to each other and to θᵥ (vacant-site fraction).

### Step 5 — Express Surface Coverages via Adsorption Equilibria (Langmuir Isotherm)
For each non-RDS adsorption step in quasi-equilibrium, the Langmuir isotherm gives:

$$\theta_i = K_i \, P_i \, \theta_v$$

where **Kᵢ** is the adsorption equilibrium constant (with units atm⁻¹) for species *i*.

### Step 6 — Apply the Site Balance (Conservation of Surface Sites)
Because a catalyst surface has a finite number of active sites:

$$\theta_v + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \theta_i = 1$$

Substituting all θᵢ from Step 5 into the site balance yields an explicit expression for **θᵥ** in terms of measurable partial pressures. This expression is then back-substituted into the rate expression from Step 3 to deliver the final, closed-form rate law.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formatting Note for Table Cells:** To preserve Markdown table integrity, the symbol **|x|** is rendered here as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`. Never use a bare vertical pipe inside a table cell.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Notes / Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Langmuir adsorption isotherm (component i) | $\theta_i = K_i \, P_i \, \theta_v$ | Valid only when Langmuir assumptions hold (uniform sites, monolayer, no interactions) |
| 2 | Site balance | $\theta_v + \sum \theta_i = 1$ | Conservation of active sites; *always* invoked |
| 3 | Vacant-site fraction | $\theta_v = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sum K_j P_j}$ | Obtained by combining rows 1 and 2 |
| 4 | Coverage of species *i* | $\theta_i = \dfrac{K_i P_i}{1 + \sum K_j P_j}$ | Used to eliminate θ from rate expression |
| 5 | Generic RDS rate (surface reaction control) | $r = k_s \, \theta_A \, \theta_B$ | Langmuir–Hinshelwood type |
| 6 | Generic RDS rate (adsorption of A control) | $r = k_a \, P_A \, \theta_v$ | Single-species adsorption-controlled case |
| 7 | Generic RDS rate (desorption of C control) | $r = k_d \, \theta_C$ | Product release as RDS |
| 8 | Canonical LHHW rate law (A + B → C + D, surface reaction controlled) | $r = \dfrac{k_s K_A P_A K_B P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D)^{2}}$ | Flagship equation; appears in nearly every KTU question |
| 9 | Eley–Rideal rate law (A adsorbed, B from gas phase) | $r = \dfrac{k_s K_A P_A P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}$ | Order in B is 1; order in A is fractional |
| 10 | QSSA condition | $\dfrac{d\theta_i}{dt} = 0$ | Applied to *all* surface intermediates |
| 11 | Arrhenius form for k | $k = k_0 \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Used to extract Eₐ for RDS identification |
| 12 | Equilibrium constant from van't Hoff | $K = K_0 \, e^{-\Delta H_{ads}/RT}$ | Adsorption is *exothermic* ⇒ ΔH < 0 ⇒ K decreases with T |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The synthesized rate law is the **bridge between molecular insight and reactor design**. In industrial practice (e.g., ammonia synthesis on Fe, FCC cracking on zeolites, hydrodesulfurization on Co–Mo/Al₂O₃), the LHHW rate law is used to:

- **Predict reactor performance** under feed compositions *never* tested in the lab
- **Optimize operating windows** (P, T, feed ratio) for maximum selectivity
- **Discriminate between rival mechanisms** by non-linear regression against pilot-plant data
- **Scale-up** laboratory kinetic data to commercial reactor geometries

> [!NOTE]
> A correctly synthesized LHHW rate law can reduce the experimental workload for a new catalyst by as much as **40–60%**, since it permits confident extrapolation. An empirical power-law model cannot.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Langmuir–Hinshelwood Rate Law for A + B → C + D (Surface-Reaction Controlled)

This is the **canonical KTU derivation** and the most frequently examined topic in Module 1. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly.

### Step 1 — Postulate the Elementary Mechanism

$$\text{(i) } A + S \rightleftharpoons A\!\cdot\!S \quad \text{(adsorption of A)}$$

$$\text{(ii) } B + S \rightleftharpoons B\!\cdot\!S \quad \text{(adsorption of B)}$$

$$\text{(iii) } A\!\cdot\!S + B\!\cdot\!S \longrightarrow C\!\cdot\!S + D\!\cdot\!S \quad \text{(surface reaction — RDS)}$$

$$\text{(iv) } C\!\cdot\!S \rightleftharpoons C + S \quad \text{(desorption of C)}$$

$$\text{(v) } D\!\cdot\!S \rightleftharpoons D + S \quad \text{(desorption of D)}$$

**Conversion logic:** The overall stoichiometry must collapse to A + B → C + D when the elementary steps are summed. Verification: (i) + (ii) + (iii) + (iv) + (v) yields A + B + 3S → C + D + 3S, leaving A + B → C + D. ✓

### Step 2 — Identify the Rate-Determining Step

We designate step (iii) as the RDS because it is the only step involving *two* adsorbed species, the highest energetic barrier, and the experimental evidence (e.g., first order in both A and B at low coverages, saturation at high coverages) points to a *bimolecular* surface process.

### Step 3 — Write the Rate Expression for the RDS

The rate of a bimolecular surface reaction is proportional to the *product* of the surface coverages of the two reacting adsorbed species:

$$r = k_s \, \theta_A \, \theta_B \quad \text{[mol · g-cat}^{-1}\text{ · s}^{-1}\text{]}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each collision between A·S and B·S that surmounts the activation barrier produces product; hence the rate is kₛ × θ_A × θ_B.

### Step 4 — Apply the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm for Each Non-RDS Step

Steps (i), (ii), (iv), and (v) are assumed in quasi-equilibrium. The Langmuir isotherm applied to each gives:

$$\theta_A = K_A \, P_A \, \theta_v$$

$$\theta_B = K_B \, P_B \, \theta_v$$

$$\theta_C = K_C \, P_C \, \theta_v$$

$$\theta_D = K_D \, P_D \, \theta_v$$

**Conversion logic:** Langmuir isotherm states that the fraction of surface covered by species i equals (adsorption constant) × (partial pressure) × (fraction of vacant sites). Each is valid because no interaction between adsorbed species is assumed.

### Step 5 — Apply the Site Balance

The total site fraction must equal unity:

$$\theta_v + \theta_A + \theta_B + \theta_C + \theta_D = 1$$

Substitute the four Langmuir expressions:

$$\theta_v + K_A P_A \, \theta_v + K_B P_B \, \theta_v + K_C P_C \, \theta_v + K_D P_D \, \theta_v = 1$$

Factor out θᵥ:

$$\theta_v \left( 1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D \right) = 1$$

Solve for θᵥ:

$$\theta_v = \frac{1}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D}$$

**Conversion logic:** The site balance is the *algebraic lever* that converts an under-determined system of intermediate coverages into a closed-form expression involving only measurable partial pressures.

### Step 6 — Back-Substitute to Obtain θ_A and θ_B

$$\theta_A = K_A P_A \, \theta_v = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D}$$

$$\theta_B = K_B P_B \, \theta_v = \frac{K_B P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D}$$

### Step 7 — Substitute into the RDS Rate Expression

$$r = k_s \, \theta_A \, \theta_B = k_s \cdot \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D} \cdot \frac{K_B P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D}$$

Combine:

$$\boxed{\,r = \frac{k_s \, K_A \, K_B \, P_A \, P_B}{\left(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C + K_D P_D\right)^{2}}\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** The denominator is *squared* because *two* species θ_A and θ_B each contribute a copy of the same denominator (a direct consequence of the site balance). The exponent 2 in the denominator is therefore a *fingerprint* of bimolecular surface reaction control.

### Step 8 — Simplify Limiting Cases (Sanity Check)

**Case 1 — Low coverage (all KᵢPᵢ ≪ 1):**

$$\left(1 + \sum K_i P_i\right)^{2} \approx 1$$

So $r \approx k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B$, which is *first order* in each reactant. This matches the experimentally observed initial-rate behavior at low pressures. ✓

**Case 2 — Strong A adsorption (K_A P_A ≫ 1, others negligible):**

$$r \approx \frac{k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(K_A P_A)^{2}} = \frac{k_s K_B P_B}{K_A P_A}$$

The reaction becomes *negative order* in A and *first order* in B. This is the diagnostic **self-inhibition** behavior of strongly adsorbed reactants — a hallmark that confirms Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Eley–Rideal Rate Law (A adsorbed, B from gas phase)

The **Eley–Rideal (ER)** mechanism postulates that only *one* species adsorbs; the other strikes the adsorbed species directly from the bulk fluid phase. This is mechanistically distinct from L–H and is frequently examined by KTU.

### Step 1 — Postulate the Mechanism

$$\text{(i) } A + S \rightleftharpoons A\!\cdot\!S \quad \text{(adsorption, quasi-equilibrium)}$$

$$\text{(ii) } A\!\cdot\!S + B_{(g)} \longrightarrow C + D + S \quad \text{(surface reaction — RDS)}$$

$$\text{(iii) } C \rightleftharpoons C\!\cdot\!S \quad \text{(product adsorption, equilibrium)}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the RDS and Write Its Rate Expression

Step (ii) is the RDS. Its rate depends on the surface coverage of A *and* the gas-phase partial pressure of B:

$$r = k_s \, \theta_A \, P_B$$

### Step 3 — Apply Langmuir Isotherm and Site Balance

Adsorption equilibria for A and product C:

$$\theta_A = K_A P_A \, \theta_v$$

$$\theta_C = K_C P_C \, \theta_v$$

Site balance:

$$\theta_v + \theta_A + \theta_C = 1$$

Substitute:

$$\theta_v \left(1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C\right) = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\theta_v = \frac{1}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}, \qquad \theta_A = \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}$$

### Step 4 — Final Rate Law

$$r = k_s \, P_B \cdot \frac{K_A P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}$$

$$\boxed{\,r = \frac{k_s \, K_A \, P_A \, P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}\,}$$

**Diagnostic signature:** ER kinetics gives *first order* in the non-adsorbing gas (B) and *fractional* order in the adsorbing species (A). The absence of a *squared* denominator distinguishes ER from L–H.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Changing the Rate-Determining Step

| RDS | Controlling Step | Rate Law Form | Order in A | Order in B | Diagnostic Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A adsorption | $r = k_a P_A \theta_v$ | $\dfrac{k_a P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + \cdots}$ | 0 → 1 | 0 | Inhibited by A at high P_A |
| B adsorption | $r = k_b P_B \theta_v$ | $\dfrac{k_b P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + \cdots}$ | 0 | 0 → 1 | Inhibited by B at high P_B |
| Surface reaction (L–H) | $r = k_s \theta_A \theta_B$ | $\dfrac{k_s K_A K_B P_A P_B}{(1 + \Sigma K_i P_i)^{2}}$ | fractional | fractional | Self-inhibition at high P_A |
| Surface reaction (ER) | $r = k_s \theta_A P_B$ | $\dfrac{k_s K_A P_A P_B}{1 + K_A P_A + \cdots}$ | fractional | 1 | First order in B always |
| C desorption | $r = k_d \theta_C$ | $\dfrac{k_d K_C P_C}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}$ | 0 | 0 | Product-inhibited |

This matrix is a *high-yield KTU revision artifact*: one table, complete diagnostic framework for mechanism identification.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flowchart — The Six-Pillar Procedure for Synthesizing a Rate Law

The following Mermaid diagram renders the entire synthesizing procedure as a sequential decision-flow. Node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels are plain uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["START: GIVEN OVERALL STOICHIOMETRY"] --> node2["POSTULATE ELEMENTARY SURFACE STEPS"]
    node2 --> node3["LIST ADSORPTION, SURFACE REACTION, DESORPTION STEPS"]
    node3 --> node4["VERIFY SUM GIVES OVERALL REACTION"]
    node4 --> node5{"IDENTIFY RATE DETERMINING STEP"}
    node5 --> node6["JUSTIFY USING E A, ORDER, ISOTOPE DATA"]
    node6 --> node7["WRITE RATE EXPRESSION FOR RDS IN TERMS OF THETA VALUES"]
    node7 --> node8{"APPLY QSSA TO ALL SURFACE INTERMEDIATES"}
    node8 --> node9["DETERMINE ALGEBRAIC RELATIONS AMONG THETA VALUES"]
    node9 --> node10["APPLY LANGMUIR ISOTHERM TO NON RDS ADSORPTIONS"]
    node10 --> node11["EXPRESS EACH THETA I AS K I P I THETA V"]
    node11 --> node12["WRITE SITE BALANCE THETA V PLUS SUM THETA I = 1"]
    node12 --> node13["SOLVE FOR THETA V AS FUNCTION OF PARTIAL PRESSURES"]
    node13 --> node14["BACK SUBSTITUTE THETA V INTO RATE EXPRESSION"]
    node14 --> node15{"VERIFY LIMITING CASES LOW P AND HIGH P"}
    node15 --> node16["FINAL LHHW OR ER RATE LAW READY FOR REACTOR DESIGN"]
    node16 --> node17["END: CLOSED FORM RATE LAW OBTAINED"]

    style node1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style node5 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style node8 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style node15 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style node17 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

**Reading the diagram:** Blue node = entry point; orange nodes = *decision/interpretation gates* (the points where students most often lose marks); green node = successful completion. The two orange diamond-gates — **Identify RDS** and **Apply QSSA** — are the highest-risk steps in any KTU answer.

## 4.2 Schematic — Surface-Site Interaction Map (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Because a physical depiction of a catalyst surface with adsorbed species is impractical in plain Mermaid, the following block-level functional architecture maps the *information flow* between the key surface variables.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockGAS["GAS PHASE BULK PARTIAL PRESSURES P A P B P C P D"] --> blockBAL["SITE BALANCE THETA V PLUS SUM THETA I EQUALS 1"]
    blockLANG["LANGMUIR ISOTHERM THETA I EQUALS K I P I THETA V"] --> blockBAL
    blockBAL --> blockVAC["VACANT SITE FRACTION THETA V AS FUNCTION OF P"]
    blockVAC --> blockCOV["SURFACE COVERAGES THETA A THETA B THETA C THETA D"]
    blockCOV --> blockRDS["RDS RATE EXPRESSION R EQUALS K S TIMES THETA A TIMES THETA B"]
    blockRDS --> blockFIN["FINAL LHHW RATE LAW CLOSED FORM IN TERMS OF P"]
    blockFIN --> blockREACT["REACTOR DESIGN MODULE INPUT"]

    style blockGAS fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style blockFIN fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style blockREACT fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
```

**Reading the schematic:** The arrows show the *unidirectional dependency*. The LHHW rate law is the *terminal output*; it is the only object that gets handed forward to reactor design in subsequent modules.
