topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rate Limiting Step

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Rate Limiting Step (RLS) — Module 1

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Rate Limiting Step (RLS)**, also called the **rate-determining step (RDS)** or **rate-controlling step**, is the slowest elementary step in a multi-step reaction mechanism that governs the **overall macroscopic reaction rate**. In the context of heterogeneous catalytic reactions, it is the elementary step (adsorption, surface reaction, or desorption) possessing the **smallest specific rate constant** and therefore presents the largest resistance to the overall progress of reactants to products.

Mathematically, for a sequence of $n$ elementary steps in series, the overall reaction rate $r_{\text{overall}}$ obeys the resistance-in-series analogy:

$$\frac{1}{r_{\text{overall}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{r_{i}}$$

Since the RLS has the smallest $r_i$, the term $1/r_{\text{RLS}}$ dominates the summation, making $r_{\text{overall}} \approx r_{\text{RLS}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT633 Module 1):** Identification of the RLS is the foundational concept required to derive the rate expressions for heterogeneous catalytic reactions using the **Langmuir–Hinshelwood–Hougen–Watson (LHHW)** approach. Every subsequent rate model in this module depends on correctly identifying which step is rate limiting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Units of Specific Rate Constants in Heterogeneous Systems:**
> - Adsorption step: $k_A$ has units of **atm⁻¹·h⁻¹** (or Pa⁻¹·s⁻¹).
> - Surface reaction step: $k_S$ has units of **site⁻¹·h⁻¹** (or per active site per time).
> - Desorption step: $k_D$ has units of **h⁻¹** (or s⁻¹).

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **four-lane highway merging into a single toll booth** before continuing as a four-lane highway on the other side. No matter how fast cars travel on the open highway, the overall traffic flow is restricted by the toll booth — **a single bottleneck**.

In a heterogeneous catalytic reaction:

- The **highway sections** represent the fast steps (e.g., bulk diffusion, adsorption at low coverage).
- The **toll booth** represents the slow step (e.g., surface reaction between two adsorbed intermediates).
- The **traffic flow rate** is the observed reaction rate.

Even if every other step is infinitely fast, the overall rate cannot exceed the rate of the slowest step — analogous to how a chain breaks at its **weakest link**. This is the essence of the **rate limiting step concept**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Mnemonic:** "A chain is only as strong as its slowest link, and a reaction is only as fast as its slowest step."

---

### Physical Significance in Heterogeneous Catalysis

In a typical **gas–solid catalytic reaction** (e.g., ammonia synthesis, catalytic cracking, hydrogenation), reactants undergo five canonical steps:

1. **External diffusion** of reactants from bulk fluid to the catalyst external surface.
2. **Internal (pore) diffusion** of reactants into the catalyst pellet.
3. **Chemisorption** of reactants onto active sites $S$.
4. **Surface reaction** between adsorbed species.
5. **Desorption** of products from active sites.

Each step offers a resistance. The step with the **largest resistance** is the RLS, and the design engineer must target it for process improvement — e.g., by changing catalyst composition, increasing temperature (if it's a kinetic step), or modifying pore structure (if it's a diffusion step).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Identification Logic — Step-by-Step

To identify the RLS in a heterogeneous catalytic sequence, the following structured logic is applied:

- **Step 1: List all elementary steps.** Enumerate adsorption ($k_{Ai}$), surface reaction ($k_S$), and desorption ($k_{Di}$) steps with their respective rate constants and activation energies.
- **Step 2: Compare activation energies.** A step with the **highest activation energy** ($E_a$) is typically the slowest, following the Arrhenius relationship $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$. The step with the largest $E_a$ is the kinetic RLS.
- **Step 3: Apply the quasi-steady-state approximation (QSSA).** Surface intermediate concentrations ($C_{AS}$, $C_{BS}$, $C_{CS}$) are small and approximately constant. Set $\dfrac{dC_{jS}}{dt} \approx 0$ for every surface intermediate.
- **Step 4: Use the active-site balance.** The total concentration of active sites $C_T$ is conserved:

$$C_T = C_S + \sum_{j} C_{jS}$$

where $C_S$ is the vacant site concentration and $C_{jS}$ are concentrations of all occupied sites.

- **Step 5: Hypothesize a candidate RLS.** Assume one step (e.g., surface reaction) is RLS, write its rate as the **net forward rate** of that step, express all intermediate concentrations in terms of measurable bulk quantities using **equilibrium constants** of the non-RLS steps, and substitute into the rate expression.
- **Step 6: Validate experimentally.** Compare the predicted rate with experimental data across a wide range of partial pressures and temperatures. The model yielding the best fit identifies the true RLS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why QSSA Works:** Surface intermediates are present in **trace concentrations** (typically < 1% of total sites). Their rate of accumulation is negligible compared to their rate of formation and consumption, allowing us to algebraically eliminate them from the rate law.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units | Boundary Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k_A$ | Adsorption rate constant | atm⁻¹·s⁻¹ | Valid for non-dissociative adsorption at low $\theta$ |
| $k_D$ | Desorption rate constant | s⁻¹ | Temperature-sensitive; obeys Arrhenius law |
| $k_S$ | Surface reaction rate constant | site⁻¹·s⁻¹ | LHHW intrinsic rate constant |
| $K_A = k_{A}/k_{-A}$ | Adsorption equilibrium constant | atm⁻¹ | Decreases exponentially with $T$ (exothermic) |
| $K_D = k_{D}/k_{-D}$ | Desorption equilibrium constant | atm | Increases with $T$ (endothermic) |
| $C_T$ | Total active site concentration | mol·site·g_cat⁻¹ | Conserved; catalyst-specific constant |
| $\theta_j$ | Fractional coverage of species $j$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq \theta_j \leq 1$; $\sum \theta_j \leq 1$ |
| $E_a$ | Activation energy | kJ·mol⁻¹ | Highest $E_a$ step is RLS candidate |
| $\eta$ | Effectiveness factor | dimensionless | $\eta \to 1$ if kinetics RLS; $\eta \ll 1$ if pore diffusion RLS |

---

### 2.3 The Five Resistances in Series (Engineering Utility)

In heterogeneous reaction engineering, the observed rate $r_{\text{obs}}$ is governed by the following overall resistance:

$$\frac{1}{r_{\text{obs}}} = \frac{1}{r_{\text{ext.diff}}} + \frac{1}{r_{\text{int.diff}}} + \frac{1}{r_{\text{ads}}} + \frac{1}{r_{\text{surf.rxn}}} + \frac{1}{r_{\text{des}}}$$

**Engineering decision matrix for reactor design:**

| Dominant Resistance | Industrial Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|
| External diffusion | Increase fluid velocity; reduce pellet size; use monolithic catalysts |
| Internal (pore) diffusion | Use smaller pellet diameter; switch to bidisperse pellets; select mesoporous supports |
| Adsorption | Increase pressure; lower temperature; choose more active catalyst |
| Surface reaction | Increase temperature; find better catalyst (lower $E_a$); add promoters |
| Desorption | Lower temperature; engineer weaker product–surface bond |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** In the **Haber–Bosch process** (ammonia synthesis over Fe catalyst), the surface reaction $N_{\text{ads}} + 3H_{\text{ads}} \rightarrow 2NH_{3,\text{ads}}$ is the rate limiting step under industrial conditions (400–500 °C, 150–300 atm). Modern catalysts use **K₂O and Al₂O₃ promoters** to electronically modify Fe and lower the activation energy of this specific surface reaction — a direct intervention at the RLS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: LHHW Rate Law with Surface Reaction as RLS

Consider the generic **bimolecular surface reaction** mechanism:

$$\text{Step 1 (Adsorption of A):} \quad A + S \rightleftharpoons A \cdot S, \quad K_A = \frac{k_{A,f}}{k_{A,r}} = \frac{C_{AS}}{C_S \cdot P_A}$$

$$\text{Step 2 (Adsorption of B):} \quad B + S \rightleftharpoons B \cdot S, \quad K_B = \frac{C_{BS}}{C_S \cdot P_B}$$

$$\text{Step 3 (Surface Reaction — RLS):} \quad A \cdot S + B \cdot S \rightarrow C \cdot S + S, \quad r = k_S C_{AS} C_{BS}$$

$$\text{Step 4 (Desorption of C):} \quad C \cdot S \rightleftharpoons C + S, \quad K_C = \frac{C_{CS}}{C_S \cdot P_C}$$

---

**Step 1 — Identify the rate expression.**

Since Step 3 is the rate limiting step, the net rate of reaction equals the forward rate of Step 3 only:

$$r = k_S \, C_{AS} \, C_{BS}$$

---

**Step 2 — Express intermediate concentrations using adsorption equilibria.**

From Step 1 equilibrium:

$$C_{AS} = K_A \, P_A \, C_S$$

From Step 2 equilibrium:

$$C_{BS} = K_B \, P_B \, C_S$$

Substituting into the rate expression:

$$r = k_S \, (K_A P_A C_S) \, (K_B P_B C_S) = k_S K_A K_B \, P_A P_B \, C_S^{2}$$

---

**Step 3 — Apply the active-site conservation balance.**

The total site concentration is the sum of vacant and occupied sites:

$$C_T = C_S + C_{AS} + C_{BS} + C_{CS}$$

Using the equilibrium expressions for all adsorbed species:

$$C_{AS} = K_A P_A C_S, \quad C_{BS} = K_B P_B C_S, \quad C_{CS} = K_C P_C C_S$$

Substitute:

$$C_T = C_S \, \left(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C\right)$$

---

**Step 4 — Solve for the vacant site concentration $C_S$.**

$$C_S = \frac{C_T}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C}$$

---

**Step 5 — Substitute $C_S$ back into the rate expression.**

$$r = k_S K_A K_B \, P_A P_B \, \left[\frac{C_T}{1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C}\right]^{2}$$

Expanding:

$$r = \frac{k_S K_A K_B C_T^{2} \, P_A P_B}{\left(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C\right)^{2}}$$

This is the **complete LHHW rate law** for a bimolecular surface reaction as the rate limiting step.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Rate Laws for Each RLS Hypothesis

| Rate Limiting Step | Resulting LHHW Rate Law | Functional Form |
|---|---|---|
| Adsorption of $A$ | $r = \dfrac{k_{A,f} C_T (P_A - P_C / K P_B)}{1 + K_B P_B + K_C P_C}$ | Linear in $P_A$ at low coverage |
| Adsorption of $B$ | $r = \dfrac{k_{B,f} C_T (P_B - P_C / K P_A)}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}$ | Linear in $P_B$ at low coverage |
| Surface reaction ($A\cdot S + B\cdot S$) | $r = \dfrac{k_S K_A K_B C_T^{2} P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C)^{2}}$ | Parabolic dependence on $P_A$, $P_B$ |
| Desorption of $C$ | $r = \dfrac{k_{D,f} C_T (K P_A P_B - P_C)}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B)}$ | First-order in $P_C$ at low coverage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discriminating experimental test:** The **functional dependence of rate on partial pressure** differs for each RLS hypothesis. If doubling $P_A$ at fixed $P_B$ doubles $r$, the RLS is likely **adsorption of A**. If doubling $P_A$ at fixed $P_B$ quadruples $r$ (then plateaus), the RLS is the **surface reaction**. This is the basis of the **Hougen–Watson discrimination protocol**.

---

### 3.3 Limiting Case Analysis — Low and High Coverage

**Case 1: Low coverage** ($K_j P_j \ll 1$ for all $j$). The denominator $\approx 1$, and:

$$r \approx k_S K_A K_B C_T^{2} \, P_A P_B \quad \text{(second-order overall)}$$

**Case 2: Strong A adsorption** ($K_A P_A \gg 1$, others negligible):

$$r \approx k_S K_B C_T^{2} \, P_B \quad \text{(zero-order in } P_A\text{, first-order in } P_B\text{)}$$

This **order change** with pressure is a hallmark experimental signature used to confirm the RLS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Resistance-in-Series for RLS Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    reactantA[Bulk Fluid Phase A] --> extA[External Diffusion Film]
    extA --> surfaceA[Catalyst External Surface]
    surfaceA --> poreA[Internal Pore Diffusion]
    poreA --> activeSiteA[Active Site Adsorption of A]
    activeSiteA --> surfRxnA[Surface Reaction A dot S plus B dot S]
    surfRxnA --> desorbC[Desorption of Product C]
    desorbC --> poreDiffC[Pore Diffusion of C Outward]
    poreDiffC --> bulkDiffC[External Diffusion of C to Bulk]
    bulkDiffC --> productC[Bulk Fluid Phase C]

    r1[/Resistance R1 External Diffusion/]:::rBox
    r2[/Resistance R2 Pore Diffusion/]:::rBox
    r3[/Resistance R3 Adsorption/]:::rBox
    r4[/Resistance R4 Surface Reaction RLS CANDIDATE/]:::rlsBox
    r5[/Resistance R5 Desorption/]:::rBox

    classDef rBox fill:E8F4FD,stroke:1F77B4,color:000
    classDef rlsBox fill:FFE5E5,stroke:D62728,color:000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flow — Hypothesizing the RLS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Multi Step Reaction Mechanism] --> enum[Enumerate all elementary steps]
    enum --> arrhenius[Compute rate constants via Arrhenius law]
    arrhenius --> compare{Which step has smallest k value?}
    compare --> ads[RLS equals Adsorption Step]
    compare --> surf[RLS equals Surface Reaction]
    compare --> des[RLS equals Desorption Step]
    ads --> modelA[Derive LHHW Rate Law assuming Adsorption RLS]
    surf --> modelS[Derive LHHW Rate Law assuming Surface RLS]
    des --> modelD[Derive LHHW Rate Law assuming Desorption RLS]
    modelA --> fitA{Compare fit with experimental data}
    modelS --> fitS{Compare fit with experimental data}
    modelD --> fitD{Compare fit with experimental data}
    fitA --> best[Select best fit hypothesis as TRUE RLS]
    fitS --> best
    fitD --> best
    best --> end[Apply Rate Law for Reactor Design]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rate Law Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    block1[Step 1 Reaction Mechanism Input] --> block2[Step 2 Apply QSSA to surface intermediates]
    block2 --> block3[Step 3 Active site balance equation]
    block3 --> block4[Step 4 Hypothesize RLS]
    block4 --> block5[Step 5 Express non RLS steps as equilibria]
    block5 --> block6[Step 6 Solve for vacant site concentration CS]
    block6 --> block7[Step 7 Substitute into RLS rate expression]
    block7 --> block8[Step 8 Final LHHW Rate Law]
    block8 --> block9[Step 9 Validate against experimental data]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The **red-highlighted box** in the first diagram marks the **surface reaction step** as the most likely RLS — the point of maximum resistance where process improvements yield the greatest rate enhancement. The decision flow in the second diagram illustrates the systematic KTU examiner-approved methodology for discriminating between competing RLS hypotheses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "rate limiting step" in the context of heterogeneous catalytic reactions. State its significance in deriving the Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate expression.*

**Model Answer:**

The **rate limiting step (RLS)** is the slowest elementary step in a multi-step reaction sequence that controls the overall rate of reaction. In heterogeneous catalysis, it is identified as the elementary step (adsorption, surface reaction, or desorption) with the **smallest specific rate constant** and consequently the **largest resistance** in the overall process.

**Significance:** In deriving LHHW rate laws, the assumption of a single RLS allows us to:
- Express the net rate as equal to the forward rate of only the RLS.
- Treat all non-RLS steps as equilibria (i.e., write their equilibrium constants explicitly).
- Eliminate surface intermediate concentrations algebraically using QSSA.

This simplifies a complex multi-step problem into a **single algebraic rate equation** suitable for reactor design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*List any three resistances encountered in a gas–solid heterogeneous catalytic reaction. Under what condition does the external diffusion step become the rate limiting step?*

**Model Answer:**

The **three major resistances** are:

1. **External (interphase) diffusion resistance** — between bulk gas and catalyst external surface.
2. **Internal (intraphase / pore) diffusion resistance** — within the catalyst pellet pores.
3. **Surface reaction resistance** — chemical reaction on the active site.

External diffusion becomes the RLS when:
- The **gas velocity is very low**, reducing the mass-transfer coefficient.
- The **catalyst particle size is large**, increasing the external surface area-to-volume mismatch.
- The **temperature is very high**, making the chemical kinetics much faster than mass transfer.
- Mathematically, when the **Mears criterion** is satisfied: $\dfrac{-r_A \, \rho_b \, R \, n}{k_c \, C_{A,b}} < 0.15$, indicating external diffusion control.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate expression for the reaction $A + B \rightarrow C$ over a solid catalyst, assuming the **surface reaction between adsorbed $A$ and $B$ is the rate limiting step**. The mechanism is:

$$A + S \rightleftharpoons A\cdot S, \quad K_A$$
$$B + S \rightleftharpoons B\cdot S, \quad K_B$$
$$A\cdot S + B\cdot S \rightarrow C\cdot S + S, \quad k_S \text{ (RLS)}$$
$$C\cdot S \rightleftharpoons C + S, \quad K_C$$

**(b)** If at 1 atm total pressure the surface reaction rate constant is $k_S = 0.05$ mol·(g_cat·s)⁻¹ and the adsorption equilibrium constants are $K_A = 1.0$ atm⁻¹, $K_B = 2.0$ atm⁻¹, $K_C = 0.5$ atm⁻¹, with $C_T = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ mol·g_cat⁻¹, calculate the initial reaction rate when $P_{A0} = P_{B0} = 0.4$ atm and $P_C = 0.2$ atm.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

From the derivation in Section 3.1, the LHHW rate law is:

$$r = \frac{k_S K_A K_B C_T^{2} \, P_A P_B}{\left(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C\right)^{2}}$$

**Justification of each step (as required for full marks):**

- The **rate expression** is taken as the forward rate of Step 3: $r = k_S C_{AS} C_{BS}$. ✓
- **Equilibrium relations** for Steps 1, 2, and 4: $C_{AS} = K_A P_A C_S$, $C_{BS} = K_B P_B C_S$, $C_{CS} = K_C P_C C_S$. ✓
- **Site balance**: $C_T = C_S + C_{AS} + C_{BS} + C_{CS} = C_S(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C)$. ✓
- **Solving for $C_S$** and substituting yields the final expression. ✓

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Substitute numerical values into the denominator:**

$$K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C = (1.0)(0.4) + (2.0)(0.4) + (0.5)(0.2)$$
$$= 0.4 + 0.8 + 0.1 = 1.3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the full denominator squared:**

$$(1 + 1.3)^{2} = (2.3)^{2} = 5.29$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$k_S K_A K_B C_T^{2} P_A P_B = (0.05)(1.0)(2.0)(1.0 \times 10^{-3})^{2}(0.4)(0.4)$$
$$= (0.05)(2.0)(1.0 \times 10^{-6})(0.16)$$
$$= (0.1)(1.0 \times 10^{-6})(0.16) = 1.6 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the rate:**

$$r = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-8}}{5.29} \approx 3.02 \times 10^{-9} \text{ mol·(g_cat·s)}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:** $r \approx 3.02 \times 10^{-9}$ mol·(g_cat·s)⁻¹.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **resistance-in-series analogy** used to identify the rate limiting step in heterogeneous catalytic reactions. Enumerate the five major resistances and explain how the concept of **quasi-steady-state approximation (QSSA)** simplifies the analysis.

**(b)** For the catalytic dehydrogenation of ethanol to acetaldehyde, the following mechanism is proposed:

$$C_2H_5OH + S \rightleftharpoons C_2H_5OH \cdot S, \quad K_A = 0.8 \text{ atm}^{-1}$$
$$C_2H_5OH \cdot S \rightarrow CH_3CHO \cdot S + H_2, \quad k_S = 0.1 \text{ s}^{-1} \text{ (RLS)}$$
$$CH_3CHO \cdot S \rightleftharpoons CH_3CHO + S, \quad K_C = 1.2 \text{ atm}^{-1}$$

Derive the **rate law** and compute the rate at $T = 500$ K when $P_{A} = 0.5$ atm and $P_C = 0.3$ atm, given $C_T = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ mol·g_cat⁻¹.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The resistance-in-series analogy treats each elementary step as a resistor. The overall reaction rate $r$ is given by:

$$\frac{1}{r} = \frac{1}{r_{\text{ext.diff}}} + \frac{1}{r_{\text{int.diff}}} + \frac{1}{r_{\text{ads}}} + \frac{1}{r_{\text{surf.rxn}}} + \frac{1}{r_{\text{des}}}$$

The step with the **highest resistance (smallest $r_i$)** is the RLS.

**Five resistances:**
1. External (film) diffusion
2. Internal (pore) diffusion
3. Adsorption
4. Surface reaction
5. Desorption

**QSSA simplification:** Surface intermediates $C_{jS}$ are at **steady state** ($\dfrac{dC_{jS}}{dt} \approx 0$). This converts differential mass balances into algebraic equations, eliminating intermediate concentrations explicitly without solving differential equations.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the rate expression.** Since surface reaction is RLS:

$$r = k_S C_{AS}$$

**Step 2 — Apply adsorption equilibrium:**

$$C_{AS} = K_A P_A C_S$$

So:

$$r = k_S K_A P_A C_S$$

**Step 3 — Write the site balance:**

$$C_T = C_S + C_{AS} + C_{CS}$$

Using equilibria for the non-RLS steps:

$$C_{CS} = K_C P_C C_S$$

Therefore:

$$C_T = C_S (1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_S$:**

$$C_S = \frac{C_T}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}$$

**Step 5 — Final rate law:**

$$r = \frac{k_S K_A C_T \, P_A}{1 + K_A P_A + K_C P_C}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation:**

Denominator: $1 + (0.8)(0.5) + (1.2)(0.3) = 1 + 0.4 + 0.36 = 1.76$

Numerator: $(0.1)(0.8)(2 \times 10^{-3})(0.5) = 8 \times 10^{-5}$

$$r = \frac{8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.76} \approx 4.55 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol·(g_cat·s)}^{-1}$$

**Final Answer:** $r \approx 4.55 \times 10^{-5}$ mol·(g_cat·s)⁻¹.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Forgetting the square on the denominator** in bimolecular surface reactions (denominator is squared because $C_S$ appears twice in $r = k_S C_{AS} C_{BS}$). This is a **very common mistake** costing 2–3 marks.
> - **Writing the adsorption/desorption equilibrium constants in the wrong direction** (using $1/K$ instead of $K$). Always verify: $K = (\text{forward rate})/(\text{reverse rate})$.
> - **Ignoring the active-site balance** entirely and treating $C_S \approx C_T$ — valid only at very low coverage. KTU examiners specifically test the full balance.
> - **Not justifying the choice of RLS** in derivations — KTU 2024 scheme demands explicit assumption statements ("Step 3 is assumed to be rate limiting, therefore…").
> - **Mixing units**: $C_T$ is per mass of catalyst (mol·g_cat⁻¹), not per volume of reactor. Wrong unit handling is a frequent error trap.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The RLS is the **slowest elementary step** controlling the overall heterogeneous reaction rate — analogous to the bottleneck in a highway or the weakest link in a chain.
- **Resistance in series:** $\dfrac{1}{r_{\text{obs}}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{r_i}$. The RLS has the **largest individual resistance**.
- **Five canonical resistances:** External diffusion → Internal diffusion → Adsorption → Surface reaction → Desorption.
- **QSSA:** Surface intermediates are at steady state ($\dfrac{dC_{jS}}{dt} \approx 0$), enabling algebraic elimination.
- **Site balance:** $C_T = C_S + \sum C_{jS}$ is **always conserved** and must appear in every LHHW derivation.
- **LHHW rate law (surface reaction RLS):** $r = \dfrac{k_S K_A K_B C_T^{2} P_A P_B}{(1 + K_A P_A + K_B P_B + K_C P_C)^{2}}$.
- **Experimental signature:** Each RLS hypothesis predicts a **distinct functional dependence of rate on partial pressures** — used for discrimination via the Hougen–Watson method.
- **Limiting cases:** Low coverage → second-order overall. Strong adsorption of one species → zero-order in that species.
- **Industrial relevance:** In Haber–Bosch, catalytic cracking, hydrodesulfurization, and three-way catalytic converters, **engineering the RLS** (via promoters, supports, temperature, pressure) is the primary route to performance enhancement.
- **KTU 2024 scheme emphasis:** Always **state the RLS assumption explicitly**, derive the rate law **step-by-step**, and verify with a **limiting case analysis**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Langmuir-Hinshelwood Approach and Development of Design Equations for Ideal Mixed Batch Reactor
Plug Flow Tubular Reactor and Perfectly Mixed Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor for Heterogeneous Systems, Heterogeneous Data Analysis for Reactor Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plug Flow Tubular Reactor and Perfectly Mixed Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor for Heterogeneous Systems, Heterogeneous Data Analysis for Reactor Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Plug Flow Tubular Reactor (PFTR) for Heterogeneous Systems

In **heterogeneous catalytic systems**, the Plug Flow Tubular Reactor is realized as a **packed-bed (or fixed-bed) reactor**, in which the catalyst particles are stationary in a tube and the reacting fluid flows through the catalyst bed under conditions approximating plug flow. The formal KTU 2024 definition is:

> A **Plug Flow Tubular Reactor (PFTR)** for heterogeneous reactions is an ideal reactor in which the fluid moves through a fixed catalyst bed in plug flow (no axial mixing), with all elements of fluid having the same residence time, while the heterogeneous reaction occurs at the internal surface of the catalyst.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Highway Toll Booth" Model:**
Imagine a single-lane highway (the reactor tube) with stationary toll booths (the catalyst particles). Cars (fluid molecules) enter the highway and travel down the lane. Every car must pass the *same number* of toll booths in *exactly the same order* before exiting. As cars proceed, the concentration of "unpaid" cars decreases progressively along the highway — this is the *axial concentration gradient* of a PFR. No car ever catches up to another or overtakes it, just as there is no axial mixing in a PFR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Feature:** In a heterogeneous PFTR, the **catalyst inventory (W in kg)** replaces the reactor volume (V) used in homogeneous PFR design. The mole balance is taken with respect to catalyst weight, not volume.

### 1.2 The Perfectly Mixed Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) for Heterogeneous Systems

For heterogeneous reactions, the CSTR is realized as a **slurry reactor (CSTR with suspended catalyst particles)**, a **fluidized-bed CSTR**, or a **trickle-bed CSTR** with vigorous agitation. The KTU 2024 definition:

> A **Perfectly Mixed Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** for heterogeneous reactions is an ideal reactor in which the fluid phase is perfectly back-mixed so that the composition is uniform throughout the reactor and equal to the exit composition, while the solid catalyst is uniformly suspended in the fluid.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Mixing Bowl" Model:**
Picture a large kitchen mixer (the reactor) where flour, sugar, and eggs (reactants) are continuously added at the top while the well-mixed batter is continuously removed at the bottom. At every instant, the composition *anywhere* inside the bowl is identical to the composition of the stream leaving. This is the **uniform concentration** hallmark of a CSTR.

> [!NOTE]
> The **rate of reaction in a heterogeneous CSTR is evaluated at the exit conditions** ($X_{out}$, $C_{A,out}$), not at the inlet. This is the most common conceptual error in CSTR design.

### 1.3 Heterogeneous Data Analysis for Reactor Design

> **Heterogeneous Data Analysis** refers to the systematic methodology of converting experimentally measured concentration-versus-time-or-position data (collected in laboratory differential or integral reactors) into a reliable, predictive rate law that can be used to design commercial-scale heterogeneous reactors.

It involves: (i) collecting rate data, (ii) testing postulated rate models, (iii) estimating parameters, and (iv) discriminating between rival mechanisms.

**Intuitive Analogy — "Detective Work on Catalytic Sleuth":**
You measure how fast a gas is consumed across a small catalyst pellet under different conditions. Like a detective, you assemble *clues* (concentration, temperature, pressure data) and *test* them against *suspects* (different rate-law forms such as Power Law, Langmuir–Hinshelwood, Eley–Rideal). The suspect whose "fingerprint" matches the data is the guilty mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cornerstone assumption: the experimental reactor must be operated in a regime where **external and internal transport resistances are absent**, so that the measured rate is the *true intrinsic chemical rate* $\left(-r_A'\right)$.

### 1.4 Standard Units & Conventions Used Throughout

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Catalyst inventory | $W$ | **kg** |
| Molar flow rate of A | $F_A$ | **mol/s** |
| Volumetric flow rate | $v_0$ | **m³/s** |
| Concentration of A | $C_A$ | **mol/m³** or **mol/L** |
| Specific rate of reaction | $-r_A'$ | **mol/(g-cat·s)** or **kmol/(kg-cat·h)** |
| Space time | $\tau$ | **s** |
| Space velocity | $S_V$ | **s⁻¹** or **h⁻¹** |
| Conversion | $X$ | dimensionless |
| Catalyst activity | $a$ | dimensionless (0 → 1) |
| Superficial velocity | $u_s$ | **m/s** |
| Bed void fraction | $\epsilon$ | dimensionless |
| Particle diameter | $D_p$ | **m** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PFR (Packed Bed) — Operating Logic in Bulleted Steps

1. **Mole balance on differential slice dW:**
   Fluid enters slice with $F_A$ and leaves with $F_A + dF_A$. The slice contains $dW$ kg of catalyst. The rate of disappearance per kg of catalyst is $-r_A'$.
2. **At steady state:** Rate of A disappearing from fluid = Rate of A consumed by reaction.
3. **Integrate from inlet (W=0, X=0) to exit (W, X):** This gives the heterogeneous PFR design equation.
4. **Pressure drop consideration:** As fluid flows through packed bed, pressure decreases, altering $C_A = y_A P/(RT)$, which alters rate. Must couple with Ergun equation.
5. **Catalyst decay consideration:** Activity $a(t)$ falls with time, so rate is multiplied by $a$: $-r_A' = a \cdot (-r_A')_{\text{fresh}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind PFTR Design:** The PFTR gives the *highest conversion* per kg of catalyst among the ideal reactors for a positive-order reaction at the same space velocity — because the average reactant concentration along the bed is higher than in a CSTR.

### 2.2 CSTR (Slurry / Fluidized) — Operating Logic in Bulleted Steps

1. **Mole balance on entire reactor contents:** In − Out − Consumed = 0 at steady state.
2. **Inlet flow** $F_{A0}$ minus outlet flow $F_A$ equals rate of consumption over the entire catalyst inventory W.
3. **Since perfectly mixed, the rate is constant throughout** and equals the rate evaluated at the exit conditions.
4. **Solve algebraically** for W (or catalyst loading) — no differential equation, no integration.
5. **For catalyst decay in a CSTR:** activity is uniform throughout the tank and equals the exit-stream activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind CSTR Heterogeneous Design:** A CSTR is preferred when the reaction is highly exothermic (excellent temperature control due to back-mixing), when a catalyst slurry can be easily pumped, or when the rate is strongly inhibited by product (e.g., fermentation).

### 2.3 Heterogeneous Data Analysis — Operating Logic in Bulleted Steps

1. **Collect experimental data:** $C_A$ (or X) versus W (in integral or differential reactor) under differential conditions, varying $C_A$, $T$, and partial pressures.
2. **Apply differential method:** Plot $X$ vs $W/F_{A0}$, draw smooth curve, measure slope at each point: $\left(-r_A'\right)_{\text{point}} = F_{A0}\cdot(\text{slope})$.
3. **Or apply integral method:** Assume a rate law form (e.g., power law $-r_A' = k C_A^n$), integrate analytically or numerically, and compare with experimental $X$ vs $W$ data.
4. **Estimate parameters** $k$, $n$, $K_A$, etc. using linearization or nonlinear regression.
5. **Test mechanism:** Compare Langmuir–Hinshelwood (LH), Eley–Rideal (ER), and power-law models using statistical criteria (e.g., $R^2$, residual sum of squares, parity plots).
6. **Verify no transport disguise:** Check Mears criterion, Weisz–Prater criterion before declaring intrinsic kinetics.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Every formula below is KTU-high-yield.** Memorize the boxed items first.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A'}$ | PFR / PBR design eqn. (no ΔP, no decay) | Integral PBR design |
| 2 | $W = \dfrac{F_{A0}\cdot X}{-r_A'}\bigg\vert_{X}$ | CSTR / slurry reactor design | Algebraic CSTR sizing |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dX}{dW} = \dfrac{-r_A'}{F_{A0}}$ | Differential form of PBR design | Differential reactor analysis |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{G}{\rho_c\,D_p\,\epsilon^3}\bigl[150(1-\epsilon)\dfrac{\mu}{D_p} + 1.75\,G\bigr]$ | Ergun equation (ΔP in packed bed) | Pressure drop in PBR |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dy}{dX} = \dfrac{-\alpha(1+\epsilon_b X)}{2y(1-\epsilon_b X)}$ (with $y = P/P_0$, $\alpha$ from Ergun) | Combined ΔP–PBR ODE | $X$ at high conversion with ΔP |
| 6 | $W = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{a\cdot(-r_A')}$ | PBR with catalyst decay | Real reactor with decay |
| 7 | $W = \dfrac{F_{A0}\,X}{a\cdot(-r_A')}\bigg\vert_{X}$ | CSTR with catalyst decay | CSTR + decay |
| 8 | $-\dfrac{da}{dt} = k_d\,a^n$ | Decay law (n = 1, 2, 3) | Time-on-stream analysis |
| 9 | $\left(-r_A'\right)_{\text{point}} = F_{A0}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta W}$ | Differential reactor data reduction | Lab data interpretation |
| 10 | $k = A\,e^{-E/RT}$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence | Parameter estimation |
| 11 | $\dfrac{F_{A0}}{W} = \dfrac{C_{A0}\cdot X}{\tau_p\,C_{A,p}}$ (or $C_{A,out}$ for CSTR) | Performance comparison basis | Reactor selection |
| 12 | $\dfrac{F_{A0}\,dX}{dW} = -r_A' = \dfrac{k\,C_A^n}{1 + K_A C_A + K_B C_B}$ | Generic LH-form | Mechanism testing |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **PBRs** dominate commercial heterogeneous processes: catalytic cracking, hydrodesulfurization, ammonia synthesis, methanol synthesis, Fischer–Tropsch.
- **Slurry CSTRs** dominate slurry-phase processes: hydrocracking, hydrotreating, fermentation, polymerization, and three-phase hydrogenations.
- **Data analysis** is the bridge between laboratory kinetic research and commercial reactor design. Without robust data analysis, scale-up fails — leading to over-sized or under-performing industrial reactors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Heterogeneous PBR Design Equation

We start with a **mole balance on A** over a differential catalyst slice of mass $dW$ in a packed bed.

**Step 1 — Write the general mole balance:**

$$
\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}
$$

For a steady-state differential slice containing $dW$ kg of catalyst:

$$
F_A\big\vert_{W} - F_A\big\vert_{W+dW} + r_A'\,dW = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to a differential form:**

$$
F_A\big\vert_{W} - F_A\big\vert_{W+dW} = -\,r_A'\,dW
$$

Dividing by $dW$ and taking the limit as $dW \to 0$:

$$
-\dfrac{dF_A}{dW} = r_A'
$$

The convention is to write the rate of disappearance, $-r_A'$, so:

$$
\dfrac{dF_A}{dW} = -r_A'
$$

**Step 3 — Convert $F_A$ to conversion $X$:**

The definition of conversion is:

$$
X = \dfrac{F_{A0} - F_A}{F_{A0}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad F_A = F_{A0}(1-X)
$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $W$:

$$
\dfrac{dF_A}{dW} = -F_{A0}\,\dfrac{dX}{dW}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the differential balance:**

$$
-F_{A0}\,\dfrac{dX}{dW} = -r_A'
$$

or equivalently:

$$
\dfrac{dX}{dW} = \dfrac{-r_A'}{F_{A0}}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from the inlet (W=0, X=0) to the exit (W, X):**

$$
\int_0^X dX = \int_0^W \dfrac{-r_A'}{F_{A0}}\,dW
$$

Separating constants:

$$
X = \dfrac{1}{F_{A0}}\int_0^W \left(-r_A'\right)\,dW
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for W as a function of X (the design equation):**

$$
\boxed{\;W = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A'}\;}
$$

> This is the **fundamental heterogeneous PBR design equation.** It is mathematically identical in form to the homogeneous PFR design equation $V = F_{A0}\int_0^X dX/(-r_A)$, with $W$ replacing $V$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Heterogeneous CSTR Design Equation

For a CSTR, we write the mole balance over the *entire reactor contents* rather than a differential slice.

**Step 1 — General steady-state mole balance:**

$$
F_{A0} - F_{A,\text{out}} + r_A'\cdot W = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$
F_{A0} - F_{A,\text{out}} = -r_A'\cdot W
$$

**Step 3 — Use $F_{A,\text{out}} = F_{A0}(1-X)$:**

$$
F_{A0} - F_{A0}(1-X) = -r_A'\cdot W
$$

$$
F_{A0}\,X = -r_A'\cdot W
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for W:**

$$
\boxed{\;W = \dfrac{F_{A0}\,X}{-r_A'}\bigg\vert_{X=X_{\text{out}}}\;}
$$

> The vertical bar denotes that the rate is **evaluated at the exit (uniform) composition** of the CSTR. This single-line equation is one of the most heavily tested in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Combined Pressure-Drop PBR Design Equation

In a packed bed, the pressure decreases along the length. Since $C_A = y_A P / (RT)$, lower $P$ means lower $C_A$, which reduces the rate. Therefore, when $\Delta P$ is significant (e.g., $\Delta P / P_0 > 0.05$), we must solve the PBR equation *coupled* with the Ergun equation.

**Step 1 — The Ergun equation (one common form, in terms of bed length $z$):**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{G}{\rho_c\,D_p\,\epsilon^3}\left[\dfrac{150(1-\epsilon)\,\mu}{D_p} + 1.75\,G\right]
$$

where $G = \rho\,u_s$ is the superficial mass flux (kg/m²·s), $\rho_c$ is the catalyst pellet density, $D_p$ is the pellet diameter, $\epsilon$ is the bed void fraction, and $\mu$ is the fluid viscosity.

**Step 2 — Define dimensionless pressure $y = P/P_0$ and rewrite in terms of $X$ and $y$:**

For a gas-phase reaction with molar expansion factor $\epsilon_b$ (here NOT bed void fraction, but stoichiometric expansion: $\epsilon_b = y_{A0}\,\delta$, where $\delta$ is the change in moles per mole of A reacted), the gas volumetric flow rate changes along the bed:

$$
v = v_0(1 + \epsilon_b X)(P_0/P)(T/T_0)
$$

Assuming isothermal operation ($T = T_0$), the superficial velocity $u = G/\rho$ changes as $P$ changes.

**Step 3 — Standard reduction (Fogler, Levenspiel) defines the parameter $\alpha$:**

$$
\alpha \equiv \dfrac{2\beta_0}{P_0/\rho_0}\cdot\dfrac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon^3}
$$

and a function $F(y)$:

$$
F(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\,(1+\epsilon_b X)}\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{1-\epsilon_b X}\right)^{2} y^{2} - 1\right]
$$

with $y = P/P_0$.

**Step 4 — The combined design equation becomes:**

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{dX}{dy} = -\dfrac{(1-\epsilon_b X)^2\,(-r_A')}{2\,y\,(1+\epsilon_b X)\,(F_{A0}/W)}\;}
$$

This is integrated numerically (e.g., Runge–Kutta) with the Ergun equation as a side constraint.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Differential Method for Data Analysis

In a **differential laboratory reactor**, the conversion is small (typically $X < 5\%$), and the rate is approximately constant across the catalyst bed. However, the differential method can be extended by measuring $X$ vs $W$ and obtaining the **slope** of the curve at any point.

**Step 1 — From the differential balance:**

$$
\dfrac{dX}{dW} = \dfrac{-r_A'}{F_{A0}}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $F_{A0}$ to obtain the rate at the local conditions:**

$$
\left(-r_A'\right)_{\text{at }X} = F_{A0}\,\dfrac{dX}{dW}
$$

**Step 3 — In practice, the slope is obtained graphically (tangent line) or by finite differences:**

$$
\left(-r_A'\right)_i = F_{A0}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta X_i}{\Delta W_i}
$$

> This is the *most reliable* method for collecting intrinsic rate data because it is local and does not require assuming a rate-law form.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of the Integral Method for Data Analysis

In an **integral laboratory reactor**, the conversion is large (typically $X > 5\%$). The data are $X$ vs $W$.

**Step 1 — Rearrange the PBR design equation:**

$$
\dfrac{W}{F_{A0}} = \int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A'}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume a rate-law form, e.g., power law: $-r_A' = k\,C_A^n$.**

For a constant-density system ($C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$):

$$
\dfrac{W}{F_{A0}} = \int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{k\,C_{A0}^n (1-X)^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**

$$
\dfrac{W}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}^n}\int_0^X (1-X)^{-n}\,dX
$$

For $n \ne 1$:

$$
\dfrac{W}{F_{A0}} = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}^n}\left[\dfrac{(1-X)^{1-n} - 1}{n-1}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to a form linear in a measurable quantity:**

Define $Y = \dfrac{W\,C_{A0}^n}{F_{A0}}$ and $Z = \dfrac{(1-X)^{1-n} - 1}{n-1}$. The relation is $Z = k\,Y$ for the correct $n$. Plot $Z$ vs $Y$ and check linearity. The slope gives $k$.

**Step 5 — Alternative linearization** (when $C_{A0}$ is constant and $n$ is unknown):

For $n=1$:

$$
-\ln(1-X) = k\,C_{A0}\,\dfrac{W}{F_{A0}}
$$

A plot of $-\ln(1-X)$ vs $W/F_{A0}$ should yield a straight line through the origin if the kinetics are first-order.

---

### 3.6 Derivation of Catalyst Decay Equations for a PBR

When activity $a$ decays with time, the rate is reduced to:

$$
-r_A' = a(t)\cdot k\,C_A^n
$$

**Step 1 — The decay law (exponential, n=1):**

$$
-\dfrac{da}{dt} = k_d\,a
$$

Integrating from $a=1$ at $t=0$:

$$
a(t) = e^{-k_d t}
$$

**Step 2 — For $n \ne 1$ (e.g., $n=2$):**

$$
-\dfrac{da}{dt} = k_d\,a^2
$$

Separate variables:

$$
-\dfrac{da}{a^2} = k_d\,dt
$$

Integrate:

$$
\dfrac{1}{a} - 1 = k_d\,t
$$

$$
\boxed{\;a(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + k_d t}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug into PBR design equation for use in a fixed-time operation:**

If the catalyst activity at time $t$ is $a(t)$ and the rate depends on $a$:

$$
W = F_{A0}\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{a(t)\,k\,C_A^n}
$$

For a *fluidized-bed* or *moving-bed* reactor where catalyst is continuously regenerated, $a$ is constant in time (steady $a$).

---

### 3.7 Worked Analytical Summary

| Method | Data Required | Output | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential** | $X$ vs $W$ from low-conversion reactor | $\left(-r_A'\right)$ at each $X$ | Single mechanism, complex kinetics, slope readable |
| **Integral** | $X$ vs $W$ from high-conversion reactor | Tests rate-law form, gives parameters | Simple power-law kinetics, well-posed guess |
| **Initial rate** | $X$ vs $W$ in limit $W \to 0$ | $\left(-r_A'\right)$ at known $C_{A0}$ | Mechanism deduction (e.g., order in A) |
| **Nonlinear regression** | Full $C_A, T, P$ matrix | Best-fit $k, n, E, K$ | Multiple parameters, models with adsorption terms |
| **Langmuir–Hinshelwood fit** | Rate vs $P_A, P_B, P_C$ | Adsorption constants $K_i$ | Surface-reaction-limited mechanism |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Heterogeneous Reactor Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Reaction Stoichiometry] --> B[Collect Intrinsic Rate Data]
    B --> C{Transport Limitations Absent?}
    C -- No --> D[Reactor Design Invalid: Revisit]
    C -- Yes --> E[Postulate Rate Law Form]
    E --> F[Apply Integral or Differential Method]
    F --> G[Estimate Parameters k, n, Ea, Ki]
    G --> H[Discriminate Between Models]
    H --> I{Mechanism Validated?}
    I -- No --> E
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply PBR Design Equation]
    J --> K{Include Pressure Drop?}
    K -- No --> L[Use Simple Integral PBR]
    K -- Yes --> M[Solve Coupled Ergun and PBR ODEs]
    L --> N[Compare PBR vs Slurry CSTR]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Select Reactor Type and Catalyst Inventory W]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each rhombus is a decision point. The loop `E → F → G → H → I → E` represents the *iterative* nature of data analysis — the analyst cycles through model selection and parameter estimation until a satisfactory fit is obtained.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Differential vs Integral Reactor Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Experimental Lab Reactor] --> B{Conversion Range}
    B -- X less than 5 percent --> C[Differential Reactor]
    B -- X greater than 5 percent --> D[Integral Reactor]
    C --> E[Measure Slope dX by dW]
    E --> F[Direct Rate at Each X]
    F --> G[Tabulate rA prime vs CA]
    D --> H[Assume Rate Law Form]
    H --> I[Integrate to Get X vs W]
    I --> J{Compare with Data}
    J -- Good Fit --> K[Parameters k and n]
    J -- Poor Fit --> H
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — PBR vs Slurry CSTR Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PBR[Plug Flow Tubular Reactor Packed Bed]
        direction TB
        P1[Inlet Fluid]
        P2[Catalyst Slice dW1]
        P3[Catalyst Slice dW2]
        P4[Catalyst Slice dWN]
        P5[Exit Fluid Lower Conversion]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    end
    subgraph CSTR[Slurry CSTR Perfectly Mixed]
        direction TB
        C1[Inlet Fluid]
        C2[Stirred Tank Uniform Composition]
        C3[Exit Fluid Same Composition as Tank]
        C1 --> C2
        C2 --> C3
    end
    PBR -.->|Higher Conversion for Same W| CMP[Decision Basis]
    CSTR -.->|Better for Exothermic or Product Inhibited| CMP
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Catalyst Decay Effects on Reactor Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Catalyst Activity a equals 1] --> B[Operation Begins t equals 0]
    B --> C[Activity Decays with Time]
    C --> D{Choose Operating Strategy}
    D -- Option A --> E[Constant Conversion Mode: Increase T with Time]
    D -- Option B --> F[Constant Temperature Mode: Conversion Drops]
    D -- Option C --> G[Moving Bed or Fluidized Bed: Continuous Regeneration]
    E --> H[Larger Initial W for Given End of Run Conversion]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Steady a, Standard PBR Design Applicable]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Differentiate between a packed-bed PFR and a slurry CSTR for heterogeneous catalytic reactions, citing three design-relevant contrasts.**

**Model Answer:**

A packed-bed PFR is a tubular reactor in which the catalyst is stationary and the fluid passes through in plug flow; a slurry CSTR is a tank in which fine catalyst particles are suspended and the fluid is perfectly back-mixed. Three contrasts are:

1. **Concentration profile:** The PFR has a varying composition along its length; the CSTR has a uniform composition equal to the exit stream.
2. **Rate evaluation:** In the PFR, the rate is a function of local position ($-r_A' = f(X)$); in the CSTR, the rate is constant and evaluated at the exit conditions.
3. **Design equation form:** The PFR design equation requires integration: $W = F_{A0}\int dX/(-r_A')$, while the CSTR design equation is algebraic: $W = F_{A0}X/(-r_A')$.

---

#### **Question 2. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **What is the difference between the differential method and the integral method of heterogeneous data analysis? State one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **differential method** extracts the rate directly from the local slope of an experimental $X$-vs-$W$ curve: $\left(-r_A'\right) = F_{A0}(dX/dW)$. It does not require a priori assumption of the rate-law form, but is limited by the difficulty of accurately measuring the slope of noisy data.

The **integral method** assumes a specific rate-law form (e.g., $-r_A' = kC_A^n$), integrates the design equation analytically, and compares the predicted $X$-vs-$W$ profile with the experimental data; the parameters are then adjusted for best fit. Its limitation is that a poor initial guess for the form can lead to no fit at all, and parameter coupling in complex LH kinetics makes the integral form intractable.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> Derive the design equation of a packed-bed plug flow reactor (PFTR) for a heterogeneous catalytic reaction. State clearly all assumptions.
>
> **Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> The catalytic reaction $A \rightarrow B$ follows the rate law $-r_A' = k C_A^2$ with $k = 0.05$ m³/(kmol·kg-cat·min) at the operating temperature. The feed is pure A at $C_{A0} = 2$ kmol/m³, $F_{A0} = 1$ kmol/min. Calculate the catalyst mass $W$ required for $X = 0.80$ in (i) a PBR and (ii) a slurry CSTR. The system is liquid-phase (constant density). Comment on which reactor needs less catalyst and why.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

(Detailed step-by-step derivation provided in **Section 3.1** above. The student should reproduce all six steps leading to $W = F_{A0}\int_0^X dX/(-r_A')$.)

Key assumptions to be stated:
1. Steady-state, isothermal operation.
2. Plug flow (no axial mixing, no radial gradients).
3. Constant catalyst activity (no decay).
4. Negligible pressure drop (constant $C_A$ profile).
5. Single rate-limiting step with intrinsic kinetics.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) PBR — Integral calculation:**

The PBR design equation:

$$
W = F_{A0}\int_0^X \dfrac{dX}{-r_A'}
$$

For a liquid-phase system (constant density), $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$. The rate law is:

$$
-r_A' = k\,C_A^2 = k\,C_{A0}^2(1-X)^2
$$

Substituting:

$$
W = F_{A0}\int_0^{0.80} \dfrac{dX}{k\,C_{A0}^2(1-X)^2}
$$

Pulling constants out:

$$
W = \dfrac{F_{A0}}{k\,C_{A0}^2}\int_0^{0.80} \dfrac{dX}{(1-X)^2}
$$

Standard integral: $\int dX/(1-X)^2 = 1/(1-X)$.

$$
W = \dfrac{F_{A0}}{k\,C_{A0}^2}\left[\dfrac{1}{1-X}\right]_0^{0.80}
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{1.0}{0.05 \times 2^2}\left[\dfrac{1}{0.20} - \dfrac{1}{1.0}\right]
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{1.0}{0.20}\left[5 - 1\right] = \dfrac{1.0 \times 4}{0.20} = 20 \text{ kg-cat}
$$

**(ii) CSTR — Algebraic calculation:**

The CSTR design equation:

$$
W = \dfrac{F_{A0}\,X}{-r_A'}\bigg\vert_{X = 0.80}
$$

The rate is evaluated at the exit:

$$
C_{A,\text{out}} = C_{A0}(1-X) = 2(0.20) = 0.40 \text{ kmol/m}^3
$$

$$
-r_A' = k\,C_{A,\text{out}}^2 = 0.05 \times (0.40)^2 = 0.05 \times 0.16 = 0.008 \text{ kmol/(kg-cat·min)}
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.80}{0.008} = 100 \text{ kg-cat}
$$

**Comparison & Explanation:**

The PBR requires only **20 kg** of catalyst, while the CSTR requires **100 kg** — five times as much. The PFR is superior for this reaction because the rate law has a positive order ($n = 2$); the average reactant concentration inside a PFR is much higher than the exit concentration of a CSTR, and so the average rate is higher in the PFR, requiring less catalyst.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
> With the help of the Ergun equation, explain how pressure drop is accounted for in the design of a packed-bed reactor. Write the coupled differential equation that must be solved when pressure drop is significant.
>
> **Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
> The elementary heterogeneous reaction $A \rightarrow B$ is carried out in a differential laboratory reactor. At three different inlet concentrations, the following rates (at $T = 500$ K) are observed:
>
> | $C_{A0}$ (kmol/m³) | $-r_A'$ (kmol/kg-cat·h) |
> |---|---|
> | 1.0 | 0.10 |
> | 2.0 | 0.40 |
> | 4.0 | 1.60 |
>
> Determine (i) the apparent order $n$ of the reaction, and (ii) the rate constant $k$.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

(Reference the derivations and equations in **Sections 3.3 and 2.4**.)

The Ergun equation in differential form is:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{G}{\rho_c D_p \epsilon^3}\left[\dfrac{150(1-\epsilon)\mu}{D_p} + 1.75 G\right]
$$

As $P$ drops, $C_A$ drops (since $C_A = y_A P / RT$ for a gas), and hence the rate $-r_A' = k f(C_A)$ falls. This makes the design equation an *ordinary differential equation* in $X$ coupled with the Ergun equation, requiring simultaneous numerical solution (e.g., by Euler or Runge–Kutta in Polymath/MATLAB).

The combined form (Fogler notation):

$$
\dfrac{dX}{dy} = -\dfrac{(1-\epsilon_b X)^2\,(-r_A')}{2\,y\,(1+\epsilon_b X)\,(F_{A0}/W)}
$$

with the Ergun constraint:

$$
\dfrac{dy}{dW} = -\dfrac{\alpha}{2y}\cdot\dfrac{(1+\epsilon_b X)}{(1-\epsilon_b X)}
$$

Boundary conditions: at $W = 0$, $y = 1$, $X = 0$. These are integrated together until the desired $W$ (or desired $X$) is reached.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Assume a power-law rate: $-r_A' = k C_A^n$. Take natural logarithms of both sides:

$$
\ln(-r_A') = \ln k + n\,\ln C_A
$$

This is a straight line: $Y = \ln(-r_A')$, $X = \ln C_A$, slope $= n$, intercept $= \ln k$.

Compute the log values:

| $\ln C_{A0}$ | $\ln(-r_A')$ |
|---|---|
| 0.000 | −2.303 |
| 0.693 | −0.916 |
| 1.386 | 0.470 |

Use the first and last points to estimate the slope:

$$
n = \dfrac{0.470 - (-2.303)}{1.386 - 0.000} = \dfrac{2.773}{1.386} = 2.0
$$

So the apparent order is $n = 2$.

Now use the first point to find $k$:

$$
0.10 = k \times (1.0)^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k = 0.10 \text{ m}^6/(\text{kmol·kg-cat·h})
$$

**Check** with $C_{A0} = 4.0$:

$$
-r_A' = 0.10 \times (4.0)^2 = 1.60 \;\checkmark
$$

The data are consistent with second-order kinetics.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to evaluate CSTR rate at *exit* conditions.** Roughly 40% of KTU scripts lose 1–2 marks on the CSTR design problem by evaluating the rate at the inlet. Always write: $W = F_{A0}X / (-r_A')\big\vert_{X_{out}}$.
> 2. **Confusing $\epsilon$ (bed void fraction) with $\epsilon_b$ (stoichiometric expansion factor).** They are completely different quantities and the symbols look similar. Use $\epsilon_b = y_{A0}\,\delta$ for stoichiometry and $\epsilon$ for void fraction in the Ergun equation.
> 3. **Omitting assumptions in derivations.** When asked to "derive the PBR design equation," a KTU examiner expects at least 4–5 explicit assumptions (steady state, isothermal, plug flow, no ΔP, etc.). Omitting them costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up integral and differential method data requirements.** The integral method needs high-conversion data; the differential method needs low-conversion data. Stating the wrong one indicates poor understanding.
> 5. **Linearization errors in data analysis.** For power-law kinetics, students often mistakenly take $\log(k)$ as $\ln(k)$ or use the wrong base. Always use natural log (ln) for the differential method derivation.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert units** in numerical problems. $k$ must be in units consistent with $C_A$ and $-r_A'$. Unit-inconsistency is the single most common computational error.
> 7. **Ignoring catalyst decay in design problems.** A problem that says "after 30 days of operation" is implicitly asking you to compute $a(t)$ and include it in the design equation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Heterogeneous Reactors & Data Analysis**

- [ ] **PBR design equation:** $W = F_{A0}\int_0^X dX/(-r_A')$ — derived from the steady-state differential mole balance $dF_A/dW = r_A'$.
- [ ] **CSTR design equation:** $W = F_{A0}X/(-r_A')\big\vert_{X_{out}}$ — algebraic, not integral.
- [ ] **Differential form:** $dX/dW = -r_A'/F_{A0}$ — the basis of the differential method.
- [ ] **Ergun equation** gives $\Delta P$; coupled with PBR when $\Delta P/P_0 > 0.05$.
- [ ] **Decay law:** $-da/dt = k_d a^n$ gives $a(t) = e^{-k_d t}$ (n=1) or $a = 1/(1+k_d t)$ (n=2).
- [ ] **With decay in PBR:** $W = F_{A0}\int dX/[a\cdot(-r_A')]$.
- [ ] **With decay in CSTR:** $W = F_{A0}X/[a\cdot(-r_A')]$ with $a$ uniform.
- [ ] **Differential method of analysis:** slope $\to$ rate; no rate-law assumption needed.
- [ ] **Integral method of analysis:** assume form $\to$ integrate $\to$ compare $\to$ get parameters.
- [ ] **Power-law linearization:** $\ln(-r_A') = \ln k + n \ln C_A$ — straight line.
- [ ] **Check transport disguises** before declaring intrinsic kinetics (Mears, Weisz–Prater criteria).
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** in derivations: steady state, isothermal, plug flow, no decay, no $\Delta P$.
- [ ] **Always state the basis of the rate expression** in numerical problems: per kg catalyst, per m² surface, etc.
- [ ] **Compare PFR vs CSTR** using $W$ for same conversion; for positive-order reactions, PFR uses less catalyst; for highly exothermic or product-inhibited reactions, CSTR is preferred.
- [ ] **Three pillars of data analysis:** data collection → parameter estimation → model discrimination. All three are required for a complete KTU answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion and Reaction in Porous Catalysts: Effective Diffusivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diffusion and Reaction in Porous Catalysts: Effective Diffusivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Effective Diffusivity (D<sub>e</sub> or D<sub>eff</sub>)** is the equivalent bulk diffusion coefficient that represents the net rate of molecular transport through the tortuous, void network of a porous catalyst pellet, accounting simultaneously for the geometric complexity of the pore structure and the dominant microscopic diffusion mechanism.

Mathematically, the molar flux of a reactant through a porous catalyst is written as:

$$N_A = -D_e \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

The effective diffusivity is rigorously related to the bulk fluid-phase diffusivity and the structural parameters of the pellet through the **Parallel Pore Model**:

$$D_e = \frac{\varepsilon_p \cdot \sigma}{\tau} \cdot D_{AB}$$

where **D<sub>AB</sub>** is the molecular (bulk) diffusivity, **ε<sub>p</sub>** is the pellet porosity, **σ** is the constriction factor (often approximated as unity), and **τ** is the **tortuosity factor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The effective diffusivity is NOT a fundamental fluid property; it is an *apparent transport parameter* of the catalyst pellet. It bundles together the pore geometry (porosity, tortuosity) and the active diffusion mechanism (molecular, Knudsen, surface).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge Highway

Imagine driving a car through a city:
- A **straight, open road** = Free molecular diffusion (no obstacles, fast travel).
- A **narrow, winding alleyway** = Knudsen diffusion (the car is now bigger than the alley, so it scrapes the walls and slows down).
- A **city with many one-ways and dead-ends** = The effect of **tortuosity** (the actual path travelled is much longer than the straight-line distance between two points).
- A **city with half the roads demolished** = The effect of **porosity** (only a fraction of the volume is available for traffic).

The **effective diffusivity (D<sub>e</sub>)** is the *average speed limit* of a vehicle that must navigate this entire city. It is therefore always **smaller** than the bulk diffusivity (D<sub>AB</sub>), typically by a factor of **5 to 10**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Range for Industrial Catalyst Pellets:**
> - Porosity (ε<sub>p</sub>): **0.4 – 0.6** (dimensionless)
> - Tortuosity (τ): **2 – 7** (commonly assumed ≈ **4**)
> - Effective Diffusivity (D<sub>e</sub>): **10<sup>-5</sup> – 10<sup>-3</sup> cm²/s** for gases; **10<sup>-7</sup> – 10<sup>-5</sup> cm²/s** for liquids
> - Mean pore radius (r<sub>p</sub>): **1 – 10 nm** (micropores) to **10² – 10³ nm** (macropores)

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Effective diffusivity is the **bridge** between the *external* fluid phase and the *internal* catalytic surface. Without it, the **Thiele modulus (φ)** — and consequently the **effectiveness factor (η)** — cannot be computed. These two parameters are the most frequently tested concepts in KTU Module 2 questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchy of Diffusion Mechanisms in a Pore

Within a single pore, transport can occur via four distinct mechanisms. The *dominant* mechanism depends on the ratio of the **mean free path (λ)** of the diffusing molecule to the **pore radius (r<sub>p</sub>)**, quantified by the **Knudsen Number (K<sub>n</sub>)**:

$$K_n = \frac{\lambda}{r_p}$$

| Regime | Knudsen Number K<sub>n</sub> | Dominant Mechanism | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk / Molecular | K<sub>n</sub> < 0.01 | Molecular Diffusion | Molecule-molecule collisions dominate |
| Transition | 0.01 < K<sub>n</sub> < 10 | Molecular + Knudsen | Both wall and intermolecular collisions matter |
| Knudsen | K<sub>n</sub> > 10 | Knudsen Diffusion | Molecule-wall collisions dominate |
| Configurational | r<sub>p</sub> ≈ molecular size | Configurational Diffusion | Molecule squeezes through; activated process |

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Mechanism

#### (a) Molecular (Bulk) Diffusion — D<sub>AB</sub>
- Occurs when the pore is much larger than the mean free path.
- Driven by concentration gradients; governed by the **Stefan-Maxwell** or **Chapman-Enskog** equations.
- Independent of pore size.
- For gases, it is roughly **inversely proportional to pressure**.

#### (b) Knudsen Diffusion — D<sub>K</sub>
- Occurs when the pore is so narrow that molecules collide *more with the wall* than with each other.
- Molecules "forget" their history between collisions → each molecule diffuses independently.
- For a circular pore of radius r<sub>p</sub>, the Knudsen diffusivity is:

$$D_K = \frac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

- An empirically convenient (KTU-favored) form is:

$$D_K = 97 \, r_p \left( \frac{T}{M_A} \right)^{0.5}$$

where **D<sub>K</sub>** is in **m²/s**, **r<sub>p</sub>** in **m**, **T** in **K**, and **M<sub>A</sub>** in **kg/kmol** (= g/mol).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction from D<sub>AB</sub>:** D<sub>K</sub> is proportional to r<sub>p</sub> (smaller pores → smaller D<sub>K</sub>) and to √T, but **independent of total pressure**.

#### (c) Surface Diffusion
- Adsorbed molecules migrate along the pore wall by hopping between adjacent adsorption sites.
- Becomes significant for **strongly adsorbed species** at **high surface coverage** and at **low temperatures**.
- Usually contributes < **10%** to total transport in industrial catalysts and is often neglected in KTU problems.

#### (d) Configurational Diffusion
- Occurs when the pore size approaches the molecular diameter (as in **zeolites**).
- The molecule "crawls" through; transport is an activated process with an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence.
- Typically not in the scope of standard KTU numerical problems but may appear as a **2-mark conceptual question**.

---

### 2.3 The Combined (Bosanquet) Diffusivity — D<sub>combined</sub>

When both molecular and Knudsen mechanisms contribute (the *transition regime*), the two resistances are added in **series** (as harmonic mean), yielding the **Bosanquet relation**:

$$D_{combined} = \left( \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_K} \right)^{-1} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_K}{D_{AB} + D_K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a harmonic mean?** A molecule traversing the pore experiences *both* types of collisions alternately. The total resistance (1/D) is the sum of the two individual resistances. This is a classic **"resistances in series"** analogy — memorize this for KTU!

**Limiting cases (verify these mentally for the exam):**
- If r<sub>p</sub> is very small → D<sub>K</sub> ≪ D<sub>AB</sub> → D<sub>combined</sub> ≈ D<sub>K</sub> (Knudsen-controlled)
- If r<sub>p</sub> is very large → D<sub>K</sub> ≫ D<sub>AB</sub> → D<sub>combined</sub> ≈ D<sub>AB</sub> (Bulk-controlled)

---

### 2.4 Structural Modifiers — Porosity & Tortuosity

The pellet is not a single straight pore. To convert a *single-pore* diffusivity into a *pellet-scale* effective diffusivity, two geometric corrections are applied:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pellet porosity | ε<sub>p</sub> | 0.4 – 0.6 | Fraction of pellet volume that is void |
| Tortuosity | τ | 2 – 7 | Ratio of actual path length to straight-line length |
| Constriction factor | σ | ≈ 1 (often neglected) | Accounts for varying pore diameters |

The full effective diffusivity becomes:

$$D_e = \frac{\varepsilon_p \cdot \sigma}{\tau} \cdot D_{combined}$$

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = -D_e \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Fick's law in porous pellet | mol/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $D_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_p \sigma}{\tau} D_{combined}$ | Effective diffusivity | m²/s |
| 3 | $D_{combined} = \left( \dfrac{1}{D_{AB}} + \dfrac{1}{D_K} \right)^{-1}$ | Bosanquet (molecular + Knudsen) | m²/s |
| 4 | $D_K = \dfrac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{\dfrac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$ | Knudsen diffusivity (rigorous) | m²/s |
| 5 | $D_K = 97 \, r_p \left( \dfrac{T}{M_A} \right)^{0.5}$ | Knudsen diffusivity (KTU form) | m²/s |
| 6 | $K_n = \dfrac{\lambda}{r_p}$ | Knudsen number | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\phi = L \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_e}}$ | Thiele modulus (slab, 1st order) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh \phi}{\phi}$ | Effectiveness factor (slab, 1st order) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\phi = R \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_e}}$ | Thiele modulus (sphere) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi} \left( \dfrac{1}{\tanh \phi} - \dfrac{1}{\phi} \right)$ | Effectiveness factor (sphere) | dimensionless |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The effective diffusivity directly controls:
1. **Reactor Sizing** — Intraparticle diffusion limitations reduce the actual rate below the intrinsic rate, demanding larger catalyst volumes.
2. **Catalyst Design** — Engineers choose pore-size distributions to *avoid* the strong pore-diffusion resistance. Macropores (~10²–10³ nm) help reactants reach the interior; micropores provide the active surface area.
3. **Selectivity Control** — In complex reactions (e.g., catalytic cracking), the *ratio* of diffusion rates of competing molecules affects product distribution. This is the basis of **shape-selective catalysis** in zeolites.
4. **Regeneration Strategies** — Coke deposits block pores, reducing ε<sub>p</sub> and increasing τ, hence lowering D<sub>e</sub>. Monitoring D<sub>e</sub> helps schedule regeneration cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Knudsen Diffusivity for a Circular Pore

We start from kinetic theory. Consider a molecule moving in a straight line between two successive wall collisions. The average time between collisions is:

$$\bar{t} = \frac{\bar{l}}{\bar{v}}$$

where **l̄** is the mean free path *between walls* and **v̄** is the mean molecular speed. For a circular pore of radius r<sub>p</sub>, the average distance between walls is:

$$\bar{l} = 2 r_p$$

The mean molecular speed from the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution is:

$$\bar{v} = \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

The mean-square displacement in one direction (say, z) due to a single step is governed by the projection of a random step. The diffusion coefficient is defined via Einstein's relation:

$$D_K = \frac{\overline{\Delta z^2}}{2 \bar{t}}$$

The variance of the z-projection of a random 3-D step in a pore (where motion is restricted to forward/backward along the axis) gives:

$$\overline{\Delta z^2} = \frac{2}{3} \bar{l}^{\,2}$$

Substituting all terms:

$$D_K = \frac{1}{2 \bar{t}} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \bar{l}^{\,2} = \frac{\bar{l}^{\,2}}{3 \bar{t}} = \frac{\bar{l}^{\,2}}{3} \cdot \frac{\bar{v}}{\bar{l}} = \frac{\bar{l} \cdot \bar{v}}{3}$$

$$D_K = \frac{1}{3} (2 r_p) \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

$$\boxed{\; D_K = \frac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}} \;}$$

Plugging in the numerical values of R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), π = 3.1416, and converting units yields the empirical KTU form:

$$D_K \;[\text{m}^2/\text{s}] = 97 \cdot r_p \;[\text{m}] \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T \;[\text{K}]}{M_A \;[\text{kg/kmol}]}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: The Bosanquet Combined Diffusivity

Consider a molecule that, while traversing a pore, alternately experiences:
- An intermolecular collision (rate ∝ 1/D<sub>AB</sub>)
- A wall collision (rate ∝ 1/D<sub>K</sub>)

If the average *time* spent between successive intermolecular collisions is τ<sub>AB</sub> and between wall collisions is τ<sub>K</sub>, the total time for a step is the sum (since these are sequential, non-parallel events):

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{AB} + \tau_K$$

The mean-square displacement is the same as for either process, so by Einstein's relation:

$$\frac{\overline{\Delta z^2}}{2 D_{combined}} = \frac{\overline{\Delta z^2}}{2 D_{AB}} + \frac{\overline{\Delta z^2}}{2 D_K}$$

Dividing both sides by $\overline{\Delta z^2}/2$:

$$\frac{1}{D_{combined}} = \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_K}$$

$$\boxed{\; D_{combined} = \left( \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_K} \right)^{-1} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_K}{D_{AB} + D_K} \;}$$

This is the **Bosanquet interpolation** — a beautiful and frequently tested result.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A gas-phase reactant A (M<sub>A</sub> = 44 kg/kmol) diffuses through a porous catalyst pellet at T = 500 K and P = 2 atm. The mean pore radius is r<sub>p</sub> = 50 nm. The molecular diffusivity of A in the carrier gas at the same T and P is D<sub>AB</sub> = 1.5 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s. The pellet has porosity ε<sub>p</sub> = 0.5 and tortuosity τ = 4. Estimate the **effective diffusivity D<sub>e</sub>**.

#### Step 1 — Compute the Knudsen Diffusivity D<sub>K</sub>

Using the KTU empirical form:

$$D_K = 97 \times (50 \times 10^{-9}) \times \sqrt{\frac{500}{44}}$$

Compute the square root first:

$$\sqrt{\frac{500}{44}} = \sqrt{11.364} = 3.371$$

Substitute:

$$D_K = 97 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 3.371$$

$$D_K = 4.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.371 \times 10^{-6}$$

Wait — recalculate step-by-step:

$$97 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 4.85 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$D_K = 4.85 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.371 = 1.635 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Combined Diffusivity

Since D<sub>K</sub> ≈ 1.64 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s and D<sub>AB</sub> = 1.5 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s are of *comparable magnitude*, we are in the **transition regime**. Apply Bosanquet:

$$D_{combined} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_K}{D_{AB} + D_K} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-5})(1.635 \times 10^{-5})}{(1.5 \times 10^{-5}) + (1.635 \times 10^{-5})}$$

Numerator:

$$(1.5 \times 10^{-5})(1.635 \times 10^{-5}) = 2.4525 \times 10^{-10}$$

Denominator:

$$(1.5 + 1.635) \times 10^{-5} = 3.135 \times 10^{-5}$$

Therefore:

$$D_{combined} = \frac{2.4525 \times 10^{-10}}{3.135 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.82 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Structural Correction

$$D_e = \frac{\varepsilon_p}{\tau} \cdot D_{combined} = \frac{0.5}{4} \times 7.82 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$D_e = 0.125 \times 7.82 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{\; D_e = 9.78 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s} \;}$$

#### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation

- The bulk D<sub>AB</sub> was 1.5 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s, but the **effective D<sub>e</sub>** is only 9.78 × 10<sup>-7</sup> m²/s — a reduction of about **15×**.
- This factor (1/15) reflects the cumulative penalty of the pellet structure: about 50% of the volume is void (ε<sub>p</sub>), and the path is ~4× longer (τ), plus a modest reduction from combined-diffusion resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** Always check the *ratio* D<sub>K</sub>/D<sub>AB</sub>. If it is **> 10**, use D<sub>combined</sub> ≈ D<sub>K</sub> (Knudsen-controlled). If it is **< 0.1**, use D<sub>combined</sub> ≈ D<sub>AB</sub> (Bulk-controlled). This saves computation time in the exam.

---

### 3.4 The Thiele Modulus — The Bridge to Effectiveness Factor

The effective diffusivity directly enters the **Thiele modulus**, which is the dimensionless ratio of the *intrinsic reaction rate* to the *diffusion rate*:

$$\phi = L \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}} \quad \text{(slab geometry)}$$

The **effectiveness factor (η)** measures how much of the catalyst is "wasted" by diffusion limitations:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Actual rate with diffusion resistance}}{\text{Rate if entire pellet were at surface concentration}}$$

For a **first-order reaction in a spherical pellet**:

$$\eta = \frac{3}{\phi} \left( \frac{1}{\tanh \phi} - \frac{1}{\phi} \right)$$

**Limiting cases (highly exam-relevant):**
- φ → 0 (slow reaction, fast diffusion) → η → 1 (no diffusion limitation)
- φ → ∞ (fast reaction, slow diffusion) → η → 3/φ (strong diffusion limitation; rate controlled by diffusion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **only place D<sub>e</sub> appears** in the entire heterogeneous reaction analysis is inside the Thiele modulus. So if you compute D<sub>e</sub> correctly, the rest of the problem is straightforward. This is why KTU loves to test effective diffusivity first!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Diffusion in a Porous Pellet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reactant Molecule A in Bulk Fluid"] --> B["External Film Surrounding Pellet"]
    B --> C["Porous Catalyst Pellet"]
    C --> D["Pore Network Geometry"]
    D --> E["Pellet Porosity epsilon_p"]
    D --> F["Tortuosity tau"]
    D --> G["Pore Radius r_p"]
    E --> H["Effective Diffusivity D_e"]
    F --> H
    G --> I["Mechanism Selection via Knudsen Number"]
    I --> J["Molecular Diffusion D_AB"]
    I --> K["Knudsen Diffusion D_K"]
    I --> L["Surface Diffusion D_S"]
    J --> M["Bosanquet Combined Diffusivity"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["Apply Structural Correction epsilon_p / tau"]
    N --> H
    H --> O["Thiele Modulus phi"]
    O --> P["Effectiveness Factor eta"]
    P --> Q["Actual Reaction Rate in Pellet"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — From D<sub>e</sub> to Overall Reactor Performance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Catalyst Manufacturer Data Sheet"] --> A2["Mean Pore Radius r_p"]
    A1 --> A3["Pellet Porosity epsilon_p"]
    A1 --> A4["Tortuosity tau assumed 4"]
    A2 --> B1["Compute D_K via kinetic theory"]
    A3 --> B2["Read D_AB from literature"]
    A4 --> B3["Apply Bosanquet series resistance"]
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1["Apply structural correction"]
    C1 --> D1["Effective Diffusivity D_e"]
    D1 --> E1["Compute Thiele Modulus phi"]
    E1 --> F1["Read effectiveness factor eta from chart"]
    F1 --> G1["Multiply by intrinsic rate to get actual rate"]
    G1 --> H1["Integrate over reactor to get conversion"]
```

### 4.3 Diffusion Regime Map (Conceptual Chart, KTU-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Small Pore r_p less than 1 nm"] --> R1["Configurational Diffusion"]
    P2["Mesopore r_p 1 to 100 nm"] --> R2["Knudsen Dominant or Transition"]
    P3["Macropore r_p greater than 1000 nm"] --> R3["Molecular Bulk Diffusion"]
    P2 --> R4["Knudsen Number K_n greater than 1"]
    P3 --> R5["Knudsen Number K_n less than 0.01"]
    R2 --> X1["Use Bosanquet Combined"]
    R4 --> X1
    R3 --> X2["D_combined approx D_AB"]
    R5 --> X2
```

```mermaid

    subgraph PELL["Catalyst Pellet Cross Section"]
        direction LR
        S1["Solid Matrix Region A"] --- P1["Pore Channel 1 radius r1"]
        S2["Solid Matrix Region B"] --- P2["Pore Channel 2 radius r2 narrow"]
        S3["Solid Matrix Region C"] --- P3["Pore Channel 3 radius r3 wide"]
        P1 --- J1["Pore Junction T intersection"]
        J1 --- P2
        J1 --- P3
    end
    EXT["External Fluid Film Surrounds Pellet"] --- PELL
    ACT["Adsorbed Species on Pore Wall Triggers Surface Diffusion"] --- P1
    ACT --- P2
```

> The above diagrams are **functional representations** of the physical concept. In the actual KTU examination, students may be asked to *sketch* a porous pellet, label the pore, solid matrix, tortuous path, and the diffusion direction. The above flowcharts capture the same information in a structured, examinable form.
