<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define effective diffusivity. Why is it always less than the molecular diffusivity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Effective diffusivity (D<sub>e</sub>) is the apparent diffusion coefficient used in Fick's law to describe the transport of a species through the tortuous, void network of a porous catalyst pellet.

$$N_A = -D_e \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

It is always **less than** the molecular (bulk) diffusivity D<sub>AB</sub> because the pellet offers geometric resistance to diffusion:

1. Only a fraction (ε<sub>p</sub> < 1) of the pellet volume is available for flow — the rest is occupied by the solid matrix. **[1 mark]**
2. The actual diffusion path is longer than the straight-line distance — quantified by the tortuosity factor (τ > 1). **[1 mark]**
3. In narrow pores, the additional Knudsen mechanism introduces a series resistance that further reduces the combined diffusivity below D<sub>AB</sub>. **[1 mark]**

Thus:

$$D_e = \frac{\varepsilon_p}{\tau} D_{combined} < D_{AB}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Distinguish between molecular diffusion and Knudsen diffusion. State the criterion for each to dominate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Molecular Diffusion (D<sub>AB</sub>) | Knudsen Diffusion (D<sub>K</sub>) |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Concentration gradient | Concentration gradient |
| Dominant collision | Molecule–molecule | Molecule–wall |
| Pore size dependence | Independent of r<sub>p</sub> | Proportional to r<sub>p</sub> |
| Pressure dependence | D<sub>AB</sub> ∝ 1/P | Independent of P |
| Temperature dependence | D<sub>AB</sub> ∝ T<sup>1.75</sup> | D<sub>K</sub> ∝ T<sup>0.5</sup> |

**Criterion (Knudsen Number):** **[1 mark]**

$$K_n = \frac{\lambda}{r_p}$$

- K<sub>n</sub> < 0.01 → **Molecular diffusion** dominates
- K<sub>n</sub> > 10 → **Knudsen diffusion** dominates
- 0.01 < K<sub>n</sub> < 10 → **Transition regime** → use Bosanquet combined diffusivity

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the expression for Knudsen diffusivity starting from kinetic theory. State clearly the assumptions used. (7 marks)**

**(b) A first-order gaseous reaction A → B takes place on a porous catalyst pellet at 1 atm and 400 K. The pellet porosity is 0.45, tortuosity is 4, mean pore radius is 80 nm, and the molecular diffusivity of A in the carrier gas is 1.2 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s. The rate constant is 0.08 s<sup>-1</sup> and the pellet is a slab of half-thickness L = 5 mm. Compute the effectiveness factor. (7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Detailed Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the system and assumptions (1 mark):**
Consider a single cylindrical pore of radius r<sub>p</sub>. We assume:
1. The pore is much smaller than the macroscopic pellet, so transport inside is 1-D.
2. Molecules collide only with the wall; intermolecular collisions are negligible (pure Knudsen regime).
3. Wall collisions are diffuse (molecule leaves the wall with a cosine distribution in direction, independent of the incoming direction).
4. The gas is ideal and dilute.

**Step 2 — Average distance and speed (2 marks):**
- Mean distance between wall collisions: $\bar{l} = 2 r_p$
- Mean molecular speed from Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution:

$$\bar{v} = \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Einstein's relation (2 marks):**
The mean-square displacement in the axial direction (z) per collision is:

$$\overline{\Delta z^2} = \frac{2}{3} \bar{l}^{\,2}$$

The mean time between collisions is $\bar{t} = \bar{l} / \bar{v}$. By Einstein's diffusion law:

$$D_K = \frac{\overline{\Delta z^2}}{2 \bar{t}} = \frac{\bar{l}^{\,2}}{3} \cdot \frac{\bar{v}}{\bar{l}} = \frac{\bar{l} \cdot \bar{v}}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify (1 mark):**

$$D_K = \frac{2 r_p}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi M_A}}$$

**Step 5 — Final empirical form (1 mark):**
Substituting R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), π = 3.1416, and converting units:

$$D_K \;[\text{m}^2/\text{s}] = 97 \cdot r_p \;[\text{m}] \cdot \sqrt{\frac{T \;[\text{K}]}{M_A \;[\text{kg/kmol}]}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Detailed Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- T = 400 K, P = 1 atm
- ε<sub>p</sub> = 0.45, τ = 4, r<sub>p</sub> = 80 nm = 80 × 10<sup>-9</sup> m
- D<sub>AB</sub> = 1.2 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s
- k = 0.08 s<sup>-1</sup>, L = 5 mm = 5 × 10<sup>-3</sup> m
- M<sub>A</sub> = 28 kg/kmol (assumed for typical reactant, e.g., CO or N₂)

**Step 1 — Compute D<sub>K</sub> (1.5 marks):**

$$D_K = 97 \times (80 \times 10^{-9}) \times \sqrt{\frac{400}{28}}$$

$$\sqrt{400/28} = \sqrt{14.286} = 3.780$$

$$D_K = 97 \times 80 \times 10^{-9} \times 3.780 = 2.933 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute D<sub>combined</sub> via Bosanquet (1.5 marks):**

$$D_{combined} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_K}{D_{AB} + D_K} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-5})(2.933 \times 10^{-5})}{(1.2 + 2.933) \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator: $3.520 \times 10^{-10}$
Denominator: $4.133 \times 10^{-5}$

$$D_{combined} = \frac{3.520 \times 10^{-10}}{4.133 \times 10^{-5}} = 8.52 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective diffusivity D<sub>e</sub> (1 mark):**

$$D_e = \frac{\varepsilon_p}{\tau} D_{combined} = \frac{0.45}{4} \times 8.52 \times 10^{-6} = 9.585 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Thiele modulus for slab (1 mark):**

$$\phi = L \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}} = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{0.08}{9.585 \times 10^{-7}}}$$

$$= (5 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{83464} = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 288.9 = 1.444$$

**Step 5 — Compute effectiveness factor (2 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{\tanh \phi}{\phi} = \frac{\tanh(1.444)}{1.444}$$

From tables, $\tanh(1.444) \approx 0.893$.

$$\eta = \frac{0.893}{1.444} = 0.618$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation:** Since η = 0.618 is significantly less than 1, the catalyst pellet is operating under **moderate pore-diffusion limitation**. Only ~62% of the catalyst's potential is being utilized. **[Bonus: 0 marks allocated but valuable insight]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the parallel pore model for effective diffusivity. Discuss how porosity, tortuosity, and constriction factor modify the molecular diffusivity. (7 marks)**

**(b) A catalyst pellet has the following properties: pellet diameter = 6 mm (assumed spherical), porosity = 0.5, tortuosity = 3, mean pore radius = 100 nm. The reaction A → Products is first-order with rate constant k = 0.5 s<sup>-1</sup> at 500 K. The molecular diffusivity of A at this condition is 2 × 10<sup>-5</sup> m²/s and the reactant molecular weight is 32 kg/kmol. Determine whether the pellet operates in the Knudsen, molecular, or transition regime, and calculate the Thiele modulus for the spherical geometry. (7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Detailed Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Concept of the parallel pore model (2 marks):**
The parallel pore model idealizes the porous pellet as a bundle of straight, non-intersecting cylindrical capillaries of uniform radius r<sub>p</sub>, oriented in the direction of diffusion. Although oversimplified (real pores are tortuous, interconnected, and of varying size), the model gives a first-order estimate of the effective diffusivity by combining the molecular diffusion coefficient with geometric correction factors.

**Step 2 — The three correction factors (3 marks):**

(i) **Porosity (ε<sub>p</sub>):** Since only a fraction ε<sub>p</sub> of the pellet cross-section is available for flow, the flux is reduced by a factor ε<sub>p</sub>:
$$D \to \varepsilon_p \cdot D_{AB}$$

(ii) **Tortuosity (τ):** In a real pellet, the diffusion path zig-zags. The actual path length is τ times the straight-line distance. Since the time taken (and hence the inverse diffusivity) is proportional to path length:
$$D \to \frac{\varepsilon_p}{\tau} D_{AB}$$

(iii) **Constriction factor (σ):** When pores vary in cross-section, the molecule experiences "bottlenecks." This is captured by σ ≤ 1. For uniform circular pores, σ ≈ 1 and is often dropped. The full expression:

$$D_e = \frac{\varepsilon_p \cdot \sigma}{\tau} \cdot D_{combined}$$

**Step 3 — Why this model works despite its simplicity (1 mark):**
The parallel pore model captures the dominant physics — reduced open area and longer path — while leaving a single empirically adjusted parameter (τ) to absorb all the geometric complexity. With τ ≈ 4 (an industry standard default), it predicts D<sub>e</sub> to within a factor of 2–3 for most industrial catalysts.

**Step 4 — Extension to Knudsen regime (1 mark):**
When Knudsen diffusion is included, the in-pore diffusivity becomes D<sub>combined</sub> (Bosanquet) and the same structural correction is applied to obtain D<sub>e</sub>.

---

#### Part (b) — Detailed Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Knudsen diffusivity (1.5 marks):**

$$D_K = 97 \times (100 \times 10^{-9}) \times \sqrt{\frac{500}{32}}$$

$$\sqrt{500/32} = \sqrt{15.625} = 3.953$$

$$D_K = 97 \times 10^{-7} \times 3.953 = 3.834 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with D<sub>AB</sub> and identify regime (1 mark):**

$$D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}, \quad D_K = 3.834 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

The ratio D<sub>K</sub>/D<sub>AB</sub> = 3.834/2 ≈ 1.92. Since this ratio is between 0.1 and 10, the system is in the **transition regime** (neither pure molecular nor pure Knudsen). Both mechanisms must be combined using the Bosanquet relation. **[1 mark]**

**Step 3 — Compute D<sub>combined</sub> (1 mark):**

$$D_{combined} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot D_K}{D_{AB} + D_K} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-5})(3.834 \times 10^{-5})}{(5.834 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$= \frac{7.668 \times 10^{-10}}{5.834 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.314 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective diffusivity (1 mark):**

$$D_e = \frac{\varepsilon_p}{\tau} D_{combined} = \frac{0.5}{3} \times 1.314 \times 10^{-5} = 2.19 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Thiele modulus for sphere (1.5 marks):**

For a spherical pellet of radius R = 3 mm = 3 × 10<sup>-3</sup> m:

$$\phi = R \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}} = (3 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{0.5}{2.19 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$= (3 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{2.283 \times 10^{5}} = (3 \times 10^{-3}) \times 477.8 = 1.433$$

**Step 6 — Final interpretation (1 mark):**
The Thiele modulus φ = 1.433 is in the **intermediate range** (φ between 0.5 and 5), implying that the pellet operates in the **transition regime** between reaction-controlled and diffusion-controlled regimes. The effectiveness factor is neither ≈ 1 nor ≈ 3/φ, and must be calculated using the spherical formula:

$$\eta = \frac{3}{\phi} \left( \frac{1}{\tanh \phi} - \frac{1}{\phi} \right) = \frac{3}{1.433} \left( \frac{1}{0.893} - \frac{1}{1.433} \right) = 2.094 \times (1.120 - 0.698) = 0.884$$

Thus, the catalyst is ~88.4% effective, indicating mild diffusion limitation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Effective Diffusivity Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing cm and m in the Knudsen formula. The empirical form D<sub>K</sub> = 97 r<sub>p</sub> √(T/M) requires r<sub>p</sub> in **meters**, T in **K**, and M in **kg/kmol**. Many students mistakenly enter M in kg/mol (off by a factor of 1000) or r<sub>p</sub> in nm without converting.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Harmonic Mean (–3 marks):** In the transition regime, students often *add* D<sub>AB</sub> and D<sub>K</sub> directly. The correct operation is the **harmonic mean** (Bosanquet relation), because the two diffusion mechanisms act in **series**, not in parallel.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Structural Correction (–2 marks):** Some students compute D<sub>combined</sub> and use it directly as D<sub>e</sub>. The pellet's porosity and tortuosity introduce a multiplicative factor ε<sub>p</sub>/τ that **must** be applied to obtain the effective diffusivity.
>
> 4. **Using the Wrong Thiele Modulus Geometry (–2 marks):** The Thiele modulus formula depends on pellet geometry. Using the slab formula $\phi = L \sqrt{k/D_e}$ for a spherical pellet (or vice versa) leads to a wrong effectiveness factor. Always identify the geometry first.
>
> 5. **Not Stating Assumptions (–1 mark):** KTU examiners consistently deduct marks if assumptions (constant T, P, ideal gas, dilute system, steady state, 1-D diffusion) are not explicitly listed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effective Diffusivity (Module 2)**

- [x] **Effective Diffusivity D<sub>e</sub>** is an *apparent* transport property of the *pellet*, not of the gas. It is always **less than** the molecular diffusivity D<sub>AB</sub>.
- [x] Four diffusion mechanisms in pores: **molecular, Knudsen, surface, configurational** — each dominates in a different pore-size regime.
- [x] The **Knudsen number K<sub>n</sub> = λ/r<sub>p</sub>** is the *master switch* that decides the regime.
- [x] **Knudsen diffusivity formula (KTU form):** D<sub>K</sub> = 97 r<sub>p</sub> √(T/M) — careful with units (r<sub>p</sub> in m, M in kg/kmol).
- [x] D<sub>K</sub> ∝ r<sub>p</sub> (smaller pore → smaller D<sub>K</sub>) and D<sub>K</sub> ∝ √T, but **independent of P**.
- [x] **Bosanquet (combined) diffusivity:** D<sub>combined</sub> = (1/D<sub>AB</sub> + 1/D<sub>K</sub>)<sup>-1</sup> — harmonic mean, NOT arithmetic mean.
- [x] **Structural correction:** D<sub>e</sub> = (ε<sub>p</sub>·σ / τ) × D<sub>combined</sub>. Typical τ ≈ 4, σ ≈ 1.
- [x] D<sub>e</sub> feeds into the **Thiele modulus φ**, which determines the **effectiveness factor η** — the ultimate goal of internal diffusion analysis.
- [x] For a slab (1st order): η = tanh φ / φ. For a sphere (1st order): η = (3/φ)[(1/tanh φ) − (1/φ)].
- [x] **Limiting cases:** φ → 0 ⇒ η → 1 (no limitation); φ → ∞ ⇒ η → 1/φ (strong limitation, rate controlled by diffusion).
- [x] **Always state assumptions:** steady state, isothermal, ideal gas, 1-D diffusion, dilute system, first-order kinetics (unless stated otherwise).

**Final KTU Mantra:** *Compute D<sub>K</sub> → Combine via Bosanquet → Apply ε<sub>p</sub>/τ → Get D<sub>e</sub> → Compute φ → Read η.* Master this chain, and Module 2 numericals become straightforward.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tortuosity-Modelling of Diffusion with Reaction on a Spherical Catalyst, Thiele Modulus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tortuosity (τ) — The Hidden Zig-Zag Path

> [!NOTE]
> **Tortuosity (τ)** is a purely geometric, dimensionless property of a porous solid that quantifies how much the actual path travelled by a diffusing molecule is longer than the straight-line distance between two points inside the pore network.

**Formal KTU Definition:**

$$\tau = \frac{\text{Actual path length travelled by molecule}}{\text{Straight-line distance between entry and exit points}}$$

For a real catalyst pellet, $\tau \geq 1$. For a perfectly straight cylindrical pore, $\tau = 1$. For a tortuous, interconnected pore network typical of industrial catalysts (γ-alumina, zeolites, silica), $\tau$ lies between **2 and 7**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine you are inside a corn-maze (the pore network). The straight-line distance from the entrance to the exit might be 10 m, but the actual walking path you must take is 30 m. The ratio 30/10 = 3 is the **tortuosity**. The reactant molecules diffusing through your catalyst pellet face the same problem — they cannot travel in a straight line because solid walls keep blocking the direct route. This detour slows diffusion dramatically.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Dimension |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tortuosity | $\tau$ | 2 – 7 (industrial catalysts) | dimensionless |
| Porosity | $\varepsilon$ | 0.4 – 0.6 | dimensionless |
| Effective diffusivity | $D_e$ | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-1}$ cm²/s | L² T⁻¹ |

The **Effective Diffusivity** combines porosity and tortuosity into a single, observable transport parameter:

$$D_e = \frac{\varepsilon \, D_{AB}}{\tau}$$

where $D_{AB}$ is the bulk molecular (or Knudsen) diffusivity, $\varepsilon$ corrects for the fact that the solid occupies part of the pellet volume, and $\tau$ corrects for the path lengthening.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all internal diffusion problems in KTU exams, you **must** use $D_e$ (not $D_{AB}$), because reactions occur on the *internal* surface area, where only the *tortuous, porous* path is available.

## 1.2 The Spherical Catalyst — Why Spheres?

Industrial catalyst pellets are often extruded cylinders, trilobes, or spheres. Spheres are the **simplest geometry** that allows closed-form analytical solution of the diffusion-with-reaction differential equation. The model developed for a sphere is then generalized to other shapes via the **equivalent characteristic length** $L = V_p / S_p$.

> [!NOTE]
> For a sphere, $L = V_p / S_p = (4/3\pi R^3) / (4\pi R^2) = R/3$. The Thiele modulus is conventionally defined using $R$ (the pellet radius), not $L$, for spheres. For other shapes, we use $L$.

## 1.3 Thiele Modulus — The "Diffusion-vs-Reaction" Compass

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thiele Modulus (φ)** is a dimensionless number that compares the rate of reaction to the rate of diffusion inside a porous catalyst. It is the single most important parameter in heterogeneous catalysis design.

For a **first-order irreversible reaction** $A \to B$ on a spherical catalyst:

$$\phi_s = R \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}}$$

- $\phi_s \to 0$: reaction is *very slow* compared to diffusion → reactant penetrates deep, no concentration gradient → **reaction-controlled regime**.
- $\phi_s \to \infty$: reaction is *very fast* compared to diffusion → reactant is consumed near the outer surface → **diffusion-controlled (pore-diffusion-limited) regime**.
- $\phi_s \sim 1$: both resistances are comparable → **mixed regime**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of φ as a "Damköhler number for internal transport." High φ = the dam (diffusion) is choking the river (reaction).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Picture: What Happens Inside a Catalyst Pellet?

When a reactant $A$ from the bulk gas stream hits the outer surface of a porous pellet:

1. It dissolves in the pore fluid (external mass transfer — assumed negligible here).
2. It **diffuses inward** through the tortuous pore network (this is where $D_e$ matters).
3. It **adsorbs** on the active site.
4. It **reacts** on the surface to form product $B$.
5. Product $B$ **diffuses outward** and desorbs.

If reaction is fast, $A$ is consumed *before* it can reach the centre. The concentration $C_A$ drops from $C_{As}$ at the surface to a much lower value at the centre. The **actual rate is less** than what the bulk concentration would predict. The **effectiveness factor η** quantifies this penalty.

## 2.2 Starting Differential Equation (Steady State, First-Order)

For a spherical pellet, taking a shell balance at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$:

$$\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r^2 \, D_e \, \frac{dC_A}{dr} \right) = k \, C_A$$

**Boundary conditions:**

$$\text{BC1 (Surface):} \quad C_A(R) = C_{As}$$

$$\text{BC2 (Centre, by symmetry):} \quad \left.\frac{dC_A}{dr}\right|_{r=0} = 0$$

## 2.3 Definition of Effectiveness Factor (η)

> [!NOTE]
> **Effectiveness Factor η** = (Actual rate of reaction in the pellet) ÷ (Rate if entire pellet were exposed to surface concentration $C_{As}$)

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Actual rate}}{\text{Rate at } C_{As}} = \frac{\int_0^R k \, C_A(r) \, 4\pi r^2 \, dr}{(4/3)\pi R^3 \, k \, C_{As}}$$

For first-order kinetics, this integral evaluates to:

$$\eta = \frac{3}{\phi_s^2} \left[ \phi_s \coth(\phi_s) - 1 \right]$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_e = \varepsilon D_{AB} / \tau$ | Effective diffusivity in porous pellet |
| 2 | $\phi_s = R \sqrt{k/D_e}$ | Thiele modulus for **first-order** reaction, **sphere** |
| 3 | $\phi_s = R \sqrt{\dfrac{k_n C_{As}^{n-1}}{D_e}}$ | Thiele modulus for **nth-order** reaction, sphere |
| 4 | $\phi = L \sqrt{\dfrac{k_n C_{As}^{n-1}}{D_e}}$ | Thiele modulus for *general shape*, $L = V_p/S_p$ |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi_s^2}\left[\phi_s \coth(\phi_s) - 1\right]$ | Effectiveness factor, **sphere, first order** |
| 6 | $\eta \approx 1$ when $\phi_s < 0.5$ | Reaction-controlled limit |
| 7 | $\eta \approx 3/\phi_s$ when $\phi_s > 3$ | Diffusion-controlled (strong pore diffusion) limit |
| 8 | $\eta \approx 1 - \phi_s^2/15$ when $\phi_s \to 0$ | Taylor expansion for small $\phi_s$ |
| 9 | $\Phi_{WP} = \dfrac{r_{obs} \, \rho_{cat} \, L^2}{D_e \, C_{As}}$ | **Weisz-Prater criterion** for detecting internal diffusion |
| 10 | $\Phi_{WP} \ll 1 \Rightarrow$ no internal diffusion limitation | If $\Phi_{WP} < 0.3$ → negligible; if $\Phi_{WP} \gg 1$ → strong limitation |
| 11 | $\eta = 1$ for slab, $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh \phi_L}{\phi_L}$ | Effectiveness factor for **slab (flat plate)** |
| 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{2}{\phi_L^2}\left[1 - e^{-\phi_L^2}...\right]$ etc. | Use standard tables for cylinders, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table-Usage Rule:** In KTU exam answer sheets, always **state the formula first**, then **substitute numerical values with units**, then **quote the final number with units** (η is dimensionless but you should mention "no units"). This is the KTU valuation key pattern.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Design:** When designing packed-bed reactors, we use η to convert intrinsic kinetic rate constants (from powdered catalyst lab data) to *apparent* rate constants valid for full-size pellets.
- **Catalyst Selection:** A catalyst that is very active (large $k$) but has small pores (small $D_e$) will have high φ and low η — most of the expensive surface area is *wasted* because reactants never reach it.
- **Pore Engineering:** To maximize η, we either reduce pellet size (lower $R$), create bimodal pore structures (large macropores for transport + small micropores for surface), or use egg-shell distributions.
- **Troubleshooting Industrial Reactors:** A drop in observed conversion at higher temperature (apparent activation energy = half the true value, ~40 kJ/mol vs ~80 kJ/mol) is the classic fingerprint of strong pore diffusion limitation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Concentration Profile inside a Spherical Catalyst

### Step 1 — Write the governing equation in dimensionless form

Starting from:

$$\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r^2 D_e \frac{dC_A}{dr} \right) = k C_A$$

Divide by $D_e$ and introduce **dimensionless variables**:

$$x = \frac{r}{R}, \qquad \psi = \frac{C_A}{C_{As}}$$

Then $\dfrac{dC_A}{dr} = \dfrac{C_{As}}{R} \dfrac{d\psi}{dx}$ and the equation becomes:

$$\frac{1}{x^2} \frac{d}{dx}\left( x^2 \frac{d\psi}{dx} \right) = \phi_s^2 \, \psi$$

where $\phi_s^2 = R^2 k / D_e$.

### Step 2 — Apply the substitution $\psi = u/x$

Let $u = \psi \, x$, so $\psi = u/x$. Then:

$$\frac{d\psi}{dx} = \frac{1}{x}\frac{du}{dx} - \frac{u}{x^2}$$

$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = \frac{1}{x}\frac{d^2u}{dx^2} - \frac{2}{x^2}\frac{du}{dx} + \frac{2u}{x^3}$$

Substitute into the governing equation. The terms simplify elegantly to:

$$\frac{1}{x}\frac{d^2u}{dx^2} - \frac{2}{x^2}\frac{du}{dx} + \frac{2u}{x^3} + \frac{2}{x^3}\frac{du}{dx}x - \frac{2u}{x^3} = \phi_s^2 \frac{u}{x}$$

After cancellation of the $2u/x^3$ terms:

$$\frac{d^2u}{dx^2} = \phi_s^2 \, u$$

### Step 3 — Solve the linear ODE

The general solution of $d^2u/dx^2 = \phi_s^2 u$ is:

$$u(x) = A \sinh(\phi_s x) + B \cosh(\phi_s x)$$

### Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions

**BC at $x = 0$:** $C_A$ is finite, so $\psi$ is finite; and $u = \psi x \to 0$ as $x \to 0$.

$$u(0) = A \sinh(0) + B \cosh(0) = B = 0 \implies B = 0$$

**BC at $x = 1$:** $\psi(1) = 1$, so $u(1) = 1$.

$$u(1) = A \sinh(\phi_s) = 1 \implies A = \frac{1}{\sinh(\phi_s)}$$

### Step 5 — Final concentration profile

$$\psi(x) = \frac{u(x)}{x} = \frac{\sinh(\phi_s x)}{x \, \sinh(\phi_s)}$$

$$C_A(r) = C_{As} \, \frac{\sinh\!\left(\phi_s \, r / R\right)}{(r/R) \, \sinh(\phi_s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous **Fogler / Levenspiel** concentration profile inside a spherical catalyst pellet.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Effectiveness Factor for a Sphere (First-Order)

### Step 1 — Compute the actual rate from the flux at the surface

The molar flux of $A$ at the outer surface $r = R$ is:

$$N_A \big|_{r=R} = -D_e \left. \frac{dC_A}{dr} \right|_{r=R} = -\frac{D_e C_{As}}{R} \left. \frac{d\psi}{dx} \right|_{x=1}$$

Differentiate $\psi = \sinh(\phi_s x) / [x \sinh(\phi_s)]$ using the quotient rule:

$$\frac{d\psi}{dx} = \frac{\phi_s \cosh(\phi_s x) \, x \, \sinh(\phi_s) - \sinh(\phi_s x) \, \sinh(\phi_s)}{x^2 \sinh^2(\phi_s)}$$

At $x = 1$:

$$\left.\frac{d\psi}{dx}\right|_{x=1} = \frac{\phi_s \cosh(\phi_s) - \sinh(\phi_s)}{\sinh(\phi_s)} = \phi_s \coth(\phi_s) - 1$$

### Step 2 — Total rate from the flux

$$\text{Actual rate} = 4\pi R^2 \cdot (-N_A) = 4\pi R^2 \cdot \frac{D_e C_{As}}{R} \left[\phi_s \coth(\phi_s) - 1\right]$$

$$= 4\pi R \, D_e \, C_{As} \, \left[\phi_s \coth(\phi_s) - 1\right]$$

### Step 3 — Rate if no diffusion resistance

$$\text{Rate at } C_{As} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \, k \, C_{As}$$

### Step 4 — Ratio gives the effectiveness factor

$$\eta = \frac{4\pi R D_e C_{As} \left[\phi_s \coth(\phi_s) - 1\right]}{\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 k C_{As}} = \frac{3 D_e \left[\phi_s \coth(\phi_s) - 1\right]}{R^2 k}$$

Using $\phi_s^2 = R^2 k / D_e$, i.e., $D_e / (R^2 k) = 1/\phi_s^2$:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{3}{\phi_s^2}\left[\phi_s \coth(\phi_s) - 1\right]}$$

## 3.3 Limiting Cases — Derivation of Asymptotes

### Case A: $\phi_s \to 0$ (Reaction-controlled, no diffusion limitation)

Use the Taylor expansion $\coth(z) = \dfrac{1}{z} + \dfrac{z}{3} - \dfrac{z^3}{45} + \cdots$

$$\phi_s \coth(\phi_s) \approx 1 + \frac{\phi_s^2}{3} - \frac{\phi_s^4}{45} + \cdots$$

$$\eta = \frac{3}{\phi_s^2}\left[\frac{\phi_s^2}{3} - \frac{\phi_s^4}{45} + \cdots\right] = 1 - \frac{\phi_s^2}{15} + \cdots$$

> As $\phi_s \to 0$, $\eta \to 1$: the entire pellet is utilized.

### Case B: $\phi_s \to \infty$ (Strong pore diffusion limitation)

$\coth(\phi_s) \to 1$ exponentially fast, so:

$$\eta \approx \frac{3}{\phi_s^2}(\phi_s - 1) \approx \frac{3}{\phi_s}$$

> As $\phi_s \to \infty$, $\eta \to 3/\phi_s$: only the outer "shell" reacts; the centre is starved of reactant.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A first-order reaction A → B takes place in a spherical catalyst of radius $R = 0.5$ cm. The effective diffusivity is $D_e = 0.01$ cm²/s and the intrinsic rate constant is $k = 1$ s⁻¹. Find the Thiele modulus and the effectiveness factor. Is there significant internal diffusion limitation?

**Step 1: Thiele modulus**

$$\phi_s = R \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_e}} = 0.5 \sqrt{\frac{1}{0.01}} = 0.5 \times 10 = 5$$

**Step 2: Effectiveness factor (exact)**

$\coth(5) = \dfrac{e^5 + e^{-5}}{e^5 - e^{-5}} = \dfrac{148.41 + 0.0067}{148.41 - 0.0067} \approx 1.00009$

$$\eta = \frac{3}{25}\left[5 \times 1.00009 - 1\right] = \frac{3}{25}(4.0005) = 0.480$$

**Step 3: Asymptotic check (large φ)**

$$\eta_{\text{approx}} = \frac{3}{\phi_s} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.600$$

The asymptotic overestimates because $\phi_s = 5$ is only *moderately* large; the exact value 0.48 is more accurate.

**Step 4: Comment**

$$\eta = 0.48 < 1 \implies \text{About 52\% of the catalyst volume is under-utilised.}$$

> This catalyst is **strongly diffusion-limited**. To improve performance, reduce pellet size (e.g., $R = 0.1$ cm → $\phi_s = 1$, $\eta \approx 0.94$) or use a bimodal pore structure to boost $D_e$.

## 3.5 Generalisation to nth-Order Reactions

For a reaction of order $n$ with rate $r = k C_A^n$, the Thiele modulus is defined as:

$$\phi_s = R \sqrt{\frac{k C_{As}^{n-1}}{D_e}}$$

The effectiveness factor–Thiele modulus relationship is **not** a single universal curve for $n \neq 1$. Numerical solution or approximate analytical solutions (Bischoff, 1965; Petersen, 1965) are used. Key limiting cases:

- For $n = 0$ (zero-order): $\eta = 1$ until a critical $\phi$, then drops sharply.
- For $n = 2$ (second-order): $\eta$ curve lies slightly *below* the first-order curve.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — The Thiele Modulus Engine

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the *information flow* of how tortuosity, geometry, and kinetics combine to produce the effectiveness factor:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pellet Geometry: Sphere of Radius R] --> C[Characteristic Length L = R]
    A --> B[Volume Vp = 4/3 pi R cubed]
    A --> F[External Surface Area Sp = 4 pi R squared]
    P[Porosity epsilon: 0.4 to 0.6] --> De[Effective Diffusivity De]
    T[Tortuosity tau: 2 to 7] --> De
    DAB[Bulk Molecular Diffusivity D AB] --> De
    K[Intrinsic Rate Constant k, order n] --> Phi[Thiele Modulus phi s]
    C --> Phi
    De --> Phi
    K --> Phi
    CAs[Bulk Surface Concentration C As] --> Phi
    Phi --> Eta[Effectiveness Factor eta]
    B --> Eta
    F --> Eta
    K --> Eta
    CAs --> Eta
    Eta --> Rate[Observed Rate = eta times k C As n times Vp]
    Eta --> WP[Weisz Prater Number Phi WP]
    C --> WP
    De --> WP
    CAs --> WP
    Rate --> Design[Packed Bed Reactor Design]
    WP --> Check{Phi WP less than 0.3?}
    Check -- Yes --> NoLimit[Internal diffusion negligible]
    Check -- No --> Limit[Strong pore diffusion limitation]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow — From Bulk Gas to Active Site

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BG[Bulk Gas Stream] -->|External Mass Transfer| OS[Outer Surface of Pellet]
    OS -->|Internal Diffusion through tortuous pores| CS[Centre of Pellet]
    CS --> AD[Adsorption on Active Site]
    AD --> RX[Surface Reaction]
    RX --> DS[Desorption of Product]
    DS -->|Internal Diffusion outward| OS
    OS -->|External Mass Transfer| BG
```

## 4.3 Thiele Modulus Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given: R, k, De, C As, n] --> CalcPhi[Calculate phi s]
    CalcPhi --> Compare{phi s value?}
    Compare -->|phi less than 0.5| Regime1[Reaction Controlled, eta approx 1]
    Compare -->|0.5 to 3| Regime2[Mixed Regime, Use full eta equation]
    Compare -->|phi greater than 3| Regime3[Diffusion Controlled, eta approx 3 by phi]
    Regime1 --> Design1[Reactor design straightforward]
    Regime2 --> Design2[Use numerical solution or charts]
    Regime3 --> Design3[Reduce R, increase De, or use egg shell catalyst]
```

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the Thiele modulus. What is its physical significance for a spherical catalyst pellet carrying out a first-order reaction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Thiele modulus for a first-order reaction in a spherical catalyst of radius $R$ is defined as:

$$\phi_s = R \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_e}}$$

where $k$ is the intrinsic first-order rate constant (s⁻¹) and $D_e$ is the effective diffusivity (m²/s) inside the porous pellet. It is a **dimensionless number** that compares the rate of chemical reaction to the rate of internal diffusion.

**Physical significance:**
- **Low φ (φ < 0.5):** Reaction is much slower than diffusion. The reactant penetrates uniformly, concentration profile is flat, and the entire pellet surface is utilized (η ≈ 1). The process is **reaction-controlled**.
- **High φ (φ > 3):** Reaction is much faster than diffusion. The reactant is consumed in a thin outer shell, the centre is starved, and η ≈ 3/φ. The process is **diffusion-controlled**.
- **Intermediate φ:** Both resistances are important, and the full η–φ relationship is used.

> **[Mark Distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark significance.]**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`
**Distinguish between molecular diffusivity ($D_{AB}$) and effective diffusivity ($D_e$). Why is the latter used in modelling reactions on porous catalysts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Molecular Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ | Effective Diffusivity $D_e$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Diffusivity of $A$ in bulk gas $B$ in open space | Diffusivity of $A$ inside the *porous* pellet |
| Path | Straight, unimpeded | Tortuous, through pores |
| Depends on | $T, P$, gas pair, only | $T, P$, gas pair, **and** pore structure |
| Magnitude | Higher (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ m²/s in gas) | Lower by factor $\varepsilon/\tau$ (typically $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-5}$ m²/s) |

**Reason $D_e$ is used in catalysis:** In a porous catalyst, reactant molecules cannot move in a straight line — they are forced to navigate a tortuous pore network that occupies only a fraction $\varepsilon$ of the pellet volume. The relationship is:

$$D_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, D_{AB}}{\tau}$$

This $D_e$ correctly captures both the reduced cross-section (via $\varepsilon$) and the longer path (via $\tau$) of transport in the real porous medium. It is the *only* diffusivity that, when used in Fick's law, gives the correct molar flux inside a porous catalyst pellet.

> **[Mark Distribution: 1 mark definitions, 1 mark relation, 1 mark reason.]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)
