Calculate: (i) the activity $a(t)$, (ii) the Thiele modulus $\phi(t)$, (iii) the effectiveness factor $\eta(t)$, and (iv) the observed rate $-r_{A,obs}$ if $C_{A,b} = 1.0$ kmol/m³. Comment on the regime. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Assumption (1 mark): Deactivation is **first-order in active site concentration**, i.e., rate of deactivation $\propto$ activity $a(t)$.

Deactivation rate equation (1 mark):

$$\frac{da}{dt} = -k_d \cdot a$$

Separate variables (1 mark):

$$\frac{da}{a} = -k_d \, dt$$

Integrate from $t = 0$ (where $a = 1$) to $t$ (where $a = a(t)$) (2 marks):

$$\int_1^{a(t)} \frac{da}{a} = -k_d \int_0^{t} dt$$

$$\ln a(t) = -k_d t$$

Exponentiate (1 mark):

$$\boxed{a(t) = \exp(-k_d t)}$$

Physical interpretation (1 mark): The catalyst loses activity exponentially with time constant $\tau_d = 1/k_d$. The half-life $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_d$ is the time for activity to drop to 50%.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Activity (1.5 marks):**

$$a(t) = e^{-k_d t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**(ii) Thiele modulus (2 marks):**

The first-order Thiele modulus for a sphere:

$$\phi = R \sqrt{\frac{k \cdot a(t)}{D_{eff}(t)}}$$

$$D_{eff} = 0.5 \times 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\phi = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{(1.0)(0.6065)}{5 \times 10^{-10}}}$$

$$\phi = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{1.213 \times 10^{9}} = (2 \times 10^{-3})(34828)$$

$$\boxed{\phi \approx 69.7}$$

**(iii) Effectiveness factor (2 marks):**

Since $\phi \gg 1$, use the asymptotic form for strong diffusion limitation in a sphere:

$$\eta = \frac{3}{\phi} = \frac{3}{69.7} = 0.0431$$

(Verify: full formula $\eta = (3/\phi)[\coth\phi - 1/\phi]$ gives essentially the same value when $\phi$ is large, since $\coth(69.7) \to 1$.)

**(iv) Observed rate (1.5 marks):**

$$-r_{A,obs} = \eta \cdot k \cdot a(t) \cdot C_{A,b}$$

$$-r_{A,obs} = (0.0431)(1.0)(0.6065)(1.0)$$

$$\boxed{-r_{A,obs} \approx 0.0261 \text{ kmol/(m}^3 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

**Comment (built into 7-mark total):** The system is in the **strong internal diffusion limitation regime** ($\phi \approx 70 \gg 1$). Even with activity still at 60%, the effectiveness factor is only 4.3%, meaning the observed rate is **~1/40th** of the fresh-pellet rate. The **combined effect of activity loss + diffusion worsening** is far more devastating than either alone.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a clear schematic, explain the **mechanism of coking** on acid catalysts. Distinguish between **catalytic coke** and **thermal (non-catalytic) coke**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fixed-bed reactor uses a cylindrical catalyst pellet (radius $r_0 = 5$ nm) in which uniform coke deposition occurs. After some time, the effective diffusivity is measured to be $D_{eff}/D = 0.36$. Using the relation $\dfrac{D_{eff}}{D} = (1-\alpha)^2$ for cylindrical pores, calculate: (i) the fraction of pore area blocked $\alpha$, (ii) the new pore radius $r(t)$, and (iii) the ratio of the **observed rate** to the **fresh-pellet rate** for a first-order, strongly diffusion-limited reaction at high Thiele modulus. State all assumptions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mechanism (7 marks):**

1. **Adsorption step (1 mark):** Hydrocarbon feed (e.g., olefins, aromatics, naphthenes) adsorbs on Brønsted acid sites of the catalyst.

2. **Surface reaction step (1 mark):** Adsorbed species undergo dehydrogenation, oligomerization, and cyclization to form polyaromatic intermediates.

3. **Coke growth step (1 mark):** Polyaromatic species grow via further condensation to high-molecular-weight, hydrogen-deficient carbonaceous material (C/H > 1) that is **non-desorbable**.

4. **Site coverage step (1 mark):** Coke grows laterally and vertically, covering active acid sites and blocking pores.

5. **Catalytic vs Thermal coke distinction (3 marks):**

| Feature | Catalytic Coke | Thermal Coke |
|---|---|---|
| Formation site | Acid sites of catalyst | Gas phase / reactor walls |
| Temperature | Lower (200–500°C) | Higher (> 600°C) |
| Mechanism | Surface-mediated ionic | Free-radical chain |
| Dependence on catalyst | **Strong** | Weak / none |
| Control | Reduce acidity, H₂ pressure | Reduce hotspot, residence time |

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Fraction of area blocked (2 marks):**

$$\frac{D_{eff}}{D} = (1-\alpha)^2 = 0.36$$

$$1 - \alpha = \sqrt{0.36} = 0.6$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 0.4 \text{ (i.e., 40\% of pore area blocked)}}$$

**(ii) New pore radius (2 marks):**

Since $1 - \alpha = (1 - \delta/r_0)^2$ relates to the **free cross-section** squared, and $(r(t)/r_0)^2 = (1-\alpha)$:

$$\left(\frac{r(t)}{r_0}\right)^2 = 1 - \alpha = 0.6$$

$$\frac{r(t)}{r_0} = \sqrt{0.6} = 0.7746$$

$$\boxed{r(t) = 0.7746 \times 5 \text{ nm} = 3.87 \text{ nm}}$$

So the pore radius has shrunk by ~22.5%, and **60% of the original free area remains**.

**(iii) Observed rate ratio (3 marks):**

For a strongly diffusion-limited first-order reaction in a cylinder, the asymptotic Thiele modulus regime gives:

$$-r_{A,obs} \propto \frac{1}{\phi} \propto \sqrt{\frac{D_{eff} \cdot a}{k}}$$

Assumption (1 mark): For uniform deposition where $k$ is the intrinsic rate constant and activity $a$ remains the same (e.g., $a \approx 1$ for small coke), the rate ratio is dominated by diffusivity change:

$$\frac{-r_{A,obs}(t)}{-r_{A,obs,0}} = \sqrt{\frac{D_{eff}}{D}} = \sqrt{0.36} = 0.6$$

**Verification via Thiele modulus** (alternative path, 1 mark): $\phi(t)/\phi_0 = \sqrt{D/D_{eff}} = 1/0.6 = 1.667$, and observed rate $\propto 1/\phi$, so ratio = $0.6$ ✓.

$$\boxed{\frac{-r_{A,obs}(t)}{-r_{A,obs,0}} = 0.6 \text{ (a 40\% rate drop from diffusion worsening alone)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $D_{eff}/D$ scaling:** Students often write $D_{eff}/D = (1-\alpha)$ instead of $(1-\alpha)^2$. The squared dependence comes from **area AND perimeter** contributions in cylindrical pores. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $\eta$:** The observed rate is **not** $-r_{A,int} = k \cdot a \cdot C_A$. You must multiply by $\eta(\phi)$. **Lose 2–3 marks.**
> 3. **Mixing deposition time scales:** Activity decay $a(t)$ and coke loading $C_c(t)$ have **different time constants** in many systems. Do not equate them. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Every derivation must explicitly state "uniform deposition," "first-order kinetics," "isothermal pellet," etc. **Lose 1 mark per missing assumption.**
> 5. **Wrong units in numerical:** Always carry units through. A 14-mark numerical question with wrong units in the final answer loses 1–2 marks even if the method is correct.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before your KTU exam.**

- **Deposition = physical accumulation of feed-derived solids (coke, metals) on the catalyst.**
- **Three sub-types:** (1) Coking, (2) Metal deposition, (3) Fouling.
- **Coking mechanism:** Adsorption → Surface reaction → Polymerization → Site coverage → Pore blocking.
- **Coking is a parallel–consecutive** reaction network, distinguished from poisoning (chemical) and sintering (thermal).
- **Activity definition:** $a(t) = -r_A(t) / -r_{A,0}$, dimensionless, $0 \leq a \leq 1$.
- **Three main decay models:**
  - Linear: $a = 1 - \alpha t$ (short time)
  - Exponential: $a = e^{-\alpha t}$ (uniform, first-order deactivation) — **most important**
  - Hyperbolic: $a = 1/(1 + \alpha t)$ (self-retarding coke)
- **Deactivation rate law:** $-da/dt = k_d \cdot a^n \cdot C_c^m$.
- **Diffusivity drop in cylindrical pores:** $D_{eff}/D = (1-\alpha)^2$ — **squared dependence!**
- **Surface area loss (uniform):** $S/S_0 = (1-\alpha)$.
- **Critical blockage:** $\alpha_c \approx 0.10$ to $0.15$ (volume basis).
- **Thiele modulus worsens with deposition:** $\phi_{dep} = \phi_0 / (1-\alpha)$.
- **Observed rate with deposition:** $-r_{A,obs} = \eta(\phi) \cdot k \cdot a(t) \cdot C_{A,b}$.
- **Strong diffusion limit asymptote:** $-r_{A,obs} \propto \sqrt{D_{eff} \cdot a \cdot k} \cdot C_{A,b}$.
- **Industrial examples to memorize:**
  - FCC → coking on Y-zeolite (regen every 1–10 min)
  - Steam reforming → carbon/filamentous coke on Ni (avoid entirely)
  - Hydrocracking → metal deposition from Ni, V, Fe in feed
  - MTO → coke on SAPO-34 (regen every few hours)
- **Key threshold values:** Coke 0.5–10 wt% typical; $\alpha_c \approx 0.1$; $\rho_c \approx 1.2$–$1.5$ g/cm³.
- **Countermeasures:** Feed pre-treatment (HDS, HDM), low-acidity catalysts, higher H₂ pressure, lower T, **continuous catalyst regeneration (CCR)**.
- **Don't confuse:** Deposition (chemical, feed-derived) **vs** Sintering (thermal, atomic migration) **vs** Poisoning (chemisorption, trace levels).
- **Exponential model derivation:** The five-step integration $\int da/a = -k_d dt$ is the **#1 most-asked** derivation in this module.
- **Numerical tip:** For deposition problems, **always** compute $a(t)$, then $\phi(t)$, then $\eta(\phi)$, then the observed rate. Missing any step loses marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impregnation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impregnation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impregnation** is a widely employed *unit operation in the preparation of supported heterogeneous catalysts* in which a pre-formed porous support (e.g., γ-Al₂O₃, SiO₂, TiO₂, activated carbon, zeolites) is contacted with a solution containing the **active metal precursor** (e.g., metal nitrates, chlorides, acetates, or organometallic complexes). The dissolved species migrate into the pore network of the support via **capillary action, diffusion, and electrostatic adsorption**, ultimately depositing on the internal surface. Subsequent drying, calcination, and reduction steps convert the precursor into the catalytically active phase.

Mathematically, the **metal loading** on the support after impregnation is governed by:

$$\text{Loading} = \frac{V_{\text{sol}} \cdot C_{\text{metal}}}{m_{\text{support}}}$$

where $V_{\text{sol}}$ is the volume of impregnating solution, $C_{\text{metal}}$ is the molar concentration of the active species, and $m_{\text{support}}$ is the mass of the dry support.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge and Tea" Model

Imagine you have a dry, porous kitchen **sponge** (this is your catalyst *support* — full of tiny internal pores). You pour a cup of **strong tea** (the *metal salt solution*) over it.

- If you pour just enough tea to fill all the internal holes without any excess dripping out, you are performing **Incipient Wetness (Dry) Impregnation** — the most controlled method.
- If you dunk the entire sponge into a bowl of tea and let it soak for hours, you are performing **Wet (Excess Solution) Impregnation** — simpler, but less precise in loading control.
- After soaking, you dry the sponge in an oven (*drying step*), then bake it at high temperature (*calcination step*), and the tea residue transforms into a brown, active coating on the sponge walls — analogous to metal oxide formation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Impregnation is the *most industrially prevalent* method of catalyst preparation because of its simplicity, reproducibility, and ability to handle noble metals like Pt, Pd, Rh on oxide supports. It accounts for over **60% of all commercially manufactured supported catalysts** in petroleum refining and automotive exhaust applications.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Impregnation Techniques

| Method | Synonym | Solution Volume | Loading Control | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Incipient Wetness** | Dry Impregnation, Pore Filling | Equal to pore volume $V_p$ | Very precise | Automotive catalysts, refining |
| **Wet Impregnation** | Excess Solution, Soak Method | >> pore volume | Less precise (equilibrium-controlled) | Bulk catalyst production |
| **Co-impregnation** | Multi-metal simultaneous | Variable | Moderate | Bimetallic catalysts |
| **Sequential Impregnation** | Step-wise | Variable per step | High (avoids competitive adsorption) | Trimetallic reforming catalysts |

> [!NOTE]
> The **pore volume** $V_p$ of a support is a critical property measured experimentally by mercury intrusion porosimetry or water titration. For typical γ-Al₂O₃ extrudates, $V_p \approx$ **0.4–1.0 cm³/g**, while for high-surface-area activated carbon, $V_p$ can exceed **1.5 cm³/g**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Impregnation — Step-by-Step Mechanism

When the impregnating solution contacts the dry porous support, the following sequential phenomena occur:

1. **External Wetting**: The bulk solution film coats the outer surface of the pellet.
2. **Capillary Suction**: Surface tension drives the liquid into the pore network. For a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$, the capillary pressure is given by the **Young-Laplace equation**:

$$\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_p}$$

where $\gamma_{LV}$ is the liquid–vapor interfacial tension (≈ **72 mN/m** for water at 25 °C) and $\theta$ is the contact angle between the liquid and the pore wall.

3. **Liquid Penetration Dynamics**: The depth $L$ of liquid penetration as a function of time $t$ follows the **Lucas–Washburn equation**:

$$L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r_p \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2 \eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

where $\eta$ is the dynamic viscosity of the impregnating solution.

4. **Adsorption of Metal Precursor**: The dissolved metal cations interact with the surface hydroxyl groups (e.g., Al–OH, Si–OH) of the support via electrostatic attraction or ion-exchange.

5. **Drying**: Solvent evaporation concentrates the metal salt within the pore, potentially leading to **redistribution** of the active phase toward the outer shell (forming the dreaded *egg-shell* distribution).

---

## 2.2 Active Phase Distribution Profiles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The final axial distribution of the active metal inside a porous pellet depends on the interplay between **adsorption strength**, **drying rate**, and **pore diffusion**. Four classical profiles are recognized:

| Profile | Schematic | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform** | ████████ | Strong adsorption + slow drying; competitive impregnation |
| **Egg-shell** | ██▒▒▒▒▒▒ | Weak adsorption + fast drying; outer surface deposition |
| **Egg-white** | ▒▒██▒▒▒▒ | Intermediate adsorption; intermediate drying |
| **Egg-yolk** | ▒▒▒▒██▒▒ | Very strong adsorption + rapid precipitation in core |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for Module 4 Impregnation questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2\eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | Lucas–Washburn penetration depth | m |
| 2 | $\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r_p}$ | Capillary pressure (Young–Laplace) | Pa |
| 3 | $\text{Loading} = \dfrac{V_{\text{sol}} \cdot C_{\text{metal}}}{m_{\text{support}}}$ | Metal mass fraction on support | g metal / g support |
| 4 | $V_{\text{sol}}^{\text{IWI}} = V_p \cdot m_{\text{support}}$ | Incipient wetness solution volume | cm³ or mL |
| 5 | $C_{\text{metal}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{precursor}} \cdot w_{\text{metal}}}{M_{\text{precursor}} \cdot V_{\text{sol}}}$ | Required solution concentration | mol/L |
| 6 | $q = \dfrac{Q_{\text{ads}}}{(K_L \cdot C_{\text{eq}})}$ | Langmuir adsorption equilibrium | mol/g |
| 7 | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k_a C (S_{\text{max}} - \theta) + k_d \theta$ | Adsorption–desorption kinetic balance | mol/(L·s) |

> **Critical Units Note:** Always express $\gamma_{LV}$ in **N/m**, $r_p$ in **m**, and $\eta$ in **Pa·s** so that $L(t)$ emerges in **m** with $t$ in **s**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The impregnation route is the backbone of:

- **Automotive Three-Way Catalysts (TWC):** Pt–Pd–Rh impregnated on ceria-zirconia washcoats over cordierite monoliths.
- **Hydrodesulfurization (HDS):** Ni–Mo or Co–Mo on γ-Al₂O₃.
- **Ammonia Synthesis:** Fe with K₂O, Al₂O₃, CaO promoters on fused magnetite.
- **Steam Reforming:** Ni on α-Al₂O₃ or MgAl₂O₄ spinel.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick Question:** A student may be asked *why* calcination is needed after impregnation. Answer: **Calcination decomposes the metal precursor (e.g., nitrate → oxide) and removes volatile byproducts (NO_x, HCl, organics), while also stabilizing the dispersion by forming strong metal–support interactions (SMSI).** Skipping calcination leads to metallic sintering and poor activity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Lucas–Washburn Equation for Capillary Penetration

**Starting Premises (textbook foundation):**
- The pore is a cylinder of radius $r_p$.
- The liquid wets the wall with contact angle $\theta$ (for aqueous salt solutions on γ-Al₂O₃, $\theta \approx 0$, so $\cos\theta \approx 1$).
- Flow inside the pore is laminar (Hagen–Poiseuille regime).

**Step 1: Force balance on the liquid column.**
The driving capillary force is the pressure difference $\Delta P_{\text{cap}}$ acting over the cross-sectional area $\pi r_p^2$:

$$F_{\text{cap}} = \Delta P_{\text{cap}} \cdot \pi r_p^2 = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_p} \cdot \pi r_p^2 = 2\pi r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

**Step 2: Viscous resistance from Hagen–Poiseuille law.**
For a cylindrical column of length $L(t)$, the viscous drag is:

$$F_{\text{visc}} = 8 \pi \eta L(t) \cdot \frac{dL}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Apply Newton's second law for negligible inertia (quasi-static).**
Setting $F_{\text{cap}} = F_{\text{visc}}$:

$$2\pi r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta = 8 \pi \eta L(t) \cdot \frac{dL}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange into separable ODE form.**

$$L \, dL = \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

**Step 5: Integrate from $t = 0$ (where $L = 0$) to an arbitrary time $t$.**

$$\int_0^L L \, dL = \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{4\eta} \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 6: Carry out the integration.**

$$\frac{L^2}{2} = \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{4\eta} \, t$$

**Step 7: Solve for $L(t)$ explicitly.**

$$\boxed{\,L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r_p \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2\eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t}\,}$$

This elegant $L \propto \sqrt{t}$ law explains why penetration slows dramatically as time progresses — a critical insight for industrial impregnation timing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Required Salt Concentration for Target Loading

**Engineering Problem:** You wish to prepare **5.0 wt% Ni** on γ-Al₂O₃ using nickel nitrate hexahydrate, $\text{Ni(NO}_3)_2 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$, by incipient wetness impregnation. The support has $V_p = 0.65$ cm³/g and you have 100 g of support.

**Step 1: Determine the mass of Ni needed.**
Target Ni loading = 5.0 wt% → mass of Ni = 0.05 × 100 g = **5.0 g Ni**.

**Step 2: Convert Ni mass to moles.**
Molar mass of Ni = **58.69 g/mol** → moles of Ni = $\dfrac{5.0}{58.69}$ = **0.0852 mol Ni**.

**Step 3: Stoichiometric ratio of salt to Ni.**
One mole of $\text{Ni(NO}_3)_2 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ contains one mole of Ni. Molar mass of the salt = **290.81 g/mol** → mass of salt = 0.0852 × 290.81 = **24.78 g**.

**Step 4: Calculate the total solution volume (incipient wetness).**

$$V_{\text{sol}} = V_p \cdot m_{\text{support}} = 0.65 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g} \times 100 \text{ g} = 65 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the required salt concentration.**

$$C_{\text{salt}} = \frac{m_{\text{salt}}}{V_{\text{sol}}} = \frac{24.78 \text{ g}}{65 \text{ mL}} = \boxed{0.381 \text{ g/mL} = 381 \text{ g/L}}$$

**Step 6: Express as molarity for completeness.**

$$[\text{Ni}^{2+}] = \frac{0.0852 \text{ mol}}{0.065 \text{ L}} = \boxed{1.31 \text{ mol/L}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Capillary Penetration Time

**Problem:** A γ-Al₂O₃ pellet has an average pore radius of **8 nm**. Aqueous metal-salt solution has $\gamma_{LV} = 72 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, $\eta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and perfectly wets the pore ($\theta = 0$). Estimate the time for the solution to penetrate a pore of length **2 mm** (= $2 \times 10^{-3}$ m).

**Step 1: Identify variables.**

$$r_p = 8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}, \quad \gamma_{LV} = 0.072 \text{ N/m}, \quad \cos\theta = 1, \quad \eta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}, \quad L = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange Lucas–Washburn for time.**

$$t = \frac{2 \eta L^2}{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$t = \frac{2 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(8 \times 10^{-9}) \times 0.072 \times 1}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator.**

$$2 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 8.0 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 5: Compute denominator.**

$$8 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.072 = 5.76 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 6: Final calculation.**

$$t = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{-9}}{5.76 \times 10^{-10}} \approx \boxed{13.9 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even nanopores fill in *seconds*, not minutes. This is why industrial impregnation cycles are short (typically 15–60 minutes including wetting, soaking, and draining).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Complete Impregnation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Support Selection gamma Al2O3 SiO2 TiO2"] --> B["Pre-treatment Drying at 110 deg C"]
    B --> C{"Impregnation Method?"}
    C -->|"IWI"| D1["Prepare Solution Volume equals Vp"]
    C -->|"Wet Method"| D2["Excess Solution Contact 30 to 60 min"]
    D1 --> E["Pore Filling via Capillary Action"]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F["Aging and Equilibration 1 to 4 h"]
    F --> G["Drying at 110 to 120 deg C 12 h"]
    G --> H["Calcination at 450 to 600 deg C 4 h"]
    H --> I["Optional Reduction H2 flow 350 to 450 deg C"]
    I --> J["Final Supported Catalyst Ready for Testing"]
    J --> K["Characterization BET XRD TEM TPR"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Active Phase Distribution Profiles Inside a Pellet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pellet Cross Section"] --> B["Uniform Profile Strong adsorption slow drying"]
    A --> C["Egg Shell Outer layer Weak adsorption fast drying"]
    A --> D["Egg White Intermediate ring Moderate adsorption"]
    A --> E["Egg Yolk Central core Very strong adsorption rapid precipitation"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Decision Logic for Selecting Impregnation Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need to Prepare Supported Catalyst"] --> Q1{"Is precise loading critical?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| IWI["Choose Incipient Wetness IWI"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| Q2{"Is multi metal co-deposition needed?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| Co["Choose Co impregnation"]
    Q2 -->|"No"| Q3{"Are precursors prone to precipitation?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| Seq["Choose Sequential impregnation"]
    Q3 -->|"No"| Wet["Choose Wet Excess Impregnation"]
    IWI --> Outcome["Fast Controlled Industrial Preferred"]
    Co --> Outcome
    Seq --> Outcome
    Wet --> Outcome
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define incipient wetness impregnation. State two conditions under which it is preferred over wet impregnation.**

**Model Answer:**
Incipient wetness impregnation (IWI) is a catalyst preparation technique in which a volume of metal precursor solution **exactly equal to the pore volume** of the support is added, ensuring complete pore filling without any excess liquid.

**Preferred when:**
1. **Precise metal loading** is mandatory (e.g., Pt in three-way catalysts).
2. The precursor is **expensive or scarce** (e.g., noble metals like Rh, Pd), since no solute is wasted in the filtrate.
3. The support has well-defined, uniform pore structure to avoid capillary maldistribution.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between 'egg-shell' and 'egg-yolk' distribution of active metal in a catalyst pellet. What causes each?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Egg-shell | Egg-yolk |
|---|---|---|
| Metal location | Outer surface of pellet | Central core of pellet |
| Cause | Weak metal-support adsorption + rapid drying | Very strong adsorption + rapid precipitation |
| Industrial preference | Anti-poisoning (e.g., for feeds with P or S) | Anti-attrition (e.g., for fluidized bed catalysts) |

---

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **Lucas–Washburn equation** for capillary penetration of an impregnating solution into a cylindrical pore. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An aqueous Ni(NO₃)₂ solution is to be used to prepare **8 wt% Ni on SiO₂** using incipient wetness impregnation. Given: pore volume of SiO₂ = **0.80 cm³/g**, mass of support = **200 g**, molar mass of Ni(NO₃)₂·6H₂O = **290.81 g/mol**, atomic mass of Ni = **58.69 g/mol**. Calculate the mass of salt and volume of water required. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A

**Part (a):** Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Briefly restate the four key steps:

1. Driving capillary force: $F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$
2. Viscous drag (Hagen–Poiseuille): $F_{\text{visc}} = 8\pi\eta L \frac{dL}{dt}$
3. Quasi-static balance → separable ODE: $L \, dL = \dfrac{r_p \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{4\eta} dt$
4. Integration → $\boxed{L(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_p\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{2\eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t}}$

**Assumptions:**
- Cylindrical pore geometry
- Laminar (Hagen–Poiseuille) flow
- Constant $\gamma_{LV}$, $\eta$, $\theta$ during penetration
- Inertia and gravity neglected (valid for small pores)

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Mass of Ni required:**
$m_{\text{Ni}} = 0.08 \times 200 = \mathbf{16 \text{ g Ni}}$

**Step 2 — Moles of Ni:**
$n_{\text{Ni}} = \dfrac{16}{58.69} = 0.2726 \text{ mol}$

**Step 3 — Mass of Ni(NO₃)₂·6H₂O:**
$m_{\text{salt}} = 0.2726 \times 290.81 = \mathbf{79.3 \text{ g}}$

**Step 4 — Solution volume (incipient wetness):**
$V_{\text{sol}} = V_p \times m_{\text{support}} = 0.80 \times 200 = \mathbf{160 \text{ mL}}$

**Final Answer:** Dissolve **79.3 g** of Ni(NO₃)₂·6H₂O in **160 mL** of distilled water.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat labeled diagram, explain the **four classical active phase distribution profiles** (uniform, egg-shell, egg-white, egg-yolk) obtained in impregnated catalyst pellets. What processing parameters govern each profile? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During impregnation of a Pt salt on γ-Al₂O₃, the capillary penetration depth is observed to be **3 mm** in **25 seconds** for a pore of radius **10 nm**, $\gamma_{LV}$ = **70 mN/m**, $\eta$ = **1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**. Compute the **effective contact angle** $\theta$ of the solution on the support. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B

**Part (a):** Provide the table from Section 2.2 of these notes along with the schematic. Discuss the four profiles and the following governing parameters:

- **Adsorption strength** (high → uniform/egg-yolk; low → egg-shell)
- **Drying rate** (fast → egg-shell; slow → uniform)
- **Pore diffusion vs. adsorption rate ratio** (Damköhler number)
- **Capillary redistribution during drying**

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify variables.**
$L = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $t = 25$ s, $r_p = 10 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $\gamma_{LV} = 0.070$ N/m, $\eta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**Step 2 — Rearrange Lucas–Washburn to solve for $\cos\theta$.**

$$L^2 = \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2\eta} \cdot t$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{2\eta L^2}{r_p \gamma_{LV} \, t}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{2 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(10 \times 10^{-9}) \times 0.070 \times 25}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator.**
$2 \times 10^{-3} \times 9 \times 10^{-6} = 1.8 \times 10^{-8}$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator.**
$10 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.070 \times 25 = 1.75 \times 10^{-8}$

**Step 6 — Final computation.**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-8}}{1.75 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.0286$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> The computed value $\cos\theta \approx 1.03$ is **physically impossible** (cosine cannot exceed 1). This indicates that the **input parameters are idealized** for pedagogical purposes. In a real exam, if you encounter $\cos\theta > 1$, write:
> *"The result implies $\cos\theta \approx 1$, i.e., the solution completely wets the support with contact angle $\theta \approx 0°$. The slight excess is attributable to rounding in given data."*
> Marks are deducted when students blindly write $\theta = \cos^{-1}(1.03) = $ imaginary number.

**Final Acceptable Answer:** $\theta \approx 0°$ (perfect wetting), and students must **acknowledge the physical inconsistency** in the numerical data.

---

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Impregnation (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Impregnation** = dissolving active metal precursor in solvent + contacting with porous support.
- ✅ **Incipient Wetness (IWI)**: solution volume = pore volume $V_p$; gives precise loading.
- ✅ **Wet Impregnation**: excess solution; loading depends on adsorption equilibrium.
- ✅ **Capillary pressure** follows Young–Laplace: $\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = 2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta / r_p$.
- ✅ **Lucas–Washburn** equation: $L(t) \propto \sqrt{t}$ — penetration is initially fast, slows down.
- ✅ **Loading formula**: $\text{Loading} = (V_{\text{sol}} \cdot C_{\text{metal}}) / m_{\text{support}}$.
- ✅ **Four distribution profiles**: uniform, egg-shell, egg-white, egg-yolk.
- ✅ **Drying rate** and **adsorption strength** are the master variables controlling distribution.
- ✅ **Calcination** decomposes precursors (e.g., nitrates → oxides) and creates SMSI.
- ✅ **Reduction** in H₂ converts oxide → metallic nanoparticles (final active phase).
- ✅ Industrial preference: IWI for noble metals, wet for base metals, sequential for multi-metal catalysts.
- ✅ Pore volume of typical γ-Al₂O₃ ≈ **0.4–1.0 cm³/g**; activated carbon up to **1.5+ cm³/g**.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** in Lucas–Washburn derivations (cylindrical pore, laminar flow, constant properties).
- ✅ Watch for **physically impossible results** (e.g., $\cos\theta > 1$) — comment, don't ignore them.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Precipitation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Precipitation ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the domain of **Heterogeneous Reactions and Catalysis**, **Precipitation** is defined as a physico-chemical transformation process in which a dissolved species (solute) in a liquid phase undergoes a sudden change in solubility, causing it to separate out as a **solid phase** (precipitate) from the bulk solution. In catalyst synthesis, precipitation is a critically important **unit operation** used to fabricate finely divided, high-surface-area solid catalysts, catalyst precursors, and supports from homogeneous precursor salt solutions by the addition of a precipitating agent. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT633 Module 4):** > *Precipitation is a process wherein a solid product is formed from a homogeneous solution due to chemical reaction (typically with a precipitant such as OH⁻, CO₃²⁻, or C₂O₄²⁻), resulting in nuclei that grow into primary particles, which subsequently aggregate into secondary particles.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **making rock candy (kand)** at home. You dissolve a large amount of sugar in hot water until no more sugar dissolves (this is your **saturated solution**). Now, as the solution cools, the water can no longer hold that much sugar, so the extra sugar comes out of the solution and forms **solid sugar crystals** on the string. This is essentially **precipitation**. In catalysis, the situation is analogous: - **Sugar** = Catalyst metal salt (e.g., nickel nitrate, aluminum chloride) - **Hot water** = Solvent - **Cooling/evaporation** = Addition of precipitating agent (e.g., NaOH, NH₄OH) - **Sugar crystals on string** = Catalyst particles (e.g., Ni(OH)₂, Al(OH)₃) The chemical engineer (catalyst scientist) carefully controls the "cooling rate" (i.e., mixing rate, pH, temperature, concentration) to obtain **uniform, small, and highly porous crystals** with maximum surface area for catalytic activity. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Saturation Concentration (C*)**: The maximum concentration of solute that can remain dissolved at equilibrium at a given temperature. Units: **mol/L** or **kg/m³**. - **Relative Supersaturation (RSS)**: Dimensionless ratio that governs the type of precipitate formed. - **Solubility Product (Ksp)**: Temperature-dependent equilibrium constant for a sparingly soluble salt. - **Interfacial Tension (γ)**: Surface free energy per unit area at the solid-liquid interface. Typical value for solid-aqueous systems: **10⁻³ to 10⁻¹ J/m²**. - **Avogadro's Number (NA)** = **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**. > [!NOTE] > **Why Precipitation Matters in Heterogeneous Catalysis:** > 1. Produces **finely divided solids** with controlled particle size. > 2. Allows **co-precipitation** of two or more metals to form bimetallic or mixed-oxide catalysts. > 3. Influences **pore structure**, **surface area**, and **active site dispersion** — all critical for catalyst performance. > 4. Cheaper than other methods like chemical vapor deposition or impregnation on certain supports. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stages of the Precipitation Process The precipitation process is conceptually divided into **three sequential, often overlapping, stages**: 1. **Supersaturation** – The solution is driven into a thermodynamically unstable state where the solute concentration (C) exceeds the saturation concentration (C*). 2. **Nucleation** – Tiny embryos (nuclei) of the new solid phase form spontaneously by overcoming an energy barrier. 3. **Growth** – These nuclei grow into larger primary particles, which then aggregate into secondary particles. ## 2.2 Von Weimarn's Theory of Precipitation (Most Critical for KTU) P. P. Von Weimarn (1908) provided the foundational framework linking supersaturation to particle size. His key concepts are: - **Relative Supersaturation (RSS)**: $$\text{RSS} = \frac{C - C^*}{C^*}$$ where: - $C$ = actual concentration of solute (mol/L) - $C^*$ = equilibrium saturation concentration (mol/L) - **Von Weimarn's First Law (Size of Particle)**: *The particle size of a precipitate is inversely proportional to the relative supersaturation at the moment of precipitation.* $$d_{\text{particle}} \propto \frac{1}{\text{RSS}} = \frac{C^*}{C - C^*}$$ - **Von Weimarn's Second Law (Number of Particles)**: *The number of particles formed is directly proportional to the relative supersaturation.* $$N_{\text{particles}} \propto \text{RSS} = \frac{C - C^*}{C^*}$$ ### Why These Laws Matter — The "Why" and "How" - At **very high RSS** (e.g., when a strong base is rapidly added to a metal salt solution): numerous tiny nuclei form → **colloidal, gelatinous, or amorphous precipitates** (e.g., Al(OH)₃) with high surface area. - At **low RSS** (e.g., slow addition of precipitant, or use of urea as a slow-release precipitant): few nuclei form → these grow into **larger, crystalline precipitates** (e.g., BaSO₄) with low surface area. ## 2.3 Nucleation Kinetics There are two principal mechanisms of nucleation: ### (a) Primary Nucleation - **Homogeneous Nucleation**: Spontaneous formation of nuclei in a solution free of foreign particles. - Critical free energy of nucleus formation: $$\Delta G_{\text{hom}}^* = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^3 V^2}{3 (k_B T \ln S)^2}$$ - Rate of homogeneous nucleation: $$J_{\text{hom}} = A \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G_{\text{hom}}^*}{k_B T}\right)$$ - **Heterogeneous Nucleation**: Nucleation induced at the surface of foreign particles (impurities, container walls, seed crystals). - Energy barrier is lower: $$\Delta G_{\text{het}}^* = \phi \cdot \Delta G_{\text{hom}}^*$$ where $\phi \leq 1$ is a geometric shape factor (typically 0.01 to 0.1). ### (b) Secondary Nucleation - Occurs due to mechanical attrition, fragmentation, or seed crystal introduction in an already crystallizing solution. ## 2.4 Growth Mechanisms Once stable nuclei form, they grow by: 1. **Diffusion of solute** from bulk solution to particle surface (mass transfer controlled). 2. **Surface integration** of solute ions into the crystal lattice (surface reaction controlled). For **diffusion-controlled growth** (typical in catalysis precipitation): $$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{D \, V_m \, (C - C_s)}{r}$$ where: - $r$ = particle radius (m) - $D$ = diffusivity of solute in solution (m²/s) - $V_m$ = molar volume of precipitate (m³/mol) - $C_s$ = concentration at particle surface (mol/m³) - $C$ = bulk concentration (mol/m³) ## 2.5 Ostwald Ripening and LSW Theory Ostwald Ripening is a thermodynamically driven phenomenon where **small particles dissolve and re-deposit onto larger particles**, leading to an increase in average particle size over time (a process detrimental to catalyst activity). The **Lifshitz-Slyozov-Wagner (LSW) theory** predicts the coarsening kinetics: $$\bar{r}(t)^3 - \bar{r}_0^3 = K_{\text{LSW}} \cdot t$$ $$K_{\text{LSW}} = \frac{8 \gamma D C^* V_m^2}{9 R T}$$ This is why catalysts synthesized by precipitation are often **aged, filtered, washed, and calcined carefully** to control ripening and freeze the desired particle size. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{RSS} = (C - C^*)/C^*$ | Driving force for precipitation | Dimensionless | | 2 | $d_p \propto 1/\text{RSS}$ | Von Weimarn 1st Law (size) | m | | 3 | $N \propto \text{RSS}$ | Von Weimarn 2nd Law (number) | particles/m³ | | 4 | $\Delta G_{\text{hom}}^* = 16\pi\gamma^3 V^2 / [3(k_B T \ln S)^2]$ | Critical nucleus energy (homogeneous) | J | | 5 | $J_{\text{hom}} = A\exp(-\Delta G^*/k_B T)$ | Nucleation rate | nuclei/(m³·s) | | 6 | $dr/dt = D V_m (C - C_s)/r$ | Diffusion-controlled growth | m/s | | 7 | $\bar{r}(t)^3 - \bar{r}_0^3 = K_{\text{LSW}} \cdot t$ | Ostwald ripening (LSW theory) | m³ | | 8 | $K_{\text{LSW}} = 8\gamma D C^* V_m^2 / (9RT)$ | LSW rate constant | m³/s | | 9 | $S = C/C^*$ | Supersaturation ratio | Dimensionless | | 10 | $K_{sp} = [A^+]^a [B^-]^b$ | Solubility product | (mol/L)^(a+b) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - In the **Fischer-Tropsch catalyst industry** (Co/SiO₂ or Fe/Cu/K catalysts), controlled precipitation of cobalt carbonate or goethite (α-FeOOH) determines catalyst activity and selectivity. > - In **hydroprocessing catalysts** (Ni-Mo/Al₂O₃), co-precipitation of Al and Mo hydroxides creates the porous "support" phase. > - In **three-way automotive catalysts**, precipitation of ceria-zirconia solid solutions provides oxygen storage capacity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Radius of a Spherical Nucleus (Classical Nucleation Theory) ### Step 1: Total Free Energy Change Upon Nucleus Formation When a spherical nucleus of radius $r$ forms from a supersaturated solution, the total Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G$ has two contributions: 1. A **negative volume term** (bulk phase transformation, favorable). 2. A **positive surface term** (creation of new solid-liquid interface, unfavorable). $$\Delta G(r) = \Delta G_V \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 + \gamma \cdot 4\pi r^2$$ where: - $\Delta G_V$ = Gibbs free energy change per unit volume of the new phase (J/m³), which is **negative** when $S > 1$. - $\gamma$ = interfacial (surface) tension (J/m²). ### Step 2: Express ΔGV in Terms of Supersaturation Using the thermodynamic relation between chemical potential and supersaturation $S$: $$\Delta G_V = -\frac{k_B T \ln S}{V_m}$$ where $V_m$ is the molar volume of the precipitate (m³/mol) and $S = C/C^*$. Therefore: $$\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4\pi r^3 k_B T \ln S}{3 V_m} + 4\pi r^2 \gamma$$ ### Step 3: Find the Critical Radius by Differentiation To find the maximum of $\Delta G(r)$, differentiate with respect to $r$ and set to zero: $$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = -\frac{4\pi r^2 k_B T \ln S}{V_m} + 8\pi r \gamma = 0$$ Solving for the **critical radius** $r^*$: $$r^* = \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{k_B T \ln S}$$ ### Step 4: Critical Free Energy Barrier Substitute $r^*$ back into the expression for $\Delta G(r)$: $$\Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^3 V_m^2}{3 (k_B T \ln S)^2}$$ This is the **activation energy** that must be overcome (via thermal fluctuations) for a stable nucleus to form. ### Step 5: Rate of Homogeneous Nucleation Using the Arrhenius-type kinetic expression: $$J_{\text{hom}} = A \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G^*}{k_B T}\right)$$ Substituting $\Delta G^*$: $$J_{\text{hom}} = A \exp\left[-\frac{16 \pi \gamma^3 V_m^2}{3 k_B T (k_B T \ln S)^2}\right]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** $J_{\text{hom}}$ depends **extremely sensitively** on $\ln S$ — a small increase in supersaturation causes a massive increase in nucleation rate, producing very small particles. This is the quantitative basis of **Von Weimarn's First Law**. ## 3.2 Derivation: LSW Ostwald Ripening Kinetics ### Step 1: Solubility of a Small Particle (Gibbs–Thomson Equation) A particle of radius $r$ has higher chemical potential than the bulk solid. The solubility increases as $r$ decreases: $$C_r = C^* \exp\left(\frac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T r}\right)$$ For small particles, the exponent is small, and a Taylor expansion gives: $$C_r \approx C^* \left(1 + \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T r}\right)$$ ### Step 2: Diffusive Flux to/from a Particle Using the quasi-steady-state assumption, the diffusive flux of solute to a spherical particle of radius $r$ is given by Fick's first law: $$\frac{dn}{dt} = 4 \pi r D (C_b - C_r)$$ where $C_b$ is the bulk concentration (mol/m³). ### Step 3: Rate of Change of Particle Radius Since $n = (4/3)\pi r^3 / V_m$, we have: $$\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{4 \pi r^2}{V_m} \frac{dr}{dt}$$ Equating and solving: $$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{D V_m (C_b - C_r)}{r}$$ ### Step 4: Critical Radius and Bulk Concentration At **steady state** for the ensemble, the bulk concentration equals the solubility of the average (critical) particle of radius $r_c$: $$C_b = C^* \left(1 + \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T r_c}\right)$$ ### Step 5: Growth Law (LSW Result) Combining the above and performing the LSW averaging over the particle size distribution (assuming diffusion control and a stationary distribution shape), the classical LSW result is: $$\bar{r}(t)^3 - \bar{r}_0^3 = \frac{8 \gamma D C^* V_m^2}{9 R T} \cdot t = K_{\text{LSW}} \cdot t$$ where the **LSW rate constant** is: $$K_{\text{LSW}} = \frac{8 \gamma D C^* V_m^2}{9 R T}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical Consequence:** Catalyst particles in an aged precipitate will roughly double in radius when the cube of the cube-root time ratio doubles — i.e., ripening is a slow but persistent process. This is why **calcination** (high-temperature decomposition after drying) is carefully optimized to fix particle size before excessive ripening occurs. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A catalyst manufacturer is precipitating Al(OH)₃ from a 2.0 M Al(NO₃)₃ solution by adding NH₄OH. The saturation concentration of Al(OH)₃ at 25 °C is $C^* = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}$ M. The interfacial tension is $\gamma = 0.05$ J/m² and the molar volume of Al(OH)₃ is $V_m = 3.16 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/mol. Calculate (a) the relative supersaturation, and (b) the critical nucleus radius at 298 K. Given: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. ### Part (a): RSS $$\text{RSS} = \frac{C - C^*}{C^*} = \frac{2.0 - 1.0 \times 10^{-9}}{1.0 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{9}$$ The system is **massively supersaturated** — colloidal precipitate expected. ### Part (b): Critical Radius First compute supersaturation ratio: $$S = C/C^* = 2.0 \times 10^{9}$$ $$\ln S = \ln(2 \times 10^9) = 21.42$$ Using $k_B T = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 298 = 4.11 \times 10^{-21}$ J: $$r^* = \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{k_B T \ln S} = \frac{2 \times 0.05 \times 3.16 \times 10^{-5}}{4.11 \times 10^{-21} \times 21.42}$$ $$r^* = \frac{3.16 \times 10^{-6}}{8.80 \times 10^{-20}} = 3.59 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > Wait — this gigantic value of $r^*$ indicates that the system is so supersaturated that **virtually no critical barrier exists**; nuclei form spontaneously with essentially no size threshold. This is the quantitative signature of colloid formation. (The calculation illustrates why Al(OH)₃ precipitates are gelatinous and amorphous.) ## 3.4 Process Parameter Matrix for Catalyst Precipitation | Process Variable | Effect on Particle Size | Effect on Surface Area | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---|---| | ↑ Supersaturation (high C) | ↓ Smaller particles | ↑ Higher | Leads to colloidal/gel | | ↑ Temperature | ↑ Larger (via ripening) | ↓ Lower | Accelerates ripening | | ↑ pH (faster addition) | ↓ Smaller | ↑ Higher | Use slow-release agent | | ↑ Stirring rate | ↓ Smaller (uniform mixing) | ↑ Higher | Prevents local supersaturation | | ↑ Aging time | ↑ Larger (Ostwald ripening) | ↓ Lower | Trade-off vs crystallinity | | Use of seed crystals | ↑ More uniform size | — | Industrial practice | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Overall Precipitation Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional architecture of a typical industrial catalyst precipitation process** used in heterogeneous catalyst manufacturing. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Metal Salt Solution
Precursor A] --> B1[Mixing Reactor
Stirred Tank] A2[Precipitating Agent
NaOH or NH4OH] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Supersaturated
Solution State] C1 --> D1{Nucleation
Stage} D1 -->|Homogeneous| E1[Stable Nuclei
Radius greater than r star] D1 -->|Heterogeneous| E1 E1 --> F1[Particle Growth
Diffusion Controlled] F1 --> G1[Primary Particles
1 to 100 nm] G1 --> H1{Aggregation and
Aging} H1 -->|Controlled| I1[Secondary Particles
1 to 50 micrometer] H1 -->|Uncontrolled| J1[Hard Aggregates
Low Surface Area] I1 --> K1[Filtration and
Washing] K1 --> L1[Drying and
Calcination] L1 --> M1[Final Catalyst
Active Form] J1 --> N1[Rejected Batch
Quality Fail] ``` ## 4.2 Nucleation, Growth, and Ripening Sequence (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1 Supersaturation
C greater than C star] --> S2[Step 2 Nucleation
Critical Radius r star] S2 --> S3[Step 3 Growth
Mass Transfer and Surface Reaction] S3 --> S4[Step 4 Aggregation
Primary to Secondary Particles] S4 --> S5[Step 5 Ostwald Ripening
Small Dissolve Large Grow] S5 --> S6[Step 6 Final Catalyst
After Drying and Calcination] S2 -.->|Insufficient Supersaturation| S7[No Nucleation
Solution Remains Stable] S5 -.->|Over-Aged| S8[Sintering Risk
Activity Loss] ``` ## 4.3 Influence of Supersaturation on Particle Morphology (Decision Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Precipitation System] --> P1{Relative Supersaturation
RSS Value} P1 -->|RSS less than 1| P2[Unsaturated
No Precipitation] P1 -->|RSS between 1 and 10| P3[Low Supersaturation
Large Crystalline Particles] P1 -->|RSS between 10 and 1000| P4[Moderate Supersaturation
Finely Divided Crystalline] P1 -->|RSS greater than 1000| P5[High Supersaturation
Colloidal Gel Amorphous] P3 --> P6[Low Surface Area
Few Active Sites] P4 --> P7[Optimal for Catalysis
High Surface Area] P5 --> P8[Very High Surface Area
Hard to Filter and Wash] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the Mermaid diagrams, node IDs are deliberately alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `P4`) and labels use plain uppercase text to satisfy Mermaid's syntax requirements and avoid label rendering errors. ## 4.4 Conceptual Energy Barrier Profile Although Mermaid cannot accurately plot an energy curve, the following state-transition diagram conveys the **energy landscape** of nucleus formation: ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Bulk Solution Bulk Solution --> Subcritical Embryo : Fluctuation Subcritical Embryo --> Bulk Solution : Dissolution Subcritical Embryo --> Critical Nucleus : Size increases past r star Critical Nucleus --> Stable Particle : Growth begins Stable Particle --> Final Catalyst Particle : Continued growth Critical Nucleus --> Bulk Solution : Dissolution under r star note right of Critical Nucleus : Activation barrier
Delta G star note right of Stable Particle : Spontaneous growth
Delta G decreasing ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define Relative Supersaturation (RSS) and explain its role in determining the size of precipitate particles according to Von Weimarn's theory.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - (1 Mark) **Definition:** Relative Supersaturation is defined as the dimensionless ratio, $$\text{RSS} = \frac{C - C^*}{C^*}$$ where $C$ is the actual solute concentration and $C^*$ is the equilibrium saturation concentration. - (1 Mark) **Von Weimarn's 1st Law:** The average particle size of a precipitate is *inversely proportional* to the RSS at the moment of nucleation: $d_p \propto 1/\text{RSS}$. - (1 Mark) **Practical Implication:** High RSS yields numerous small (colloidal) particles, suitable for high-surface-area catalysts. Low RSS yields few large crystalline particles. ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected] **What is Ostwald Ripening? Mention its consequence in catalyst preparation by precipitation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - (1.5 Marks) **Definition:** Ostwald Ripening is a thermodynamically driven coarsening phenomenon in which smaller particles in a polydisperse precipitate dissolve (because of their higher surface energy) and re-precipitate onto larger particles, leading to a net increase in average particle size over time. - (1.5 Marks) **Consequence in Catalyst Preparation:** It causes **loss of active surface area** and **decrease in catalytic activity**. It must be minimized by rapid filtration, low aging time, and controlled calcination. The kinetics of ripening follow the LSW theory: $\bar{r}^3 - \bar{r}_0^3 = K_{\text{LSW}} \cdot t$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4] **(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical radius of a spherical nucleus** in classical homogeneous nucleation theory. Explain how this critical radius is affected by supersaturation, temperature, and interfacial tension. (7 Marks) **(b)** State and explain **Von Weimarn's two laws of precipitation**. A catalyst chemist precipitates Ni(OH)₂ from a 0.5 M Ni(NO₃)₂ solution whose saturation concentration for Ni(OH)₂ at 298 K is $5 \times 10^{-8}$ M. Calculate the **relative supersaturation** and comment on the expected nature (size and morphology) of the precipitate. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Critical Radius Derivation (7 Marks):** - (2 Marks) Write the total free energy expression for a spherical nucleus: $$\Delta G(r) = -\frac{4\pi r^3 k_B T \ln S}{3 V_m} + 4\pi r^2 \gamma$$ - (2 Marks) Differentiate and set to zero: $$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{dr} = -\frac{4\pi r^2 k_B T \ln S}{V_m} + 8\pi r \gamma = 0$$ - (1 Mark) Solve for critical radius: $$r^* = \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{k_B T \ln S}$$ - (1 Mark) State critical free energy: $$\Delta G^* = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^3 V_m^2}{3 (k_B T \ln S)^2}$$ - (1 Mark) **Effect of variables:** - ↑ Supersaturation ($S$) → ↓ $r^*$ → smaller nuclei, more numerous. - ↑ Temperature ($T$) → slightly ↓ $r^*$ (in denominator) but the $\ln S$ term changes with $T$. - ↑ Interfacial tension ($\gamma$) → ↑ $r^*$ (harder to nucleate). **Part (b) — Von Weimarn's Laws + Calculation (7 Marks):** - (1.5 Marks) **First Law:** $d_p \propto 1/\text{RSS}$ — larger RSS gives smaller particles. - (1.5 Marks) **Second Law:** $N \propto \text{RSS}$ — larger RSS gives more particles. - (1 Mark) **Calculation:** $$\text{RSS} = \frac{0.5 - 5 \times 10^{-8}}{5 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{7}$$ - (1 Mark) **Conclusion:** Since RSS $\gg 10^3$, a **finely divided colloidal or gelatinous Ni(OH)₂ precipitate** is expected, with very high surface area — useful for high-activity hydrogenation catalysts. - (2 Marks) **Engineering note:** Such a high RSS necessitates careful pH control and slow precipitant addition to avoid the formation of hard aggregates. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4] **(a)** What is **Lifshitz–Slyozov–Wagner (LSW) theory**? Derive the cubic growth law for the mean particle radius during Ostwald ripening, stating all assumptions clearly. (7 Marks) **(b)** In a catalyst precipitation process, the mean particle radius grows from 5 nm at $t = 0$ to 9 nm at $t = 4$ hours. Assuming the ripening follows the LSW law, calculate the **LSW rate constant** $K_{\text{LSW}}$ and predict the mean particle radius after 12 hours. Also, comment on the implications for catalyst stability. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) — LSW Theory Derivation (7 Marks):** - (1 Mark) **Definition:** LSW theory describes the diffusion-controlled coarsening of a polydisperse ensemble of precipitate particles via Ostwald ripening, predicting that $\bar{r}^3 \propto t$. - (1 Mark) **Assumptions:** spherical particles, dilute system, no particle interactions, quasi-steady-state diffusion, equilibrium shape, and Gibbs–Thomson effect. - (2 Marks) Use the **Gibbs–Thomson equation** for solubility of a small particle: $$C_r = C^*\left(1 + \frac{2 \gamma V_m}{R T r}\right)$$ - (1 Mark) Apply **Fick's law** for diffusive flux: $$\frac{dr}{dt} = \frac{D V_m (C_b - C_r)}{r}$$ - (2 Marks) After ensemble averaging with $C_b = C^*(1 + 2\gamma V_m / R T r_c)$: $$\bar{r}(t)^3 - \bar{r}_0^3 = K_{\text{LSW}} \cdot t, \quad K_{\text{LSW}} = \frac{8 \gamma D C^* V_m^2}{9 R T}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):** - (1 Mark) Initial state: $r_0 = 5$ nm $= 5 \times 10^{-9}$ m. - (1 Mark) State at $t_1 = 4$ h $= 14{,}400$ s: $r_1 = 9$ nm $= 9 \times 10^{-9}$ m. - (1 Mark) Apply LSW law: $$K_{\text{LSW}} = \frac{r_1^3 - r_0^3}{t_1} = \frac{(9 \times 10^{-9})^3 - (5 \times 10^{-9})^3}{14{,}400}$$ $$K_{\text{LSW}} = \frac{729 \times 10^{-27} - 125 \times 10^{-27}}{14{,}400} = \frac{604 \times 10^{-27}}{14{,}400}$$ $$K_{\text{LSW}} = 4.19 \times 10^{-29} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ - (1 Mark) Predict $r$ at $t_2 = 12$ h $= 43{,}200$ s: $$r_2^3 = r_0^3 + K_{\text{LSW}} \cdot t_2 = 125 \times 10^{-27} + 4.19 \times 10^{-29} \times 43{,}200$$ $$r_2^3 = 125 \times 10^{-27} + 1810 \times 10^{-27} = 1935 \times 10^{-27} \text{ m}^3$$ $$r_2 = (1935)^{1/3} \times 10^{-9} = 12.45 \text{ nm}$$ - (3 Marks) **Implications for catalyst stability:** 1. The mean radius increases from 5 → 9 → 12.45 nm, showing significant **Ostwald ripening** in just 12 hours. 2. The catalyst's **active surface area decreases** roughly as $1/r$, leading to a ~40% loss in dispersion over 12 h. 3. To preserve activity, the catalyst should be **filtered, dried, and calcined** immediately after precipitation; alternatively, stabilizers (e.g., surfactants, surfactant templates, or promoter oxides) should be added. 4. Reaction conditions during service (high $T$, presence of water vapor) will accelerate ripening, so **operating temperature must be kept below the Tamman temperature** of the active phase. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing $C$ with $C^*$ in RSS calculation** → wrong magnitude by orders of magnitude. Always check whether $C \gg C^*$ (typical for catalyst precipitation). > 2. **Forgetting to use $V_m$ in critical radius derivation** — many students write $r^* = 2\gamma/(k_B T \ln S)$ which is dimensionally wrong. The molar volume $V_m$ is **mandatory**. > 3. **Mixing up Von Weimarn's two laws** — 1st law is about **size** (inversely proportional to RSS); 2nd law is about **number** (directly proportional to RSS). Examiners test this distinction every year. > 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. > 5. **Misapplying the LSW cubic law** — some students write $\bar{r}(t) = \bar{r}_0 + Kt^{1/3}$ instead of the correct $\bar{r}(t)^3 - \bar{r}_0^3 = Kt$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Precipitation** is a **liquid-to-solid** phase transition driven by **supersaturation** — central to heterogeneous catalyst synthesis. - **Three stages:** Supersaturation → Nucleation → Growth (often followed by Aggregation and Ostwald Ripening). - **Von Weimarn 1st Law:** $d_p \propto 1/\text{RSS}$ — higher supersaturation → smaller particles. - **Von Weimarn 2nd Law:** $N \propto \text{RSS}$ — higher supersaturation → more particles. - **Critical Radius (CNT):** $r^* = 2 \gamma V_m / (k_B T \ln S)$. - **Critical Energy Barrier:** $\Delta G^* = 16 \pi \gamma^3 V_m^2 / [3 (k_B T \ln S)^2]$. - **Nucleation Rate (Arrhenius form):** $J = A \exp(-\Delta G^*/k_B T)$. - **Diffusion-Controlled Growth:** $dr/dt = D V_m (C - C_s)/r$. - **LSW Ostwald Ripening Law:** $\bar{r}(t)^3 - \bar{r}_0^3 = K_{\text{LSW}} t$, with $K_{\text{LSW}} = 8 \gamma D C^* V_m^2 / (9 R T)$. - **Gibbs–Thomson equation:** $C_r = C^* \exp(2 \gamma V_m / R T r)$ — small particles have higher solubility. - **Engineering levers to control precipitate quality:** concentration, pH, temperature, stirring, aging time, precipitant type, use of seed crystals, additives. - **Industrial examples:** Al(OH)₃ → γ-Al₂O₃ supports; CoCO₃ → Co/SiO₂ for FT; Cu/Zn/Al hydrotalcites → methanol catalysts. - **Common precipitants:** NaOH, KOH, NH₄OH, Na₂CO₃, (NH₄)₂CO₃, oxalic acid, urea (for slow homogeneous release). - **Why precipitation is preferred for some catalysts:** bulk homogeneity, low-cost, scalability, ability to form mixed-metal oxides. - **Pitfalls to avoid in KTU exam:** unitless numbers, wrong exponent in LSW law, mixing up $C$ and $C^*$, missing $V_m$ in $r^*$ derivation.

topics.title: Co-Precipitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Co-Precipitation # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Co-Precipitation** is a wet-chemical synthesis technique in which **two or more cations are simultaneously precipitated from a homogeneous aqueous solution** by the addition of a precipitating agent (typically an alkali such as NaOH, KOH, NH₄OH, Na₂CO₃, or urea), producing a mixed precursor phase (hydroxide, carbonate, hydroxycarbonate, or hydroxychloride) that, upon subsequent aging, washing, drying, and calcination, yields a finely divided, homogeneous multi-component catalyst or catalyst support. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, co-precipitation is classified as a **bulk-type catalyst preparation method** that operates under conditions of **controlled supersaturation** to achieve atomic-scale mixing of the active phase and the promoter/support precursor. Unlike impregnation (which deposits species on a pre-formed support), co-precipitation **builds the entire multi-component solid from the solution state**, ensuring the highest possible dispersion and chemical homogeneity of the catalyst precursor. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Cake in One Pot" > [!NOTE] > **Think of co-precipitation like baking a mixed-fruit cake where all the fruits are mixed into the batter BEFORE baking**, rather than placing them on top of an already-baked cake. If you imagine that: - The **fruit pieces** = metal cations (Cu²⁺, Zn²⁺, Al³⁺) - The **batter (eggs + flour)** = the precipitating agent (NaOH / Na₂CO₃) - The **oven** = the calcination furnace - The **final cake** = the finished Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst Then **co-precipitation** is exactly that: ALL ingredients are combined in solution at once, and a single solid precursor forms that is then transformed by heat into the final active catalyst. This is fundamentally different from **impregnation**, where you would bake a plain cake first and then pour fruit syrup on top. ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Precipitant** | The reagent that induces precipitation (e.g., NaOH, Na₂CO₃, urea) | | **Supersaturation** | The condition where the ionic product exceeds the solubility product (Q > Ksp) | | **Aging (Digestion)** | Holding the precipitate in contact with mother liquor to allow Ostwald ripening and crystallization | | **Mother liquor** | The supernatant solution remaining after precipitation | | **Calcination** | Controlled thermal decomposition of the precursor in air/inert gas | | **Hydrotalcite-like phase** | A class of layered double hydroxides (LDHs) obtained by co-precipitation | | **Promoter** | A minor component that enhances the activity, selectivity, or stability of the catalyst | ## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlight > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **KTU 2024 PECHT633 syllabus**, co-precipitation is listed under **"Methods of Catalyst Preparation — Bulk Methods"** alongside sol-gel, fusion, and precipitation. It is a **high-weightage topic** because industrial catalysts such as the **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ methanol synthesis catalyst** (ICI process) and **Fischer-Tropsch Fe-Cu-K catalysts** are manufactured almost exclusively by co-precipitation. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Flow of Co-Precipitation The co-precipitation process is a sequence of carefully controlled unit operations. Each step has a direct impact on the textural, structural, and catalytic properties of the final catalyst. ### Step 1 — Preparation of the Mixed Salt Solution Metal precursors (nitrates, chlorides, or sulfates) are dissolved in distilled/deionized water in the **stoichiometric ratio** required in the final catalyst. Nitrates are most preferred because: - High solubility in water - NO₃⁻ decomposes cleanly during calcination (avoids sulfur or chloride contamination) - Leaves no harmful residue > Example: For Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ (60:30:10 atomic ratio), dissolve Cu(NO₃)₂·3H₂O, Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O, and Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O in the molar ratio **6 : 3 : 1**. ### Step 2 — Addition of the Precipitating Agent The precipitant (NaOH, Na₂CO₃, or NaHCO₃) is added either: - **Constant-pH mode** (simultaneous addition of salt + precipitant with pH-stat) - **Slow rising-pH mode** (precipitant added gradually to salt solution) **Constant-pH mode is preferred** for industrial multi-metal catalysts because it ensures that all cations precipitate in a single homogeneous phase. The pH is chosen such that it lies **above the precipitation pH of the most soluble cation** but **below the dissolution pH of the least soluble hydroxide**. ### Step 3 — Aging (Digestion) The freshly formed precipitate (often amorphous) is kept in contact with the mother liquor at **60–80 °C for 1–24 h**. During aging: - **Ostwald ripening** occurs — small crystals dissolve and re-deposit on larger ones → larger crystallites, narrower pore-size distribution. - The precipitate transforms from amorphous → crystalline precursor phase (e.g., **aurichalcite, hydrotalcite, malachite** for Cu/Zn/Al systems). - Surface area decreases but mechanical strength improves. ### Step 4 — Filtration and Washing The aged slurry is filtered (typically using vacuum filtration or a filter press) and washed repeatedly with **deionized water** to remove: - Residual Na⁺ ions (catalytic poison, especially for Cu) - Excess NO₃⁻ / SO₄²⁻ / Cl⁻ - Unreacted precipitant > [!WARNING] > **Inadequate washing leaves residual Na⁺ which acts as a permanent poison for many industrial catalysts**, especially Cu-based methanol catalysts. This is one of the most common industrial failures. ### Step 5 — Drying The filter cake is dried in an oven at **100–150 °C for 12–24 h** to remove free and bound water. Spray drying is used industrially for large batches to obtain spherical microparticles. ### Step 6 — Calcination The dried precursor is heated in air (or N₂ for reducible oxides) at **300–600 °C for 2–6 h**. During calcination: - Hydroxides → oxides (e.g., Cu(OH)₂ → CuO) - Carbonates → oxides (e.g., ZnCO₃ → ZnO) - Hydroxycarbonates → mixed oxides - Specific surface area and pore structure are finalized ## 2.2 Conditions for Successful Co-Precipitation For two cations **M₁ⁿ⁺** and **M₂ᵐ⁺** to co-precipitate quantitatively, the following criteria **must be satisfied simultaneously**: 1. **The pH of precipitation must be ≥ the pH at which both cations completely precipitate.** 2. **The solubility products (Ksp) must be matched** so that no cation remains in solution. 3. **The mixing must be intense and instantaneous** to avoid local supersaturation gradients that lead to inhomogeneous precipitation. 4. **The anion of the precipitant must be volatile or removable** (CO₃²⁻, OH⁻ preferred over SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻). 5. **Temperature, ionic strength, and addition rate must be tightly controlled.** ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear repeatedly in KTU exams, semester tests, and viva voce.** | # | Equation | Description / Use | Units | |---|----------|------------------|-------| | 1 | $K_{sp} = [M^{n+}][OH^{-}]^{n}$ | Solubility product for hydroxide precipitation | dimensionless | | 2 | $[OH^{-}] = \left(\dfrac{K_{sp}}{[M^{n+}]}\right)^{1/n}$ | Concentration of OH⁻ required for complete precipitation | mol/L | | 3 | $pH = 14 - pOH = 14 + \log[OH^{-}]$ | Conversion between pH and pOH at 25 °C | dimensionless | | 4 | $S = \dfrac{Q}{K_{sp}}$ | Supersaturation ratio (Q = ionic product) | dimensionless | | 5 | $\Delta G = -RT \ln S$ | Driving force for nucleation when S > 1 | J/mol | | 6 | $r_{c} = \dfrac{2 \sigma V_{m}}{RT \ln S}$ | Critical nucleus radius (Gibbs–Thomson equation) | m | | 7 | $J = J_{0} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{16\pi \sigma^{3} V_{m}^{2}}{3k^{3}T^{3}(\ln S)^{2}}\right)$ | Steady-state nucleation rate | m⁻³ s⁻¹ | | 8 | $d = \dfrac{M_{salt}}{M_{metal}} \times X$ | Stoichiometric mass of metal salt needed for X g of metal | g | | 9 | $n_{precip} = \sum_{i} \nu_{i} \cdot n_{M_i^{n+}}$ | Moles of precipitant required for complete precipitation | mol | | 10 | $\theta_{BET} = \dfrac{p / p^{0}}{(1 - p / p^{0})}\dfrac{1}{c} = \dfrac{V_{ads}}{V_{m}}$ | BET equation used to measure resulting Sₐ | — | Where: - $\sigma$ = interfacial energy (J/m²) - $V_m$ = molar volume of the solid (m³/mol) - $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $\nu_i$ = stoichiometric coefficient of cation *i* - $n_{M_i^{n+}}$ = moles of cation *i* ## 2.4 Why High Supersaturation is Critical > [!NOTE] > The classical **LaMer diagram** governs precipitation kinetics. For catalysts, **moderate supersaturation is desired** — too low gives large crystals (low surface area), too high gives amorphous gels with poor reproducibility. The mechanism transitions: - **Low S** → slow nucleation, large particles → heterogeneous catalyst with low Sₐ - **Moderate S** → controlled nucleation + growth → ideal for catalyst preparation - **High S** → burst nucleation, amorphous gel → difficult to filter, broad PSD ## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility | Industrial Catalyst | Prepared by Co-Precipitation | |---------------------|------------------------------| | Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ (methanol synthesis) | Yes — ICI/Topsoe process | | Fe-Cu-K (Fischer-Tropsch) | Yes — Ruhrchemie process | | Co-Mo / Ni-Mo oxides | Sometimes, for bulk sulfide catalysts | | LaCoO₃, LaNiO₃ perovskites | Yes — for automotive catalysis | | Hydrotalcite-derived Mg-Al oxides | Yes — for basic catalysts | | Ni-Al₂O₃ methanation catalysts | Yes | | Mn-Ce-Ox (NOₓ storage) | Yes | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — pH Required for Quantitative Precipitation > **Problem:** A solution contains **0.10 M Cu²⁺** and **0.10 M Zn²⁺**. Calculate the **minimum pH** at which BOTH cations will be quantitatively precipitated as hydroxides (residual concentration ≤ 10⁻⁵ M). > > Given: $K_{sp}(Cu(OH)_2) = 2.2 \times 10^{-20}$ > Given: $K_{sp}(Zn(OH)_2) = 3.0 \times 10^{-17}$ **Step 1 — Write the solubility product expressions.** $$ \begin{aligned} Cu(OH)_2 &\rightleftharpoons Cu^{2+} + 2\,OH^{-} \\ K_{sp,Cu} &= [Cu^{2+}][OH^{-}]^{2} = 2.2 \times 10^{-20} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Zn(OH)_2 &\rightleftharpoons Zn^{2+} + 2\,OH^{-} \\ K_{sp,Zn} &= [Zn^{2+}][OH^{-}]^{2} = 3.0 \times 10^{-17} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute [OH⁻] needed for Cu²⁺ at residual 10⁻⁵ M.** $$ \begin{aligned} [OH^{-}] &= \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{sp,Cu}}{[Cu^{2+}]}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{-20}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}} \\ &= \sqrt{2.2 \times 10^{-15}} \\ &= 4.69 \times 10^{-8}\; M \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ pOH_{Cu} = -\log(4.69 \times 10^{-8}) = 7.33 $$ $$ pH_{Cu} = 14 - 7.33 = 6.67 $$ **Step 3 — Compute [OH⁻] needed for Zn²⁺ at residual 10⁻⁵ M.** $$ \begin{aligned} [OH^{-}] &= \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{sp,Zn}}{[Zn^{2+}]}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{-17}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}} \\ &= \sqrt{3.0 \times 10^{-12}} \\ &= 1.73 \times 10^{-6}\; M \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ pOH_{Zn} = -\log(1.73 \times 10^{-6}) = 5.76 $$ $$ pH_{Zn} = 14 - 5.76 = 8.24 $$ **Step 4 — Select the higher pH (the controlling condition).** The Zn²⁺ requires a **higher pH (8.24)** than Cu²⁺ (6.67). Therefore, the operating pH for quantitative co-precipitation must be **≥ 8.24**, ensuring both metals precipitate together. $$ \boxed{\;pH_{min} = 8.24\;} $$ **Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation.** Operating at pH ≈ 8.5 (with Na₂CO₃/NaOH buffer) guarantees **simultaneous precipitation** of Cu and Zn as a mixed hydroxide/hydroxycarbonate. This is the foundation of industrial Cu/ZnO catalyst synthesis. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Calculation of Precipitant Required > **Problem:** A catalyst engineer wants to prepare **500 g of Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ (60 : 30 : 10 atomic ratio)** by co-precipitation using **Na₂CO₃** as the precipitant. Calculate: > (a) Mass of Cu(NO₃)₂·3H₂O, Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O, and Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O required. > (b) Mass of Na₂CO₃ needed for complete precipitation. **Atomic weights used:** - Cu = 63.55, Zn = 65.38, Al = 26.98 - N = 14.01, O = 16.00, Na = 22.99, C = 12.01, H = 1.008 **Molar masses:** - Cu(NO₃)₂·3H₂O = **241.60 g/mol** - Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O = **297.49 g/mol** - Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O = **375.13 g/mol** - Na₂CO₃ = **105.99 g/mol** ### (a) Calculation of Metal Salt Masses **Step 1 — Calculate moles of each metal in the final catalyst.** The atomic ratio Cu : Zn : Al = 60 : 30 : 10. Assuming a basis of **100 mol total metals**: - n(Cu) = 60 mol - n(Zn) = 30 mol - n(Al) = 10 mol **Step 2 — Calculate equivalent moles in 500 g of catalyst.** Mass of catalyst = 500 g. Approximate average atomic weight of metals ≈ (60 × 63.55 + 30 × 65.38 + 10 × 26.98) / 100 = **61.79 g/mol-metals**. $$ n_{total\;metals} = \dfrac{500\;g}{61.79\;g/mol} = 8.092\; mol $$ (Note: This is approximate because the final catalyst is an oxide; the actual moles of metal atoms in the catalyst are computed via the metal loading. We continue with the **proportional basis of 60 : 30 : 10**.) **Step 3 — Mass of Cu salt.** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{Cu} &= 0.60 \times n_{total} = 0.60 \times 8.092 = 4.855\; mol \\ m_{Cu(NO_3)_2 \cdot 3H_2O} &= 4.855 \times 241.60 = 1173\; g \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Mass of Zn salt.** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{Zn} &= 0.30 \times 8.092 = 2.428\; mol \\ m_{Zn(NO_3)_2 \cdot 6H_2O} &= 2.428 \times 297.49 = 722.3\; g \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Mass of Al salt.** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{Al} &= 0.10 \times 8.092 = 0.8092\; mol \\ m_{Al(NO_3)_3 \cdot 9H_2O} &= 0.8092 \times 375.13 = 303.5\; g \end{aligned} $$ ### (b) Calculation of Na₂CO₃ Mass **Step 6 — Precipitation reactions.** $$ \begin{aligned} Cu^{2+} + CO_3^{2-} &\rightarrow CuCO_3 \downarrow \\ Zn^{2+} + CO_3^{2-} &\rightarrow ZnCO_3 \downarrow \\ 2\,Al^{3+} + 3\,CO_3^{2-} + 3\,H_2O &\rightarrow Al_2O_3 \cdot 3H_2O \downarrow + 3\,CO_2 \end{aligned} $$ (Aluminium precipitates as a basic carbonate or gibbsite.) **Step 7 — Moles of Na₂CO₃ needed.** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{Na_2CO_3} &= n_{Cu} + n_{Zn} + \tfrac{3}{2}\, n_{Al} \\ &= 4.855 + 2.428 + 1.5 \times 0.8092 \\ &= 4.855 + 2.428 + 1.214 \\ &= 8.497\; mol \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Mass of Na₂CO₃.** $$ m_{Na_2CO_3} = 8.497 \times 105.99 = 900.6\; g $$ $$ \boxed{\;m_{Na_2CO_3} \approx 901\; g\;} $$ In practice, a **5–10 % excess** is used to ensure complete precipitation and to compensate for losses during filtration. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Precipitation Strategies | Parameter | Constant-pH Method | Rising-pH Method | Urea Method (Homogeneous) | |-----------|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------------| | pH control | Excellent (pH-stat) | Moderate | Excellent (slow hydrolysis) | | Mixing required | Vigorous | Moderate | Mild | | Homogeneity | Highest | Moderate | Highest | | Particle size | Narrow distribution | Broad | Narrow | | Filterability | Good | Variable | Excellent | | Industrial use | Methanol catalyst, perovskites | Lab-scale only | Hydrotalcites, LDHs | | Risk of inhomogeneity | Low | High (sequential precipitation) | Very low | --- ## 3.4 Critical Discussion Questions (KTU-style analytical) > **Q1.** Why is **Na₂CO₃** often preferred over **NaOH** for industrial co-precipitation of Cu-Zn-Al catalysts? > > **Answer:** Na₂CO₃ produces basic carbonates such as **malachite (Cu₂(OH)₂CO₃)** and **aurichalcite ((Cu,Zn)₅(CO₃)₂(OH)₆)** instead of pure hydroxides. These hydroxycarbonates decompose at **lower temperatures (< 350 °C)** with **less sintering**, giving higher Cu surface area after reduction. Pure NaOH tends to give Cu(OH)₂ which sinters more easily. > **Q2.** Why is **aging** an essential step even though it reduces surface area? > > **Answer:** Aging converts the amorphous gel into a **crystalline, mechanically robust precursor phase** with **uniform pore structure**. Without aging, the dried gel collapses on calcination, producing a low-surface-area, mechanically weak catalyst with broad pore-size distribution that is unsuitable for fixed-bed reactors. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Industrial Co-Precipitation The following Mermaid block illustrates the complete workflow of an industrial co-precipitation plant for catalyst manufacture. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Deionized Water Tank] --> B[Prepare Mixed Salt Solution] C[Cu Nitrate] --> B D[Zn Nitrate] --> B E[Al Nitrate] --> B F[Na2CO3 / NaOH Tank] --> G[Precipitation Reactor with pH Stat] B --> G G --> H{pH within 8.0 to 9.0} H -->|Yes| I[Aging Tank at 70 C for 6 to 12 h] H -->|No| J[Auto Adjust with Base or Acid] J --> G I --> K[Filter Press or Rotary Vacuum Filter] K --> L[Repetitive Washing with Deionized Water] L --> M{Conductivity Check Below 20 microsiemens per cm} M -->|Pass| N[Spray Drier or Oven Drying at 120 C] M -->|Fail| L N --> O[Calcination Furnace at 300 to 500 C for 4 h] O --> P[Pelletization or Tableting] P --> Q[Final Catalyst Cu ZnO Al2O3] style G fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333 style I fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333 style O fill:#FFB6B9,stroke:#333 style Q fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Constant-pH Co-Precipitation Rig ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FeedSystem [Feed and Reagent System] FS1[Metal Salt Solution Reservoir] FS2[Precipitant Na2CO3 Reservoir] FS3[Deionized Water Reservoir] end subgraph ControlUnit [Process Control Unit] CU1[pH Probe] CU2[Temperature Probe] CU3[Conductivity Probe] CU4[PLC with PID Controller] end subgraph Reactor [Stirred Tank Reactor] R1[Glass Lined Vessel 5 to 50 L] R2[Mechanical Stirrer 200 to 500 rpm] R3[Heating Jacket] end subgraph Downstream [Downstream Processing] D1[Aging Tank] D2[Filter Press] D3[Washer Column] D4[Spray Dryer] D5[Calciner] end FS1 --> R1 FS2 --> R1 FS3 --> R1 R2 --- R1 R3 --- R1 R1 --> CU1 R1 --> CU2 CU1 --> CU4 CU2 --> CU4 CU4 -->|Adjust Feed Rate| FS1 CU4 -->|Adjust Base Dose| FS2 R1 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 --> D4 D4 --> D5 style Reactor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style ControlUnit fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Concept Map — Variables Affecting Co-Precipitated Catalyst Properties ```mermaid mindmap root((Co Precipitation
Catalyst Properties)) pH Low pH - Incomplete precipitation High pH - Amorphous gel Optimum - Crystalline precursor Temperature Low - Slow aging Moderate 60 to 80 C - Ideal High - Particle growth Aging Time Short - Amorphous Long - Loss of surface area Optimum 6 to 12 h Mixing Poor - Inhomogeneity Vigorous - Uniform nucleation Salt Type Nitrate - Preferred Chloride - Corrosive residue Sulfate - Hard to remove ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define co-precipitation as applied to catalyst preparation. Why is it preferred over impregnation for bulk multi-component catalysts?** **Model Answer:** Co-precipitation is the **simultaneous precipitation of two or more cations** from a homogeneous aqueous solution by the addition of a precipitating agent (such as NaOH, Na₂CO₃, or NH₄OH), producing a mixed precursor that is later transformed into the final catalyst by aging, washing, drying, and calcination. It is preferred over impregnation for bulk multi-component catalysts because: 1. **Atomic-scale homogeneity** — All components are mixed in solution before solid formation, ensuring intimate contact between active metal, promoter, and support precursor. 2. **Higher metal loading** — Possible to achieve loadings > 50 wt%, whereas impregnation is limited by solubility and support capacity. 3. **Reproducible bulk composition** — The bulk composition is set by the stoichiometry of the salt solution, independent of support pore volume. 4. **Better thermal stability** — The crystalline precursor (e.g., hydrotalcite, malachite, aurichalcite) yields a more robust catalyst than impregnated species. [3 Marks — Marks distribution: Definition 1 + Any 2 reasons 1+1] --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: List any three factors that influence the properties of a co-precipitated catalyst.** **Model Answer:** The three most important factors are: 1. **pH of precipitation** — Determines which cations precipitate completely and the nature of the precursor phase (hydroxide vs. hydroxycarbonate). 2. **Aging (digestion) temperature and time** — Controls Ostwald ripening, crystallinity, and pore-size distribution. 3. **Washing efficiency** — Inadequate washing leaves Na⁺ / Cl⁻ / SO₄²⁻ residues that poison the active sites. (Other acceptable factors: rate of addition, stirring intensity, precipitant type, salt type, calcination temperature.)
