[3 Marks — 1 mark per factor with brief justification] --- ## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from the module.* Both questions below are **fully worked alternative choices**, as per KTU 2024 Scheme. --- ### ❖ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model Paper 2)]** > **(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure for preparing a Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ (60:30:10) methanol synthesis catalyst by co-precipitation**, with a neat process flow diagram. Mention the role of each step. **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** A solution contains 0.05 M Cu²⁺ and 0.05 M Zn²⁺. Calculate the **minimum pH** required for the **quantitative precipitation** of both ions as hydroxides (residual ≤ 10⁻⁵ M). Given: $K_{sp}(Cu(OH)_2) = 2.2 \times 10^{-20}$, $K_{sp}(Zn(OH)_2) = 3.0 \times 10^{-17}$. **[7 Marks]** ### MODEL ANSWER A: #### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure **Step 1 — Salt solution preparation.** Dissolve Cu(NO₃)₂·3H₂O (60 mol parts), Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O (30 mol parts) and Al(NO₃)₃·9H₂O (10 mol parts) in deionized water at 70 °C to give a 1.0 M total metal concentration. Nitrates are chosen because they decompose cleanly during calcination without leaving harmful residues. **Step 2 — Precipitation at constant pH.** Add this solution and a 1.0 M Na₂CO₃ solution **simultaneously** to a stirred reactor maintained at 70 °C, with pH held at **7.0 ± 0.2** using an automated pH-stat. The pH-stat continuously compares the measured pH with the set-point and adjusts the base feed rate. This produces a uniform blue precipitate of mixed hydroxycarbonate (malachite + aurichalcite + Al-hydroxycarbonate). **Step 3 — Aging.** The slurry is held at 70–80 °C for 6–12 h with gentle stirring. Aging converts the initially amorphous precipitate to crystalline hydroxycarbonates and reduces the filter cake resistance. **Step 4 — Filtration and washing.** Vacuum-filter the slurry and wash the cake repeatedly with hot deionized water until the conductivity of the filtrate falls below **20 µS/cm**, ensuring Na⁺ is removed. **Step 5 — Drying.** Dry the filter cake at 110–120 °C for 12 h in an oven (or by spray-drying for industrial scale). The dried mass is a greenish powder of mixed hydroxycarbonates. **Step 6 — Calcination.** Heat the dried precursor in air at **350 °C for 4 h**. The hydroxycarbonates decompose to a black CuO/ZnO/Al₂O₃ powder. **Step 7 — Reduction (in-situ).** Before use, reduce the catalyst in dilute H₂ (5 % H₂ in N₂) at 230 °C to obtain metallic Cu° dispersed on ZnO with Al₂O₃ as a structural promoter. The Cu crystallite size is typically **5–10 nm**, giving a Cu surface area of 25–40 m²/g. **Role of each step (summary):** | Step | Role | |------|------| | Salt solution | Sets bulk composition | | Constant pH precipitation | Ensures atomic-scale mixing | | Aging | Crystallization, pore uniformity | | Washing | Removes Na⁺ poison | | Drying | Removes water without collapse | | Calcination | Decomposes precursor to oxides | | Reduction | Generates active Cu⁰ sites | [7 Marks — Steps 4, neat flow diagram 2, role of each step 1] #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Cu(OH)₂ precipitation:** $$ \begin{aligned} [OH^{-}] &= \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{sp,Cu}}{[Cu^{2+}]_{residual}}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{-20}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}} \\ &= 4.69 \times 10^{-8}\; M \\ pOH_{Cu} &= -\log(4.69 \times 10^{-8}) = 7.33 \\ pH_{Cu} &= 14 - 7.33 = 6.67 \end{aligned} $$ **Zn(OH)₂ precipitation:** $$ \begin{aligned} [OH^{-}] &= \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{sp,Zn}}{[Zn^{2+}]_{residual}}} \\ &= \sqrt{\dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{-17}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}} \\ &= 1.73 \times 10^{-6}\; M \\ pOH_{Zn} &= -\log(1.73 \times 10^{-6}) = 5.76 \\ pH_{Zn} &= 14 - 5.76 = 8.24 \end{aligned} $$ **Controlling pH:** Since Zn²⁺ requires the higher pH (8.24), the operating pH for complete co-precipitation must be **≥ 8.24**. $$ \boxed{\;pH_{min} = 8.24 \;\text{(or practically 8.5)}\;} $$ **Conclusion:** At pH 8.5, both Cu²⁺ and Zn²⁺ will be precipitated to ≤ 10⁻⁵ M, ensuring quantitative co-precipitation. [7 Marks — Cu calc 3, Zn calc 3, conclusion 1] --- ### ❖ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Supplementary)]** > **(a)** Explain the **role of aging (digestion)** in co-precipitation. How does it affect the surface area, pore-size distribution, and mechanical strength of the catalyst? **[7 Marks]** > > **(b)** An industrial chemist wants to prepare **1 kg of Cu/ZnO (50:50 atomic) catalyst precursor** as CuCO₃ + ZnCO₃ using **Na₂CO₃** as precipitant. Calculate: > (i) Moles of Cu²⁺ and Zn²⁺ in the precursor. > (ii) Mass of Cu(NO₃)₂·3H₂O and Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O needed. > (iii) Mass of Na₂CO₃ required. > Given atomic weights: Cu = 63.5, Zn = 65.4, Na = 23, C = 12, O = 16, N = 14, H = 1. **[7 Marks]** ### MODEL ANSWER B: #### Part (a) — Role of Aging Aging (also called digestion) is the **holding of the freshly precipitated slurry in contact with its mother liquor at elevated temperature (60–80 °C) for several hours** after precipitation is complete. It is a critically important step that governs the **structural and textural quality** of the final catalyst. **Mechanism during aging:** 1. **Ostwald ripening** — Smaller, less thermodynamically stable crystallites dissolve and re-precipitate onto larger ones. The driving force is the reduction in total interfacial energy. Result: larger, more uniform crystallites. 2. **Phase transformation** — Amorphous gel → crystalline precursor phase (e.g., malachite → more ordered aurichalcite in Cu-Zn systems; or amorphous Al(OH)₃ → bayerite/gibbsite). 3. **Pore reorganization** — Micropores collapse; mesopores become more uniform. 4. **Impurity expulsion** — Incorporated foreign ions (Na⁺, NO₃⁻) are expelled to the surface and washed away in the subsequent washing step. **Effects on catalyst properties:** | Property | Effect of Aging | |----------|-----------------| | **Specific surface area (Sₐ)** | Decreases (crystallite growth) — typically from 200 → 100 m²/g | | **Pore-size distribution** | Narrows; mean pore diameter shifts to mesoporous range (5–20 nm) | | **Mechanical strength** | Increases significantly — gel network strengthens via sintering necks | | **Reproducibility** | Improves — by relaxing kinetic supersaturation gradients | | **Catalytic activity** | Optimum at intermediate aging time; too long gives sintering | [7 Marks — Mechanism 3, effect on Sₐ 1, PSD 1, mechanical strength 1, conclusion 1] #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** 1 kg = 1000 g of CuCO₃ + ZnCO₃ in 50 : 50 atomic ratio. **Step 1 — Molar masses of precursors.** - CuCO₃ = 63.5 + 12 + 48 = **123.5 g/mol** - ZnCO₃ = 65.4 + 12 + 48 = **125.4 g/mol** - Cu(NO₃)₂·3H₂O = 63.5 + 2(14 + 48) + 3(18) = 241.5 g/mol - Zn(NO₃)₂·6H₂O = 65.4 + 2(14 + 48) + 6(18) = 297.4 g/mol - Na₂CO₃ = 106 g/mol **Step 2 — Average molar mass of mixed carbonate.** $$ M_{avg} = 0.5 \times 123.5 + 0.5 \times 125.4 = 124.45\; g/mol $$ **(i) Moles of each cation:** $$ n_{total\;carbonates} = \dfrac{1000}{124.45} = 8.036\; mol $$ Since atomic ratio Cu : Zn = 50 : 50: $$ n_{Cu^{2+}} = n_{Zn^{2+}} = \dfrac{8.036}{2} = 4.018\; mol $$ **(ii) Mass of metal salts:** $$ m_{Cu(NO_3)_2 \cdot 3H_2O} = 4.018 \times 241.5 = 970.3\; g $$ $$ m_{Zn(NO_3)_2 \cdot 6H_2O} = 4.018 \times 297.4 = 1194.9\; g $$ **(iii) Mass of Na₂CO₃ required:** Reactions: $$ Cu^{2+} + CO_3^{2-} \rightarrow CuCO_3 \downarrow $$ $$ Zn^{2+} + CO_3^{2-} \rightarrow ZnCO_3 \downarrow $$ $$ n_{Na_2CO_3} = n_{Cu^{2+}} + n_{Zn^{2+}} = 4.018 + 4.018 = 8.036\; mol $$ $$ m_{Na_2CO_3} = 8.036 \times 106 = 851.8\; g $$ $$ \boxed{\;m_{Na_2CO_3} \approx 852\; g\;(\text{plus 5\% excess})\;} $$ [7 Marks — Molar masses 1, total moles 1, individual cation moles 1, salt masses 2, Na₂CO₃ mass 2] --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-losing pitfalls in Co-Precipitation questions:** > > 1. **Skipping the unit analysis** in numerical problems — always write [M], mol, g, m²/g explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting that for CO-PRECIPITATION, the controlling pH is the HIGHER of the two individual pH values**, not the lower. Students often pick the wrong value. > 3. **Ignoring aging** in the process description — it carries at least 2 marks and is frequently missed. > 4. **Confusing co-precipitation with impregnation** — KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. > 5. **Not justifying why Na₂CO₃ is preferred over NaOH** in Cu/ZnO catalyst synthesis — losing the "industrial application" credit. > 6. **Writing "Na" instead of "Na⁺"** in washing step explanations — small but penalised in strict valuation. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Co-precipitation = simultaneous precipitation of ≥ 2 cations** to form a mixed precursor. - [x] It is a **bulk preparation method**, classified under "wet chemical" synthesis. - [x] **Process steps:** Salt solution → constant-pH precipitation → aging → filtration → washing → drying → calcination → (optional reduction). - [x] **Constant-pH mode is preferred** for multi-metal industrial catalysts. - [x] **pH control is the most critical parameter** — must exceed the precipitation pH of the most soluble cation. - [x] **Aging** improves crystallinity, pore uniformity, and mechanical strength but reduces Sₐ. - [x] **Na₂CO₃ gives hydroxycarbonates** which decompose at lower T → higher Cu surface area. - [x] **Washing must be thorough** to remove Na⁺ poison. - [x] **Industrial examples:** Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ (methanol), Fe-Cu-K (FT), hydrotalcites, perovskites. - [x] **Ksp equation:** $[M^{n+}][OH^-]^n = K_{sp}$. - [x] **Quantitative precipitation pH:** $[OH^-] = \sqrt{K_{sp}/[M^{n+}]_{residual}}$. - [x] **Operating pH = max(pH₁, pH₂, ..., pHₙ)** for all cations. - [x] **Supersaturation ratio** $S = Q/K_{sp}$ controls particle size — moderate S gives optimum catalysts. - [x] **Advantages:** high metal loading, atomic-scale homogeneity, reproducible bulk composition. - [x] **Disadvantages:** large volumes of waste water, sensitive to small pH changes, batch-process limitations.

topics.title: Sol-Gel Formation, Characterization of Catalysts: Pore Volume
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sol-Gel Formation, Characterization of Catalysts: Pore Volume

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sol-Gel Formation — Formal Definition

**Sol-Gel Formation** is a wet-chemical technique used in materials science and catalysis engineering for the synthesis of inorganic oxides, ceramics, and porous catalyst supports. In this process, a chemical solution (the **sol**) undergoes a gradual transition into a three-dimensional, cross-linked, rigid network (the **gel**) through a series of hydrolysis and polycondensation reactions of metal alkoxides or metal salts.

The term **sol** refers to a colloidal suspension of solid particles (typically 1–1000 nm in diameter) dispersed in a liquid medium, while the **gel** is a non-fluid, interconnected porous network of solid phase enclosing a continuous liquid phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT633 — Module 4):** Sol-Gel chemistry is fundamentally important for synthesizing high-surface-area, mesoporous catalytic supports such as **SiO₂, Al₂O₃, TiO₂, and ZrO₂**. The product uniformity at the molecular level and the low processing temperatures (~25–150 °C) make it indispensable in modern catalyst preparation.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cement Bucket" Analogy

Imagine mixing cement powder with water in a bucket.

- Initially, the cement particles float independently in the water — this is your **sol**.
- After some time, the particles begin sticking to one another (condensation), forming bridges throughout the bucket — this is the **gel**.
- The water that remains trapped inside the solid network represents the **pore liquid** — once evaporated, it leaves behind a porous solid (xerogel) with measurable **pore volume**.

Just as the cement-and-water bucket transitions from a free-flowing liquid to a rigid mass while preserving a huge internal surface area, sol-gel chemistry transforms a liquid precursor into a porous, catalytically active solid.

---

## 1.2 Pore Volume (Vp) — Formal Definition

**Pore Volume (V_p)** is defined as the total volume of pores per unit mass of a solid catalyst, typically expressed in **cm³/g** (or **ml/g**). It quantifies the accessible internal void space available for reactant molecules to diffuse, adsorb, and react at the active sites.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Pore volume is reported in **cm³/g**, with **typical values** for industrial catalysts ranging from **0.1 to 1.5 cm³/g**. Microporous zeolites (e.g., ZSM-5) show Vp ≈ **0.1–0.3 cm³/g**, while mesoporous silicas (e.g., MCM-41) exhibit Vp ≈ **0.6–1.2 cm³/g**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge Capacity" Analogy

Think of a kitchen sponge:

- A denser, less porous sponge absorbs little water — *low pore volume*.
- A fluffy, open-cell sponge soaks up a lot — *high pore volume*.
- The total water it retains (per gram) is essentially its **pore volume**.

For a heterogeneous catalyst, the pore volume tells you how much reactant fluid the catalyst can "hold" within its internal architecture during a reaction — directly influencing conversion in diffusion-limited systems.

---

## 1.3 Why These Topics Matter Together

Sol-gel processing allows engineers to **deliberately tune** the pore volume, pore size distribution, and surface area of a catalyst by simply adjusting the precursor chemistry, pH, water-to-alkoxide ratio, aging time, and drying conditions. Thus, studying sol-gel formation directly leads to the ability to engineer pore volume as a catalyst characterization parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sol-Gel Process — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The sol-gel route to metal oxides is governed by **two parallel reaction families** occurring simultaneously:

### Step 1: Hydrolysis (Reaction with Water)

A metal alkoxide M(OR)ₙ reacts with water to form a hydroxyl group and release an alcohol.

$$\text{M(OR)}_n + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{M(OH)(OR)}_{n-1} + \text{ROH}$$

For tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, Si(OC₂H₅)₄), the classic precursor:

$$\text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_3\text{OH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

### Step 2: Polycondensation (Network Formation)

Two hydroxylated species (or one hydroxylated + one alkoxide) link together, expelling water or alcohol.

- **Alcohol-producing condensation:**
  $$\text{(OR)}_{n-1}\text{M–OH} + \text{RO–M(OR)}_{n-1} \longrightarrow (\text{OR})_{n-1}\text{M–O–M(OR)}_{n-1} + \text{ROH}$$

- **Water-producing condensation:**
  $$2\,\text{(OR)}_{n-1}\text{M–OH} \longrightarrow (\text{OR})_{n-1}\text{M–O–M(OR)}_{n-1} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **M–O–M** linkage formed here is the **oxo-bridge** that builds up the eventual three-dimensional gel network. For TEOS, this forms the **Si–O–Si (siloxane)** backbone.

### Step 3: Gelation and Aging

As the condensation proceeds, oligomers link into colloids (the **sol**), which then aggregate into a 3-D cross-linked **gel**. **Aging** under controlled temperature and pH allows further condensation, strengthening the network and expelling pore liquor.

### Step 4: Drying

- **Xerogel:** dried by evaporation under ambient conditions — capillary forces cause partial pore collapse, lowering pore volume.
- **Aerogel:** supercritically dried (e.g., with CO₂ above 31 °C and 7.38 MPa) — preserves the original pore structure, retaining very high pore volume (~2–10 cm³/g).
- **Cryogel:** freeze-dried — preserves macroporosity.

### Process Variables Affecting Final Pore Volume

| Variable | Effect on Pore Volume (V_p) |
|---|---|
| ↑ H₂O / OR ratio | Increases hydrolysis rate; larger pores, higher V_p |
| ↑ Acid catalyst | Faster gelation; finer pores |
| ↑ Base catalyst | Slower gelation; particulate gel, larger V_p |
| ↑ Aging time | Stiffer network, lower V_p |
| Supercritical drying | Maximizes V_p (preserves gel skeleton) |

---

## 2.2 Pore Volume — Classification of Pores

According to the **IUPAC classification**:

| Pore Type | Pore Width | Examples / Catalysts |
|---|---|---|
| Micropores | **< 2 nm** | Zeolites (ZSM-5, Faujasite), Activated Carbon |
| Mesopores | **2 – 50 nm** | MCM-41, SBA-15, Alumina, Titania |
| Macropores | **> 50 nm** | Pellets, extrudates, monolith supports |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Pore Volume & Sol-Gel Master Table

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}}}{\rho_{\text{liq}}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{m_{\text{cat}}}$ | Pore volume from amount of liquid adsorbed (Gurvich rule) | cm³/g |
| 2 | $V_p = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{pore}}} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{skeleton}}}$ | True pore volume from skeletal (He) and pore-fluid densities | cm³/g |
| 3 | $V_p = V_{\text{liq}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{\text{liq}}}{\rho_{\text{Hg}}}$ | Pore volume via mercury porosimetry (corrected) | cm³/g |
| 4 | $\rho_{\text{pore}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{cat}}}{V_{\text{particle}}}$ | Apparent (particle) density | g/cm³ |
| 5 | $\rho_{\text{skeleton}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{cat}}}{V_{\text{He}}}$ | Skeletal (true) density measured by He pycnometry | g/cm³ |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_p = V_p \cdot \rho_{\text{particle}}$ | Particle porosity (dimensionless, 0 < ε_p < 1) | — |
| 7 | $r_{\text{pore}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_p}{S_{\text{BET}}}$ | Mean pore radius (cylindrical pore model) | nm |
| 8 | $V_{\text{monolayer}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot p}{(1-p/p_0)}$ | BET monolayer capacity equivalent volume | cm³ STP / g |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{skeleton}} - \rho_{\text{pore}}}{\rho_{\text{skeleton}}}$ | Porosity in terms of densities | — |
| 10 | $V_p^{\text{total}} = V_{\text{micro}} + V_{\text{meso}} + V_{\text{macro}}$ | Total pore volume sum (t-plot + BJH + Hg) | cm³/g |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constant for KTU Problems:** The density of liquid N₂ at 77 K used in **Gurvich's rule** is **ρ_liq = 0.808 g/cm³**. Memorize this value.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Refining:** Vp of FCC catalyst particles (≈ **0.4 cm³/g**) is tuned via sol-gel aging to optimize heavy-oil diffusion.
- **Automotive Catalytic Converters:** Sol-gel-derived **CeO₂–ZrO₂** solid solutions with high Vp (~0.5 cm³/g) enhance oxygen storage capacity.
- **Pharmaceutical Catalysis:** Sol-gel mesoporous silicas (MCM-41, Vp ≈ 1.0 cm³/g) act as size-selective drug-delivery carriers.
- **Environmental Catalysis:** TiO₂ aerogels (Vp ≈ 5–10 cm³/g) for photocatalytic VOC abatement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pore Volume from Adsorption Isotherm (Gurvich Rule)

### Background Principle

When a mesoporous solid is exposed to a condensable vapor (e.g., N₂ at 77 K) at a relative pressure **p/p₀ → 1**, all pores become completely filled with **condensed liquid adsorbate**. The volume of this condensed liquid, divided by the liquid's density, gives the total pore volume.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $V_{\text{ads}}$ = total volume of vapor adsorbed at $p/p_0 \rightarrow 1$ (cm³ STP per gram)
- $V_m$ = monolayer volume from BET (cm³ STP per gram)
- $V_{\text{liq}}$ = volume of condensed liquid phase (cm³/g)
- $\rho_{\text{liq}}$ = density of condensed adsorbate (g/cm³)
- $m_{\text{ads}}$ = mass of adsorbed gas (g/g)

**Step A: Convert STP volume to mass of gas adsorbed.**

By the ideal gas law at STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm), 1 mole occupies **22,414 cm³ STP** and contains a mass equal to the molar mass M:

$$m_{\text{ads}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}}}{22{,}414} \cdot M$$

For N₂, M = 28 g/mol, so:

$$m_{\text{ads}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 28}{22{,}414} = V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 1.249 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{g/cm}^3_{\text{STP}}$$

**Step B: Convert the mass of gas to volume of liquid using liquid density.**

The volume of liquid formed by the condensed adsorbate is:

$$V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{ads}}}{\rho_{\text{liq}}}$$

For liquid N₂ at 77 K, $\rho_{\text{liq}} = 0.808 \;\text{g/cm}^3$:

$$V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 1.249 \times 10^{-3}}{0.808}$$

**Step C: Identify the pore volume.**

By the **Gurvich rule**, the condensed liquid volume equals the pore volume:

$$V_p = V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 1.249 \times 10^{-3}}{0.808}$$

This simplifies to the **standard KTU working formula**:

$$\boxed{\,V_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 1.547 \times 10^{-3}}{m_{\text{cat}}}\,} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{cm}^3}{\text{g}_{\text{cat}}}\right]$$

where the factor $1.547 \times 10^{-3}$ is the ratio $(1.249 \times 10^{-3}/0.808)$ accounting for STP-to-liquid conversion.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Pore Volume Calculation

> **Problem:** A 0.500 g sample of sol-gel alumina is subjected to N₂ physisorption at 77 K. The volume of N₂ adsorbed at $p/p_0 = 0.99$ is **220 cm³ STP**. Calculate the pore volume.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the molar volume at STP.

$$V_m^{\text{STP}} = 22{,}414 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the moles of N₂ adsorbed.

$$n = \dfrac{220}{22{,}414} = 9.815 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{mol}$$

**Step 3:** Compute mass of adsorbed N₂.

$$m_{\text{ads}} = n \cdot M_{N_2} = 9.815 \times 10^{-3} \times 28 = 0.2748 \;\text{g}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to volume of liquid N₂ using $\rho_{\text{liq}} = 0.808$ g/cm³.

$$V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{0.2748}{0.808} = 0.3401 \;\text{cm}^3$$

**Step 5:** Normalize by catalyst mass.

$$V_p = \dfrac{0.3401}{0.500} = 0.6802 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_p \approx \mathbf{0.680 \; cm^3/g}$ — indicating a moderately porous mesoporous alumina, consistent with sol-gel-derived γ-Al₂O₃ supports.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Mean Pore Radius (Cylindrical Model)

For an idealized cylindrical pore geometry, the BET surface area $S_{\text{BET}}$ and pore volume $V_p$ are related to the average pore radius $r_p$ as follows.

**Surface of cylinder (per unit length):**

$$S = 2\pi r_p \cdot L$$

**Volume of cylinder (per unit length):**

$$V = \pi r_p^2 \cdot L$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\dfrac{V_p}{S_{\text{BET}}} = \dfrac{\pi r_p^2 L}{2\pi r_p L} = \dfrac{r_p}{2}$$

Solving for $r_p$:

$$\boxed{\,r_p = \dfrac{2 V_p}{S_{\text{BET}}}\,}$$

For the worked alumina example above, if $S_{\text{BET}} = 200$ m²/g = $200 \times 10^4$ cm²/g = $2 \times 10^6$ cm²/g:

$$r_p = \dfrac{2 \times 0.680}{2 \times 10^6} = 6.80 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{cm} = \mathbf{6.80 \; nm}$$

This places the alumina firmly in the **mesoporous regime (2–50 nm)**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Porosity from Densities

Let:
- $\rho_{\text{sk}}$ = skeletal (true) density from He pycnometry
- $\rho_{\text{pr}}$ = particle density from Hg pycnometry
- $V_{\text{sk}} = m/\rho_{\text{sk}}$ = volume of solid skeleton
- $V_{\text{pr}} = m/\rho_{\text{pr}}$ = total particle volume

The pore volume per gram:

$$V_p = V_{\text{pr}} - V_{\text{sk}} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{pr}}} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{sk}}}$$

Particle porosity:

$$\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{V_p}{V_{\text{pr}}} = \dfrac{(1/\rho_{\text{pr}}) - (1/\rho_{\text{sk}})}{1/\rho_{\text{pr}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{pr}}}{\rho_{\text{sk}}}$$

Equivalent expression using skeletal density:

$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon_p = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{pr}}}{\rho_{\text{sk}}}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,V_p = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{pr}}} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{sk}}}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Porosity from Density Data

> **Problem:** A sol-gel silica pellet has $\rho_{\text{sk}} = 2.20$ g/cm³ and $\rho_{\text{pr}} = 0.92$ g/cm³. Compute (a) pore volume, and (b) particle porosity.

### Solution

**(a) Pore Volume:**

$$V_p = \dfrac{1}{0.92} - \dfrac{1}{2.20} = 1.087 - 0.4545 = 0.6325 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**(b) Particle Porosity:**

$$\varepsilon_p = 1 - \dfrac{0.92}{2.20} = 1 - 0.4182 = 0.5818 \approx \mathbf{58.2\%}$$

This high porosity (~58%) is characteristic of well-aged sol-gel xerogels.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Underlying Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Microporous catalyst (zeolite) | **t-plot method + Dubinin–Radushkevich** | $V_{\text{micro}}$ from t-plot intercept |
| Mesoporous catalyst (alumina, MCM-41) | **BET + BJH at p/p₀ → 1 (Gurvich)** | $V_p = V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 1.547 \times 10^{-3} / m$ |
| Macroporous pellet (extrudate) | **Mercury porosimetry** | $V_p$ from cumulative Hg intrusion |
| Quick bulk estimate | **He & Hg pycnometry** | $V_p = 1/\rho_{\text{pr}} - 1/\rho_{\text{sk}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sol-Gel Process Flow (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the complete sol-gel processing sequence and its influence on pore volume, mapped as a **block-level functional architecture flow** (safe rendering guaranteed).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Precursor: Metal Alkoxide M OR n] --> B[Hydrolysis with H2O]
    B --> C[Hydroxyl Intermediate M OH OR n-1]
    C --> D[Polycondensation]
    D --> E[Sol: Colloidal Suspension]
    E --> F[Gelation: 3D Network Forms]
    F --> G[Aging: Strengthening Network]
    G --> H{Drying Method}
    H --> H1[Ambient Drying]
    H --> H2[Supercritical Drying]
    H --> H3[Freeze Drying]
    H1 --> I1[Xerogel: Vp 0.2 to 0.8 cm3 per g]
    H2 --> I2[Aerogel: Vp 2 to 10 cm3 per g]
    H3 --> I3[Cryogel: Vp 1 to 3 cm3 per g]
    I1 --> J[Calcination]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Final Mesoporous Catalyst Support]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The drying branch is the **single most decisive factor** controlling final pore volume — supercritical drying preserves the original network geometry, while ambient drying collapses pores via capillary forces.

---

## 4.2 Catalyst Pore Architecture — Hierarchical Structure

This block diagram illustrates how pore volume is partitioned across the hierarchical pore network of a typical sol-gel catalyst pellet.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MACRO["Macropores greater than 50 nm"]
        MP[Pellet and Interparticle Voids]
    end
    subgraph MESO["Mesopores 2 to 50 nm"]
        MS[Primary Aggregate Pores]
    end
    subgraph MICRO["Micropores less than 2 nm"]
        MC[Intraparticle Cages and Windows]
    end
    MP --> MS
    MS --> MC
    MC --> PORE[(Total Pore Volume Vp cm3 per g)]
    MS --> PORE
    MP --> PORE
```

---

## 4.3 Pore Volume Measurement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Sol Gel Catalyst Sample] --> S1[Outgas at 200 to 300 degrees C]
    S1 --> S2[N2 Physisorption at 77 K]
    S2 --> S3[Record Adsorption Isotherm]
    S3 --> S4[Apply BET at p over p0 0.05 to 0.30]
    S4 --> S5[Obtain SBET and Vm]
    S3 --> S6[Read Vads at p over p0 0.99]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Gurvich Rule]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Vp cm3 per g]
    S5 --> S9[Compute Mean Pore Radius rp]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Report Catalyst Texture: SBET, Vp, rp]
```

---

## 4.4 Sol-Gel Chemistry at the Molecular Level (Reaction Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Si OC2H5 4 TEOS] -->|H2O acid or base| A2[Si OC2H5 3 OH]
    A2 --> A3[Si OC2H5 2 OH 2]
    A3 --> A4[Si O Si Siloxane Bridge]
    A4 --> A5[Crosslinked SiO2 Network]
    A5 --> A6[Wet Gel Containing EtOH and H2O]
    A6 --> A7[Porous SiO2 Catalyst Support]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define sol-gel process and list any two precursors commonly used for silica synthesis.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The sol-gel process is a low-temperature wet-chemical route for the synthesis of inorganic oxides in which a metal alkoxide (or salt) undergoes hydrolysis and polycondensation reactions to form a colloidal suspension (**sol**) that gradually cross-links into a rigid, three-dimensional solid network (**gel**) enclosing a continuous liquid phase.

Two common precursors used for silica synthesis are:

1. **Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS):** Si(OC₂H₅)₄ — the most widely used silicon alkoxide.
2. **Tetramethyl orthosilicate (TMOS):** Si(OCH₃)₄ — higher reactivity, faster gelation.

---

### Question A2
**Q: What is pore volume? State the IUPAC classification of pores with size ranges.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Pore volume (V_p)** is defined as the total volume of pores accessible per unit mass of a porous solid catalyst, typically expressed in **cm³/g**.

**IUPAC Classification of Pores:**

| Type | Pore Width |
|---|---|
| Micropores | Less than 2 nm |
| Mesopores | 2 to 50 nm |
| Macropores | Greater than 50 nm |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the sol-gel process for the synthesis of a metal oxide catalyst support. Discuss the role of hydrolysis, condensation, aging, and drying in determining the final pore structure. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for pore volume using the **Gurvich rule** from gas adsorption data, clearly defining all terms and stating the standard value of liquid nitrogen density. A sol-gel silica sample (mass 0.250 g) adsorbs 180 cm³ STP of N₂ at $p/p_0 = 0.99$. Compute its pore volume.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

The sol-gel synthesis of a metal oxide support (e.g., SiO₂ from TEOS) proceeds through four sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis:** The metal alkoxide M(OR)ₙ reacts with water to yield a hydroxyl intermediate:

$$\text{M(OR)}_n + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{M(OH)(OR)}_{n-1} + \text{ROH}$$

**Stage 2 — Polycondensation:** Hydroxylated species link via oxo-bridges (M–O–M), releasing water or alcohol. For TEOS:

$$\equiv \text{Si–OH} + \text{HO–Si} \equiv \longrightarrow \equiv \text{Si–O–Si} \equiv + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\equiv \text{Si–OH} + \text{RO–Si} \equiv \longrightarrow \equiv \text{Si–O–Si} \equiv + \text{ROH}$$

**Stage 3 — Gelation and Aging:** Continued condensation converts the sol of colloidal particles into a 3-D gel. Aging allows Ostwald ripening and syneresis, strengthening the network and setting the incipient pore geometry.

**Stage 4 — Drying:**
- **Ambient drying → xerogel:** Capillary forces partially collapse pores; pore volume typically 0.2–0.8 cm³/g.
- **Supercritical drying → aerogel:** Pore structure preserved; pore volume up to 5–10 cm³/g.

**Role of Each Step:**
- **Hydrolysis** controls the number of reactive –OH groups.
- **Condensation** determines the cross-link density and primary particle size.
- **Aging** stiffens the network, preventing collapse.
- **Drying** is the single most decisive step in retaining the original pore volume.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Derivation (Recap from Section 3.1):**

Let $V_{\text{ads}}$ = volume of N₂ adsorbed at $p/p_0 \rightarrow 1$ in cm³ STP/g.

Step 1 — Mass of adsorbed N₂:

$$m_{\text{ads}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}}}{22{,}414} \cdot M_{N_2} = V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 1.249 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{g}$$

Step 2 — Volume of liquid N₂ (using $\rho_{\text{liq}} = 0.808$ g/cm³):

$$V_{\text{liq}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{ads}}}{\rho_{\text{liq}}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 1.249 \times 10^{-3}}{0.808}$$

Step 3 — Apply Gurvich rule ($V_p = V_{\text{liq}}$):

$$\boxed{\,V_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{ads}} \cdot 1.547 \times 10^{-3}}{m_{\text{cat}}}\,}$$

**Numerical Substitution:**
- $V_{\text{ads}}$ at $p/p_0 = 0.99$ = 180 cm³ STP
- $m_{\text{cat}} = 0.250$ g

$$V_p = \dfrac{180 \times 1.547 \times 10^{-3}}{0.250} = \dfrac{0.2785}{0.250} = 1.114 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_p \approx \mathbf{1.11 \; cm^3/g}$ — characteristic of a high-pore-volume mesoporous silica (likely an aerogel-grade or SBA-15 type material).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the IUPAC classification of pores. With suitable diagrams, describe the **t-plot method** and **BJH method** for determining micropore and mesopore volumes respectively. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relation between mean pore radius, pore volume, and BET surface area assuming a cylindrical pore model. A catalyst has $V_p = 0.50$ cm³/g and $S_{\text{BET}} = 250$ m²/g. Compute the mean pore radius and classify the pore regime.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**IUPAC Classification:** Micropores (< 2 nm), Mesopores (2–50 nm), Macropores (> 50 nm).

**t-plot Method (Micropore Volume):**

The **t-plot** (de Boer / Lippens) is a graphical technique in which the statistical thickness **t** of the adsorbed film (calculated from a reference isotherm on a non-porous solid of the same chemistry) is plotted against the volume adsorbed $V_{\text{ads}}$.

- **Linear region passing through origin** → no micropores; only external/mesopore surface.
- **Positive intercept on the V-axis** → presence of micropores; intercept = micropore volume $V_{\text{micro}}$.
- **Downward deviation** → pore filling via capillary condensation in mesopores.

From the linear region slope:

$$S_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{\text{slope} \cdot 0.001547}{0.162} \;\text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

**BJH Method (Mesopore Volume & Distribution):**

The **Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH)** method, derived from the **Kelvin equation**, calculates the mesopore size distribution by analyzing the desorption branch of the isotherm:

$$\ln \dfrac{p}{p_0} = -\dfrac{2 \gamma V_m \cos\theta}{r_k \, RT}$$

where $\gamma$ = surface tension, $V_m$ = molar volume of liquid adsorbate, $\theta$ = contact angle, and $r_k$ = Kelvin radius. Combined with the statistical thickness, the true pore radius $r_p$ is:

$$r_p = r_k + t$$

The cumulative BJH pore volume between two pressure steps gives the mesopore volume $V_{\text{meso}}$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Derivation (Recap from Section 3.3):**

For a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$ and length $L$:

$$\text{Surface area} = 2 \pi r_p L$$

$$\text{Pore volume} = \pi r_p^2 L$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\dfrac{V_p}{S_{\text{BET}}} = \dfrac{\pi r_p^2 L}{2\pi r_p L} = \dfrac{r_p}{2}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\,r_p = \dfrac{2 V_p}{S_{\text{BET}}}\,}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V_p = 0.50$ cm³/g, $S_{\text{BET}} = 250$ m²/g = $250 \times 10^4$ cm²/g = $2.5 \times 10^6$ cm²/g.

$$r_p = \dfrac{2 \times 0.50}{2.5 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{1.0}{2.5 \times 10^6} = 4.0 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{cm}$$

Convert to nm (1 cm = $10^7$ nm):

$$r_p = 4.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^7 = \mathbf{4.0 \; nm}$$

**Classification:** Since 2 nm < $r_p$ = 4 nm < 50 nm, this catalyst is **mesoporous**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by sample mass.** Many students compute $V_{\text{liq}}$ but forget to normalize by $m_{\text{cat}}$, losing 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Wrong liquid N₂ density.** Use **0.808 g/cm³** at 77 K, *not* 1.0 g/cm³ (which is water at 273 K).
> 3. **Unit mismatch in $r_p$ formula.** $V_p$ must be in **cm³/g** and $S_{\text{BET}}$ in **cm²/g** (not m²/g) when applying $r_p = 2V_p/S_{\text{BET}}$ directly. Convert m² to cm² by multiplying by $10^4$.
> 4. **Confusing Gurvich rule with BET monolayer volume.** $V_m$ is the monolayer; $V_p$ is at $p/p_0 \rightarrow 1$ — they are not equal.
> 5. **Mixing up xerogel/aerogel definitions.** Xerogel = ambient dried; Aerogel = supercritically dried. Marks are deducted for swapping these.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Sol-gel process** = hydrolysis + polycondensation of metal alkoxides → sol → gel → dried oxide.
- The **M–O–M oxo-bridge** is the building block of every sol-gel oxide network.
- **Drying method controls pore volume:** xerogel < cryogel < aerogel.
- **Pore volume units:** cm³/g (always per gram of catalyst).
- **IUPAC pore classes:** Micro (< 2 nm) | Meso (2–50 nm) | Macro (> 50 nm).
- **Gurvich rule** (must memorize): $V_p = (V_{\text{ads}} \times 1.547 \times 10^{-3}) / m_{\text{cat}}$.
- **Liquid N₂ density at 77 K = 0.808 g/cm³** — required for all Gurvich-rule problems.
- **Mean pore radius (cylindrical model):** $r_p = 2 V_p / S_{\text{BET}}$.
- **Porosity from densities:** $\varepsilon_p = 1 - \rho_{\text{pr}} / \rho_{\text{sk}}$; pore volume $V_p = 1/\rho_{\text{pr}} - 1/\rho_{\text{sk}}$.
- **t-plot method → micropore volume**; **BJH method → mesopore volume and distribution**.
- **BET analysis at relative pressure 0.05–0.30** gives $S_{\text{BET}}$; **Vp read at $p/p_0 \approx 0.99$**.
- Higher **H₂O : alkoxide ratio** → larger pores, higher $V_p$ (but weaker network).
- **Acid catalysis → fine pores; base catalysis → coarse, particulate gel.**
- Industrial sol-gel catalysts span $V_p$ ≈ **0.1 (zeolite) → 10 cm³/g (aerogel)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pore Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT633 - HETEROGENEOUS REACTIONS AND CATALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pore Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In heterogeneous catalysis, the vast majority of industrially relevant solid catalysts (such as zeolites, silica-alumina, activated carbon, and supported metals) are **porous solids**. The **Pore Size** refers to the characteristic physical dimension (typically diameter, $d_p$, or radius, $r_p$) of the internal void spaces, channels, and cavities within a catalyst particle through which reactant molecules must travel to reach the active sites located on the internal surface.

The internal architecture of a porous catalyst is characterized by three interconnected parameters:
1. **Pore Size ($d_p$ or $r_p$)** – the physical dimension of the void.
2. **Specific Surface Area ($S_g$)** – the total internal area per unit mass (typically $m^2/g$).
3. **Pore Volume ($V_p$)** – the total void volume per unit mass (typically $cm^3/g$).

According to the **IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) classification**, pore sizes are strictly divided into three categories based on their internal width:

| IUPAC Classification | Pore Width ($d_p$) | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Micropores** | $d_p < \mathbf{2\ nm}$ | Zeolites (ZSM-5, Faujasite), Activated Carbon, MOFs |
| **Mesopores** | $2\ \text{nm} \leq d_p \leq \mathbf{50\ nm}$ | Silica Gel, Alumina, MCM-41, Titania |
| **Macropores** | $d_p > \mathbf{50\ nm}$ | Cordierite monoliths, Extrudates, Pellet supports |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pore size is a CRITICAL design variable because it governs molecular diffusion, reactant accessibility, and **shape selectivity** (a phenomenon exclusively observed in microporous zeolites where only molecules fitting within the pore aperture can react).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a multi-story underground parking garage built for cars.

- **Macropores** are like the wide, open ramps and driving lanes ($> 50\ nm$). Large trucks and cars can move freely and quickly. There is not much "wall surface" available to stick posters on.
- **Mesopores** are like the regular parking slots ($2-50\ nm$). They offer a good balance – cars (medium-sized molecules) can park (adsorb/react), and the garage walls have plenty of space for posters (active sites).
- **Micropores** are like the narrow cracks between tightly packed cars or narrow stairwells ($< 2\ nm$). Only small molecules (like a person walking through) can squeeze in. The total wall area is enormous (you can stick posters on every inch), but only small "people-shaped" molecules are allowed entry. A large molecule is simply **excluded** – this is **shape selectivity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Smaller pores $\Rightarrow$ Higher surface area, BUT greater diffusion resistance. Larger pores $\Rightarrow$ Easier transport, BUT lower surface area. **Pore size optimization is always a trade-off** between catalytic activity (surface area) and mass transfer (diffusion).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pore Size Matters: The Engineering Rationale

Pore size directly controls the following four critical phenomena in heterogeneous catalysis:

1. **Specific Surface Area ($S_g$):** For a given particle of fixed mass and density, decreasing the pore size dramatically increases the available internal surface area. The relationship is inversely proportional:
$$S_g \propto \frac{1}{d_p}$$

2. **Internal Diffusional Resistance:** Reactants must navigate from the bulk fluid, through the external boundary layer, and down the tortuous pore network to reach internal active sites. The **effective diffusivity** ($D_{eff}$) depends strongly on the ratio of molecular size to pore size (the **Knox parameter**, $\lambda = \sigma/d_p$, where $\sigma$ is the molecular collision diameter).

3. **Shape Selectivity** (microporous systems only):
   - **Reactant Selectivity:** Bulky reactant molecules are excluded.
   - **Product Selectivity:** Only molecules small enough to diffuse out appear in the product.
   - **Transition State Selectivity:** Bulky intermediate transition states cannot form inside narrow pores.

4. **Catalyst Deactivation:** Pore blocking by coke (carbonaceous deposits) and metal sintering are directly influenced by the pore size.

## 2.2 Pore Structure Geometry Models

The actual shape of pores within a catalyst particle is irregular. However, for mathematical modeling, pores are idealized into three standard geometries:

- **Cylindrical Pores:** Open-ended tubes of uniform diameter (most common assumption for mesopores).
- **Slit-Shaped Pores:** Parallel plates separated by a distance (model used for activated carbon).
- **Ink-Bottle Pores:** Wide cavities connected to the surface by narrow necks (cause hysteresis in adsorption isotherms).

## 2.3 The Thiele Modulus & Internal Effectiveness Factor

This is the most important mathematical relationship connecting pore size to catalytic reaction rate.

The **Thiele Modulus ($\phi$)** is a dimensionless number that compares the rate of surface reaction to the rate of diffusion through the pore. For a **first-order irreversible reaction** in a **cylindrical pore** of radius $r_p$ and length $L$:

$$\phi = L \sqrt{\frac{k_s \cdot S_v}{D_{eff}}}$$

Where:
- $L$ = pore length (m)
- $k_s$ = intrinsic surface reaction rate constant (m/s)
- $S_v$ = internal surface area per unit pore volume ($m^2/m^3$) = $2/r_p$ for cylindrical pores
- $D_{eff}$ = effective diffusivity inside the pore ($m^2/s$)

The **Internal Effectiveness Factor ($\eta$)** quantifies how much of the catalyst is actually being utilized:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Actual Reaction Rate with Diffusion Resistance}}{\text{Rate if Entire Surface Were Exposed to Bulk Concentration}}$$

For a cylindrical pore geometry:

$$\eta = \frac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi} \quad \text{(for slab/flat plate: } \eta = \frac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi} \text{, for sphere: } \eta = \frac{3}{\phi}\left[\frac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \frac{1}{\phi}\right] \text{)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IUPAC Micropore Limit | $d_p < 2 \times 10^{-9}$ | m (or nm) |
| 2 | IUPAC Mesopore Range | $2\ \text{nm} \leq d_p \leq 50\ \text{nm}$ | nm |
| 3 | IUPAC Macropore Limit | $d_p > 50\ \text{nm}$ | nm |
| 4 | Surface-to-Volume Ratio (Cylinder) | $S_v = \dfrac{2}{r_p}$ | $m^2/m^3$ |
| 5 | Thiele Modulus (1st order) | $\phi = L\sqrt{\dfrac{k_s S_v}{D_{eff}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Thiele Modulus (Cylinder, spherical grain form) | $\phi_s = R\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_p k}{D_{eff}}}$ | dimensionless (R = grain radius) |
| 7 | Effectiveness Factor (Slab, 1st order) | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}$ | $0 < \eta \leq 1$ |
| 8 | Effectiveness Factor (Sphere, 1st order) | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi}\left[\dfrac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \dfrac{1}{\phi}\right]$ | $0 < \eta \leq 1$ |
| 9 | Weisz-Prater Criterion | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{R_{obs} \rho_p L^2}{C_{s} D_{eff}}$ | dimensionless; $C_{WP} \ll 1 \Rightarrow$ no diffusion limitation |
| 10 | Knudsen Diffusivity | $D_K = 97 \cdot r_p \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{M_w}}$ | $m^2/s$ (r in m, T in K, M in kg/kmol) |
| 11 | Mean Free Path | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi\,\sigma^2\, n}$ | m (relates molecular size to regime) |
| 12 | Total Porosity | $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{V_{pore}}{V_{particle}}$ | dimensionless, $0 < \varepsilon_p < 1$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refining (FCC – Fluid Catalytic Cracking):** Zeolite Y (Faujasite) with supercages of $\sim 1.2\ \text{nm}$ selectively cracks long-chain hydrocarbons while resisting coke formation.
- **Automotive Catalytic Converters:** Cordierite monolith washcoated with $\gamma$-alumina (mesoporous, $d_p \approx 10-20\ \text{nm}$) loaded with Pt/Pd/Rh. Mesopores provide surface area without choking diffusion of exhaust gases.
- **Ammonia Synthesis (Haber-Bosch):** Fused iron catalyst with pores in the **macropore/mesopore** range ($50-200\ \text{nm}$) – because $N_2$ and $H_2$ are very small gases, microporosity is not required.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effectiveness Factor for a First-Order Reaction in a Cylindrical Pore (Slab Approximation)

We consider a catalyst slab of thickness $2L$. Reactant A diffuses from the external surface (at $x = L$, concentration $C_{As}$) into the pore and reacts on the internal surface following first-order kinetics. We solve the steady-state diffusion-reaction equation.

### Step 1: Set up the differential mole balance on a differential slice $dx$

At steady state, the diffusive flux entering at $x$ equals the flux leaving at $x + dx$ minus the amount consumed by reaction within the slice.

$$-D_{eff} \frac{dC_A}{dx}\bigg|_x = -D_{eff} \frac{dC_A}{dx}\bigg|_{x+dx} + (-r_A^{''})\, dS_v\, dx$$

### Step 2: Apply Fick's First Law and First-Order Surface Kinetics

The rate of consumption per unit surface area follows first-order kinetics:

$$-r_A^{''} = k_s \cdot C_A$$

Combining with the differential balance yields:

$$D_{eff}\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - k_s S_v C_A = 0$$

### Step 3: Rearrange into the Standard Second-Order ODE Form

Divide through by $D_{eff}$:

$$\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \frac{k_s S_v}{D_{eff}} C_A = 0$$

Define the **Thiele Modulus squared**:

$$\phi^2 \equiv \frac{k_s S_v}{D_{eff}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \phi = L\sqrt{\frac{k_s S_v}{D_{eff}}}$$

So the ODE becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - \frac{\phi^2}{L^2} C_A = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve the ODE

The general solution is:

$$C_A(x) = A \cosh\!\left(\frac{\phi x}{L}\right) + B \sinh\!\left(\frac{\phi x}{L}\right)$$

### Step 5: Apply Boundary Conditions (Symmetry)

By symmetry, the concentration profile is symmetric about $x = 0$ (the centerline of the slab). Thus, the gradient at $x = 0$ must be zero:

$$\frac{dC_A}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad B = 0$$

So:

$$C_A(x) = A \cosh\!\left(\frac{\phi x}{L}\right)$$

At the external surface $x = L$, the concentration is the bulk surface concentration $C_{As}$:

$$C_{As} = A \cosh(\phi) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A = \frac{C_{As}}{\cosh(\phi)}$$

The complete concentration profile is therefore:

$$C_A(x) = C_{As} \frac{\cosh\!\left(\dfrac{\phi x}{L}\right)}{\cosh(\phi)}$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Actual Rate of Reaction

Integrate the local reaction rate across the entire slab (factor 2 because of symmetry):

$$r_{A,actual} = 2 \int_{0}^{L} k_s S_v C_A(x)\, dx = 2 k_s S_v \frac{C_{As}}{\cosh(\phi)} \int_{0}^{L} \cosh\!\left(\frac{\phi x}{L}\right) dx$$

Evaluate the integral: $\int_{0}^{L} \cosh\!\left(\frac{\phi x}{L}\right) dx = \frac{L}{\phi}\sinh(\phi)$

So:

$$r_{A,actual} = 2 k_s S_v \frac{C_{As}}{\cosh(\phi)} \cdot \frac{L}{\phi} \sinh(\phi) = 2 k_s S_v L \cdot C_{As} \cdot \frac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}$$

### Step 7: Calculate the Rate if There Were No Diffusion Limitation

If the entire internal surface saw concentration $C_{As}$:

$$r_{A,ideal} = k_s S_v \cdot (2L) \cdot C_{As}$$

### Step 8: Form the Effectiveness Factor

$$\eta = \frac{r_{A,actual}}{r_{A,ideal}} = \frac{2 k_s S_v L \cdot C_{As} \cdot \dfrac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}}{2 k_s S_v L \cdot C_{As}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}}$$

### Step 9: Limiting Case Analysis (Examiner's Favorite!)

**Case A: $\phi \ll 1$ (Small Thiele Modulus — Reaction-Limited Regime)**
For small $\phi$, $\tanh(\phi) \approx \phi - \phi^3/3 + \cdots$. Therefore:

$$\eta \approx \frac{\phi - \phi^3/3}{\phi} = 1 - \frac{\phi^2}{3} \approx 1$$

This means the entire catalyst is utilized – no diffusion limitation. The reaction rate is low, and diffusion is fast by comparison.

**Case B: $\phi \gg 1$ (Large Thiele Modulus — Diffusion-Limited Regime)**
For large $\phi$, $\tanh(\phi) \rightarrow 1$. Therefore:

$$\eta \approx \frac{1}{\phi}$$

This means only a thin outer shell of the catalyst is utilized. The interior is "wasted" because reactants cannot reach it. The observed rate $\propto \sqrt{k_s}$ rather than $k_s$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relation Between Pore Radius and Knudsen Diffusivity

When the pore diameter is comparable to or smaller than the mean free path of the molecules, gas-surface collisions dominate over gas-gas collisions. This is the **Knudsen diffusion regime**. From kinetic theory of gases:

Consider a molecule of mass $m$ and diameter $\sigma$ moving in a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$. Its average velocity from Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics is:

$$\bar{v} = \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M_w}}$$

The molecule travels an average distance of $2r_p$ before hitting the wall. The Knudsen diffusivity is $D_K = \frac{1}{3}\bar{v} \lambda_{wall}$, where $\lambda_{wall} \approx 2r_p$ (mean free path limited by pore wall):

$$D_K = \frac{1}{3} \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M_w}} \cdot (2r_p) = \frac{2}{3} r_p \sqrt{\frac{8RT}{\pi M_w}}$$

Simplifying with the numerical constant:

$$\boxed{D_K = 97\, r_p \sqrt{\frac{T}{M_w}} \quad \text{(with } r_p \text{ in m, T in K, M in kg/kmol)}}$$

This shows that **Knudsen diffusivity scales linearly with pore radius** – doubling the pore size doubles the internal diffusion coefficient.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Classify pores according to IUPAC. Give one example catalyst material for each class.**

**Model Answer:**
According to the **IUPAC classification**, pores in solid catalysts are categorized based on their internal width ($d_p$):
1. **Micropores** ($d_p < 2\ \text{nm}$): Possess extremely high surface area and exhibit shape selectivity. *Example:* Zeolites (e.g., ZSM-5, Faujasite-Y), Activated Carbon, MOFs.
2. **Mesopores** ($2\ \text{nm} \leq d_p \leq 50\ \text{nm}$): Offer a balance between surface area and diffusion. *Example:* Mesoporous silica (MCM-41, SBA-15), $\gamma$-alumina.
3. **Macropores** ($d_p > 50\ \text{nm}$): Provide low-resistance transport channels. *Example:* Cordierite monolith supports, pores within extruded catalyst pellets.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Thiele modulus and state its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Thiele modulus ($\phi$)** is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of the surface reaction rate to the rate of diffusion through the catalyst pore. For a first-order reaction in a slab geometry:

$$\phi = L \sqrt{\frac{k_s S_v}{D_{eff}}}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- When $\phi \ll 1$: Reaction is the rate-limiting step; the entire internal surface is utilized ($\eta \to 1$).
- When $\phi \gg 1$: Diffusion is the rate-limiting step; only a thin outer shell of the catalyst is effective ($\eta \to 1/\phi$).
- It quantifies the extent of internal mass-transfer limitation in porous catalysts.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the effectiveness factor for a first-order irreversible reaction occurring in a porous catalyst pellet modeled as a slab of thickness $2L$. Assume isothermal conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order catalytic reaction ($k_s = 0.05\ \text{m/s}$) takes place on the internal surface of a porous cylindrical catalyst pellet. The pellet has a radius $R = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, internal surface area per unit volume $S_v = 4 \times 10^5\ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$, and the effective diffusivity inside the pore is $D_{eff} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate: (i) the Thiele modulus, and (ii) the internal effectiveness factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Refer to **Section 3.1** for the complete derivation. Summary of key steps:

- **Mole Balance:** $D_{eff}\frac{d^2 C_A}{dx^2} - k_s S_v C_A = 0$
- **Define:** $\phi^2 = \frac{k_s S_v L^2}{D_{eff}}$
- **Solution:** $C_A(x) = C_{As}\dfrac{\cosh(\phi x / L)}{\cosh(\phi)}$
- **Effectiveness Factor:** $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}$
- **Limiting cases:** $\eta \to 1$ for $\phi \ll 1$; $\eta \to 1/\phi$ for $\phi \gg 1$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Calculate the Thiele modulus for a spherical grain form**
$$\phi = R \sqrt{\frac{k_s S_v}{D_{eff}}}$$

$$\phi = (5 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{\frac{(0.05)(4 \times 10^{5})}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\phi = (5 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}} = (5 \times 10^{-3})\sqrt{1.667 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$\phi = (5 \times 10^{-3})(1.291 \times 10^{5}) = 645.5$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Effectiveness Factor**
Since $\phi = 645.5 \gg 3$, we are in the strong diffusion-limited regime:
$$\eta \approx \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{1}{645.5} = 1.549 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**
Only ~0.15% of the catalyst's internal surface area is effectively utilized. The reaction is severely diffusion-limited. The catalyst pellet is far too large for the given reaction kinetics; **reducing pellet size or using a mesoporous/macroporous support with larger $D_{eff}$** would dramatically improve utilization.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the three different idealized pore geometries used to model catalyst pore structure. Discuss how pore size affects Knudsen diffusivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gaseous reactant with molecular weight $M_w = 44\ \text{kg/kmol}$ diffuses through a catalyst pore of radius $r_p = 50 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$ at temperature $T = 773\ \text{K}$. Compute: (i) the Knudsen diffusivity, and (ii) whether Knudsen or molecular diffusion dominates, given the total pressure is $1\ \text{atm}$ and the molecular diameter $\sigma = 3.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The three idealized pore geometries (refer to **Section 4.4** for diagrams) are:

1. **Cylindrical pores** – uniform open-ended tubes; $S_v = 2/r_p$. Used for mesoporous oxides.
2. **Slit-shaped pores** – parallel plates separated by a uniform width. Used for activated carbons.
3. **Ink-bottle pores** – wide cavities with narrow necks. Cause hysteresis loops in $N_2$ adsorption-desorption isotherms (Type H2 hysteresis, IUPAC).

**Effect of pore size on Knudsen diffusivity:**
$$D_K = 97 \cdot r_p \sqrt{\frac{T}{M_w}}$$
$D_K$ is **directly proportional to the pore radius $r_p$**. Therefore, doubling the pore size doubles the Knudsen diffusivity. In micropores ($< 2\ \text{nm}$), Knudsen diffusion becomes extremely slow; configurational diffusion (molecules squeezing through) takes over.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Knudsen Diffusivity Calculation**

Using $D_K = 97\, r_p \sqrt{T / M_w}$:

$$D_K = 97 \times (50 \times 10^{-9}) \sqrt{\frac{773}{44}}$$

$$D_K = 97 \times (5 \times 10^{-8}) \times \sqrt{17.57}$$

$$D_K = (4.85 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4.192 = 2.033 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Molecular Diffusivity Estimate**
At 1 atm and 773 K, the binary molecular diffusivity of a typical gas is on the order of $D_{AB} \approx 1 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (can be computed via Chapman-Enskog). 

Since $D_K \approx 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ is **comparable to or larger than** $D_{AB}$, and given that the pore is in the mesopore range (50 nm is the boundary), both Knudsen and molecular diffusion contribute. The effective diffusivity is given by:

$$\frac{1}{D_{eff}} = \frac{1}{D_{AB}} + \frac{1}{D_K}$$

**Conclusion:** Neither mechanism fully dominates at 50 nm pore radius and atmospheric pressure; this is the **transition regime**. For confirmation, one can check the Knudsen number ($Kn = \lambda / d_p$). With $\lambda$ at 1 atm and 773 K on the order of $\sim 0.3\ \mu m$, $Kn \approx 0.3/0.1 = 3$, confirming the Knudsen-dominated regime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Units check is mandatory:** Using $r_p$ in nm while expecting $D_K$ in $m^2/s$ gives wrong answers. Always convert $r_p$ to **meters**.
> 2. **Geometry trap:** The Thiele modulus for a *sphere* is $\phi = R\sqrt{\rho_p k / D_{eff}}$, but for a *slab* it is $\phi = L\sqrt{k_s S_v / D_{eff}}$. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting $\eta$ limits:** Writing "$\eta = 1/\phi$" without mentioning the asymptotic condition ($\phi > 3$) loses marks.
> 4. **Effectiveness factor is dimensionless** — never attach units to $\eta$ or $\phi$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **IUPAC classification:** Micro ($< 2\ \text{nm}$), Meso ($2-50\ \text{nm}$), Macro ($> 50\ \text{nm}$).
- ✅ **Smaller pores $\Rightarrow$ Higher surface area**, but **lower Knudsen diffusivity**.
- ✅ **Thiele modulus** $\phi$ is the ratio of reaction rate to diffusion rate; $\phi \gg 1$ means diffusion-limited.
- ✅ **Effectiveness factor** $\eta = \tanh(\phi)/\phi$ (slab); $\eta = 1$ (no limitation); $\eta \approx 1/\phi$ (severe limitation).
- ✅ **Knudsen diffusivity** scales linearly with pore radius: $D_K = 97 r_p \sqrt{T/M_w}$.
- ✅ **Weisz-Prater criterion** ($C_{WP} \ll 1$) is used experimentally to confirm absence of internal diffusion limitations.
- ✅ **Shape selectivity** (reactant, product, transition state) is exclusive to **microporous** zeolites.
- ✅ **Ink-bottle pores** produce hysteresis loops in $N_2$ adsorption isotherms.
- ✅ **Mesopores** (e.g., MCM-41, $\gamma$-alumina) are the workhorses of the chemical industry due to their balance of area and transport.
- ✅ **Industrial tip:** For reactions with very fast kinetics, use **bimodal pore distributions** – macropores for transport + mesopores/micropores for active sites.
